cases.title: Sudden Low Back Pain After Lifting
cases.chiefComplaint: Low back pain for 4 days that started while lifting a heavy box at work.
cases.history: A 34-year-old warehouse worker presents with low back pain that began suddenly four days ago while lifting a 40-pound box with a twisting motion. The pain is dull and aching, localized to the lower lumbar paraspinal region, and worsens with bending and prolonged sitting. It improves with lying down and over-the-counter ibuprofen. He denies radiation below the knee, numbness, tingling, or weakness in the legs. He reports no bowel or bladder incontinence, no saddle anesthesia, no fever, chills, or night sweats, and no unintentional weight loss. He has no history of cancer, IV drug use, or recent infection. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: ibuprofen 400 mg as needed. No known drug allergies. He smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily and drinks socially. He works full-time in shipping and is otherwise active. Family history is noncontributory. This is his first episode of significant back pain, and he is anxious because he cannot miss work.
cases.exam: Alert and in no acute distress, ambulates with a slightly guarded antalgic gait. Lumbar paraspinal muscles are tender to palpation bilaterally with palpable spasm; range of motion is limited by pain on flexion. No midline spinous process tenderness or step-off. Straight-leg raise is negative bilaterally. Strength is 5/5 in all major lower-extremity muscle groups. Sensation is intact to light touch throughout. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are 2+ and symmetric. Babinski downgoing. Normal rectal tone and perianal sensation. No costovertebral angle tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature in this presentation, if present, would be a red flag warranting urgent imaging or further workup?
options: Pain that worsens with bending and prolonged sitting
Paraspinal muscle spasm and a guarded antalgic gait
Saddle anesthesia with new urinary retention
First episode of back pain after a clear lifting injury
stages.explanation: Saddle anesthesia with urinary retention suggests cauda equina syndrome, a surgical emergency requiring urgent MRI and neurosurgical evaluation. This is the classic red flag. Pain worsening with bending and sitting is typical of mechanical/discogenic pain and is not concerning. Paraspinal spasm and antalgic gait are expected findings in acute lumbar strain. A first episode after an identifiable mechanical trigger in a young patient without neurologic deficit is the textbook benign presentation. Red flags to screen for include bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, progressive motor deficit, fever, IV drug use, history of cancer, unexplained weight loss, and age over 50 with new pain — none of which this patient has.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in management at this visit?
options: Order lumbar spine MRI to rule out disc herniation
Reassurance, activity modification, and early return to normal activity as tolerated
Prescribe strict bed rest for 1 week
Order plain lumbar radiographs to evaluate for fracture
stages.explanation: In acute mechanical low back pain without red flags, the evidence strongly supports reassurance, staying active, and early return to normal activity rather than imaging or bed rest. Most cases resolve within 4–6 weeks. MRI is not indicated in the absence of red flags or persistent neurologic deficit and frequently reveals incidental findings that prompt unnecessary intervention. Bed rest beyond 1–2 days actually worsens outcomes by promoting deconditioning and delayed recovery. Plain radiographs add little for a young patient with a clear mechanical mechanism and no trauma or red flags, and they expose him to unnecessary radiation. Education and early mobilization are the cornerstone of care.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which pharmacologic and non-pharmacologic regimen is best supported for this patient's acute symptoms?
options: Scheduled oral opioids and a lumbar corset brace
Systemic corticosteroid taper and strict immobilization
NSAIDs, heat therapy, and a short course of a muscle relaxant if needed
Gabapentin and a referral for epidural steroid injection
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for acute nonspecific low back pain is NSAIDs combined with non-pharmacologic measures such as superficial heat and staying active; a short course of a non-benzodiazepine muscle relaxant (e.g., cyclobenzaprine) can help when spasm is prominent. Opioids are not recommended first-line because of limited benefit and risk of dependence, and bracing promotes deconditioning. Systemic corticosteroids have not shown benefit for acute nonradicular low back pain. Gabapentin and epidural steroid injections target neuropathic/radicular pain, which this patient does not have given his negative straight-leg raise and absent neurologic findings. Heat therapy has modest but real evidence for short-term relief.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He asks when he should follow up or seek further care. What is the most appropriate guidance?
options: Return only if symptoms persist beyond 6 months
Return promptly for new leg weakness, numbness, or bowel/bladder changes; otherwise expect improvement over a few weeks
Schedule an MRI in 2 weeks regardless of symptoms
Begin a sedentary leave of absence from work until pain fully resolves
stages.explanation: Appropriate anticipatory guidance is to reassure the patient that most acute low back pain improves substantially within 2–6 weeks, while instructing him to return promptly for red-flag symptoms — new or progressive leg weakness, numbness, saddle anesthesia, or bowel/bladder dysfunction. Waiting 6 months before reassessment risks missing evolving pathology and prolonging disability. Routine MRI without an evolving indication is low-value care. A prolonged sedentary leave is counterproductive; early return to modified-duty work improves outcomes and reduces chronic disability. Encouraging graded resumption of activity, including work with temporary lifting restrictions, is the recommended approach.

cases.diagnosis: Acute lumbar strain (mechanical low back pain)
pearls: Acute nonspecific low back pain without red flags needs no imaging — reassurance and staying active outperform bed rest and MRI.
Screen every back-pain patient for red flags: saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder dysfunction, progressive weakness, fever, IV drug use, cancer history, weight loss.
First-line therapy is NSAIDs plus heat and early mobilization; a brief muscle relaxant course helps when spasm dominates.
Early return to modified-duty work reduces the risk of progression to chronic disability.

cases.title: Leg Pain and a Dragging Foot
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of sharp pain shooting down the back of the right leg with a foot that catches on the ground.
cases.history: A 42-year-old woman presents with three weeks of right-sided low back pain that radiates down the posterior thigh, into the lateral calf, and to the dorsum of the foot. The pain began after a weekend of gardening and is sharp and electric, worsened by sitting, coughing, and sneezing, and partially relieved by lying down. Over the past week she has noticed numbness over the top of her foot and that her foot 'slaps' the floor and occasionally catches on stairs. She denies bowel or bladder incontinence, saddle numbness, fever, or weight loss. Past medical history includes mild hypothyroidism. Medications: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily and acetaminophen as needed. No known drug allergies. She works as an accountant, does not smoke, and drinks rarely. Family history is unremarkable. She is concerned because the foot weakness is new and frightening, and the leg pain is now worse than her back pain.
cases.exam: Antalgic gait with a subtle right steppage pattern; she cannot heel-walk on the right. Right straight-leg raise reproduces radiating pain down the leg at 40 degrees; crossed straight-leg raise is negative. Right ankle dorsiflexion and great-toe extension are 4-/5; plantarflexion, inversion, and eversion are 5/5. Sensation is decreased over the dorsum of the right foot and first web space. Right patellar reflex 2+; ankle reflexes 2+ and symmetric. No saddle hypoesthesia; normal rectal tone. Left lower extremity exam is normal.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: The pattern of weakness, sensory loss, and reflexes best localizes to which nerve root?
options: L4 nerve root
L5 nerve root
S1 nerve root
L3 nerve root
stages.explanation: Weak ankle dorsiflexion and great-toe extension, foot drop with a steppage gait, numbness over the dorsum of the foot and first web space, and preserved ankle reflexes are hallmark features of an L5 radiculopathy — classically from an L4-L5 disc herniation, though a paracentral L5-S1 herniation can also impinge the traversing L5 root. S1 radiculopathy would cause weak plantarflexion, a diminished ankle reflex, and sensory loss over the sole and lateral foot. L4 radiculopathy affects knee extension and the patellar reflex with anteromedial leg sensory loss. L3 involves hip flexion/knee extension higher up. The selectively weak dorsiflexors with a normal ankle jerk pinpoint L5.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the new, progressive motor deficit (foot drop), what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Continue conservative care for 6 weeks before any imaging
Order MRI of the lumbar spine
Order plain lumbar radiographs only
Order electromyography immediately to confirm the lesion
stages.explanation: Although most lumbar radiculopathy is managed conservatively without early imaging, the presence of a progressive motor deficit (a true foot drop with weak dorsiflexion) is an indication for prompt MRI to define the herniation, its level, and any surgical lesion. Waiting 6 weeks is appropriate only for radiculopathy without significant or progressive weakness. Plain films cannot visualize disc herniation or nerve-root compression. EMG can confirm and characterize the radiculopathy but is most useful after 3–4 weeks (for membrane instability and reinnervation changes) and does not visualize the compressive lesion; it complements rather than replaces MRI when surgical planning is needed for progressive weakness.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI lumbar spine without contrast:
Right paracentral disc extrusion at L5-S1
Disc material compresses the traversing right L5 nerve root in the lateral recess
Moderate right L5-S1 lateral recess stenosis
No central canal stenosis; conus and cauda equina normal
No fracture, infection, or mass

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With a confirmed disc herniation, mild-moderate L5 weakness, and tolerable pain, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate surgical discectomy
Structured physical therapy, NSAIDs, activity modification, and close monitoring of strength
Strict bed rest for 2 weeks
Long-term opioid therapy
stages.explanation: Most lumbar disc herniations with radiculopathy improve with conservative care, and even with mild-to-moderate stable weakness an initial trial of structured physical therapy, NSAIDs, neuropathic adjuncts as needed, activity modification, and close neurologic monitoring is appropriate. An epidural steroid injection can be added for refractory radicular pain. Immediate surgery is reserved for cauda equina syndrome, severe or rapidly progressive motor deficit, or failure of 6–12 weeks of conservative care with concordant imaging. Strict bed rest worsens outcomes. Long-term opioids are not appropriate for radicular pain and carry dependence risk. The key is to recheck strength frequently and escalate to surgery if the foot drop worsens.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding during follow-up would most strongly prompt urgent surgical referral?
options: Persistent leg pain at the same intensity after 1 week
Progression of the foot drop with worsening dorsiflexion strength
Numbness over the dorsum of the foot that is unchanged
A positive straight-leg raise that remains positive
stages.explanation: Progressive motor weakness — worsening of the foot drop on serial exams — is a clear indication for urgent surgical evaluation to prevent permanent deficit. Stable pain after only one week is expected during a conservative trial and does not by itself warrant surgery. Unchanged sensory numbness, while bothersome, is not an emergency and often lags behind motor recovery. A persistently positive straight-leg raise simply confirms ongoing root irritation consistent with the known herniation and is not in itself a surgical trigger. The decision to operate hinges on progressive neurologic deficit, cauda equina signs, or failure of an adequate conservative trial.

cases.diagnosis: L5-S1 disc herniation with L5 radiculopathy and foot drop
pearls: L5 radiculopathy: weak dorsiflexion/great-toe extension, dorsal foot and first-web-space numbness, foot drop, with a PRESERVED ankle reflex.
A positive straight-leg raise (pain reproduced 30–70°) is sensitive; a crossed SLR is more specific for herniation.
Progressive or severe motor deficit, or cauda equina syndrome, converts radiculopathy from a 'watch' to an urgent MRI/surgical problem.
Most herniations resolve with conservative care; surgery offers faster pain relief but similar 1–2 year outcomes for stable deficits.

cases.title: Neck Pain Radiating to the Thumb
cases.chiefComplaint: Five weeks of neck pain shooting into the right arm with tingling in the thumb.
cases.history: A 49-year-old man presents with five weeks of right-sided neck pain that radiates across the top of the shoulder, down the lateral arm and forearm, and into the thumb and index finger. The pain is described as a deep ache with intermittent electric shocks and tingling in the thumb. Symptoms worsen with looking up and turning the head to the right and improve when he rests his hand on top of his head. He denies bilateral symptoms, gait imbalance, hand clumsiness, or bowel/bladder changes. There is no history of trauma, fever, weight loss, or cancer. Past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, naproxen 440 mg twice daily as needed. No known drug allergies. He works as a software developer at a desk, does not smoke, and drinks one or two beers weekly. Family history is noncontributory. He is frustrated because the arm pain interrupts his sleep and limits typing at work.
cases.exam: Comfortable, normal mental status. Reduced cervical extension and right lateral rotation reproduce arm symptoms. Spurling maneuver (extension, right lateral flexion, axial compression) reproduces radiating pain into the right thumb. Shoulder abduction relief sign is positive. Right biceps and wrist-extensor strength 4+/5; deltoid, triceps, and intrinsics 5/5. Diminished sensation over the lateral forearm and thumb. Right brachioradialis reflex diminished; biceps, triceps reflexes normal and symmetric. No Hoffman sign, no clonus, normal gait. Left arm and both legs neurologically normal.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: The thumb paresthesias, weak biceps/wrist extension, and diminished brachioradialis reflex best localize to which cervical root?
options: C5 nerve root
C6 nerve root
C7 nerve root
C8 nerve root
stages.explanation: C6 radiculopathy classically causes pain and paresthesias radiating to the thumb (and index finger), weakness of the biceps and wrist extensors, and a diminished brachioradialis (and sometimes biceps) reflex — exactly this pattern. C5 affects the deltoid and biceps with lateral shoulder/arm sensory loss and a depressed biceps reflex but spares the thumb. C7 affects the triceps and wrist flexors/finger extensors with middle-finger sensory loss and a diminished triceps reflex. C8 affects the hand intrinsics and finger flexors with little/ring-finger and medial-hand sensory loss. The thumb-side distribution and brachioradialis reflex change pinpoint C6, most often from C5-C6 foraminal stenosis or disc.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The Spurling maneuver reproduced his arm pain. What is the clinical significance of a positive Spurling sign?
options: It is highly sensitive, so a negative test rules out radiculopathy
It is fairly specific for cervical radiculopathy but not very sensitive
It confirms cervical myelopathy
It distinguishes a disc herniation from foraminal stenosis
stages.explanation: Spurling's test (neck extension, ipsilateral lateral flexion, and axial loading reproducing radicular arm symptoms) is fairly specific but relatively insensitive for cervical radiculopathy — a positive test supports the diagnosis, but a negative test does not exclude it. It does not confirm myelopathy; myelopathic signs would include hyperreflexia, Hoffman sign, clonus, and gait disturbance, all absent here. The shoulder abduction relief sign (improvement when the hand is placed on the head, off-loading the root) further supports radiculopathy. Spurling cannot differentiate the underlying cause (disc vs. spondylotic foraminal narrowing); imaging is needed for that. Its high specificity makes it a useful confirmatory bedside maneuver.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has no myelopathic signs and only mild weakness. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Urgent cervical MRI and surgical referral
Conservative care: relative rest, NSAIDs, physical therapy, and possibly a short oral steroid course
Cervical traction with strict collar immobilization for 6 weeks
Immediate selective nerve root block
stages.explanation: Most cervical radiculopathy without myelopathy or significant/progressive weakness resolves with conservative management: relative rest, NSAIDs, physical therapy emphasizing posture and gentle mobilization, and sometimes a short oral corticosteroid course for severe radicular pain. The majority improve within 4–8 weeks. Urgent MRI and surgery are reserved for myelopathy, severe or progressive motor deficit, or failure of conservative care — none present here. Prolonged rigid collar immobilization promotes stiffness and deconditioning and is not recommended. A selective nerve root block is a reasonable next step for radicular pain that fails initial conservative care, but it is not the first-line intervention before a conservative trial.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After 6 weeks of physical therapy and NSAIDs his neck pain improved, but disabling thumb-radiating arm pain persists with stable mild weakness. MRI shows a C5-C6 foraminal disc-osteophyte complex compressing the C6 root. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue the same conservative program for another 6 months
Proceed directly to anterior cervical discectomy and fusion
Cervical selective (transforaminal) nerve root block at C6
Start long-term opioid therapy
stages.explanation: For cervical radiculopathy with persistent radicular pain after an adequate conservative trial, concordant imaging, and no surgical emergency, a fluoroscopically guided cervical selective/transforaminal nerve root block at the symptomatic level is the appropriate next step — it provides both therapeutic benefit and diagnostic confirmation that the C6 root is the pain generator. Simply repeating the same failed program for six more months delays relief. ACDF is effective but is reserved for refractory cases (often after injection fails), progressive deficit, or myelopathy; jumping to fusion is premature here. Long-term opioids are inappropriate for radicular pain given poor efficacy and dependence risk. Injection bridges conservative care and surgery.

cases.diagnosis: C6 cervical radiculopathy from C5-C6 foraminal disc-osteophyte complex
pearls: C6 radiculopathy → thumb/index paresthesias, weak biceps and wrist extensors, diminished brachioradialis reflex.
Spurling sign is specific but not sensitive; the shoulder abduction relief sign (hand-on-head) further supports radiculopathy.
Always screen for myelopathy (Hoffman, clonus, hyperreflexia, gait/balance, hand clumsiness) — its presence changes management urgency.
A transforaminal selective nerve root block both treats and confirms the level when conservative care fails before considering ACDF.

cases.title: Hand Numbness That Wakes Her at Night
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of numbness and tingling in the hand that wakes her from sleep.
cases.history: A 38-year-old woman presents for electrodiagnostic testing after months of numbness and tingling in her right hand, predominantly involving the thumb, index, and middle fingers. The symptoms are worst at night and wake her from sleep; she shakes her hand to get relief ('flick sign'). Tingling also occurs while driving and holding her phone. She reports dropping objects and a sense of clumsiness but no neck pain or radiation above the wrist. She is right-hand dominant and works as a data-entry clerk with prolonged keyboarding. Past medical history includes hypothyroidism and a recent diagnosis of prediabetes; she is 14 weeks postpartum. Medications: levothyroxine 88 mcg daily, prenatal vitamin. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history: mother with type 2 diabetes. She is worried the numbness is becoming constant and is affecting her work and caring for her newborn.
cases.exam: Inspection shows mild thenar eminence flattening on the right. Sensation is decreased to light touch over the palmar thumb, index, and middle fingers, with sparing of the thenar palm. Tinel sign at the wrist and Phalen maneuver each reproduce paresthesias in the median distribution within 30 seconds. Right thumb abduction (abductor pollicis brevis) strength is mildly reduced (4+/5). Sensation over the little finger and hypothenar region is normal. No neck tenderness; Spurling sign negative. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric throughout. Left hand exam is normal.

stages.label: ANATOMY
stages.question: The sparing of sensation over the thenar palm in this patient is explained by which anatomical detail?
options: The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve arises proximal to the carpal tunnel
The ulnar nerve supplies the thenar palm
The recurrent motor branch is purely sensory to the palm
The radial nerve supplies the central palm
stages.explanation: The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve branches off proximal to the carpal tunnel and therefore travels superficial to (outside) the flexor retinaculum, so it is spared in carpal tunnel syndrome — explaining preserved sensation over the thenar/central palm despite numb median-innervated fingers. This is a classic distinguishing feature from a more proximal median lesion. The ulnar nerve supplies the hypothenar region and little finger, not the thenar palm. The recurrent (thenar) motor branch innervates the thenar muscles and is motor, not a palmar sensory nerve. The radial nerve supplies the dorsal hand, not the central palm.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step to confirm the suspected diagnosis and assess severity?
options: MRI of the cervical spine
Nerve conduction studies and needle EMG
Wrist radiographs
Empiric carpal tunnel release without testing
stages.explanation: Nerve conduction studies (NCS) with needle EMG are the diagnostic gold standard to confirm carpal tunnel syndrome and grade its severity by demonstrating focal slowing of median sensory and motor conduction across the wrist, with comparison to the ulnar nerve to exclude a generalized neuropathy. She is already in the EMG lab and has a classic presentation. Cervical MRI would be pursued if there were neck pain or a radicular pattern, which she lacks. Wrist radiographs do not diagnose nerve compression. Proceeding to surgical release without electrodiagnostic confirmation is inappropriate when the diagnosis and severity should first be objectively established, particularly to document a baseline.

stages.label: ELECTRODIAGNOSTICS
stages.content:
Right median sensory distal latency: 4.8 ms (normal <3.5 ms)
Right median sensory conduction velocity across wrist: slowed
Median-ulnar sensory latency difference: 1.2 ms (abnormal >0.5 ms)
Right median motor distal latency: 5.6 ms (normal <4.2 ms)
Right ulnar motor and sensory studies: normal
Needle EMG of abductor pollicis brevis: mild chronic reinnervation changes
Contralateral and proximal median segments: normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Electrodiagnostics confirm moderate carpal tunnel syndrome with mild thenar denervation. Given mild motor involvement but no severe axon loss, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate carpal tunnel release
Nocturnal neutral wrist splinting, activity/ergonomic modification, and consideration of a corticosteroid injection
Oral corticosteroid taper indefinitely
Reassurance with no treatment and repeat EMG in 1 year
stages.explanation: For mild-to-moderate CTS without severe or rapidly progressive axon loss, first-line conservative care is a neutral-position wrist splint worn at night (and during aggravating activities), ergonomic/activity modification, and a local corticosteroid injection for symptom relief, which has good short-to-intermediate-term efficacy. Surgical carpal tunnel release is reserved for severe CTS (marked thenar atrophy, significant denervation) or failure of conservative measures — her thenar changes are only mild. Long-term systemic corticosteroids are not used due to systemic risks and limited durability. Doing nothing ignores her functional impairment and progressive symptoms. Addressing reversible contributors (hypothyroidism control, postpartum fluid shifts, repetitive keyboarding) is also worthwhile.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which exam or electrodiagnostic finding would most strongly indicate that she should be referred for surgical decompression rather than continued conservative care?
options: Positive Tinel and Phalen signs
Nighttime paresthesias relieved by shaking the hand
Progressive thenar atrophy with severe axon loss on EMG
Sensory symptoms during driving
stages.explanation: Progressive thenar muscle atrophy together with severe axon loss (marked denervation/low motor amplitudes) on electrodiagnostics indicates advanced median nerve injury and is a clear indication for surgical decompression, since further delay risks permanent weakness. Positive Tinel and Phalen signs are useful provocative tests but only support the diagnosis; they do not gauge severity or mandate surgery. Nocturnal paresthesias relieved by hand-shaking (the flick sign) and symptoms while driving are classic CTS features but reflect symptom burden, not the structural urgency that drives surgical timing. The decision to operate is driven primarily by severity (atrophy, axon loss) and failure of conservative therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Carpal tunnel syndrome (median mononeuropathy at the wrist)
pearls: Classic CTS: nocturnal median-distribution paresthesias relieved by shaking the hand (flick sign), with thenar-palm sparing due to the palmar cutaneous branch arising proximal to the tunnel.
Nerve conduction studies (prolonged median distal latencies, abnormal median-ulnar comparison) confirm the diagnosis and grade severity.
Mild-moderate CTS → night splinting, ergonomics, and corticosteroid injection; thenar atrophy or severe axon loss → surgical release.
Look for contributors: hypothyroidism, diabetes/prediabetes, pregnancy/postpartum fluid shifts, and repetitive wrist use.

cases.title: Outer Elbow Pain in a Weekend Tennis Player
cases.chiefComplaint: Six weeks of pain on the outside of the elbow that worsens with gripping.
cases.history: A 45-year-old recreational tennis player presents with six weeks of pain over the lateral aspect of his right (dominant) elbow. The pain began after he increased his playing frequency and switched to a heavier racquet. It is aggravated by gripping, shaking hands, turning a doorknob, lifting a coffee mug, and backhand strokes, and it sometimes radiates down the dorsal forearm. There is no locking, no instability, and no neck pain or numbness in the fingers. He denies prior elbow injury, fever, or systemic symptoms. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: occasional ibuprofen. No known drug allergies. He works in construction management with some manual tasks and plays tennis twice weekly. He does not smoke and drinks socially. Family history is noncontributory. He is eager to keep playing and asks whether he needs an MRI or a 'cortisone shot' to fix it quickly.
cases.exam: Point tenderness is maximal just distal to the lateral epicondyle over the common extensor origin. Pain is reproduced with resisted wrist extension (Cozen test) and with resisted long-finger (middle-finger) extension. Passive wrist flexion with the elbow extended stretches and reproduces the pain. Grip strength is reduced by pain. The elbow has full, painless flexion-extension and pronation-supination; there is no joint-line tenderness, effusion, or instability to varus/valgus stress. Distal neurovascular exam is intact; no Tinel over the cubital or radial tunnel. Sensation in the hand is normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which physical exam finding most specifically supports the diagnosis of lateral epicondylitis?
options: Tenderness over the medial epicondyle
Pain reproduced by resisted wrist extension (Cozen test)
Pain with resisted forearm pronation
A positive Tinel sign at the cubital tunnel
stages.explanation: Lateral epicondylitis (tendinosis of the extensor carpi radialis brevis origin) is supported by point tenderness just distal to the lateral epicondyle and reproduction of pain with resisted wrist extension — the Cozen test — and resisted middle-finger extension (the Maudsley test). Medial epicondyle tenderness points to medial epicondylitis (golfer's elbow). Pain with resisted pronation and wrist flexion also localizes medially. A positive Tinel at the cubital tunnel indicates ulnar neuropathy at the elbow, a distinct entity. The combination of lateral epicondylar-origin tenderness and pain on resisted wrist/finger extension is the classic, specific examination signature of tennis elbow.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: He asks whether he needs imaging. What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic approach?
options: Order MRI of the elbow now to confirm tendinosis
Make the diagnosis clinically; imaging is not routinely required
Order EMG/nerve conduction studies
Order elbow radiographs to look for a fracture
stages.explanation: Lateral epicondylitis is a clinical diagnosis; with a classic history and exam, no imaging is required initially. MRI or ultrasound is reserved for atypical presentations, diagnostic uncertainty, or symptoms refractory to months of appropriate care. EMG/NCS would be pursued only if there were signs of nerve entrapment (e.g., radial/posterior interosseous or ulnar neuropathy), which this exam excludes. Radiographs are not needed without trauma, mechanical locking, or suspicion of arthritis or loose body; here the joint exam is normal. Routine advanced imaging adds cost without changing the initial, evidence-based conservative plan.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which rehabilitation strategy has the best evidence for durable improvement in chronic lateral epicondylitis?
options: Complete rest and immobilization in a sling
An eccentric (and isometric) wrist-extensor strengthening/loading program
Early corticosteroid injection as first-line therapy
Long-term opioid analgesia
stages.explanation: A progressive eccentric (often combined with isometric) wrist-extensor loading program is the rehabilitation cornerstone with the best evidence for durable recovery, as it remodels the degenerative tendon. Adjuncts include activity modification, a counterforce brace, and a check on equipment/technique. Prolonged rest and immobilization promote deconditioning and do not address the underlying tendinosis. Corticosteroid injection provides short-term pain relief but is associated with worse outcomes at 6–12 months and higher recurrence compared with exercise or even watchful waiting, so it is not first-line. Long-term opioids are inappropriate for a self-limited tendinopathy. Most patients improve within 6–12 months with load-based rehab.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding a corticosteroid injection, which counseling point is most accurate?
options: It offers the best long-term cure and should be done first
It provides short-term relief but may worsen outcomes and increase recurrence at 6–12 months versus exercise
It has no risks and can be repeated as often as desired
It is contraindicated in all cases of tendinopathy
stages.explanation: The accurate counseling point is that corticosteroid injection can give meaningful short-term pain relief but is associated with poorer long-term outcomes and higher recurrence rates at 6–12 months compared with eccentric exercise or watchful waiting — so it should not be first-line and, if used, should be reserved and limited. It is not a long-term cure. Repeated steroid injections carry real risks, including subcutaneous atrophy, depigmentation, and tendon weakening, so they should be limited. It is not absolutely contraindicated; it is a reasonable option for severe short-term symptom control in select patients who understand the trade-offs. Load-based rehabilitation remains the foundation of care.

cases.diagnosis: Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow)
pearls: Lateral epicondylitis is a clinical diagnosis: tenderness just distal to the lateral epicondyle with pain on resisted wrist extension (Cozen) and middle-finger extension (Maudsley).
It is a degenerative tendinosis of the ECRB origin, not an inflammatory 'itis' — load-based rehab is the cure, not rest.
Eccentric/isometric wrist-extensor loading plus a counterforce brace and activity modification give the best durable outcomes.
Corticosteroid injections help short-term pain but worsen 6–12 month outcomes and increase recurrence — reserve and limit them.

cases.title: A Stiffening, Painful Shoulder
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of progressive shoulder pain and stiffness that now limits reaching and dressing.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman presents with about four months of progressive right shoulder pain and stiffness. The pain began insidiously without injury, was initially worse at night and with movement, and has gradually given way to marked stiffness so that she now struggles to reach behind her back to fasten her bra, comb her hair, or reach overhead. She has not been able to lie on that side. There is no neck pain, numbness, tingling, or hand weakness. She denies fever, weight loss, or trauma. Past medical history is notable for type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.8%) and hypothyroidism. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, levothyroxine 100 mcg daily, acetaminophen as needed. No known drug allergies. She is a non-smoker, drinks rarely, and works as a schoolteacher. Family history: mother with type 2 diabetes. She is frustrated that the stiffness has worsened despite resting the shoulder and asks whether she needs surgery.
cases.exam: Right shoulder shows no swelling, deformity, or atrophy. There is diffuse tenderness without a single focal trigger point. Active range of motion is globally restricted, and — importantly — passive range of motion is equally restricted in the same directions, with external rotation most limited, followed by abduction and internal rotation. Attempted motion is painful at end range. Rotator cuff strength is difficult to assess due to pain but grossly preserved within available range. No drop-arm sign. Neurovascular exam of the arm is intact; sensation normal. Cervical spine range of motion is full and painless; Spurling sign negative.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which exam finding most distinguishes adhesive capsulitis from a rotator cuff tear or tendinopathy?
options: Pain with active overhead reaching
Loss of BOTH active and passive range of motion, especially external rotation
A positive drop-arm sign
Tenderness over the greater tuberosity
stages.explanation: The hallmark of adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) is loss of both active AND passive range of motion in a capsular pattern, with external rotation typically the most restricted — reflecting global capsular contracture. In rotator cuff disease, passive motion is largely preserved even when active motion is limited by pain or weakness. Pain with active overhead reaching is nonspecific and occurs in many shoulder conditions. A positive drop-arm sign suggests a large/full-thickness cuff tear, not capsulitis. Greater tuberosity tenderness points to cuff/subacromial pathology. The equal restriction of active and passive ROM, disproportionately affecting external rotation, is the defining feature here, especially in this diabetic patient.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Which feature of this patient's history most increases her risk for adhesive capsulitis?
options: Her occupation as a teacher
Diabetes mellitus
Use of acetaminophen
Social alcohol use
stages.explanation: Diabetes mellitus is the strongest and best-established risk factor for adhesive capsulitis; diabetics develop frozen shoulder more often, more severely, more frequently bilaterally, and with a more protracted course than non-diabetics. Thyroid disease (which she also has) is another recognized association, as are prior immobilization, shoulder surgery/trauma, and female sex in the 40–60 age range. Her teaching occupation is not a specific risk factor. Acetaminophen use is incidental and not causative. Social alcohol consumption is not associated with capsulitis. Recognizing the diabetes-capsulitis link helps anticipate a slower, more difficult recovery and informs counseling about realistic timelines and glycemic optimization.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: She is in the painful 'freezing' phase with significant night pain and end-range pain. How does staging guide her therapy intensity?
options: Aggressive end-range stretching is best during the painful freezing phase
During the painful freezing phase, emphasize pain control and gentle motion; reserve aggressive stretching for the later stiffness-predominant (frozen/thawing) phase
Immobilize the shoulder completely until pain resolves
Proceed directly to manipulation under anesthesia
stages.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis evolves through freezing (painful), frozen (stiff), and thawing (recovery) phases, and treatment intensity should match the phase. In the painful freezing phase, aggressive end-range stretching provokes pain and can worsen symptoms, so management emphasizes pain control (NSAIDs, possibly an intra-articular corticosteroid injection) and gentle range-of-motion within tolerance. More aggressive stretching and capsular mobilization are appropriate once pain subsides in the stiffness-predominant frozen/thawing phases. Complete immobilization promotes further contracture and is harmful. Manipulation under anesthesia or capsular release is reserved for refractory cases after prolonged failure of conservative therapy, not as an early intervention.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate overall management plan for this patient?
options: Reassurance that it will resolve, with no treatment
Patient education, analgesia/NSAIDs, a phase-appropriate physical therapy program, glycemic optimization, and consideration of an intra-articular corticosteroid injection
Early surgical capsular release
Prolonged sling immobilization
stages.explanation: The mainstay of frozen-shoulder care is education about the often prolonged (months-to-years) but generally self-limited natural history, analgesia/NSAIDs, and a phase-appropriate physical therapy program (gentle ROM during the painful phase, progressing to stretching as pain allows). An intra-articular corticosteroid injection can meaningfully reduce pain and speed early recovery, especially in the painful phase. Optimizing glycemic control matters given her diabetes-associated risk and slower course. 'No treatment' abandons her without symptom relief or guided rehab. Surgical release or manipulation under anesthesia is reserved for cases refractory to extended conservative care. Sling immobilization worsens contracture. A multimodal, phase-tailored plan is best.

cases.diagnosis: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder)
pearls: Adhesive capsulitis = loss of BOTH active and passive ROM in a capsular pattern, with external rotation most limited.
Diabetes is the strongest risk factor (also thyroid disease, female 40–60, immobilization); expect a longer, harder course.
Match therapy to phase: pain control and gentle motion during the painful freezing phase, aggressive stretching later.
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection plus phase-appropriate PT speeds early recovery; surgery/MUA is for refractory cases.

cases.title: Shoulder Pain With Overhead Reaching
cases.chiefComplaint: Two months of shoulder pain that flares with overhead activity and disturbs sleep.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man presents with two months of right shoulder pain that began after he spent a weekend painting his ceiling. The pain is felt over the lateral deltoid and worsens with overhead reaching, lifting, and reaching into the back seat of his car; it is worse at night, particularly when lying on the affected side. He denies a single traumatic event, neck pain, numbness, or hand weakness, and reports no sense that the arm 'gives way.' He has not lost the ability to lift the arm. Past medical history includes hyperlipidemia. Medications: atorvastatin 20 mg daily, ibuprofen as needed. No known drug allergies. He works in an office, plays recreational softball, does not smoke, and drinks socially. Family history is noncontributory. He is functional but limited overhead and asks whether he needs an MRI and a cortisone injection.
cases.exam: Inspection shows no atrophy or deformity. There is tenderness over the greater tuberosity and the subacromial space. A painful arc is present between roughly 60 and 120 degrees of active abduction. Neer and Hawkins-Kennedy impingement signs are positive. Empty-can (Jobe) testing produces pain with preserved 5/5 strength against resistance. External-rotation and lift-off strength are intact; the drop-arm sign is negative. Passive range of motion is full in all planes. Neurovascular exam of the arm is intact; sensation normal. Cervical spine range of motion is full and painless; Spurling sign negative.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The painful arc with positive Neer and Hawkins signs but full passive ROM and preserved strength best fits which diagnosis?
options: Full-thickness rotator cuff tear
Rotator cuff tendinopathy / subacromial impingement
Adhesive capsulitis
Glenohumeral osteoarthritis
stages.explanation: A painful arc between 60–120° of abduction with positive Neer and Hawkins-Kennedy signs, full passive ROM, and preserved strength is the classic picture of rotator cuff tendinopathy with subacromial impingement. A full-thickness tear would more often show weakness on testing (e.g., a positive drop-arm or external-rotation lag), which is absent here. Adhesive capsulitis is excluded by the full passive range of motion (capsulitis restricts both active and passive motion). Glenohumeral osteoarthritis typically produces crepitus, restricted motion, and radiographic joint-space loss rather than an impingement pattern with full passive ROM. Pain-limited Jobe testing with full strength supports tendinopathy rather than a structural tear.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic approach for this classic impingement presentation?
options: Obtain MRI of the shoulder immediately
Diagnose clinically and begin conservative management; reserve imaging for failure or suspected tear
Order EMG/nerve conduction studies
Order a CT arthrogram now
stages.explanation: Rotator cuff tendinopathy/impingement is a clinical diagnosis, and in a classic presentation without weakness or trauma, the best initial approach is to diagnose clinically and start conservative management, reserving imaging for patients who fail to improve or who have features suggesting a full-thickness tear (e.g., significant weakness, drop-arm, acute trauma in an older patient). Plain radiographs may be obtained if osteoarthritis, calcific tendinitis, or acromial morphology is in question. MRI is sensitive but frequently shows age-related changes and should not drive early management. EMG/NCS is for suspected neurologic causes, not present here. A CT arthrogram is invasive and not a first-line test.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment before considering a subacromial injection?
options: Subacromial corticosteroid injection as the initial intervention
Structured physical therapy focusing on rotator cuff and scapular strengthening, plus activity modification and NSAIDs
Sling immobilization for 4 weeks
Early surgical subacromial decompression
stages.explanation: First-line management of rotator cuff tendinopathy/impingement is a structured rehabilitation program emphasizing rotator cuff and scapular stabilizer strengthening, posture and mechanics, activity modification, and NSAIDs for pain. Exercise therapy is well supported and addresses the underlying biomechanical contributors. A subacromial corticosteroid injection is a reasonable adjunct for pain that limits participation in therapy but is not the preferred first step before a rehab trial. Sling immobilization promotes stiffness and is counterproductive. Surgical subacromial decompression is reserved for patients who fail an adequate course of conservative care (typically several months), and current evidence shows limited benefit over structured exercise for impingement without a tear.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After 8 weeks of well-performed physical therapy, his pain and overhead function are only minimally improved, but he still has full passive ROM and no weakness. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Proceed directly to arthroscopic surgery
Offer a subacromial corticosteroid injection and continue/advance the rehabilitation program
Stop all activity and immobilize the shoulder
Begin long-term opioid therapy
stages.explanation: When a patient with impingement/tendinopathy has only minimal improvement after an adequate trial of physical therapy but no weakness or red flags, a subacromial corticosteroid injection is a reasonable next step to reduce pain and facilitate continued rehabilitation, which should be advanced rather than abandoned. Proceeding straight to surgery is premature without a longer failed conservative trial and is of questionable benefit for impingement without a structural tear. Complete immobilization risks stiffness and deconditioning. Long-term opioids are inappropriate for a chronic musculoskeletal tendinopathy. Imaging (MRI) would be appropriate if symptoms persist despite injection and rehab, or if a tear is suspected. Injection bridges and supports ongoing therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Rotator cuff tendinopathy with subacromial impingement
pearls: Painful arc (60–120°) with positive Neer and Hawkins signs, full passive ROM, and preserved strength = tendinopathy/impingement, not a tear.
Weakness, a positive drop-arm, or an external-rotation lag should raise suspicion for a full-thickness rotator cuff tear and prompt imaging.
First-line treatment is rotator cuff and scapular strengthening (rehab before injection); subacromial steroid injection is an adjunct for pain.
Surgery for impingement without a tear shows limited benefit over structured exercise — reserve it for refractory cases.

cases.title: Aching Knees That Are Worse by Evening
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of bilateral knee pain and stiffness that worsen with activity and improve with rest.
cases.history: A 68-year-old woman presents with several months of gradually worsening bilateral knee pain, worse in the right knee. The pain is deep and aching, worse with weight-bearing activities like climbing stairs and rising from a chair, and worse by the end of the day; it eases with rest. She reports brief morning stiffness lasting about 10 minutes and occasional grinding and crackling in the knees. There is no significant swelling, locking, or giving way, and no warmth or redness. She denies fever, prolonged morning stiffness, or small-joint involvement of the hands. Past medical history includes obesity (BMI 33), hypertension, and type 2 diabetes. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, acetaminophen as needed. No known drug allergies. She is a retired clerk, does not smoke, drinks rarely, and walks her dog daily but has cut back due to pain. Family history: mother had knee replacements.
cases.exam: Body habitus is obese. Gait is mildly antalgic. The right knee shows bony enlargement along the joint line with palpable crepitus on flexion-extension and mild medial joint-line tenderness. There is a small, cool effusion without warmth or erythema. Range of motion is mildly reduced at terminal flexion. Ligamentous exam (Lachman, varus/valgus, McMurray) is stable without mechanical block. There is a mild varus alignment. No first-MTP, MCP, or PIP synovitis; no rheumatoid nodules. Distal pulses and neurologic exam are normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which set of features best supports osteoarthritis rather than an inflammatory arthritis?
options: Prolonged morning stiffness over 1 hour with symmetric small-joint synovitis
Activity-related pain, brief (<30 min) morning stiffness, crepitus, and bony enlargement
A hot, red, exquisitely tender first MTP joint
Migratory large-joint arthritis with fever
stages.explanation: Osteoarthritis is supported by mechanical, activity-related pain that worsens with use and improves with rest, brief morning stiffness (typically under 30 minutes), crepitus, and bony enlargement (osteophytes) — exactly this patient's picture. Prolonged morning stiffness over an hour with symmetric small-joint synovitis suggests rheumatoid arthritis. A hot, red, exquisitely tender first MTP is classic for gout. Migratory large-joint arthritis with fever suggests reactive or septic/rheumatic processes. The combination of gradual, weight-bearing-related knee pain, short-lived stiffness, crepitus, and bony joint-line enlargement without warmth or systemic features is the textbook presentation of knee osteoarthritis.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Weight-bearing AP and lateral radiographs, right knee:
Medial compartment joint-space narrowing
Marginal osteophytes
Subchondral sclerosis
Small subchondral cysts
No fracture, lytic lesion, or chondrocalcinosis
Mild varus alignment

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has moderate symptomatic knee OA and obesity. What is the most appropriate FIRST-LINE management?
options: Refer immediately for total knee arthroplasty
Structured exercise (land-based strengthening/aerobic), weight loss, and education as the foundation, with analgesics as needed
Start chronic oral opioids
Proceed directly to intra-articular corticosteroid injection without other measures
stages.explanation: Core first-line management of knee OA is non-pharmacologic: a structured exercise program (land-based strengthening and aerobic activity), weight loss in patients with overweight/obesity, and patient education/self-management — these are strongly recommended and modify symptoms and function. Topical or oral NSAIDs (with attention to her comorbidities) and acetaminophen are pharmacologic adjuncts. Arthroplasty is reserved for advanced OA with significant pain and functional limitation refractory to comprehensive nonoperative care, not as a first step. Chronic opioids are not recommended for OA given poor efficacy and harms. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection can help symptomatic flares but should complement — not replace — the foundational exercise and weight-loss program.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She adheres to exercise and loses some weight but still has activity-limiting pain. She wants to delay surgery. What is the most appropriate next intervention?
options: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection for symptomatic relief
Arthroscopic debridement/lavage for osteoarthritis
Long-term systemic corticosteroids
Glucosamine-chondroitin as a proven disease-modifying cure
stages.explanation: For knee OA with persistent symptoms despite exercise and weight loss in a patient wishing to delay surgery, an intra-articular corticosteroid injection is an appropriate next step to provide short-term pain relief and support continued activity. Arthroscopic debridement/lavage for OA is not recommended — trials show no benefit over sham/conservative care in the absence of true mechanical locking from a loose body. Long-term systemic corticosteroids are inappropriate for OA and carry significant risks, especially with her diabetes and hypertension. Glucosamine-chondroitin has not demonstrated reliable disease-modifying or symptomatic benefit and is not a cure. Injections offer reasonable temporizing relief while she continues nonoperative management.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: When is referral for total knee arthroplasty most appropriate?
options: As soon as radiographs show any joint-space narrowing
When severe, activity-limiting pain and functional impairment persist despite comprehensive nonoperative management
Only after she has tried at least five corticosteroid injections
Whenever the patient simply requests it, regardless of symptoms
stages.explanation: Total knee arthroplasty is appropriate when a patient has severe, persistent, activity-limiting knee pain and functional impairment with corresponding radiographic OA that has not responded adequately to comprehensive nonoperative management (exercise, weight loss, analgesics, and reasonable use of injections). Radiographic severity alone does not justify surgery — many patients with significant radiographic OA have manageable symptoms, and treatment is driven by symptoms and function, not images. There is no requirement for a fixed number of injections; repeated steroid injections should actually be limited. Surgery is not driven by patient request in the absence of appropriate symptom burden and failed conservative care. The decision integrates symptoms, function, imaging, and shared decision-making.

cases.diagnosis: Knee osteoarthritis (medial compartment predominant)
pearls: OA = mechanical, activity-related pain, brief (<30 min) morning stiffness, crepitus, and bony enlargement without systemic inflammatory features.
Foundation of treatment is exercise, weight loss, and education — these are strongly recommended for every patient with knee OA.
Intra-articular corticosteroid injections give short-term relief; arthroscopic debridement/lavage and glucosamine-chondroitin are NOT recommended for OA.
Refer for arthroplasty based on severe symptoms and functional loss refractory to nonoperative care — not on radiographic severity alone.

cases.title: Sharp Heel Pain With the First Steps of the Day
cases.chiefComplaint: Two months of stabbing heel pain that is worst with the first steps in the morning.
cases.history: A 47-year-old woman presents with two months of right heel pain. The pain is sharp and stabbing, located at the bottom of the heel toward the inside, and is worst with the first few steps when she gets out of bed in the morning or after sitting for a while; it eases somewhat after she walks a bit but worsens again after prolonged standing at work. She recently started a walking-for-fitness program and wears flat, unsupportive shoes. She denies numbness, tingling, night pain unrelated to first steps, ankle instability, trauma, fever, or rash. Past medical history includes obesity (BMI 31) and well-controlled hypothyroidism. Medications: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, ibuprofen as needed. No known drug allergies. She works as a nurse on her feet for long shifts, does not smoke, and drinks rarely. Family history is noncontributory. She is worried she has a 'heel spur' that needs to be removed.
cases.exam: Gait is normal without a limp at rest. There is point tenderness over the medial calcaneal tubercle at the origin of the plantar fascia; tenderness increases with passive dorsiflexion of the toes and ankle (windlass maneuver). The plantar fascia is taut and mildly tender along its medial band. Ankle and subtalar range of motion are normal; gastrocnemius-soleus tightness is noted with limited ankle dorsiflexion. There is no tenderness over the posterior calcaneus or Achilles insertion, no tenderness with calcaneal squeeze, and a negative Tinel sign over the tarsal tunnel. Sensation and pulses in the foot are normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature is most characteristic of plantar fasciitis and helps distinguish it from a calcaneal stress fracture?
options: Pain reproduced by medial-lateral compression of the calcaneus (positive squeeze test)
Sharp medial heel pain worst with the first steps in the morning and point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tubercle
Numbness and tingling radiating into the sole
Posterior heel pain at the Achilles insertion
stages.explanation: Plantar fasciitis classically causes sharp inferomedial heel pain that is worst with the first steps in the morning (or after rest) and improves with initial walking, with point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tubercle and pain reproduced by the windlass maneuver. A positive calcaneal squeeze test (pain on medial-lateral compression) suggests a calcaneal stress fracture, not fasciitis. Numbness and tingling radiating into the sole point to tarsal tunnel syndrome (posterior tibial nerve entrapment). Posterior heel pain at the Achilles insertion indicates insertional Achilles tendinopathy. The 'first-step' pattern with focal medial tubercle tenderness is the hallmark distinguishing feature here.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: She asks whether she needs an X-ray to find the 'heel spur.' What is the most appropriate diagnostic approach?
options: Order an MRI of the foot now
Diagnose clinically; imaging is not routinely required, and heel spurs do not reliably correlate with symptoms
Order a bone scan to find the spur
Order nerve conduction studies of the foot
stages.explanation: Plantar fasciitis is a clinical diagnosis, and imaging is not routinely needed when the history and exam are classic. Notably, calcaneal heel spurs are common in asymptomatic people and do not reliably correlate with plantar fasciitis symptoms, so finding (or removing) a spur is not the goal. Imaging (radiographs, ultrasound, or MRI) is reserved for atypical features, suspicion of fracture or other pathology, or symptoms refractory to months of appropriate care. A bone scan would only be considered for suspected stress fracture, which her exam does not support. Nerve conduction studies are for suspected nerve entrapment (e.g., tarsal tunnel), not present here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for her plantar fasciitis?
options: Plantar fasciotomy surgery
Plantar fascia and calf (gastrocnemius) stretching, supportive footwear/orthotics, activity modification, and NSAIDs
Immediate corticosteroid injection into the plantar fascia
Cast immobilization for 6 weeks
stages.explanation: First-line management is conservative and multimodal: plantar fascia-specific stretching and gastrocnemius/Achilles stretching (addressing her calf tightness), supportive footwear with arch support and/or heel cushioning, prefabricated or custom orthotics, activity modification, and NSAIDs for pain. The large majority of patients improve within several months with these measures. Surgery (plantar fasciotomy) is a last resort reserved for cases refractory to 6–12 months of comprehensive conservative care. Corticosteroid injection may be considered for refractory pain but is not first-line and carries risks of fat-pad atrophy and plantar fascia rupture. Prolonged cast immobilization promotes stiffness and deconditioning and is not standard initial therapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which additional simple intervention is most appropriate to add for her morning symptoms and overall recovery?
options: Strict bed rest until pain resolves
A night splint to keep the ankle in slight dorsiflexion plus a calf-stretching routine
High-dose chronic opioids
Switching to completely flat, unsupportive shoes
stages.explanation: A night splint that holds the ankle in slight dorsiflexion keeps the plantar fascia and calf on gentle stretch overnight, directly targeting the painful first-step phenomenon, and is a useful adjunct alongside a consistent calf- and fascia-stretching routine. Strict bed rest is unnecessary and counterproductive, leading to deconditioning. Chronic opioids are inappropriate for a self-limited tendinopathy/enthesopathy. Switching to flat, unsupportive shoes would worsen symptoms; she should instead wear supportive footwear with arch support and cushioning. Other reasonable adjuncts include taping, a short course of activity modification, and addressing body weight. The night splint and stretching combination specifically improves the characteristic morning pain.

cases.diagnosis: Plantar fasciitis
pearls: Hallmark: sharp inferomedial heel pain worst with the first steps in the morning/after rest, with point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tubercle and a positive windlass test.
It is a clinical diagnosis; heel spurs are common in asymptomatic people and do not need to be imaged or removed.
First-line care is plantar fascia and calf stretching, supportive footwear/orthotics, activity modification, and NSAIDs — most resolve within months.
Night splints help the morning first-step pain; reserve corticosteroid injection (fat-pad atrophy/rupture risk) and surgery for refractory cases.

cases.title: Pain on the Side of the Hip When Lying Down
cases.chiefComplaint: Three months of pain over the outer hip that is worst when lying on that side at night.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman presents with three months of pain over the lateral aspect of her right hip. The pain is aching and localized to the bony prominence on the side of the hip, sometimes radiating down the outer thigh but not below the knee. It is worst when she lies on the affected side at night, when she rises from sitting, and after walking or climbing stairs. She denies groin pain, locking, giving way, numbness, tingling, or low back pain radiating down the leg. There is no fever, weight loss, or trauma. Past medical history includes obesity (BMI 32), osteoarthritis, and hypertension. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed. No known drug allergies. She is retired, walks for exercise, does not smoke, and drinks rarely. Family history is noncontributory. She is frustrated that the pain disrupts her sleep and assumed it was 'bursitis' or her hip arthritis acting up, and asks if she needs a hip replacement.
cases.exam: Gait is mildly antalgic; a single-leg stance on the right reproduces lateral hip pain and shows mild pelvic drop. There is focal point tenderness directly over the greater trochanter. Pain is reproduced with resisted hip abduction and with the FABER and single-leg-stance (Trendelenburg) tests for lateral hip pathology. Passive internal and external rotation of the hip with the knee flexed is full and pain-free, and there is no groin pain on these maneuvers. Straight-leg raise is negative; lumbar range of motion is full and painless. Strength is otherwise 5/5; sensation and reflexes are normal in both legs.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The point tenderness over the greater trochanter and pain with resisted abduction, while passive hip rotation is pain-free, best localizes the problem to which structure?
options: The hip joint (intra-articular osteoarthritis)
The lateral hip abductor tendons/peritrochanteric tissues (gluteal tendinopathy / greater trochanteric pain syndrome)
The L3 nerve root
The sacroiliac joint
stages.explanation: Focal greater trochanteric tenderness, reproduction of pain with resisted abduction and single-leg stance, and — importantly — pain-free passive hip internal/external rotation without groin pain localize the problem to the lateral peritrochanteric structures, namely the gluteus medius/minimus tendons (gluteal tendinopathy), the core lesion in greater trochanteric pain syndrome. Intra-articular hip OA typically produces groin pain and painful, restricted passive rotation, both absent here. An L3 radiculopathy would cause anterior thigh pain with possible weakness/sensory and reflex changes, not isolated trochanteric tenderness. Sacroiliac joint pathology causes pain over the posterior pelvis/buttock with provocative SI maneuvers, not focal trochanteric tenderness. The exam clearly points to the abductor tendons.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is now understood to be the true primary driver of most greater trochanteric pain syndrome?
options: Isolated inflammation of the trochanteric bursa
Gluteus medius/minimus tendinopathy (with or without secondary bursal involvement)
Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
Iliotibial band rupture
stages.explanation: Although historically labeled 'trochanteric bursitis,' the condition now termed greater trochanteric pain syndrome is most often driven by gluteus medius/minimus tendinopathy (degenerative tendinopathy and sometimes partial tearing of the hip abductor tendons), with bursal inflammation being secondary or absent in many cases. This reframing matters because management mirrors tendinopathy care (load-based rehab) rather than treating a primary bursitis. Avascular necrosis of the femoral head causes groin pain and abnormal joint imaging, not focal trochanteric tenderness. A complete iliotibial band rupture is not the mechanism. Recognizing gluteal tendinopathy as the true driver guides appropriate exercise-based treatment and counseling.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: She asks whether she needs a hip replacement evaluation. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer for total hip arthroplasty
Diagnose GTPS clinically and begin conservative management; imaging only if atypical or refractory
Order MRI of the lumbar spine
Order EMG/nerve conduction studies of the leg
stages.explanation: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome is a clinical diagnosis based on lateral hip tenderness and provocative findings, with pain-free passive hip rotation arguing against intra-articular disease — so a hip arthroplasty evaluation is not appropriate. The best next step is to make the clinical diagnosis and start conservative management, reserving imaging (ultrasound or MRI to assess the abductor tendons/partial tears, or radiographs if joint pathology is suspected) for atypical or refractory cases. Lumbar MRI is unwarranted given the absent radicular features and negative straight-leg raise. EMG/NCS is for suspected neuropathy/radiculopathy, which her exam excludes. Conservative, tendinopathy-directed care is the right starting point.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for her gluteal tendinopathy / GTPS?
options: Immediate surgical bursectomy
Activity/load modification, hip abductor (gluteal) strengthening and gait/posture training, avoiding hip adduction (e.g., leg-crossing, side-lying on the painful hip), and NSAIDs
Repeated corticosteroid injections every few weeks indefinitely
Strict bed rest and a wheelchair
stages.explanation: First-line treatment mirrors tendinopathy management: relative load modification, a progressive hip abductor (gluteal) strengthening and gait/posture program, and education to avoid provocative adducted positions (leg-crossing, standing 'hanging' on one hip, and side-lying on the painful side at night). NSAIDs and weight management help symptoms. Surgical intervention (e.g., for abductor tendon tears or bursectomy) is reserved for refractory cases after extended conservative care. A single corticosteroid injection can provide short-term relief for severe pain, but repeated frequent injections are not appropriate and may impair tendon healing. Strict bed rest and wheelchair use cause deconditioning and worsen abductor function. Load-based rehab is the evidence-based foundation.

cases.diagnosis: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome (gluteal tendinopathy)
pearls: GTPS = focal greater trochanteric tenderness and pain with resisted abduction/single-leg stance, with PAIN-FREE passive hip rotation (which argues against intra-articular OA).
The true driver is usually gluteus medius/minimus tendinopathy, not a primary 'bursitis' — treat it like a tendinopathy.
First-line care is load modification, hip abductor strengthening/gait training, and avoiding adducted positions (leg-crossing, side-lying on the painful hip).
Reserve corticosteroid injection for short-term relief and surgery for refractory cases or significant abductor tendon tears.

cases.title: A Finger That Catches and Locks
cases.chiefComplaint: Her right middle finger keeps catching and then snaps painfully when she straightens it.
cases.history: A 53-year-old administrative assistant presents with 3 months of right middle-finger discomfort. The finger catches in a flexed position, then suddenly releases with a painful pop when she forces it straight, sometimes requiring her to use the other hand to pry it open. Symptoms are worst in the morning and after prolonged gripping or typing. She has noticed a tender lump at the base of the finger on the palm side. No trauma, redness, or systemic symptoms. PMH is significant for type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.8%) on metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. No prior hand surgery. NKDA. She drinks alcohol socially, does not smoke, and works long hours at a keyboard. Family history is unremarkable. She is right-hand dominant and is increasingly frustrated because the catching interrupts her work and occasionally wakes her at night. She denies numbness, tingling, or wrist pain, and has tried over-the-counter ibuprofen with minimal relief.
cases.exam: Right hand: a palpable, tender nodule overlies the A1 pulley at the base of the third metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joint on the volar surface. Active flexion produces a triggering sensation, and extension reproduces a palpable and audible catch with brief locking. No MCP or PIP joint swelling, warmth, or erythema. Heberden and Bouchard nodes are absent. Sensation to light touch is intact in all digits; Tinel and Phalen tests at the wrist are negative. Grip strength is mildly reduced by guarding. No thenar atrophy. The remaining digits move smoothly without catching.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which structure is the primary site of pathology responsible for this patient's catching and locking?
options: The flexor tendon and the A1 pulley at the metacarpophalangeal level
The median nerve within the carpal tunnel
The proximal interphalangeal joint cartilage
The extensor tendon central slip
stages.explanation: Trigger finger (stenosing tenosynovitis) results from a size mismatch between a thickened flexor tendon/nodule and the first annular (A1) pulley, causing the tendon to catch as it passes through, then snap free, producing triggering and locking. The palpable nodule over the A1 pulley confirms this. Median nerve compression (carpal tunnel) causes numbness and a positive Tinel/Phalen, which are absent here. PIP cartilage degeneration causes joint pain and stiffness, not triggering with a volar nodule. Central slip extensor injury produces a boutonniere deformity (PIP flexion, DIP extension), not catching during flexion. The clinical hallmark — a tender A1-pulley nodule with mechanical triggering — points squarely to flexor tendon entrapment.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Which feature of this patient's history most increases her risk for this condition?
options: Social alcohol use
Diabetes mellitus
Levothyroxine therapy
Right-hand dominance
stages.explanation: Diabetes mellitus is a well-established risk factor for trigger finger; diabetics develop it more frequently, often in multiple digits, and respond less reliably to corticosteroid injection. Her HbA1c of 7.8% confirms suboptimal control. Hypothyroidism is also associated, but levothyroxine treatment itself is not the risk — the underlying endocrinopathy is, and her thyroid is being replaced. Social alcohol use has no established link to stenosing tenosynovitis. Hand dominance influences which hand is symptomatic but is not an independent risk factor; repetitive gripping contributes, yet diabetes is the strongest, most exam-relevant association and also predicts a less robust injection response.

stages.label: INITIAL MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has mild, intermittent triggering and wants to try the least invasive option first. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: Open A1 pulley release in the operating room
Percutaneous needle release in clinic
MCP-blocking splint, activity modification, and NSAIDs
Wrist immobilization in a cock-up splint
stages.explanation: For mild, early trigger finger, conservative care is first-line: an MCP-blocking (extension) splint worn especially at night limits the tendon excursion that drives catching, combined with activity modification and NSAIDs for symptom relief. Many patients improve without injection or surgery. Open A1 release is reserved for refractory cases after failed conservative and injection therapy. Percutaneous needle release is an option but is more invasive than splinting and typically follows failed conservative measures. A wrist cock-up splint targets the carpal tunnel, not the A1 pulley at the MCP joint, and would not control digital triggering. Stepwise escalation beginning with splinting is the standard approach.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP VISIT
stages.content:
6 weeks later:
Continued painful triggering despite nightly MCP splint and NSAIDs
Finger now intermittently locks in flexion requiring manual unlocking
Persistent tender A1-pulley nodule
No new numbness, infection, or skin breakdown
Finger fully passively extendable

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Conservative therapy has failed. What is the most appropriate next step before considering surgery?
options: Proceed directly to open A1 pulley release
Corticosteroid injection into the flexor tendon sheath
Oral prednisone taper for two weeks
Repeat the same splinting regimen for another 6 weeks
stages.explanation: After failed splinting, a corticosteroid injection into the flexor tendon sheath at the A1 pulley is the next step and is effective in the majority of non-diabetic patients; even diabetics benefit, though with lower and less durable success and a transient rise in blood glucose she should be warned about. Surgery (open A1 release) is reserved for failure of one or two injections or for fixed locking. Oral prednisone is not standard and exposes her to systemic effects, especially risky with diabetes. Simply repeating an already-failed splinting course wastes time. The correct stepwise escalation is splint to injection to surgery.

cases.diagnosis: Trigger finger (stenosing tenosynovitis) of the right middle finger
pearls: The catch occurs as a thickened flexor tendon nodule passes the A1 pulley; the tender nodule sits over the volar MCP joint, not the PIP.
Diabetes predicts both higher incidence and a poorer, less durable response to corticosteroid injection.
Stepwise management: MCP-blocking splint and activity modification, then sheath corticosteroid injection, then A1 pulley release for refractory or locked digits.
Warn diabetic patients that a steroid injection can transiently elevate blood glucose.

cases.title: Burning Between the Toes
cases.chiefComplaint: She has burning, electric pain in the ball of her foot that feels like a pebble in her shoe.
cases.history: A 49-year-old retail manager reports 5 months of progressive burning, tingling pain in the ball of her right foot, localized between the third and fourth toes. She describes a sensation of walking on a bunched-up sock or a pebble, with intermittent electric shocks radiating into the adjacent toes. Pain worsens with tight dress shoes and high heels worn at work and improves dramatically when she removes her shoes and massages the forefoot. She sometimes feels numbness in the contiguous sides of the third and fourth toes. No trauma, fevers, or swelling. PMH includes mild hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily. NKDA. She walks several miles daily on hard store floors and frequently wears narrow, pointed shoes. She does not smoke, drinks wine occasionally, and has no diabetes. Family history is noncontributory. She tried wider sneakers on weekends with partial relief but cannot avoid dress shoes at work, and the pain now intrudes on her ability to stand for long shifts.
cases.exam: Right forefoot: tenderness is localized to the third intermetatarsal (web) space. Lateral compression of the metatarsal heads while pressing the symptomatic web space produces a palpable, audible click that reproduces her pain (positive Mulder sign). Decreased sensation along the contiguous borders of the third and fourth toes. No erythema, warmth, swelling, or focal bony tenderness over the metatarsal heads or shafts. No hammertoe deformity or hallux valgus. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+. Ankle and subtalar motion are full and painless. Tinel at the tarsal tunnel is negative.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which physical exam finding best supports the most likely diagnosis?
options: Pain reproduced by dorsiflexing the great toe
A palpable click on mediolateral metatarsal compression (Mulder sign)
Focal tenderness over the second metatarsal shaft
A positive Tinel sign behind the medial malleolus
stages.explanation: A positive Mulder sign — a palpable, often audible click when the metatarsal heads are squeezed together while compressing the web space — is characteristic of Morton neuroma, an interdigital perineural fibrosis most common in the third web space. Combined with burning pain and contiguous-toe numbness, it strongly supports the diagnosis. Pain on great-toe dorsiflexion suggests turf toe or hallux rigidus. Tenderness over the second metatarsal shaft suggests a stress fracture. A positive Tinel behind the medial malleolus indicates tarsal tunnel syndrome (posterior tibial nerve), which would cause plantar, not isolated interdigital, symptoms. The Mulder click localizes the pathology to the interdigital nerve.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: If imaging is obtained to confirm the diagnosis and exclude mimics, which modality is most useful?
options: Weight-bearing plain radiographs only
Bone scan of the forefoot
Non-contrast CT of the midfoot
Ultrasound of the involved web space
stages.explanation: The diagnosis of Morton neuroma is primarily clinical, but when imaging is needed, ultrasound of the web space readily demonstrates the hypoechoic interdigital mass and can guide injection; it is inexpensive and dynamic. MRI is an alternative. Plain radiographs are useful to exclude stress fracture or arthritis but cannot visualize the neuroma itself, so they alone do not confirm it.
