A bone scan is nonspecific and better suited to occult fractures or osteomyelitis. CT excels at bony detail but poorly characterizes soft-tissue neural pathology. Ultrasound directly images the offending lesion, making it the best confirmatory study here.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Weight-bearing foot radiographs: no fracture, no significant arthritis, normal metatarsal alignment
Forefoot ultrasound: 6 mm hypoechoic, well-defined mass in the third intermetatarsal space consistent with an interdigital neuroma
No bursal fluid collection or osseous erosion

stages.label: INITIAL MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for this patient?
options: Surgical neuroma excision
Corticosteroid injection into the web space
Wide toe-box footwear with a metatarsal pad and activity modification
Radiofrequency ablation of the interdigital nerve
stages.explanation: First-line treatment is conservative: roomy, low-heeled shoes with a wide toe box plus a metatarsal pad placed just proximal to the metatarsal heads to splay them and offload the compressed nerve, alongside activity modification. Many patients improve substantially. Corticosteroid injection is a reasonable second step if footwear and padding fail, offering temporary relief. Surgical excision (with risk of recurrent stump neuroma and permanent numbness) is reserved for refractory cases. Radiofrequency or alcohol ablation are later-line options. Because her symptoms are clearly provoked by tight, high-heeled shoes and eased by wider footwear, mechanical offloading should be optimized before any injection or procedure.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After 8 weeks of optimized footwear and a metatarsal pad, she has only mild improvement and still cannot tolerate work shifts. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Image-guided corticosteroid injection into the web space
Immediate surgical excision of the neuroma
Start gabapentin and continue current footwear indefinitely
Order MRI of the lumbar spine
stages.explanation: When footwear modification and a metatarsal pad provide inadequate relief, an image-guided corticosteroid injection into the affected web space is the appropriate next step, often delivering meaningful, if sometimes temporary, relief and confirming the pain generator. Surgical excision is premature before an injection trial and carries risks of permanent numbness and recurrent stump neuroma. Gabapentin may be a useful adjunct but is not the definitive next mechanical step and should not replace targeted treatment. Lumbar MRI would be indicated only if a radiculopathy were suspected; her focal interdigital findings and positive Mulder sign localize the lesion to the forefoot, making spinal imaging unnecessary.

cases.diagnosis: Morton neuroma (third intermetatarsal interdigital neuroma) of the right foot
pearls: The third web space is the most common site; burning pain with contiguous-toe numbness and a positive Mulder click is classic.
Diagnosis is clinical; ultrasound or MRI confirms and helps exclude stress fracture or arthritis.
Begin with wide toe-box footwear and a metatarsal pad placed proximal to the metatarsal heads, then corticosteroid injection, then surgery for refractory cases.
Warn patients that surgical excision can cause permanent toe numbness and recurrent stump neuroma.

cases.title: Burning Soles and a Tingling Arch
cases.chiefComplaint: He has burning and tingling on the sole of his foot that worsens with standing and at night.
cases.history: A 45-year-old warehouse worker is referred to the EMG lab for 6 months of burning, tingling paresthesias on the plantar surface of his right foot, involving the heel, arch, and toes. Symptoms intensify with prolonged standing and walking on concrete and frequently wake him at night; shaking the foot and elevating it give partial relief. He recalls a severe inversion ankle sprain a year ago that was treated nonoperatively. He denies back pain, sciatica, or symptoms in the contralateral foot. PMH is unremarkable; he takes no daily medications and has no diabetes. NKDA. He is on his feet 10 hours a day, has overpronated flat feet, smokes half a pack daily, and drinks beer on weekends. Family history is noncontributory. He has tried over-the-counter arch supports and ibuprofen without relief, and is concerned because the foot now feels weak and clumsy when he pushes off. He reports no bowel or bladder symptoms and no weakness elsewhere.
cases.exam: Right ankle: a positive Tinel sign behind and below the medial malleolus reproduces burning paresthesias radiating into the sole. Decreased light-touch and pinprick sensation over the plantar foot, sparing the medial ankle and dorsum. Mild weakness and atrophy of the toe flexors and intrinsic foot muscles with subtle clawing. Pes planus with hindfoot valgus is present. No tenderness at the plantar fascia origin, and the windlass test is negative. Ankle dorsiflexion, inversion, and eversion strength are normal proximally. Straight-leg raise is negative, and reflexes (including ankle jerk) are symmetric. Dorsal foot sensation and pulses are intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which nerve is most likely entrapped in this patient?
options: Common peroneal nerve at the fibular head
Sural nerve along the lateral foot
Posterior tibial nerve within the tarsal tunnel
S1 nerve root in the lumbar spine
stages.explanation: Plantar burning paresthesias with a positive Tinel behind the medial malleolus, nocturnal worsening, and intrinsic foot atrophy point to posterior tibial nerve entrapment in the tarsal tunnel. The prior ankle sprain and hindfoot valgus are recognized precipitants. Common peroneal entrapment causes a foot drop and dorsolateral leg/dorsal-foot sensory loss, not plantar symptoms. The sural nerve supplies the lateral foot only and does not cause plantar burning or intrinsic weakness. An S1 radiculopathy would typically produce back pain, a positive straight-leg raise, a diminished ankle reflex, and a dermatomal pattern — all absent here. The medial-ankle Tinel and plantar distribution localize the lesion to the tarsal tunnel.

stages.label: ELECTRODIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which electrodiagnostic finding would most specifically confirm posterior tibial nerve entrapment at the tarsal tunnel?
options: Prolonged distal motor latencies to the abductor hallucis and abductor digiti minimi with normal proximal tibial conduction
Diffusely reduced conduction velocities in all four limbs
Reduced peroneal motor amplitude with normal tibial studies
Fibrillations limited to the lumbar paraspinal muscles
stages.explanation: Tarsal tunnel syndrome is confirmed when the medial and lateral plantar nerves show prolonged distal motor latencies (to abductor hallucis and abductor digiti minimi) and slowed or absent plantar sensory responses, while tibial conduction proximal to the tunnel is normal — localizing the lesion across the tunnel. Mixed plantar sensory/nerve action potentials are the most sensitive measures. Diffuse slowing in all limbs indicates a generalized polyneuropathy, not a focal entrapment. Reduced peroneal amplitude points to peroneal pathology. Paraspinal fibrillations indicate radiculopathy. Demonstrating focal distal latency prolongation with preserved proximal conduction is the electrodiagnostic hallmark of entrapment at the tarsal tunnel.

stages.label: ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES
stages.content:
Medial plantar mixed NAP: prolonged latency, reduced amplitude on the right; normal on the left
Lateral plantar mixed NAP: absent on the right
Tibial motor distal latency to abductor hallucis: 7.2 ms (normal <6.1 ms)
Tibial motor distal latency to abductor digiti minimi: prolonged
Proximal tibial motor conduction velocity (popliteal fossa to ankle): normal
Peroneal motor and sural sensory studies: normal
Needle EMG: chronic neurogenic changes in abductor hallucis; tibialis posterior and gastrocnemius normal; lumbar paraspinals normal

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Given confirmed tarsal tunnel syndrome and a remote ankle injury with hindfoot valgus, which imaging study best evaluates for a treatable structural cause?
options: Non-contrast head CT
MRI of the ankle and tarsal tunnel
Whole-body bone scan
Lumbar spine MRI
stages.explanation: MRI of the ankle is the best study to identify space-occupying or structural causes of tarsal tunnel syndrome — ganglion cysts, varicosities, tenosynovitis, an accessory muscle, post-traumatic scar, or a talocalcaneal coalition — many of which are surgically treatable. Identifying a mass changes management toward decompression with excision. A head CT is irrelevant to a peripheral entrapment. A whole-body bone scan is nonspecific and would not characterize the soft-tissue contents of the tunnel. Lumbar MRI would be appropriate only if a radiculopathy were suspected, but the electrodiagnostic study already localized the lesion distally with normal paraspinals, making spinal imaging unnecessary. Ankle MRI directly visualizes the tunnel.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: MRI shows no discrete mass but confirms hindfoot valgus and flexor tenosynovitis. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate tarsal tunnel release surgery
Custom orthotics with medial posting, NSAIDs, neuropathic agents, and activity modification
Ankle fusion
Long-leg casting for 8 weeks
stages.explanation: Without a discrete surgically correctable mass, initial management is conservative: a custom orthotic with medial posting to correct the hindfoot valgus and reduce traction on the posterior tibial nerve, NSAIDs and a neuropathic agent (e.g., gabapentin) for symptoms, activity modification, and possibly a corticosteroid injection. Surgical tarsal tunnel release is reserved for failure of conservative care or a clear compressive lesion, and its results are best when a structural cause is found. Ankle fusion is far too aggressive and not indicated. Prolonged casting would promote stiffness and disuse without addressing the underlying traction biomechanics. Correcting alignment and controlling neuropathic pain is the appropriate first step.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After 3 months of orthotics, NSAIDs, gabapentin, and a corticosteroid injection, his plantar burning and intrinsic weakness persist and the prior MRI showed flexor tenosynovitis without a discrete mass. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue the identical conservative regimen indefinitely
Refer for tarsal tunnel release with tenosynovectomy
Start chronic opioid therapy
Perform a lumbar epidural steroid injection
stages.explanation: When a well-conducted conservative program (orthotics correcting hindfoot valgus, NSAIDs, a neuropathic agent, and a corticosteroid injection) fails and electrodiagnosis has confirmed focal posterior tibial entrapment, surgical tarsal tunnel release — with tenosynovectomy to address the flexor tenosynovitis identified on MRI — is the appropriate next step, particularly with progressive motor involvement and intrinsic atrophy. Continuing an identical failed regimen wastes time and risks further axonal loss. Chronic opioids are inappropriate for a focal neuropathic entrapment and do not treat the cause. A lumbar epidural targets radicular pathology, which was excluded by normal paraspinals and proximal conduction; the lesion is localized to the tunnel. Decompression is the logical escalation after failed conservative care.

cases.diagnosis: Tarsal tunnel syndrome (posterior tibial nerve entrapment) of the right foot
pearls: Plantar burning with a positive medial-ankle Tinel and intrinsic foot atrophy localizes to the posterior tibial nerve in the tarsal tunnel.
Electrodiagnosis hinges on prolonged distal motor latencies to abductor hallucis and abductor digiti minimi with absent/slowed plantar sensory responses and normal proximal tibial conduction.
MRI of the ankle searches for treatable space-occupying causes (ganglion, varicosities, accessory muscle, coalition); surgery works best when a structural lesion is found.
Conservative care with medial-posted orthotics to correct hindfoot valgus addresses the traction biomechanics before considering release.

cases.title: A Patch of Burning on the Outer Thigh
cases.chiefComplaint: He has burning, tingling, and numbness over the outer part of his thigh.
cases.history: A 41-year-old truck driver presents with 3 months of burning, tingling, and patchy numbness over the anterolateral aspect of his right thigh. The area feels hypersensitive to clothing and his tight belt, yet partly numb to touch. Symptoms worsen with prolonged standing, walking, and wearing his work belt low on the abdomen, and improve when he sits and loosens his belt. He has gained 30 pounds over the past year. He denies back pain, leg weakness, knee buckling, or bowel and bladder changes. PMH includes prediabetes and obesity. He takes no regular medications. NKDA. He is sedentary, smokes occasionally, and wears a heavy tool belt and tight jeans for work. Family history includes type 2 diabetes. He is worried about a pinched nerve in his back and has tried acetaminophen without benefit. The symptoms are confined entirely to the thigh and do not cross the knee, and he reports no change in his gait other than discomfort.
cases.exam: BMI 34 kg/m². Right anterolateral thigh: a well-demarcated patch of altered sensation with both hyperesthesia and reduced light-touch/pinprick, not crossing the midline or extending below the knee. Tinel-like tapping just medial to the anterior superior iliac spine reproduces the thigh paresthesias. Hip extension and prolonged standing exacerbate symptoms. Motor strength is full throughout the lower extremities, including hip flexion, knee extension, and ankle dorsiflexion. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are symmetric. Straight-leg raise is negative. No spinal tenderness. Gait is normal without a Trendelenburg lurch or foot drop.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which nerve is responsible for this patient's symptoms?
options: Lateral femoral cutaneous nerve
Femoral nerve
L4 nerve root
Obturator nerve
stages.explanation: Meralgia paresthetica is entrapment of the purely sensory lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, usually as it passes near the anterior superior iliac spine under the inguinal ligament. The well-demarcated anterolateral thigh dysesthesia not crossing the knee, with a reproducible Tinel near the ASIS, and provocation by a tight belt and obesity is classic. The femoral nerve supplies motor function (knee extension) and the medial leg via the saphenous branch — motor exam is normal here. An L4 radiculopathy would cause back pain, a positive straight-leg raise, weakness, and reflex changes. The obturator nerve supplies the medial thigh and hip adductors. A pure sensory deficit in the lateral femoral cutaneous distribution confirms the diagnosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most strongly distinguishes this condition from a lumbar radiculopathy?
options: Presence of burning pain
Worsening with prolonged standing
Absence of any motor weakness or reflex change with a purely sensory thigh deficit
Improvement with sitting
stages.explanation: The most discriminating feature is that meralgia paresthetica is purely sensory — there is no motor weakness, no reflex change, and no deficit below the knee, because the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve carries only sensation. A radiculopathy typically adds motor weakness, reflex asymmetry, a positive straight-leg raise, and back pain. Burning pain occurs in both conditions and does not differentiate them. Worsening with standing and improvement with sitting can be seen in entrapment and also in some spinal conditions, so neither is specific. The confinement to a sensory patch on the anterolateral thigh with a completely normal motor and reflex exam localizes the problem to this cutaneous nerve.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Surgical decompression or neurectomy of the nerve
Weight loss, avoiding tight belts and waistbands, and loose clothing
Lumbar epidural steroid injection
Lumbar microdiscectomy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of meralgia paresthetica is conservative and removes the source of compression: weight loss, avoiding tight belts, low-slung tool belts, and constrictive waistbands, plus loose clothing; most patients improve. Neuropathic agents or a local nerve block can be added if needed. Surgical decompression or neurectomy is reserved for refractory, disabling cases after conservative measures and injections fail. A lumbar epidural injection and microdiscectomy treat radicular pathology, which this patient does not have — his exam is purely sensory with a normal back and no nerve-root signs. Because his belt and obesity are the obvious mechanical contributors, addressing those is the appropriate starting point.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP VISIT
stages.content:
10 weeks later:
Lost 8 lb; switched to suspenders and looser clothing
Still significant burning dysesthesia limiting long drives
Persistent positive Tinel medial to the right ASIS
No new weakness or sensory change below the knee
Neurologic exam otherwise unchanged

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Conservative measures have only modestly helped and symptoms remain disabling. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Local anesthetic and corticosteroid nerve block near the anterior superior iliac spine, with or without a neuropathic agent
Immediate neurectomy of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve
Lumbar decompression surgery
Begin high-dose oral opioids
stages.explanation: When conservative measures partially fail, a targeted local anesthetic plus corticosteroid block of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve near the ASIS is the appropriate next step — it is diagnostic and therapeutic and often provides durable relief; a neuropathic agent such as gabapentin can be added. Neurectomy or surgical decompression is reserved for refractory cases that fail blocks and is not the immediate next step. Lumbar decompression addresses a problem this patient does not have. Chronic opioids are inappropriate for a neuropathic entrapment, carry dependence risk, and do not address the mechanical cause. Escalating from lifestyle modification to a targeted nerve block before any surgical consideration is the standard pathway.

cases.diagnosis: Meralgia paresthetica (lateral femoral cutaneous nerve entrapment) of the right thigh
pearls: Meralgia paresthetica is a purely sensory mononeuropathy of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve — no motor weakness, no reflex change, no deficit below the knee.
Obesity, tight belts, low-slung tool belts, pregnancy, and diabetes are common contributors; a Tinel sign near the ASIS reproduces symptoms.
First-line care is conservative: weight loss, loose clothing, and removing constrictive waistbands; add neuropathic agents or a nerve block if needed.
Reserve surgical decompression or neurectomy for disabling cases refractory to conservative care and nerve blocks.

cases.title: Relentless Burning Below an Old Injury
cases.chiefComplaint: He has constant burning pain in his legs and feet years after a spinal cord injury.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man with a T6 ASIA Impairment Scale A (complete) paraplegia from a motor vehicle collision 4 years ago presents to the SCI clinic with 2 years of relentless burning, electric, and squeezing pain in both legs and feet — regions where he otherwise has no voluntary movement or sensation. The pain is constant, rated 8/10, worse at night, and unrelieved by repositioning; it disrupts sleep and mood. He distinguishes this from a separate band of aching, hypersensitive pain at his trunk near the injury level. He denies recent fever, worsening spasticity, new pressure injuries, or autonomic dysreflexia episodes. PMH: neurogenic bladder on intermittent catheterization, neurogenic bowel, prior pressure injury (healed). Meds: baclofen 20 mg three times daily, acetaminophen as needed. NKDA. He lives independently in a wheelchair-accessible apartment, does not smoke, and has supportive family. He has tried tramadol with little benefit and is frustrated, asking whether the pain will ever fully resolve.
cases.exam: Alert, in no acute distress. Motor: 5/5 in bilateral upper limbs; 0/5 throughout both lower limbs. Sensory: intact above T6; a narrow band of hyperesthesia/allodynia at the T6–T7 trunk; absent light touch, pinprick, and proprioception below T6 including both lower limbs, yet he reports severe spontaneous burning pain in those anesthetic legs and feet. Reflexes brisk in the lower limbs with sustained clonus and bilateral upgoing toes; tone increased (spasticity). No new pressure injuries, no calf swelling or asymmetry, no abdominal tenderness or distended bladder. Skin intact. Upper-limb sensation and strength normal.

stages.label: CLASSIFICATION
stages.question: How is the constant burning pain in his anesthetic legs and feet best classified?
options: Nociceptive musculoskeletal pain
At-level neuropathic pain
Below-level (infralesional) neuropathic pain
Visceral pain from the neurogenic bladder
stages.explanation: Pain perceived diffusely in regions caudal to the neurologic level of injury — here the anesthetic legs and feet below T6 — is below-level (infralesional) neuropathic pain, arising from central reorganization and deafferentation within the cord and brain. At-level neuropathic pain occupies a narrow band at or just below the injury level (his T6–T7 allodynic band) and is segmental. Nociceptive musculoskeletal pain is provoked by movement or palpation of muscles/joints and is not burning or electric in anesthetic territory. Visceral pain localizes to the abdomen/pelvis and relates to bladder or bowel. The constant burning in insensate distal limbs is the textbook picture of below-level neuropathic pain.

stages.label: DIFFERENTIATION
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes his at-level pain from his below-level pain?
options: At-level pain occupies a narrow segmental band near the injury, while below-level pain is felt diffusely caudal to the lesion
Below-level pain is always nociceptive while at-level pain is neuropathic
At-level pain only occurs with movement
Below-level pain resolves spontaneously within weeks
stages.explanation: At-level neuropathic pain is segmental — a narrow band of allodynia/hyperesthesia at or within a few segments of the neurologic level (his T6–T7 trunk band) — whereas below-level pain is diffuse, spreading through regions well caudal to the lesion (his legs and feet). Both are neuropathic, so calling below-level pain nociceptive is incorrect. At-level pain is not purely movement-evoked; that description fits musculoskeletal nociceptive pain. Below-level neuropathic pain is typically chronic and persistent rather than self-limited, often becoming a long-term challenge. The spatial relationship to the neurologic level — segmental versus diffusely infralesional — is the defining distinction.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Tramadol has failed. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic therapy for his below-level neuropathic pain?
options: Long-acting oral opioids
A gabapentinoid (gabapentin or pregabalin) and/or a tricyclic/SNRI antidepressant
Intravenous corticosteroids
Increase baclofen to maximal dose
stages.explanation: First-line pharmacotherapy for neuropathic pain after spinal cord injury is a gabapentinoid (pregabalin, which has the strongest SCI-specific evidence, or gabapentin) and/or an antidepressant such as a tricyclic (amitriptyline) or an SNRI (duloxetine); these target central neuropathic mechanisms. Opioids are not first-line for chronic central neuropathic pain given limited efficacy and substantial risks, including in this complete-injury patient. Corticosteroids have no role in chronic below-level neuropathic pain. Baclofen treats spasticity, not neuropathic pain, and escalating it will not address the burning. Starting an anticonvulsant or appropriate antidepressant, titrated to effect, is the evidence-based approach.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
MRI of the spinal cord: no syrinx, cord compression, or new pathology; expected myelomalacia at the injury level
CBC, CMP: within normal limits
No evidence of infection or new pressure injury
Urinalysis: no infection

stages.label: EXPECTATIONS
stages.question: When counseling him on realistic outcomes, which statement is most accurate?
options: Below-level neuropathic pain is usually curable with the right single medication
Treatment aims for partial pain reduction and improved function and sleep, often using a multimodal approach, as complete relief is uncommon
Surgical cordotomy reliably eliminates the pain permanently
The pain indicates his injury is progressing and he will lose upper-limb function
stages.explanation: Below-level neuropathic SCI pain is notoriously refractory; realistic counseling sets the goal as meaningful partial reduction in pain with improved sleep, mood, and function rather than cure, typically through a multimodal plan combining pharmacology, physical activity, psychological strategies (e.g., cognitive-behavioral therapy), and sometimes neuromodulation. No single drug is curative. Ablative procedures such as cordotomy are not reliable, durable solutions and carry significant risks. His normal MRI and stable upper-limb exam reassure that the pain does not signal ascending deterioration; a new syrinx would be a concern only with progressive signs, which are absent. Honest, function-focused expectations improve adherence and satisfaction.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After 10 weeks on titrated pregabalin plus amitriptyline he reports a roughly 40% reduction in pain and better sleep, but pain remains bothersome. Which adjunctive approach is most appropriate to add next?
options: Switch entirely to long-acting opioids
Add nonpharmacologic measures such as cognitive-behavioral therapy, a structured exercise program, and consider transcranial or transcutaneous neuromodulation
Stop all medications because partial relief means they are ineffective
Add chronic systemic corticosteroids
stages.explanation: A meaningful partial response to first-line agents should be built upon, not abandoned. The appropriate next step is a multimodal addition: psychological therapy (cognitive-behavioral therapy), a structured exercise/activity program, and consideration of neuromodulation (e.g., transcranial direct-current or repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation, or TENS) for refractory central neuropathic pain — improving function, mood, and pain together. Switching wholesale to opioids is not indicated; they are not first-line for chronic central neuropathic pain and carry major risks. Stopping medications that produced a 40% improvement and better sleep discards genuine benefit. Systemic corticosteroids have no role in chronic below-level neuropathic pain. Layering nonpharmacologic and neuromodulatory strategies onto effective drugs reflects best practice.

cases.diagnosis: Below-level (infralesional) neuropathic pain in chronic T6 complete spinal cord injury
pearls: Below-level neuropathic pain is felt diffusely caudal to the lesion (even in anesthetic regions); at-level pain is a narrow segmental band of allodynia near the injury.
Both at-level and below-level SCI pain are neuropathic; distinguish them from nociceptive musculoskeletal and visceral pain by location and provoking features.
First-line pharmacotherapy is a gabapentinoid (pregabalin has the best SCI evidence) and/or a tricyclic/SNRI; opioids are not first-line for chronic central pain.
Counsel toward partial relief and functional gains via a multimodal plan; a new syrinx should be considered if ascending neurologic signs appear.

cases.title: A Chin That Drifts Toward the Chest
cases.chiefComplaint: Her head keeps dropping forward and she cannot hold it up by the end of the day.
cases.history: A 70-year-old retired teacher presents with 6 months of progressive difficulty holding her head upright. By afternoon her chin drifts toward her chest and she must use a hand to lift it; lying down or pushing the head back relieves it temporarily. She has trouble looking ahead while walking and reading, and the forward posture causes posterior neck ache. She denies dysphagia, diplopia, ptosis, slurred speech, limb weakness, fasciculations, or sensory loss, and reports no fatigable chewing. PMH: hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily and hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. NKDA. She is a lifelong nonsmoker, drinks rarely, and lives with her husband. Family history is unremarkable. She has no Parkinsonian tremor, no prior radiation, and no known neuromuscular disease. She is anxious that she may have a serious neurologic illness and notes the head drop is purely a problem of holding the head up — once supported, her neck and limbs feel normal and she can voluntarily extend the neck when reminded.
cases.exam: Marked passively correctable forward flexion of the head (chin-on-chest) due to weak neck extensors; she can transiently extend the neck against gravity but fatigues. Neck extensor strength is markedly reduced; neck flexors relatively preserved. No ptosis; extraocular movements full without fatigability on sustained upgaze; no facial weakness. Bulbar function intact, gag normal, speech clear. Limb strength 5/5 proximally and distally with no fasciculations, atrophy, or fatigable weakness on repetitive testing. Deep tendon reflexes normal and symmetric; no Hoffman sign, no Babinski, no spasticity. Sensation intact. Gait stable but with compensatory trunk posture. No resting tremor or rigidity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which clinical feature most strongly suggests a neuromuscular cause specifically localized to the cervical extensors rather than a generalized neurologic disorder?
options: Passively correctable head drop with isolated, profound neck extensor weakness and an otherwise normal neurologic exam
Brisk reflexes with bilateral Babinski signs
A resting pill-rolling tremor
Diffuse limb fasciculations with atrophy
stages.explanation: Dropped head syndrome is defined by passively correctable chin-on-chest posture from disproportionate, often isolated weakness of the cervical paraspinal extensors with an otherwise normal exam — pointing to a focal extensor myopathy. Brisk reflexes with Babinski signs indicate an upper-motor-neuron process such as ALS or myelopathy, which must be excluded but are absent here. A resting pill-rolling tremor suggests Parkinson disease (which can cause antecollis but with rigidity/tremor). Diffuse fasciculations with atrophy point to motor neuron disease. The combination of selective extensor weakness with correctability and no long-tract, bulbar, ocular, or sensory signs localizes the problem to the neck extensor muscles.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which two conditions are the most important to actively exclude before diagnosing isolated neck extensor myopathy?
options: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and myasthenia gravis
Cervical disc herniation and rotator cuff tear
Migraine and tension headache
Carpal tunnel syndrome and de Quervain tenosynovitis
stages.explanation: Dropped head can be the presenting sign of serious systemic neuromuscular disease, and the two most critical to exclude are ALS (motor neuron disease, where head drop reflects extensor motor-neuron loss, usually with fasciculations, atrophy, or UMN signs) and myasthenia gravis (where fatigable extensor weakness produces head drop, often with ptosis, diplopia, or bulbar fatigability). Both can present with neck extensor weakness before other findings. Cervical disc disease and rotator cuff pathology do not cause symmetric extensor failure with correctable head drop. Headache syndromes are unrelated. Distal upper-limb entrapments are irrelevant. Identifying or excluding ALS and myasthenia fundamentally changes prognosis and treatment, so they top the differential.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which workup is most appropriate to evaluate the leading treatable and dangerous causes?
options: Plain cervical radiographs alone
Lumbar puncture for oligoclonal bands
Echocardiogram and carotid ultrasound
Acetylcholine receptor antibodies, EMG/nerve conduction studies, creatine kinase, TSH, and cervical spine MRI
stages.explanation: A targeted workup covers the treatable and dangerous mimics: acetylcholine receptor (and MuSK) antibodies for myasthenia; EMG/NCS to detect myopathy versus neurogenic/motor-neuron disease and decrement on repetitive stimulation; creatine kinase for inflammatory or other myopathy; TSH because hypothyroid/endocrine myopathy can contribute; and cervical spine MRI to exclude structural myelopathy or paraspinal pathology. Plain radiographs alone miss neuromuscular and cord pathology. A lumbar puncture for oligoclonal bands targets multiple sclerosis, which is not the leading concern. An echocardiogram and carotid ultrasound assess cardiovascular disease, irrelevant to extensor weakness. The combined antibody, electrodiagnostic, enzyme, endocrine, and imaging panel efficiently sorts the differential.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Acetylcholine receptor (binding) antibody: negative
MuSK antibody: negative
Creatine kinase: 180 U/L (mildly elevated)
TSH: 2.4 µIU/mL (normal on replacement)
EMG/NCS: myopathic motor unit potentials limited to the cervical paraspinal extensors; no decrement on repetitive nerve stimulation; no widespread fasciculations or denervation; limb muscles normal
Cervical spine MRI: no cord compression or myelopathy; fatty atrophy of the cervical paraspinal extensor muscles
ESR/CRP: normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Workup confirms isolated cervical extensor myopathy without ALS or myasthenia. What is the most appropriate management?
options: High-dose immunosuppression with IVIG and chronic steroids
Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion
Cervical extensor strengthening and postural physical therapy plus a head-support (cervical) collar/brace, with treatment of any contributing condition
Pyridostigmine and thymectomy
stages.explanation: For isolated neck extensor myopathy without a treatable systemic cause, management is supportive and rehabilitative: a head-support collar or semirigid cervical brace to maintain horizontal gaze and offload the extensors, plus physical therapy emphasizing extensor strengthening, posture, and stretching of tightened flexors, while treating any contributing condition. IVIG and chronic steroids are reserved for confirmed inflammatory myopathy, not idiopathic isolated extensor myopathy. Cervical fusion is for structural instability or deformity, not a primary muscle problem, and would not restore active extension. Pyridostigmine and thymectomy treat myasthenia, which was excluded by negative antibodies and a normal repetitive-stimulation study. Bracing plus targeted rehabilitation is the appropriate, lowest-risk approach.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Six months into bracing and extensor-strengthening therapy she returns with new fatigable double vision late in the day and intermittent difficulty swallowing. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure her these are expected with cervical bracing
Re-evaluate for myasthenia gravis with repeat acetylcholine receptor and MuSK antibodies, repetitive nerve stimulation/single-fiber EMG, and chest imaging for thymoma
Increase the rigidity of the cervical collar
Refer directly for cervical fusion
stages.explanation: New fatigable diplopia and dysphagia are red flags for evolving myasthenia gravis, which can initially present as isolated neck extensor weakness before ocular and bulbar features emerge; her dropped head may have been the heralding sign. The correct step is to re-evaluate with repeat AChR and MuSK antibodies, repetitive nerve stimulation or single-fiber EMG, and chest CT for thymoma, because earlier antibody testing can be negative early in the disease. Calling fatigable diplopia and dysphagia expected effects of a collar is wrong and dangerous. Tightening the collar does not address an emerging neuromuscular junction disorder. Cervical fusion treats structural deformity, not myasthenia, and would be inappropriate. New fatigable bulbar/ocular signs mandate reassessment for myasthenia.

cases.diagnosis: Dropped head syndrome from isolated cervical extensor myopathy
pearls: Dropped head syndrome is a passively correctable chin-on-chest posture from disproportionate cervical extensor weakness; an otherwise normal exam suggests isolated extensor myopathy.
Always exclude ALS (fasciculations, atrophy, UMN signs) and myasthenia gravis (fatigability, ptosis, diplopia, AChR/MuSK antibodies) before labeling it idiopathic.
Workup: AChR/MuSK antibodies, EMG/NCS with repetitive stimulation, CK, TSH, and cervical MRI to find treatable or dangerous causes.
Idiopathic isolated extensor myopathy is managed with a head-support brace and extensor-strengthening physical therapy, not immunosuppression or fusion.

cases.title: A Leg That Gives Way Without a Lesion
cases.chiefComplaint: Her right leg drags and buckles unpredictably, making walking nearly impossible.
cases.history: A 29-year-old graphic designer presents with 4 months of a dragging, buckling right leg and unsteady walking that began abruptly after a minor fall. The disability fluctuates dramatically — some days she can barely stand, yet she has been seen moving the leg normally when distracted. She reports the leg feels heavy and 'not hers.' There is no pain radiating to the leg, no numbness in a dermatomal pattern, and no bowel or bladder dysfunction. She had a prior episode of transient arm weakness that fully resolved and an extensive prior negative neurologic workup. PMH includes anxiety and depression treated with sertraline 100 mg daily; she experienced significant work stress and a relationship breakup before onset. NKDA. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history is unremarkable. She is distressed and fears multiple sclerosis or a stroke, and has had multiple ER visits. She notes the symptoms vary with attention and can briefly normalize, which has made her feel disbelieved by previous providers.
cases.exam: Right leg: give-way (collapsing) weakness on direct hip flexion testing that fluctuates with effort and improves with distraction. Hoover sign is positive — hip extension strength in the 'weak' right leg is absent on direct testing but returns to normal when she flexes the contralateral hip against resistance. Gait is dramatic and effortful with excessive slowness and a dragging, non-circumducting leg that is internally inconsistent and changes with distraction. Tone is normal; no spasticity, atrophy, or fasciculations. Deep tendon reflexes are normal and symmetric; plantar responses flexor. Sensory exam shows non-dermatomal, midline-splitting loss that varies on repeat testing. No cerebellar signs, no cranial nerve deficits.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What does a positive Hoover sign in this patient indicate?
options: An upper motor neuron lesion causing pyramidal weakness
Functional (non-organic) weakness, demonstrated by normal involuntary hip extension that is absent on direct voluntary testing
An L5 radiculopathy
A peripheral femoral neuropathy
stages.explanation: Hoover sign is a positive (rule-in) sign of functional limb weakness: hip extension in the affected leg is weak on direct voluntary testing but normal involuntarily when the patient flexes the opposite hip against resistance, demonstrating intact motor pathways and a discrepancy between voluntary and automatic movement. This internal inconsistency, not merely the absence of a structural lesion, supports a functional neurological disorder. An upper motor neuron lesion would produce consistent weakness with spasticity, hyperreflexia, and an extensor plantar response — absent here. An L5 radiculopathy or femoral neuropathy yields fixed, anatomically patterned weakness and sensory loss that do not vary with attention or distraction. The reproducible discrepancy is the diagnostic key.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which principle is central to correctly diagnosing functional neurological (conversion) disorder?
options: It is made on positive, rule-in clinical signs of internal inconsistency, not solely by excluding organic disease
It is a diagnosis of exclusion made only after every test is negative
It requires an identifiable acute psychological stressor in every case
It can only be diagnosed after a normal lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: Modern criteria diagnose functional neurological disorder on positive, rule-in signs — Hoover sign, give-way weakness, distractibility, tremor entrainment, internal inconsistency, and incongruence with recognized disease — rather than purely excluding organic pathology. Treating it as a pure diagnosis of exclusion delays care and misses the point. A psychological stressor is supportive but no longer required by DSM-5; many patients have none identified, so its absence does not exclude the diagnosis. A lumbar puncture is not a prerequisite. Reasonable investigation to exclude mimics is still warranted, but the diagnosis rests on demonstrable, reproducible inconsistency, which is reassuring to explain to the patient as a real and potentially reversible condition.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
MRI brain and complete spine: no demyelinating lesions, infarct, cord compression, or structural abnormality
Nerve conduction studies and EMG of the right leg: normal
CBC, CMP, TSH, B12, ESR: within normal limits
Neurologic signs remain internally inconsistent with positive Hoover sign on repeat exam

stages.label: COMMUNICATION
stages.question: After confirming the diagnosis, what is the most effective way to communicate it to her?
options: Tell her the tests are normal so there is nothing physically wrong and it is all in her head
Avoid naming the condition to prevent distress
Explain it is a recognized, real disorder of nervous-system functioning, demonstrate the positive Hoover sign, and frame it as potentially reversible
Refer her only to psychiatry without further explanation
stages.explanation: Effective communication is therapeutic: give the diagnosis a clear, validating name (functional neurological disorder), explain it is a genuine problem with how the nervous system functions rather than its structure, and use the patient's own positive signs — showing her the Hoover sign — to demonstrate that the pathways work and the condition can improve. Dismissing it as 'nothing wrong' or 'all in your head' is invalidating, worsens outcomes, and increases health-care use. Withholding the diagnosis prevents understanding and engagement in treatment. A psychiatry-only referral without explanation or rehabilitation misframes the condition and often fails; a positive, transparent explanation is the foundation of recovery.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment for her functional gait disorder?
options: Long-term wheelchair use to protect the leg
Multidisciplinary functional rehabilitation — physiotherapy retraining automatic movement plus psychological therapy (CBT) — built on a clear diagnosis
High-dose corticosteroids
Lifelong benzodiazepines
stages.explanation: The cornerstone is multidisciplinary rehabilitation: specialized physiotherapy that retrains normal, automatic movement patterns (using distraction and movement re-education rather than effortful voluntary control), combined with psychological therapy such as cognitive-behavioral therapy and treatment of comorbid anxiety/depression, all built upon a clearly delivered, believed diagnosis. Promoting wheelchair dependence reinforces disability and abnormal movement patterns and should be avoided. Corticosteroids have no role — there is no inflammatory or demyelinating lesion. Lifelong benzodiazepines risk dependence and do not address the functional movement disorder. Engaging the patient in active, graded functional restoration with a coordinated team offers the best chance of meaningful recovery.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which factor is most strongly associated with a better prognosis in functional neurological disorder?
options: Long symptom duration before diagnosis
Early diagnosis, patient acceptance of the diagnosis, and engagement in functional rehabilitation
Pursuing repeated extensive investigations
Avoiding any discussion of psychological factors
stages.explanation: Outcomes in functional neurological disorder are best when the diagnosis is made early, clearly communicated, and accepted by the patient, who then engages actively in functional rehabilitation (movement-retraining physiotherapy and psychological therapy). Acceptance and engagement are consistently linked to improvement. Long symptom duration before diagnosis predicts a worse outcome, as entrenched abnormal patterns are harder to reverse. Repeated extensive investigations reinforce illness beliefs, delay treatment, and worsen prognosis. Refusing to address psychological contributors forecloses an important treatment avenue and can hinder recovery, even though a stressor is not required for diagnosis. Prompt diagnosis plus an engaged, rehabilitation-focused approach offers the best chance of recovery.

cases.diagnosis: Functional (conversion) gait disorder / functional neurological disorder
pearls: Diagnose functional neurological disorder on positive rule-in signs (Hoover sign, distractibility, internal inconsistency), not merely by excluding organic disease.
A psychological stressor supports but is not required for the diagnosis under DSM-5.
Deliver the diagnosis transparently and validate it — demonstrate the patient's own Hoover sign and frame it as real and potentially reversible.
Treatment is multidisciplinary functional rehabilitation (movement-retraining physiotherapy plus CBT), avoiding reinforcement of disability such as unnecessary wheelchair use.

cases.title: An Inward-Turning Foot After Stroke
cases.chiefComplaint: His left foot turns in and down when he walks, causing him to trip and roll the ankle.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man is referred to the motion analysis lab 14 months after a right middle cerebral artery ischemic stroke that left him with left hemiparesis. He ambulates with a cane but trips frequently because his left foot turns inward and points down during walking, and he bears weight on the lateral border of the foot, spraining the ankle repeatedly. He has plateaued in outpatient therapy and wants to optimize his walking before any surgery. He reports stiffness and occasional painful spasms in the calf. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and the prior stroke. Meds: aspirin 81 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, lisinopril 20 mg daily; he has tried oral baclofen with sedation. NKDA. He is a former smoker, lives with his wife in a single-story home, and is motivated to walk more safely. Family history is noncontributory. He has no prior orthopedic foot surgery and currently wears no brace. He asks specifically whether a tendon operation, an injection, or a brace would help him most, and whether the deformity is from spasticity he can still overcome.
cases.exam: Left lower limb with spastic hemiparesis: equinovarus posturing of the foot at rest and accentuated in stance and swing. Increased tone (modified Ashworth 3) in the gastrocsoleus and tibialis posterior; sustained ankle clonus; hyperreflexia; upgoing plantar response. Passive ankle dorsiflexion is limited but achievable to neutral with the knee flexed, indicating dynamic spasticity rather than a fixed contracture. Manual strength shows preserved volitional firing of tibialis anterior on attempted dorsiflexion. Antagonist (gastrocsoleus/tibialis posterior) over-activity during swing is observed. Sensation mildly reduced distally. Right leg normal. Hip and knee posture acceptable; deformity localizes to the ankle/foot complex.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Why is dynamic (instrumented) gait analysis with dynamic EMG especially valuable before intervening in this patient?
options: It measures bone density to plan surgery
It identifies which specific muscles are over-active during which phases of gait and whether the deformity is dynamic versus fixed, guiding targeted treatment
It replaces the need for a physical examination
It is required for prescribing oral antispasticity medication
stages.explanation: Dynamic EMG during instrumented gait analysis reveals the timing and magnitude of activity in individual muscles across the gait cycle — for example, inappropriate swing-phase firing of the gastrocsoleus and tibialis posterior driving equinovarus, and whether tibialis anterior fires appropriately — and distinguishes a dynamic, spasticity-driven deformity from a fixed contracture. This precise phasic information directs treatment: which muscles to target with botulinum toxin or to lengthen/transfer surgically, and whether bracing alone suffices. It does not measure bone density, does not replace the clinical exam (which it complements), and is not a prerequisite for prescribing oral medication. Targeting the actual offending muscles at the right gait phase is the entire rationale for the study.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: On examination his ankle passively dorsiflexes to neutral with the knee flexed, and the inverted-plantarflexed posture worsens during walking. What does this indicate about the nature of his deformity?
options: A fixed bony contracture requiring arthrodesis
A dynamic, spasticity-driven deformity that is passively correctable and therefore potentially responsive to focal tone reduction and bracing
A flaccid foot drop from peripheral nerve injury
A purely sensory ataxic gait disturbance
stages.explanation: Because the ankle passively dorsiflexes to neutral (with the knee flexed to relax the gastrocnemius) and the equinovarus posture is accentuated dynamically during gait, the deformity is dynamic and spasticity-driven rather than a fixed contracture. This distinction is pivotal: a passively correctable, dynamic deformity is amenable to focal tone reduction (botulinum toxin) and an ankle-foot orthosis, and dynamic EMG can pinpoint the offending muscles. A fixed bony contracture would not correct passively and would point toward arthrodesis or lengthening. A flaccid foot drop from peripheral nerve injury would show hypotonia and no spasticity, opposite to his hyperreflexia and increased tone. A sensory ataxic gait would feature proprioceptive loss and a wide-based unsteady gait, not spastic equinovarus. Correctability defines the treatment path.

stages.label: DYNAMIC EMG & GAIT ANALYSIS
stages.content:
Kinematics: ankle in plantarflexion and inversion throughout stance and swing; foot contact on lateral border
Dynamic EMG — gastrocnemius/soleus: premature and prolonged activity continuing into swing phase
Dynamic EMG — tibialis posterior: continuous over-activity throughout the gait cycle
Dynamic EMG — tibialis anterior: weak, out-of-phase but present voluntary activity
Dynamic EMG — peroneals (evertors): markedly reduced activity
Passive range of motion: ankle dorsiflexes to neutral with knee flexed (no fixed contracture)
Result: dynamic spastic equinovarus, no fixed bony deformity

stages.label: TARGETED INTERVENTION
stages.question: Given dynamic spasticity without fixed contracture and over-active gastrocsoleus and tibialis posterior, which targeted intervention is most appropriate to trial?
options: Immediate triple arthrodesis of the foot
Lifelong wheelchair use
Increase oral baclofen until the deformity resolves
Botulinum toxin injection into the over-active gastrocsoleus and tibialis posterior, combined with an ankle-foot orthosis and therapy
stages.explanation: Because the deformity is dynamic (correctable passively, no fixed contracture) and EMG localizes the over-activity to the gastrocsoleus and tibialis posterior, focal chemodenervation with botulinum toxin into those muscles plus an ankle-foot orthosis to control equinovarus and stretching/strengthening therapy is the appropriate, reversible next step — and it also serves as a 'test' of how the gait responds before any irreversible surgery. Triple arthrodesis is for fixed bony deformity, not dynamic spasticity. Wheelchair use abandons his ambulation goal. Escalating oral baclofen already caused intolerable sedation and lacks the focal selectivity needed; systemic agents cannot target individual swing-phase muscles. Focal toxin with orthotic and therapy is the guideline-concordant trial.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He responds well to botulinum toxin and an AFO but the benefit predictably wanes by 3-4 months. Dynamic EMG continues to show focal, consistent tibialis posterior over-activity with preserved tibialis anterior firing. What is the most appropriate longer-term surgical option?
options: Tendon-balancing surgery such as split anterior tibial tendon transfer with gastrocnemius/Achilles lengthening
Below-knee amputation
Spinal cord stimulator placement
Repeat the same oral baclofen that previously failed
stages.explanation: When focal toxin gives a clear but temporary benefit and EMG identifies a consistent muscular driver with usable voluntary tibialis anterior activity, a durable surgical solution is tendon-balancing: a split anterior tibial tendon transfer (SPLATT) to rebalance inversion, often combined with gastrocnemius or Achilles lengthening for equinus and tibialis posterior lengthening/transfer for varus — guided by the dynamic EMG findings. A positive but transient toxin response predicts a good surgical outcome. Amputation is not indicated for a correctable functional deformity in an ambulator. A spinal cord stimulator does not address focal spastic equinovarus. Repeating an agent that already failed with sedation is futile. EMG-guided tendon transfer offers lasting correction.

stages.label: PRINCIPLE
stages.question: Which overarching principle does this case illustrate about treating hemiplegic equinovarus gait?
options: All spastic deformities should be corrected surgically as early as possible

Treatment is staged and EMG-guided — reversible focal toxin and bracing first, with tendon surgery reserved for fixed or consistently confirmed muscular drivers, selecting the level matched to the deformity
Oral antispasticity drugs are always superior to focal therapy
Bracing should be avoided in stroke patients
stages.explanation: The principle is a staged, individualized, EMG-guided ladder: confirm whether the deformity is dynamic or fixed, trial reversible measures first (botulinum toxin to the specific over-active muscles plus an appropriately chosen ankle-foot orthosis and therapy), and reserve tendon-balancing surgery for fixed contractures or when a transient-but-positive toxin response confirms the muscular driver and the patient needs durable correction. Routine early surgery ignores reversible options and risks over-correction. Oral antispasticity agents lack focal selectivity and often cause sedation, as they did here. Bracing is a mainstay, not something to avoid. Matching the intervention and its anatomic level to the precise, phase-specific dysfunction is the core lesson.

cases.diagnosis: Dynamic spastic equinovarus gait following right MCA stroke (left hemiplegia)
pearls: Dynamic EMG in instrumented gait analysis reveals which muscles fire in which gait phase and distinguishes dynamic spasticity from fixed contracture, directing targeted therapy.
Spastic equinovarus is driven by over-active gastrocsoleus (equinus) and tibialis posterior (varus); check for voluntary tibialis anterior activity when planning transfers.
Stepwise care: botulinum toxin to the offending muscles plus an AFO and therapy first; a positive-but-transient toxin response predicts good surgical results.
Tendon-balancing surgery (e.g., SPLATT with Achilles/gastrocnemius lengthening) is reserved for fixed deformity or confirmed consistent muscular drivers.

cases.title: Aching at the Base of the Thumb
cases.chiefComplaint: She has pain at the base of her thumb when she pinches, grips, or opens jars.
cases.history: A 62-year-old retired seamstress presents with 8 months of aching pain at the base of her right thumb, worse with pinching, gripping, turning keys, opening jars, and sewing. The pain has gradually worsened and is now accompanied by weakness of pinch and a sense that the thumb base is enlarging. She denies numbness, tingling, night pain waking her, or finger triggering. There was no acute injury. PMH includes well-controlled hypertension on hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily and osteoarthritis of the knees. NKDA. She is right-hand dominant, does fine handwork and gardening, does not smoke, and drinks wine occasionally. Family history: mother had 'knobby' finger joints and base-of-thumb arthritis. She has tried acetaminophen with partial relief and is concerned about losing the ability to do detailed handwork. She reports stiffness in the thumb in the morning that loosens with use, and the pain is clearly localized to the base of the thumb rather than the wrist or the fingertips.
cases.exam: Right thumb: tenderness localized over the carpometacarpal (CMC, trapeziometacarpal) joint at the thumb base, with mild bony prominence and a 'shoulder' (squaring) appearance. The grind test (axial compression with rotation of the thumb metacarpal on the trapezium) reproduces pain and crepitus. Pinch and grip strength are reduced by pain. No tenderness over the first dorsal compartment, and the Finkelstein test is negative. Tinel and Phalen at the wrist are negative; no thenar atrophy or sensory loss. Heberden nodes are present at several distal interphalangeal joints. No triggering, no MCP instability. Range of motion of the thumb is mildly limited with discomfort at end-range.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination finding most specifically supports the likely diagnosis?
options: Positive Finkelstein test
Positive CMC grind test reproducing pain and crepitus at the thumb base
Positive Phalen test
Triggering of the thumb on flexion
stages.explanation: A positive grind test — axial loading and rotation of the thumb metacarpal against the trapezium reproducing pain and crepitus — is specific for trapeziometacarpal (thumb CMC) osteoarthritis, which fits her base-of-thumb pain with pinch/grip activities and joint squaring. A positive Finkelstein test indicates de Quervain tenosynovitis of the first dorsal compartment, which is over the radial styloid, not the CMC joint, and is negative here. A positive Phalen test indicates carpal tunnel syndrome (median nerve), with numbness rather than mechanical joint pain. Thumb triggering indicates stenosing tenosynovitis of the flexor pollicis longus, absent here. The grind test localizing pain and crepitus to the CMC joint clinches the diagnosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study to confirm the diagnosis?
options: MRI of the wrist
Plain radiographs of the thumb (including a stress/pinch view)
Ultrasound of the first dorsal compartment
CT angiography of the hand
stages.explanation: Plain radiographs of the thumb, including dedicated CMC and stress (pinch) views, are the appropriate first-line imaging and typically show joint-space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, osteophytes, and subluxation that grade trapeziometacarpal osteoarthritis. The diagnosis is clinical, but radiographs confirm and stage it cost-effectively. MRI is rarely needed and reserved for atypical or soft-tissue questions. Ultrasound of the first dorsal compartment evaluates de Quervain tenosynovitis, not the CMC joint. CT angiography assesses vasculature and is irrelevant to a degenerative joint problem. X-rays directly visualize the arthritic CMC joint and correlate with severity to guide management.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Thumb radiographs: trapeziometacarpal joint-space narrowing with subchondral sclerosis and marginal osteophytes
Mild radial subluxation of the metacarpal base on the trapezium
No fracture
Scaphotrapezial joint relatively preserved
Findings consistent with Eaton stage II-III thumb CMC osteoarthritis

stages.label: INITIAL MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for her thumb CMC osteoarthritis?
options: Trapeziectomy with ligament reconstruction
A thumb spica (CMC) splint, joint-protection education, activity modification, NSAIDs, and hand therapy
Wrist arthrodesis
Long-term oral opioids
stages.explanation: First-line management of thumb CMC osteoarthritis is conservative: a thumb spica or CMC orthosis to support and rest the joint, joint-protection education (using larger-handled tools, avoiding sustained forceful pinch), activity modification, NSAIDs for pain, and hand therapy to strengthen the thenar/first dorsal interosseous and stabilize the joint; most patients improve substantially. Trapeziectomy with ligament reconstruction (LRTI) is effective but reserved for advanced disease refractory to conservative care and possibly injection. Wrist arthrodesis is not the treatment for isolated CMC arthritis. Chronic opioids are inappropriate for a degenerative joint condition and carry dependence risk. Splinting plus joint protection and therapy is the standard, lowest-risk starting point.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After 3 months of splinting, joint protection, therapy, and NSAIDs, she has persistent pain limiting her handwork but is not ready for surgery. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection into the CMC joint
Proceed directly to trapeziectomy
Stop all treatment and observe
Begin a chronic opioid regimen
stages.explanation: When splinting, joint protection, therapy, and NSAIDs fail to control symptoms but the patient prefers to avoid surgery, an intra-articular corticosteroid injection into the trapeziometacarpal joint is the appropriate next step, often providing temporary relief that delays or avoids surgery, especially in earlier-stage disease. Trapeziectomy (with ligament reconstruction and tendon interposition) is reserved for advanced, refractory disease and is premature when she is not ready for surgery and an injection has not been tried. Stopping all treatment leaves her in pain and abandons care. Chronic opioids are inappropriate for degenerative joint pain. A targeted corticosteroid injection is the logical intermediate step between conservative care and surgery.

cases.diagnosis: Thumb carpometacarpal (trapeziometacarpal) osteoarthritis
pearls: Base-of-thumb pain with pinch/grip, joint squaring, and a positive grind test reproducing crepitus indicates thumb CMC osteoarthritis.
Distinguish from de Quervain tenosynovitis (positive Finkelstein, radial styloid) and carpal tunnel syndrome (numbness, positive Phalen).
Plain radiographs confirm and stage disease (joint-space narrowing, osteophytes, subluxation; Eaton staging).
Stepwise care: thumb spica splint, joint protection, therapy, and NSAIDs first; then intra-articular corticosteroid injection; reserve trapeziectomy/LRTI for refractory advanced disease.

cases.title: Unable to Breathe After a Diving Injury
cases.chiefComplaint: He cannot breathe on his own and is ventilator-dependent after a high cervical spinal cord injury.
cases.history: A 22-year-old man is in the ICU 10 days after a shallow-water diving accident causing a C4 ASIA Impairment Scale A (complete) tetraplegia. He is mechanically ventilated through a tracheostomy and cannot breathe spontaneously for sustained periods. He has thick secretions with weak, ineffective cough and has had one mucus-plugging desaturation event. He has no movement or sensation below the clavicles, with intact partial shoulder/neck function. He is alert and communicating by mouthing words and a speaking valve during trials. PMH: previously healthy, no smoking, no lung disease. Meds: enoxaparin for DVT prophylaxis, a bowel regimen, and a stress-ulcer prophylactic. NKDA. He lives with his parents and was a college athlete; the family is asking about long-term breathing options and whether he will always need the ventilator. He has had no pneumonia yet but staff are vigilant. There is concern about secretion clearance, ventilator weaning potential, and candidacy for diaphragm pacing as the team plans his rehabilitation trajectory.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, mouthing words around a tracheostomy. Tetraplegia: motor 0/5 in both lower limbs; upper limbs show preserved shoulder shrug and some shoulder/elbow movement consistent with a C4-5 level, with absent hand function. Respiratory: minimal spontaneous tidal volumes off the ventilator; absent intercostal and abdominal muscle activity; diaphragmatic excursion weak. Sensory level at the clavicles (C4); absent below. Reflexes diminished acutely (spinal shock resolving) with emerging hyperreflexia. Weak, ineffective cough with audible secretions; lungs with scattered coarse breath sounds clearing partially with suctioning. No fever or focal consolidation currently. Abdomen soft; bladder catheterized.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Why is this C4 complete injury associated with ventilator dependence?
options: It damages the brainstem respiratory center directly
It impairs the phrenic nerve/diaphragm (C3-C5) innervation along with intercostal and abdominal muscle function, severely reducing ventilation and cough
It causes upper-airway obstruction from tongue weakness
It produces bronchospasm from autonomic injury
stages.explanation: The diaphragm is innervated by the phrenic nerve arising from C3-C5; a complete C4 injury compromises diaphragmatic drive and eliminates the intercostal (thoracic) and abdominal muscle function needed for chest expansion and an effective cough, producing inadequate spontaneous ventilation and ventilator dependence. The brainstem respiratory center is intact — the lesion is below it in the cervical cord, so central drive is preserved but cannot reach the muscles. Tongue/upper-airway weakness is not the mechanism of his hypoventilation. Autonomic injury can cause bronchial hyperreactivity and secretions but does not by itself explain ventilator dependence. Loss of phrenic-driven diaphragm plus accessory and expiratory muscle function is the core reason.

stages.label: ACUTE PRIORITY
stages.question: Given thick secretions, a weak cough, and a mucus-plugging event, which is the highest-priority aspect of his respiratory management?
options: Aggressive diuresis
Immediate extubation/decannulation trial
Empiric long-term oral antibiotics
Comprehensive secretion clearance — mechanical insufflation-exsufflation (cough assist), suctioning, airway humidification, and positioning
stages.explanation: In high tetraplegia, ineffective cough leads to retained secretions, mucus plugging, atelectasis, and pneumonia — the leading cause of morbidity and mortality. The highest priority is an aggressive secretion-clearance program: mechanical insufflation-exsufflation (cough assist), frequent suctioning, airway humidification, assisted (quad) coughing, chest physiotherapy, and regular repositioning. Diuresis does not address neuromuscular cough failure and could harm a normovolemic patient. Decannulation/extubation is unsafe in an acutely ventilator-dependent C4 complete injury and risks catastrophic respiratory failure. Empiric long-term antibiotics promote resistance and do not prevent plugging; antibiotics are for documented infection. Optimizing secretion clearance directly prevents the most dangerous complications during this period.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC STUDIES
stages.content:
Chest radiograph: low lung volumes with bibasilar atelectasis; no lobar consolidation
Arterial blood gas (on ventilator): pH 7.40, PaCO2 41 mmHg, PaO2 92 mmHg, HCO3 24 mEq/L
Bedside spirometry (off vent trial): vital capacity markedly reduced, weak negative inspiratory force
Phrenic nerve conduction study and diaphragm EMG: intact phrenic nerve conduction with viable diaphragm responses bilaterally
Fluoroscopic sniff test: diaphragmatic movement present with phrenic stimulation
MRI: complete cord injury at C4; cord intact above the lesion

stages.label: ADVANCED OPTION
stages.question: Phrenic nerve conduction and diaphragm EMG are intact, with a viable diaphragm. What does this finding most importantly establish about diaphragm pacing?
options: He is a poor candidate because pacing requires complete phrenic destruction
Diaphragm pacing cures the spinal cord injury
Intact phrenic nerves and a viable diaphragm make him a potential candidate for diaphragm/phrenic pacing, which can reduce or eliminate ventilator dependence
Pacing eliminates the need for any secretion clearance
stages.explanation: Diaphragm (phrenic nerve) pacing requires an intact phrenic nerve and a viable, stimulable diaphragm because the system electrically stimulates the nerve/muscle to produce breaths; his preserved phrenic conduction, diaphragm EMG responses, and fluoroscopic movement establish him as a potential pacing candidate, which can reduce or free him from the ventilator and improve quality of life. Pacing is contraindicated when the phrenic nerve or diaphragm is non-viable (e.g., lower motor neuron injury at C3-C5) — the opposite of the stated requirement. Pacing does not reverse the cord injury itself. It also does not eliminate the ongoing need for secretion clearance, since cough remains impaired. Demonstrating phrenic/diaphragm viability is the key eligibility step.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: How should the team counsel the family about his long-term respiratory trajectory and care?
options: He will certainly be fully weaned within days with no equipment

Long-term ventilatory support is likely needed, but with a structured program (secretion clearance, possible diaphragm pacing given viable phrenic nerves, ventilator weaning trials, vaccination, and aspiration/pneumonia prevention) many patients achieve stability and improved quality of life
Nothing more can be done and outcomes are uniformly fatal
He should be decannulated immediately to encourage independence
stages.explanation: Realistic, hopeful counseling is appropriate: a C4 complete injury often entails long-term ventilatory support, but a structured plan markedly improves outcomes — aggressive secretion clearance, progressive ventilator weaning trials as tolerated, diaphragm pacing candidacy (supported here by intact phrenic nerves and a viable diaphragm), pneumococcal/influenza vaccination, aspiration precautions, and infection surveillance — and many patients reach respiratory stability with good quality of life and even pacing-enabled freedom from the ventilator. Promising full, immediate weaning is false. Nihilism is inaccurate; survival and meaningful function are achievable. Immediate decannulation would be dangerous given his ventilator dependence. A comprehensive, staged respiratory plan frames expectations correctly.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Weeks later, during a ventilator-weaning trial, he suddenly develops a pounding headache, flushing and sweating above the chest, nasal congestion, and a blood pressure of 180/110 mmHg with bradycardia. What is the most likely cause and immediate action?
options: Anxiety; administer a sedative and continue the trial
Autonomic dysreflexia; sit him upright, loosen constrictive items, and urgently identify and remove the noxious stimulus (commonly a blocked catheter or bowel distension)
Septic shock; start broad-spectrum antibiotics and fluids
Pulmonary embolism; give thrombolytics immediately
stages.explanation: This is classic autonomic dysreflexia — a potentially life-threatening reflex in injuries at or above T6 triggered by a noxious stimulus below the lesion (most often bladder distension/blocked catheter or bowel impaction), producing sudden severe hypertension, pounding headache, flushing and sweating above the level, nasal congestion, and reflex bradycardia. Immediate management is to sit the patient upright to lower blood pressure, loosen tight clothing/devices, and rapidly find and remove the trigger (check and relieve the catheter first, then the bowel), with short-acting antihypertensives if pressure stays dangerously high. It is not anxiety, and sedation would mask a dangerous emergency. The pattern and trigger relationship are not septic shock (which causes hypotension) or pulmonary embolism. Prompt recognition prevents stroke, seizure, and death.

cases.diagnosis: C4 complete (ASIA A) tetraplegia with ventilator dependence; candidate for diaphragm pacing
pearls: The diaphragm is innervated by C3-C5 (phrenic nerve); a complete C4 injury causes ventilator dependence by impairing diaphragm plus intercostal and abdominal (cough) muscle function while central drive stays intact.
Secretion clearance (mechanical insufflation-exsufflation, suctioning, assisted cough, humidification, positioning) is the top priority — retained secretions and pneumonia are the leading killers.
Diaphragm/phrenic pacing requires an intact phrenic nerve and viable diaphragm; confirm with phrenic nerve conduction, diaphragm EMG, and fluoroscopy before considering it.
Pacing can reduce or eliminate ventilator dependence but does not cure the injury or remove the need for ongoing secretion management; add vaccination and aspiration precautions.

cases.title: Thumb-Side Wrist Pain in a New Mother
cases.chiefComplaint: Pain on the thumb side of the right wrist for the past three weeks.
cases.history: A 31-year-old right-handed woman presents with three weeks of aching pain over the radial aspect of the right wrist, worse with lifting and twisting. She delivered her first child seven weeks ago and is exclusively breastfeeding. She reports the pain began after she started repeatedly lifting the baby out of the crib with her wrists, and it now radiates up the forearm and occasionally to the thumb. Pain worsens when she opens jars, wrings out cloths, or texts. She denies numbness, tingling, locking, or trauma. There is no morning stiffness beyond a few minutes and no other joint complaints. PMH is unremarkable; pregnancy was uncomplicated. Medications: prenatal vitamins and acetaminophen 500 mg as needed (minimal relief). No allergies. She works as a graphic designer, currently on maternity leave, and does not smoke or drink. Family history is notable for rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. She is anxious that she has "arthritis" and worried about caring for her newborn.
cases.exam: Afebrile, well-appearing. Localized tenderness and mild swelling over the radial styloid at the first dorsal compartment. Pain reproduced over the radial styloid with ulnar deviation of the wrist while the thumb is held flexed within the fist (positive Finkelstein/Eichhoff). Thumb carpometacarpal grind test is negative without crepitus. No erythema or warmth. Full active and passive wrist range of motion. Median nerve sensation intact; Tinel and Phalen signs negative. Grip strength mildly reduced by pain. No synovitis at the MCP or PIP joints; remaining joint exam normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which physical exam maneuver is most specific for confirming the suspected diagnosis?
options: Phalen test reproducing thumb numbness
Finkelstein (Eichhoff) test reproducing radial styloid pain
Thumb CMC grind test eliciting crepitus
Tinel sign at the carpal tunnel
stages.explanation: The Finkelstein/Eichhoff maneuver (ulnar deviation of the wrist with the thumb flexed in the fist) stresses the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons in the first dorsal compartment, reproducing pain over the radial styloid and confirming De Quervain tenosynovitis. Phalen test (numbness) and Tinel sign localize to the median nerve and suggest carpal tunnel syndrome, not a tendinopathy. The CMC grind test with crepitus indicates first carpometacarpal (basilar thumb) osteoarthritis, which is uncommon at this age and presents with joint-line pain rather than tendon pain at the styloid.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the underlying pathophysiology of this condition?
options: Compression of the median nerve in the carpal tunnel
Stenosing tenosynovitis of the first dorsal compartment tendons
Inflammatory pannus eroding the radiocarpal joint
Crystal deposition within the wrist joint capsule
stages.explanation: De Quervain tenosynovitis is a stenosing tenosynovitis: thickening of the extensor retinaculum and myxoid degeneration of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendon sheaths within the first dorsal compartment, often from repetitive thumb abduction/wrist motion (e.g., lifting an infant). Median nerve compression describes carpal tunnel syndrome. Inflammatory pannus eroding the joint describes rheumatoid arthritis, which causes symmetric synovitis rather than isolated tendon-sheath pain. Crystal deposition describes gout/pseudogout, typically acute, hot, and not localized to the radial styloid in a young postpartum woman.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Wrist X-ray (PA/lateral/oblique): No fracture, no joint-space narrowing, no erosions; soft-tissue prominence over radial styloid
ESR: 12 mm/hr (normal)
CRP: 0.3 mg/dL (normal)
Rheumatoid factor: Negative
Anti-CCP antibody: Negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Surgical release of the first dorsal compartment
Thumb spica splint, activity modification, and NSAIDs
Oral prednisone taper over two weeks
Wrist arthrodesis referral
stages.explanation: First-line conservative management of De Quervain tenosynovitis is a forearm-based thumb spica splint (immobilizing the wrist and thumb MCP while leaving the IP free), activity modification to reduce provocative thumb/wrist loading, and NSAIDs for pain; ergonomic coaching on lifting the baby is key. Surgical first dorsal compartment release is reserved for refractory cases after conservative measures and corticosteroid injection fail. Oral systemic steroids are not standard and carry unnecessary systemic risk versus a targeted local injection. Wrist arthrodesis is an aggressive, inappropriate intervention for a self-limited tendinopathy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She returns after 5 weeks of splinting and NSAIDs with persistent pain limiting infant care. What is the best next step?
options: Corticosteroid injection into the first dorsal compartment
Begin oral methotrexate
Order MRI of the wrist
Refer immediately for surgical release
stages.explanation: When splinting, activity modification, and NSAIDs fail to control De Quervain symptoms, the next step is an ultrasound-guided or landmark corticosteroid injection into the first dorsal compartment, which has high success rates and can be curative; clinicians should counsel about a separate subcompartment for EPB. Methotrexate treats inflammatory arthritis, which this patient's negative RF/anti-CCP and normal inflammatory markers exclude. MRI is unnecessary because the diagnosis is clinical and X-rays already exclude bony pathology. Surgical release is reserved for failure of both conservative care and injection, not the immediate next step.

cases.diagnosis: De Quervain tenosynovitis
