pearls: De Quervain is a stenosing tenosynovitis of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis in the first dorsal compartment, classically in new mothers and from repetitive thumb/wrist use.
The Finkelstein/Eichhoff test (ulnar deviation with thumb flexed in fist) reproducing radial styloid pain is the key bedside finding.
First-line treatment is a thumb spica splint, activity modification, and NSAIDs; corticosteroid injection is the next step, with surgical release reserved for refractory cases.
Negative RF/anti-CCP and normal ESR/CRP help distinguish it from early inflammatory arthritis.

cases.title: Twisted Ankle After a Pickup Basketball Game
cases.chiefComplaint: Right ankle pain and swelling after rolling the ankle during basketball two hours ago.
cases.history: A 22-year-old man presents to the emergency department two hours after inverting his right ankle landing from a jump during a pickup basketball game. He felt immediate lateral ankle pain and noticed rapid swelling. He was able to bear weight initially with a limp but stopped because of pain. He denies an audible pop with persistent giving-way, denies numbness, and reports no prior ankle injuries. He has no proximal leg, knee, or foot pain. PMH is unremarkable, no medications, no allergies. He is a college student who plays recreational sports several times weekly and is eager to return to play for an upcoming intramural tournament. He does not smoke; social alcohol use. Family history noncontributory. He took ibuprofen 400 mg at home with partial relief and applied ice. He is concerned he may have broken his ankle and asks whether he needs an X-ray.
cases.exam: Alert, no distress. Right lateral ankle with swelling and early ecchymosis anterior and inferior to the lateral malleolus. Maximal tenderness over the anterior talofibular ligament; no tenderness over the posterior edge or tip of the lateral or medial malleolus, the navicular, or the base of the fifth metatarsal. Mild anterior drawer laxity with a soft endpoint; talar tilt mildly increased. Able to take four steps in the ED with a limp. Neurovascular exam intact: 2+ dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses, normal capillary refill, intact sensation and motor function. Knee and proximal fibula nontender. No deformity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Using the Ottawa ankle rules, what is the most appropriate next step regarding imaging?
options: Obtain ankle radiographs because of swelling and ecchymosis
No ankle radiographs are required given the exam findings
Obtain foot radiographs because he plays sports
Obtain CT of the ankle to rule out occult fracture
stages.explanation: The Ottawa ankle rules indicate ankle radiographs only if there is bony tenderness at the posterior edge/tip of either malleolus OR inability to bear weight for four steps both immediately and in the ED. This patient has tenderness only over the ATFL (a ligament, not bone), no malleolar bony tenderness, and can take four steps, so no radiographs are required—a highly sensitive rule for excluding clinically significant fracture. Swelling/ecchymosis alone do not mandate imaging. Foot films apply only with midfoot/navicular or fifth-metatarsal base tenderness, which he lacks. CT is not indicated for a low-risk sprain.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which ligament is most commonly injured in this mechanism, and what is the likely diagnosis?
options: Deltoid ligament; medial (eversion) ankle sprain
Calcaneofibular ligament; high ankle syndesmotic sprain
Anterior talofibular ligament; lateral ankle sprain
Anterior inferior tibiofibular ligament; syndesmotic sprain
stages.explanation: Inversion of a plantarflexed ankle most commonly injures the anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL), the weakest lateral stabilizer, producing a lateral ankle sprain—consistent with maximal ATFL tenderness and a positive anterior drawer. The deltoid ligament is injured in eversion (medial sprains), which are far less common. The calcaneofibular ligament is the next lateral ligament injured with more severe inversion but after the ATFL. The anterior inferior tibiofibular ligament is part of the syndesmosis (high ankle sprain), typically from external rotation/dorsiflexion with proximal tenderness and a positive squeeze test, not seen here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management of this lateral ankle sprain?
options: Below-knee cast immobilization for 6 weeks
Strict non-weight-bearing on crutches for 4 weeks
Protection, relative rest, ice, compression, elevation, and early functional rehabilitation
Urgent orthopedic surgery for ligament repair
stages.explanation: Acute lateral ankle sprains are best treated with the PRICE approach (protection, relative rest, ice, compression, elevation) plus analgesia, followed by early functional rehabilitation—early protected weight-bearing, range-of-motion, peroneal strengthening, and proprioceptive/balance training. Functional rehab yields faster return to activity and less chronic instability than prolonged immobilization. Cast immobilization for 6 weeks and strict non-weight-bearing promote stiffness, muscle atrophy, and worse outcomes for low-grade sprains. Surgical repair is not indicated acutely for most lateral sprains; it is reserved for select chronic instability after failed rehabilitation.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Before clearing him for return to basketball, which criterion best indicates readiness to return to play?
options: Resolution of visible swelling alone
Pain-free full range of motion, restored strength, and sport-specific functional testing without instability
A normal follow-up ankle radiograph
Two weeks elapsed since injury regardless of function
stages.explanation: Return-to-play after an ankle sprain is functional and criteria-based: the athlete should have pain-free full range of motion, near-symmetric strength (especially peroneals), restored proprioception/balance, and the ability to perform sport-specific tasks (hop, cut, pivot) without pain or instability—often with a brace or taping initially. Resolution of swelling alone does not confirm restored stability or strength. A radiograph is not part of return-to-play decisions for a ligamentous sprain. A fixed time interval ignores individual healing and functional recovery, risking re-injury if function has not returned.

cases.diagnosis: Lateral ankle sprain (anterior talofibular ligament)
pearls: Ottawa ankle rules: image only with malleolar bony tenderness or inability to bear weight 4 steps both immediately and in the ED—highly sensitive for excluding fracture.
The ATFL is the most commonly injured ankle ligament, typically from inversion of a plantarflexed foot.
Early functional rehabilitation (weight-bearing, peroneal strengthening, proprioceptive training) beats prolonged immobilization and reduces chronic instability.
Return-to-play is criteria-based: pain-free ROM, restored strength/proprioception, and sport-specific testing without instability—often with bracing.

cases.title: Anterior Knee Pain Climbing Stairs
cases.chiefComplaint: Aching pain in the front of both knees, worse with stairs and prolonged sitting.
cases.history: A 19-year-old woman, a recreational runner, presents with three months of gradual, dull anterior knee pain, worse on the right. Pain increases when climbing or descending stairs, squatting, and after sitting through long lectures (the "theater sign"). She recently increased her running mileage to train for a half-marathon. She denies trauma, locking, true giving-way, night pain, or swelling, though she occasionally feels grinding behind the kneecap. There is no history of clicking that locks the joint. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: occasional ibuprofen. No allergies. She is a college sophomore, runs 20 miles per week on roads, and recently bought new shoes. Menses are regular. She does not smoke; rare alcohol. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems, she reports no fevers, weight loss, or other joint pains. She is worried about a torn ligament or cartilage and whether she can keep training.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, normal gait. Knees without effusion, erythema, or warmth. Tenderness along the medial patellar facet; pain and crepitus reproduced with the patellar compression/grind test during quadriceps contraction. Pain with single-leg squat, which also reveals dynamic knee valgus and contralateral pelvic drop, suggesting hip abductor weakness. Hip abductor (gluteus medius) strength 4/5 bilaterally. Patellar apprehension test negative; no lateral patellar instability. Lachman, anterior/posterior drawer, and McMurray tests negative. Joint lines nontender. Full range of motion. Neurovascular exam intact. Foot exam shows mild pes planus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis based on the history and exam?
options: Medial meniscus tear
Patellofemoral pain syndrome
Anterior cruciate ligament tear
Pes anserine bursitis
stages.explanation: Diffuse anterior/peripatellar knee pain that worsens with stairs, squatting, and prolonged sitting (theater sign), with a positive patellar grind test and no effusion or instability, is classic for patellofemoral pain syndrome, commonly associated with training overload and proximal (hip abductor) weakness causing dynamic valgus. A meniscus tear typically follows a twisting injury with locking/catching and a positive McMurray, absent here. An ACL tear causes acute instability, an effusion, and a positive Lachman, none present. Pes anserine bursitis causes focal medial tenderness ~5 cm below the joint line, not retropatellar pain with grinding.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which contributing biomechanical factor identified on exam is the key target for rehabilitation?
options: Hip abductor and external rotator weakness causing dynamic knee valgus
Posterior cruciate ligament laxity
Lateral meniscus degeneration
Hamstring tightness causing a flexion contracture
stages.explanation: In patellofemoral pain syndrome, proximal weakness of the hip abductors and external rotators (gluteus medius/maximus) allows femoral internal rotation/adduction and dynamic knee valgus, increasing lateral patellar maltracking and contact stress—seen here as valgus collapse and contralateral pelvic drop on single-leg squat. Targeting hip strength is central to evidence-based rehab. PCL laxity (posterior drawer) is not present and does not cause anterior patellar pain. Lateral meniscus degeneration would cause mechanical joint-line symptoms. Hamstring tightness can contribute but is not the principal, exam-identified driver here.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Weight-bearing knee radiographs (AP, lateral, Merchant/sunrise view): No fracture
No joint-space narrowing or osteophytes
Patella normally positioned without lateral tilt or subluxation on sunrise view
No joint effusion
No loose bodies

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Knee immobilizer and non-weight-bearing for 4 weeks
Diagnostic knee arthroscopy
Structured physical therapy emphasizing hip and quadriceps strengthening with activity modification
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of patellofemoral pain syndrome is a structured exercise program emphasizing hip abductor/external rotator and quadriceps (including VMO) strengthening, combined with relative activity modification (reduce mileage, address training errors), stretching, and short-term analgesia; patellar taping or foot orthoses may help selected patients. Immobilization and non-weight-bearing promote stiffness and quadriceps atrophy, worsening the condition. Arthroscopy is not indicated for a clinical diagnosis without mechanical/structural pathology. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection has no role in this noninflammatory overuse condition and does not address the underlying biomechanics.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate counseling regarding her running?
options: Stop all running permanently to prevent arthritis
Continue increasing mileage as tolerated to build endurance
Temporarily reduce mileage and gradually resume with a slow, progressive program once symptoms improve
Switch immediately to high-intensity plyometric training
stages.explanation: Patellofemoral pain syndrome is typically an overuse/training-error problem, so counseling should emphasize relative rest—temporarily reducing mileage and cross-training (e.g., cycling, swimming) while rehab restores strength, then a gradual, progressive return to running guided by symptoms. Permanent cessation is unnecessary and overly restrictive; PFPS does not equate to inevitable arthritis. Continuing to increase mileage perpetuates the overload that caused the problem. Immediate high-intensity plyometrics would spike patellofemoral joint stress before strength and mechanics improve, risking symptom flare and poor adherence.

cases.diagnosis: Patellofemoral pain syndrome
pearls: PFPS presents with anterior/peripatellar pain worsened by stairs, squatting, and prolonged sitting (theater sign), with a positive patellar grind and no effusion or instability.
Hip abductor/external rotator weakness producing dynamic knee valgus is a key driver—single-leg squat assessment is high-yield.
Treatment is rehabilitation-focused: hip and quadriceps strengthening plus activity modification, not immobilization or surgery.
Imaging is usually normal and not required for diagnosis; reserve it for atypical features or to exclude alternatives.

cases.title: Daily Headaches Starting at the Back of the Neck
cases.chiefComplaint: Frequent one-sided headaches that start at the base of the skull and spread forward.
cases.history: A 40-year-old woman reports six months of nearly daily right-sided headaches that begin in the suboccipital region and radiate over the back of the head toward the eye. Pain is dull and steady, moderate in intensity, and worsens with sustained desk posture and turning her head. She works long hours at a computer and notes neck stiffness. She denies aura, photophobia/phonophobia beyond mild, nausea, or pulsatile quality. There are no thunderclap features, fever, vision loss, or jaw claudication. To cope, she has been taking combination acetaminophen-caffeine analgesics most days for months, and over-the-counter ibuprofen, without lasting relief. PMH: anxiety. Medications: the daily analgesics noted, escitalopram 10 mg daily. No allergies. She is a software analyst, married, does not smoke, drinks occasional wine. Family history: migraine in her sister. She is frustrated by medication that "isn't working" and worried about a brain tumor. No focal weakness, numbness, or speech difficulty reported.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, no acute distress. Cranial nerves II–XII intact; no papilledema. Reduced cervical rotation to the right with reproduction of the typical headache; tenderness over the right upper cervical facets (C1–C3) and suboccipital muscles. Palpation of right suboccipital trigger points reproduces the radiating headache. Limited cervical extension. Temporal arteries nontender, non-thickened. No neck rigidity or meningismus. Motor strength 5/5, normal reflexes and sensation in all limbs; gait normal. No scalp allodynia during a pain-free interval. Fundoscopic exam normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly supports cervicogenic headache over migraine in this patient?
options: Pulsatile quality with nausea and photophobia
Headache reproduced by neck movement and palpation of upper cervical structures
Visual aura preceding the headache
Bilateral band-like pressure without neck findings
stages.explanation: Cervicogenic headache is a referred pain from upper cervical structures (C1–C3); hallmark features are unilateral side-locked pain starting in the neck/occiput, reduced cervical range of motion, and reproduction of the typical headache by neck movement or palpation of upper cervical segments—exactly this patient. Pulsatile pain with nausea/photophobia and visual aura are features of migraine. Bilateral band-like pressure without neck findings characterizes tension-type headache. The mechanical reproducibility tied to the cervical spine is what distinguishes cervicogenic headache here.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given her daily analgesic use, which comorbid diagnosis must also be addressed?
options: Medication overuse (rebound) headache
Giant cell arteritis
Idiopathic intracranial hypertension
Trigeminal neuralgia
stages.explanation: Taking acute analgesics (especially combination/caffeine-containing products) on most days for months can produce medication overuse headache, which perpetuates daily headache and undermines other treatments—so it must be identified and the overused agents withdrawn. Giant cell arteritis affects older patients (>50) with scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, and elevated ESR; her temporal arteries are normal. Idiopathic intracranial hypertension causes papilledema and visual symptoms, absent here. Trigeminal neuralgia causes brief, electric, lancinating facial pain in a trigeminal distribution, not a steady occipital-to-frontal headache reproduced by neck movement.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management strategy?
options: Start high-dose daily opioids for pain control
Begin physical therapy with posture correction and trigger-point therapy while limiting/withdrawing overused analgesics
Initiate empiric high-dose corticosteroids
Order urgent MRI and refer for surgical fusion
stages.explanation: First-line management of cervicogenic headache is conservative and mechanism-directed: physical therapy emphasizing posture correction and cervical/scapular strengthening, manual therapy, and trigger-point treatment of the suboccipital/upper cervical musculature, while simultaneously limiting and withdrawing the overused acute analgesics to break the medication overuse cycle. Daily opioids worsen medication overuse headache and risk dependence. Empiric corticosteroids are appropriate for giant cell arteritis, which is excluded. Urgent MRI/surgical fusion is not indicated without red flags or structural pathology; surgery is rarely needed and only after extensive conservative failure and confirmatory diagnostic blocks.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Despite posture work, PT, and analgesic withdrawal, her side-locked headaches persist. Which intervention can serve both diagnostic and therapeutic roles?
options: Greater occipital nerve / cervical facet (C2–C3) anesthetic block
Empiric triptan at headache onset
Lumbar puncture with opening pressure
Botulinum toxin to the frontalis muscle only
stages.explanation: An anesthetic block of the greater occipital nerve or the upper cervical (C2–C3) facet joints/medial branches can be both diagnostic—confirming a cervical source if it abolishes the typical headache—and therapeutic, often providing relief and guiding consideration of radiofrequency ablation in refractory cases. Triptans target migraine pathophysiology and are not the diagnostic test for cervicogenic headache. Lumbar puncture assesses CSF pressure/infection (e.g., IIH, meningitis), not applicable here. Botulinum toxin to frontalis alone does not address the upper cervical generators and is not the diagnostic-therapeutic step for cervicogenic headache.

cases.diagnosis: Cervicogenic headache
pearls: Cervicogenic headache is unilateral, side-locked pain referred from C1–C3, with reduced cervical ROM and headache reproduced by neck movement or palpation.
Always screen for coexisting medication overuse headache in patients using acute analgesics on most days—withdrawal is essential.
First-line therapy is posture correction, physical therapy, manual/trigger-point treatment; greater occipital or cervical facet blocks are diagnostic and therapeutic.
Distinguish from migraine (aura, nausea, photophobia, pulsatile) and giant cell arteritis (age >50, jaw claudication, elevated ESR).

cases.title: Sudden Pull in the Back of the Thigh While Sprinting
cases.chiefComplaint: Sharp pain in the back of the right thigh that struck mid-sprint yesterday.
cases.history: A 24-year-old competitive sprinter presents the day after feeling a sudden sharp "grab" in the back of his right thigh during the acceleration phase of a 100-meter sprint. He stopped immediately, unable to continue. Overnight he developed swelling and bruising in the posterior thigh. He can walk but cannot run or fully straighten the leg without pain. He denies a loud pop with a sense of the muscle balling up high in the buttock, denies numbness or back pain, and reports no prior hamstring injuries this season. PMH unremarkable. Medications: none. No allergies. He trains six days per week; this is mid-competition season. He does not smoke; occasional alcohol. Family history noncontributory. He took ibuprofen and iced overnight. He is anxious about an upcoming championship meet in five weeks and asks how soon he can return to sprinting.
cases.exam: Antalgic gait. Ecchymosis and swelling over the mid-posterior right thigh. Focal tenderness over the muscle belly of the biceps femoris approximately mid-thigh; no palpable gap or mass and no tenderness or step-off at the ischial tuberosity. Pain with resisted knee flexion and with passive straight-leg raise (active knee extension reproduces stretch pain). Knee flexion strength 4/5 limited by pain; full but painful range of motion. Distal neurovascular exam intact; straight-leg raise does not reproduce radicular/sciatic symptoms. No knee effusion or ligamentous laxity. Lumbar spine nontender.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which muscle is most commonly injured in this sprinting mechanism?
options: Rectus femoris
Biceps femoris (long head)
Gastrocnemius medial head
Adductor longus
stages.explanation: Sprint-type hamstring strains most commonly involve the biceps femoris long head, injured during the terminal swing/late stance phase when the hamstring contracts eccentrically to decelerate the extending knee—matching the mid-thigh biceps femoris tenderness and pain with resisted knee flexion. The rectus femoris is an anterior quadriceps muscle (a kicking-injury muscle). The gastrocnemius medial head causes calf strains ("tennis leg"). The adductor longus produces groin strains. The posterior-thigh location and mechanism point clearly to the biceps femoris.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the exam (focal pain, weakness, but no palpable defect and intact strength against gravity), how is this hamstring strain best graded?
options: Grade I (mild, microscopic injury, minimal strength loss)
Grade II (partial tear with pain, swelling, and notable strength deficit)
Grade III (complete rupture with palpable gap)
Proximal avulsion from the ischial tuberosity
stages.explanation: A grade II hamstring strain is a partial tear with pain, ecchymosis/swelling, tenderness, and a measurable strength deficit (here 4/5 with painful resisted flexion) but no complete loss of function or palpable gap—fitting this patient. Grade I is mild with minimal strength loss and little swelling/bruising. Grade III is a complete rupture with marked weakness and a palpable defect. A proximal avulsion presents with tenderness/ecchymosis at the ischial tuberosity (and sometimes a high "balled-up" muscle), which is absent on his exam.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Ultrasound of posterior thigh: Partial-thickness tear of the biceps femoris long head at the musculotendinous junction, ~2 cm in length
Intramuscular hematoma without complete discontinuity
No retraction; intact proximal tendon and ischial origin
MRI (obtained for prognosis): Grade II strain, <50% cross-sectional involvement, no tendon avulsion

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate acute and rehabilitative management?
options: Immediate surgical repair of the partial tear
Complete bed rest and immobilization until pain-free
PRICE acutely, then progressive rehab with eccentric strengthening and gradual running progression
Return to full sprint training in 3 days with NSAIDs
stages.explanation: Grade II hamstring strains are managed nonoperatively: early PRICE (protection, relative rest, ice, compression, elevation) and analgesia, then a progressive rehabilitation program emphasizing pain-free range of motion, progressive (especially eccentric) strengthening such as Nordic hamstring exercises, neuromuscular control, and a graded running/sprint progression. Surgery is reserved for complete proximal ruptures/avulsions with retraction, not partial tears. Complete bed rest/immobilization causes atrophy and delays recovery. Returning to full sprinting in 3 days risks re-injury, which is common when athletes return before strength and mechanics are restored.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which criterion best indicates he is ready to return to sprinting?
options: Complete resolution of bruising on the skin
Pain-free, near-symmetric hamstring strength and full pain-free sprinting mechanics on functional testing
A specific calendar date two weeks post-injury
Normal repeat MRI signal regardless of strength
stages.explanation: Return-to-play after a hamstring strain is functional and criteria-based: the athlete should have pain-free full range of motion, near-symmetric strength (eccentric strength testing), restored sprint mechanics, and the ability to perform sport-specific high-speed running without pain or apprehension. Resolution of bruising is a cosmetic marker that lags or leads recovery and does not reflect strength. Fixed calendar dates ignore individual healing and re-injury risk. MRI signal changes can persist after functional recovery and should not gate return; clinical/functional criteria take precedence.

cases.diagnosis: Hamstring strain (biceps femoris, grade II)
pearls: Sprint-mechanism hamstring strains most often involve the biceps femoris long head via eccentric overload during terminal swing.
Grading: I = minimal strength loss; II = partial tear with deficit; III = complete rupture with palpable gap; check the ischial tuberosity for proximal avulsion.
Management is PRICE then progressive eccentric strengthening (e.g., Nordic curls) with a graded running program; surgery only for proximal avulsions with retraction.
Return-to-play is criteria-based (pain-free symmetric strength and full sprint mechanics)—re-injury is common with premature return.

cases.title: Rebuilding Function After a One-Sided Weakness
cases.chiefComplaint: Left-sided weakness after a stroke, transferred for inpatient rehabilitation.
cases.history: A 66-year-old right-handed man is admitted to the inpatient rehabilitation unit on hospital day 6 after a right middle cerebral artery ischemic stroke. He presented with acute left hemiparesis, left facial droop, and left neglect; he received IV thrombolysis within the window and was not a thrombectomy candidate. He is medically stable. PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and atrial fibrillation, for which apixaban 5 mg twice daily was started. Other medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily. No known allergies. He lives in a two-story home with his wife (bedroom and full bath upstairs) and worked as an accountant. Former smoker, quit 10 years ago; rare alcohol. Family history: father had stroke. He requires moderate assistance for transfers and dressing and has impaired safety awareness. Goals: return home and regain independence. He is motivated but frustrated by his deficits.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented x3 but with left visual/spatial neglect and impaired insight. Left lower facial weakness; speech fluent without aphasia (right-hemisphere stroke). Left hemiparesis: upper limb 2/5 proximal and 1/5 distal, lower limb 3/5; early increased tone at the left elbow. Left-sided sensory inattention to double simultaneous stimulation. Left homonymous-pattern inattention on confrontation. Reflexes brisk on the left with an upgoing left plantar response. Requires moderate assistance for bed-to-chair transfers; sits with truncal instability and leans left. Swallow screen passed. Skin intact; no contractures.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: To qualify for and benefit from acute inpatient rehabilitation, which requirement must this patient meet?
options: Ability to ambulate independently before admission
Tolerance of intensive therapy (about 3 hours/day, generally 5 days/week) and need for ≥2 disciplines with physician oversight
Complete neurologic recovery expected within one week
No need for nursing care
stages.explanation: Acute inpatient rehabilitation requires that the patient can tolerate and benefit from an intensive program—generally about 3 hours of therapy per day, 5 days per week (or 15 hours/week)—needs at least two therapy disciplines (commonly PT and OT, often plus speech), and requires close physician supervision and rehabilitation nursing. Independent ambulation beforehand is not required; in fact, functional deficits are why patients are admitted. Complete recovery within a week is not expected after MCA stroke and is not a criterion. Patients on a rehab unit do need skilled nursing, so "no nursing care" is incorrect.

stages.label: FUNCTIONAL ASSESSMENT (FIM)
stages.content:
FIM motor — Transfers (bed/chair): 3 (moderate assistance, performs 50–74% of effort)
FIM — Toileting: 3 (moderate assistance)
FIM — Upper-body dressing: 3; Lower-body dressing: 2 (maximal assistance)
FIM — Walking/wheelchair: 2 (maximal assistance, 25–49% effort)
FIM cognitive — Problem solving: 4 (minimal prompting); Memory: 5 (supervision)
FIM scale reference: 1 = total assistance ... 7 = complete independence

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: On the FIM scale, what does his transfer score of 3 indicate about the assistance he requires?
options: He is modified independent (uses a device but no helper)
He needs only supervision/standby assistance
He needs moderate assistance and performs 50–74% of the task himself
He requires total assistance and performs less than 25%
stages.explanation: The Functional Independence Measure scores each task from 1 (total assistance) to 7 (complete independence). A score of 3 means moderate assistance: the patient performs 50–74% of the effort while a helper provides more than touch contact. Modified independence is a 6 (device, no helper). Supervision/standby (no physical help, cueing only) is a 5. Total assistance, in which the patient contributes less than 25%, is a 1. Accurately interpreting FIM levels drives goal-setting, tracks progress, and informs discharge planning and burden-of-care estimates.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which rehabilitation strategy is most appropriate to address his left-sided neglect and impaired safety awareness?
options: Restrict therapy to the right (unaffected) side to maximize strength
Visual scanning training, environmental cueing toward the left, and caregiver safety education
Bed rest with minimal stimulation to avoid frustration
Defer all cognitive work until motor strength normalizes
stages.explanation: Left hemispatial neglect from a right MCA stroke is addressed with active strategies: left-sided visual scanning training, cueing and arranging the environment to draw attention to the neglected side, limb activation, and education of staff and caregivers about safety (e.g., approaching from the left, fall precautions). Restricting therapy to the unaffected side reinforces neglect and learned nonuse. Bed rest with minimal stimulation worsens deconditioning and does not remediate neglect. Cognitive and perceptual rehabilitation should proceed concurrently with motor therapy—deferring it until strength normalizes wastes the early neuroplasticity window and leaves dangerous safety deficits unaddressed.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: By discharge he transfers with contact-guard assist, walks 150 feet with a hemi-walker and standby assist, and his wife is trained; he still cannot manage stairs independently. What is the most appropriate discharge disposition?
options: Skilled nursing facility because he cannot do stairs
Home with outpatient/home therapy and adaptive setup (e.g., sleeping/bathing on the main floor) and caregiver support
Independent living alone without services
Long-term acute care hospital
stages.explanation: He has progressed to contact-guard transfers and household/short-community ambulation with standby assistance and a trained caregiver; the remaining barrier (stairs) is solvable with home modification—relocating sleeping and bathing to the main floor—plus continued home or outpatient therapy. This supports a safe discharge home, the preferred outcome when caregiver support and the environment can be adapted. A skilled nursing facility is unnecessary given his gains and available support. Living alone without services is unsafe given residual deficits and neglect. A long-term acute care hospital is for patients needing prolonged hospital-level medical care, which he does not.

cases.diagnosis: Right MCA ischemic stroke with left hemiparesis and neglect, inpatient rehabilitation
pearls: Acute inpatient rehab requires tolerance of intensive therapy (~3 hrs/day, 5 days/week), need for ≥2 disciplines, and physician oversight with rehab nursing.
FIM scores tasks 1 (total assist) to 7 (independent); a 3 = moderate assistance (patient does 50–74%), 5 = supervision, 6 = modified independence.
Right MCA strokes cause left hemiparesis with hemispatial neglect; treat with scanning training, environmental cueing, and caregiver safety education—not by working only the strong side.
Discharge home is favored when caregiver training and home modifications (e.g., main-floor living) can offset residual deficits like stair limitations.

cases.title: Agitation and Confusion During Recovery From a Head Injury
cases.chiefComplaint: Restlessness, confusion, and combative behavior during rehabilitation after a head injury.
cases.history: A 28-year-old man is on the inpatient rehabilitation unit recovering from a moderate traumatic brain injury sustained in a motorcycle collision 12 days ago (helmeted; initial Glasgow Coma Scale 10, frontotemporal contusions and a small subdural on CT, no surgery required). Over the past two days he has become increasingly restless, pulling at lines, attempting to climb out of bed, and intermittently combative with staff during care. He is disoriented and unable to retain new information, with day-night reversal. He has no fever or new focal deficits. PMH: none. Medications: acetaminophen for pain, scheduled bowel regimen, and a melatonin trial for sleep; he is not on benzodiazepines. No known allergies. Pre-injury he was an electrician, lived with his girlfriend, and was independent. Social: occasional alcohol, no recreational drugs reported, no tobacco. Family history noncontributory. Nursing reports the agitation worsens with multiple simultaneous demands and a noisy environment, and improves with quiet, one-on-one redirection.
cases.exam: Awake, markedly restless, intermittently agitated and confused; oriented to self only. Attention severely impaired; cannot follow multistep commands consistently and is unable to form new memories (asks the same questions repeatedly). Speech is disinhibited but fluent; no aphasia. Cranial nerves grossly intact; pupils equal and reactive. Moves all four extremities with normal strength; no focal motor deficit. No nuchal rigidity. No tremor, asterixis, or focal seizure activity observed. Behavior escalates with overstimulation and de-escalates with calm, low-stimulus, one-to-one interaction. Skin intact; lines and Foley present (he repeatedly tugs at the Foley).

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His behavior—confused, agitated, unable to form new memories, responding to internal more than external cues—best corresponds to which Rancho Los Amigos level?
options: Level II (Generalized Response)
Level III (Localized Response)
Level IV (Confused–Agitated)
Level VIII (Purposeful–Appropriate)
stages.explanation: Rancho Los Amigos Level IV (Confused–Agitated) describes a patient who is markedly confused, agitated, and may be combative or non-purposeful, with severely impaired attention and inability to form new memories—exactly this presentation. Level II (Generalized Response) is inconsistent, non-purposeful responses to stimuli in a minimally responsive patient. Level III (Localized Response) shows inconsistent reactions directly related to stimuli (e.g., turning toward sound) but still low responsiveness. Level VIII is purposeful and appropriate with independence, far beyond his current state. Identifying the Rancho level guides expectations and tailored interventions.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given his Rancho Level IV agitation, what is the most appropriate first-line management approach?
options: Apply four-point restraints and start scheduled lorazepam
Environmental modification: reduce stimulation, ensure safety, consistent staff, structured routine, and remove unnecessary noxious stimuli
Start an antipsychotic immediately at high dose
Confront and reorient him repeatedly until he complies
stages.explanation: Management of post-traumatic agitation begins with non-pharmacologic, environmental strategies: a quiet low-stimulation setting, ensuring safety (e.g., a low bed, an enclosure/sitter rather than rigid restraints when possible), consistent caregivers, structured routines, limiting simultaneous demands, and removing noxious stimuli (pain, a tugging Foley, constipation, urinary retention). Benzodiazepines like lorazepam are generally avoided—they can worsen confusion and impair cognitive recovery. High-dose antipsychotics are not first-line and some agents may impair neurorecovery; medications are reserved for when behavior threatens safety despite environmental measures. Repeated forceful confrontation/reorientation tends to escalate agitation at this level.

stages.label: WORKUP FOR REVERSIBLE CAUSES
stages.content:
Bladder scan: 480 mL post-void residual (urinary retention contributing to Foley discomfort)
Basic metabolic panel: Na 139, K 4.1, glucose 112 mg/dL — within normal limits
CBC: WBC 8.2 ×10³/µL, no leukocytosis
Urinalysis: no pyuria, nitrite negative
Non-contrast head CT (repeat): stable contusions, no new hemorrhage or hydrocephalus
Pain assessment: grimacing with movement; bowel regimen 2 days without a bowel movement

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Based on the workup, which intervention should be prioritized to reduce his agitation?
options: Increase IV fluids and obtain an EEG first
Address reversible contributors: relieve urinary retention/manage the catheter, treat pain, and correct constipation
Start scheduled haloperidol around the clock
Transfer to a locked psychiatric unit
stages.explanation: The workup reveals modifiable, noxious contributors to agitation—urinary retention with a high post-void residual and an irritating Foley, untreated pain, and constipation. Correcting these (intermittent catheterization or proper catheter management/retention treatment, scheduled analgesia, and an effective bowel regimen) often markedly reduces agitation without sedating medications. Empiric IV fluids/EEG are not indicated when electrolytes are normal and there is no seizure evidence. Around-the-clock haloperidol is premature and potentially harmful before reversible causes are addressed. Psychiatric transfer is inappropriate—this is organic, injury-related agitation managed by the rehab team, not a primary psychiatric illness.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If agitation persists and endangers safety despite optimizing the environment and reversible causes, which pharmacologic choice is most appropriate?
options: A long-acting benzodiazepine for sedation
A carefully selected agent such as a beta-blocker (e.g., propranolol) or an atypical antipsychotic, used judiciously and reassessed
Chronic high-dose typical antipsychotics indefinitely
Diphenhydramine scheduled for sedation
stages.explanation: When environmental and reversible-cause management is insufficient and safety is threatened, judicious pharmacotherapy is added with the least cognitively impairing option and frequent reassessment. Beta-blockers such as propranolol have evidence for reducing agitation/aggression after TBI; atypical antipsychotics may be used short-term for dangerous behavior. Long-acting benzodiazepines are avoided because they worsen confusion and may impede recovery. Chronic high-dose typical antipsychotics carry sedation, extrapyramidal effects, and potential negative impact on neurorecovery. Anticholinergics like diphenhydramine worsen confusion and are inappropriate. Medications should target safety, be time-limited, and be tapered as the patient progresses through Rancho levels.

cases.diagnosis: Moderate traumatic brain injury with post-traumatic agitation (Rancho Level IV)
pearls: Rancho Los Amigos Level IV (Confused–Agitated) features marked confusion, agitation, impaired attention, and inability to form new memories—use the level to guide interventions.
First-line management of post-traumatic agitation is environmental: low stimulation, safety, consistent staff, structure, and removing noxious stimuli.
Always hunt for reversible drivers—pain, urinary retention, constipation, infection—before reaching for sedatives.
Avoid benzodiazepines and anticholinergics; if medication is needed for safety, consider propranolol or short-term atypical antipsychotics with frequent reassessment.

cases.title: Paralyzed Legs After a Fall From Height
cases.chiefComplaint: Loss of movement and sensation in both legs after a traumatic spinal injury.
cases.history: A 25-year-old man is admitted to inpatient rehabilitation three weeks after a fall from a roof caused a T6 burst fracture treated with posterior spinal fusion. Since injury he has had complete loss of motor and sensory function below the mid-trunk, with no anal sensation or voluntary anal contraction documented during prior exams. He is medically stable, out of spinal precautions for mobilization per spine surgery. He has a Foley catheter and a bowel program initiated. PMH: none. Medications: enoxaparin 40 mg daily (DVT prophylaxis), docusate/senna, acetaminophen, and a baclofen trial for early spasticity. No allergies. Pre-injury he was a warehouse worker, independent, living in a ground-floor apartment with a roommate; he is single. Social: social alcohol, no tobacco or drugs. Family history noncontributory. He is engaged in therapy but anxious about independence, work, and bladder/bowel management. No fevers; skin has been intact with diligent turning.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented. Upper limbs and intercostals largely spared; normal strength in all key muscles of the upper extremities and at trunk levels above the lesion. Sensory exam: intact light touch and pinprick down to the T6 dermatome (nipple line is T4, xiphoid T6); absent sensation below. Lower-extremity key muscles 0/5 bilaterally (hip flexion through plantarflexion). Deep anal pressure absent; no voluntary anal sphincter contraction; no sacral sparing (S4–S5). Reflexes returning below the level with developing spasticity; bulbocavernosus reflex present (spinal shock resolving). Skin intact; no pressure injuries. Abdomen soft.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: How is the neurologic level of injury defined in the ISNCSCI (ASIA) examination?
options: The most rostral vertebral level fractured on imaging
The lowest (most caudal) segment with intact sensation and antigravity (≥3/5) motor function bilaterally, with normal function above
The highest dermatome with any sensation
The level where reflexes first become brisk
stages.explanation: In ISNCSCI/ASIA, the neurologic level of injury is the most caudal spinal segment with normal (intact) sensory function and antigravity motor function (strength ≥3/5) on both sides, provided sensory and motor function above that level is also normal. It is a neurologic, not a radiographic, definition, so the fractured vertebral level is not the answer. The highest dermatome with any sensation and the level where reflexes become brisk are not how the neurologic level is determined. Here, intact sensation to T6 with absent function below and no sacral sparing defines a T6 neurologic level.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given absent motor and sensory function below T6 with no sacral sparing (no deep anal pressure and no voluntary anal contraction), what is the ASIA Impairment Scale grade?
options: ASIA A (complete)
ASIA B (sensory incomplete)
ASIA C (motor incomplete, most key muscles <3/5)
ASIA D (motor incomplete, most key muscles ≥3/5)
stages.explanation: ASIA A (complete) is defined by the absence of all sensory and motor function in the lowest sacral segments (S4–S5)—no deep anal pressure, no light touch/pinprick at S4–S5, and no voluntary anal contraction—exactly this patient (no sacral sparing). ASIA B is sensory-incomplete: some sensory but no motor function preserved below the level, including sacral sparing. ASIA C and D are motor-incomplete grades requiring preserved voluntary anal contraction or sacral sparing with motor function below, graded by the proportion of key muscles at ≥3/5. Sacral sparing is the linchpin distinguishing complete from incomplete injuries.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For bladder management of this T6 ASIA A injury once spinal shock resolves, which approach is generally preferred long-term?
options: Indwelling Foley catheter left in place indefinitely
Clean intermittent catheterization on a scheduled basis
Crede maneuver/abdominal straining as the primary method
No bladder program; await spontaneous recovery
stages.explanation: After a suprasacral SCI such as T6, the bladder becomes neurogenic (typically upper-motor-neuron, reflexic/spastic). The preferred long-term strategy in a patient with adequate hand function is clean intermittent catheterization on a schedule, which lowers rates of urinary tract infection, stones, and upper-tract damage compared with chronic indwelling catheters. Indefinite indwelling Foley use increases infection, stones, and bladder cancer risk and is reserved for those who cannot perform CIC. Crede/straining can dangerously raise pressures and risk reflux in a spastic bladder. Doing nothing risks retention, infection, and renal injury—a structured program is essential.

stages.label: REHAB & BOWEL PROGRAM
stages.content:
Goals set with team: independent wheelchair-level mobility, transfers, and self-care expected for a T6 paraplegic (UMN injury)
Bladder: reflexic (UMN) pattern anticipated; CIC every 4–6 hours initiated
Bowel: reflexic (UMN) bowel — scheduled program using suppository/digital stimulation after a meal to use the gastrocolic reflex
Skin: pressure-injury prevention with cushion, weight shifts every 15–30 min, and turning schedule
DVT prophylaxis continued; spasticity managed with baclofen and stretching

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which functional outcome is the most realistic primary goal for a motivated patient with a complete T6 paraplegia?
options: Independent community ambulation without devices
Independent wheelchair mobility, transfers, and self-care at the wheelchair level
Dependence on others for all transfers and self-care
Return to unrestricted manual labor without accommodation
stages.explanation: For complete (ASIA A) paraplegia at T6, expected outcomes with rehabilitation include independence in a manual wheelchair, independent transfers, dressing, bowel/bladder self-management, and most activities of daily living at the wheelchair level; functional ambulation is generally not a realistic primary goal at this level because of the high energy cost and lack of trunk/lower-limb innervation, though limited exercise standing/orthotic walking may be options for some. Full dependence underestimates achievable independence and would reflect inadequate rehabilitation. Returning to unrestricted manual labor without accommodation is unrealistic; vocational rehabilitation with accommodations or retraining is the appropriate path.

cases.diagnosis: Traumatic complete paraplegia, T6 ASIA A
pearls: The neurologic level of injury is the most caudal segment with normal sensation and antigravity (≥3/5) motor function, with normal function above—a neurologic, not radiographic, definition.
Sacral sparing (S4–S5 sensation, deep anal pressure, voluntary anal contraction) distinguishes incomplete from complete (ASIA A) injuries.
Suprasacral (UMN) SCI yields a reflexic bladder/bowel; preferred management is scheduled clean intermittent catheterization and a triggered bowel program using the gastrocolic reflex.
Realistic goals for complete T6 paraplegia: independent wheelchair mobility, transfers, and self-care; emphasize pressure-injury prevention and vocational rehabilitation.

cases.title: Pounding Headache and Sweating in a Wheelchair User
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe pounding headache, sweating, and flushing during therapy.
cases.history: A 33-year-old man with a chronic T4 complete spinal cord injury (ASIA A, injured 8 months ago) is on the rehabilitation unit and develops a sudden, severe, pounding headache during a therapy session, accompanied by profuse sweating and skin blotching above the chest. Staff note he was due for catheterization but the last cath was delayed and his leg bag appears kinked. He feels anxious and "awful." He denies chest pain or dyspnea. He has a history of recurrent similar, milder episodes triggered by a full bladder. PMH: T4 SCI with neurogenic bladder/bowel, prior pressure injury (healed). Medications: baclofen, oxybutynin, docusate/senna, and a CIC program. Allergies: none. Social: lives with family, uses a manual wheelchair, former graphic artist. No tobacco/drugs. Family history noncontributory. He is on the rehab unit for spasticity optimization and equipment training. Nurses immediately recognize the cluster of symptoms and call for help.
cases.exam: Anxious, diaphoretic, with flushing and blotchy erythema of the face and upper chest (above the level of injury); skin below the lesion is pale and cool with piloerection. Pounding bitemporal headache. Pupils equal; no focal neurologic change from baseline. Bladder is palpably distended; the catheter tubing is kinked and the leg bag appears obstructed. Abdomen otherwise soft. No new motor/sensory change above his chronic T4 level. Cardiopulmonary exam: bradycardic, regular rhythm; lungs clear. No fever. Rectal exam deferred initially; a stool mass is later noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis given a T4 SCI with severe hypertension, pounding headache, sweating/flushing above the lesion, and bradycardia?
options: Acute myocardial infarction
Autonomic dysreflexia
Hypertensive emergency from primary hypertension
Panic attack
stages.explanation: Autonomic dysreflexia is a potentially life-threatening syndrome in patients with SCI at or above T6, triggered by a noxious stimulus below the level of injury. Unopposed sympathetic outflow below the lesion causes severe hypertension, while baroreceptor-mediated parasympathetic responses above the lesion produce bradycardia, flushing/sweating above the level, and pounding headache—classic here with a distended bladder/kinked catheter as the trigger. Myocardial infarction would feature chest pain/ECG changes, not flushing above an SCI level with an identifiable noxious trigger. Primary hypertensive emergency lacks the trigger-and-level pattern. A panic attack does not cause BP of 210/118 with bradycardia and an organic trigger.

stages.label: IMMEDIATE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important first action?
options: Lay the patient flat and elevate the legs
Sit the patient upright, loosen constrictive clothing/devices, and begin searching for and removing the noxious stimulus
Give IV labetalol immediately before anything else
Order a stat head CT before any treatment
stages.explanation: The first steps in autonomic dysreflexia are to sit the patient upright (to induce orthostatic lowering of blood pressure), loosen any constrictive clothing, binders, or devices, and immediately begin identifying and removing the noxious stimulus while monitoring blood pressure frequently. Laying the patient flat would worsen the hypertension by removing the helpful orthostatic drop. Pharmacologic therapy is reserved for when BP remains dangerously high despite stimulus removal—it should not precede the upright positioning and source search. A head CT delays life-saving treatment; imaging is considered only if focal neurologic signs or suspected complications arise after stabilization.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After sitting him up, what is the most appropriate next step to address the likely trigger?
options: Immediately disimpact the rectum with a gloved finger
Relieve the bladder first—unkink/replace the catheter or catheterize—using lidocaine jelly to avoid worsening the response
Administer a large IV fluid bolus
Start an esmolol drip and observe
stages.explanation: Bladder distention is the most common trigger of autonomic dysreflexia, so after positioning, the bladder should be addressed first: check for and relieve any catheter kink/obstruction, drain the bladder, or catheterize if needed—using lidocaine jelly because instrumentation itself can be a noxious stimulus that worsens the crisis. If symptoms persist after the bladder is emptied, evaluate the bowel for impaction and disimpact gently with topical anesthetic. Disimpacting first is out of order when the bladder is the obvious cause. IV fluids do not treat the hypertension and may aggravate it. An esmolol drip without removing the stimulus treats the number, not the cause.

stages.label: RESPONSE TO INTERVENTION
stages.content:
Catheter unkinked and bladder drained: 600 mL returned
Repeat BP after bladder drainage: 188/104 mmHg (still elevated, symptoms persisting)
Gloved rectal exam with lidocaine jelly: firm stool in the vault
Gentle disimpaction performed with topical anesthetic
Repeat BP 5 minutes later: 158/92 mmHg, headache improving
HR: 64 bpm; flushing resolving

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His BP remained dangerously high (≈180–210 systolic) before disimpaction with persistent symptoms. Which pharmacologic agent is most appropriate as a rapid-acting, short-duration antihypertensive while sources are being addressed?
options: Long-acting oral amlodipine
A rapid-onset, short-acting agent such as nitroglycerin or nitroprusside (or immediate-release nifedipine/captopril per protocol)
Intravenous normal saline bolus
Oral atenolol once daily
stages.explanation: When blood pressure remains dangerously elevated (e.g., systolic ≥150 in someone with low baseline, or persistent severe hypertension) despite removing the stimulus, a rapid-onset, short-duration antihypertensive is used so the pressure can be titrated and will not plummet once the trigger is cleared. Options include nitroglycerin (or nitroprusside) and immediate-release nifedipine or captopril per institutional protocol, with close monitoring. Long-acting amlodipine or once-daily atenolol act too slowly and risk prolonged hypotension after stimulus removal. IV saline does not lower BP and may worsen it. Nitrates are contraindicated if the patient recently used a PDE5 inhibitor—always ask.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: After resolution, what is the most important counseling to prevent recurrence?
options: Start daily prophylactic antihypertensives for life
Educate the patient and caregivers on triggers (bladder/bowel/skin), adherence to catheterization and bowel programs, and an emergency action plan including carrying a medical alert
Avoid all physical therapy going forward
Restrict fluids severely to keep the bladder empty
stages.explanation: Prevention centers on education: the patient and caregivers must recognize early symptoms and common triggers—bladder distention/blocked catheter, bowel impaction, pressure injuries, ingrown nails, tight clothing—and maintain consistent intermittent catheterization and bowel programs, regular skin checks, and an emergency action plan (sit up, find and remove the stimulus, seek help), often with a medical-alert card describing AD management. Lifelong daily antihypertensives are not first-line; AD is episodic and trigger-driven. Avoiding therapy is unnecessary and harmful to rehabilitation. Severe fluid restriction risks dehydration, stones, and infection and does not safely prevent AD; scheduled bladder emptying does.

cases.diagnosis: Autonomic dysreflexia (T4 spinal cord injury)
pearls: Autonomic dysreflexia occurs in SCI at/above T6: a noxious stimulus below the lesion triggers severe hypertension with bradycardia, pounding headache, and flushing/sweating above the level.
First actions: sit the patient upright, loosen constrictive items, and find/remove the trigger—bladder first (unkink/drain/cath with lidocaine jelly), then bowel.
Use rapid-onset, short-acting antihypertensives (nitroglycerin/nitroprusside, IR nifedipine, captopril) only if BP stays dangerously high; avoid long-acting agents and nitrates after PDE5 inhibitors.
Prevention is education on triggers, strict bladder/bowel/skin routines, and an emergency action plan with a medical-alert card.

cases.title: Recurrent Infections and Leakage After Spinal Cord Injury
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent urinary tract infections and urinary leakage despite a catheterization routine.
cases.history: A 40-year-old woman with an incomplete cervical (C7) spinal cord injury sustained four years ago presents to the SCI clinic with recurrent urinary tract infections, episodes of leakage between catheterizations, and a sensation of incomplete emptying. She performs clean intermittent catheterization but lately notes higher catheterized volumes and occasional difficulty. She has had two febrile UTIs in the past year. She denies flank pain currently but had one prior episode. PMH: cervical SCI with neurogenic bladder, baclofen-managed spasticity. Medications: baclofen, oxybutynin, a recent course of antibiotics for UTI. Allergies: sulfa (rash). Social: lives independently with home modifications, works remotely, has reasonable hand function for self-cath. No tobacco; rare alcohol. Family history noncontributory. She is concerned about kidney damage after reading that high bladder pressures can harm the kidneys, and wants a clear plan. No autonomic dysreflexia symptoms reported with bladder filling at this visit.
cases.exam: Alert, comfortable. Suprapubic region nontender; no palpable distended bladder at present (recently catheterized). Costovertebral angles nontender. Lower-extremity tone increased with spasticity; partial preserved sensation consistent with incomplete injury. Reflexic lower-limb reflexes; bulbocavernosus reflex present. No suprapubic or urethral discharge. Skin intact without pressure injuries. Genitourinary exam: no obvious external abnormality. No peripheral edema. Abdomen soft, nondistended. Neurologic exam otherwise stable from baseline for a C7 incomplete injury.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic study to characterize her neurogenic bladder dysfunction?
options: Renal biopsy
Urodynamic study (with measurement of detrusor pressure and assessment of sphincter coordination)
Empiric long-term antibiotics without further testing
Plain abdominal radiograph only
stages.explanation: Urodynamic studies are the cornerstone for characterizing neurogenic bladder after SCI: they measure detrusor pressure during filling and voiding, bladder capacity and compliance, and the coordination between detrusor contraction and sphincter activity (looking for detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia), and they identify high storage pressures that threaten the upper tracts. This directly guides therapy. Renal biopsy evaluates intrinsic renal parenchymal disease, not bladder dynamics. Empiric long-term antibiotics neither diagnose the problem nor address the mechanism and promote resistance. A plain radiograph cannot assess bladder pressures or sphincter coordination, though imaging of the upper tracts is complementary.

stages.label: URODYNAMICS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Cystometrogram: reduced bladder compliance with elevated detrusor filling pressures; detrusor overactivity with involuntary contractions
Detrusor leak point pressure: ~45 cm H2O (elevated; >40 cm H2O risks upper-tract damage)
Voiding study: simultaneous detrusor contraction with external sphincter contraction (failure to relax) — detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia
Post-void residual: 180 mL
Renal/bladder ultrasound: mild bilateral hydronephrosis; no stones
Serum creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (normal)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which urodynamic finding poses the greatest threat to her kidneys and explains the hydronephrosis?
options: Low post-void residual
High detrusor storage pressures with detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia
Normal bladder compliance
An underactive, areflexic detrusor
stages.explanation: The dangerous combination is high detrusor storage/voiding pressures plus detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia—the detrusor contracts against a non-relaxing external sphincter, generating sustained high intravesical pressures that are transmitted to the ureters and kidneys, causing reflux/hydronephrosis and progressive renal damage. A detrusor leak point pressure above ~40 cm H2O is a recognized risk threshold, and her mild hydronephrosis reflects this. A low post-void residual and normal compliance would be reassuring, not threatening. An underactive areflexic detrusor causes retention/overflow but a different pressure profile; her study shows overactivity with dyssynergia, which is the higher-pressure, higher-risk pattern.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Based on the urodynamic findings, what is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Stop catheterization and allow reflex voiding
Optimize anticholinergic/antimuscarinic therapy (or add intradetrusor botulinum toxin) to lower detrusor pressure, combined with scheduled clean intermittent catheterization
Place a chronic indwelling Foley as the long-term solution
Begin the Crede maneuver to empty the bladder
stages.explanation: With detrusor overactivity, poor compliance, high pressures, and dyssynergia, the goal is a low-pressure storage reservoir that is emptied regularly. This is achieved by suppressing detrusor activity—optimizing antimuscarinic therapy (e.g., titrating oxybutynin or switching agents) or adding intradetrusor onabotulinumtoxinA for refractory overactivity—combined with scheduled clean intermittent catheterization to ensure complete, low-pressure emptying and protect the kidneys. Allowing reflex voiding against a dyssynergic sphincter perpetuates high pressures and upper-tract damage. A chronic indwelling Foley is a last resort with higher complication rates. The Crede maneuver raises pressures and is contraindicated with dyssynergia/reflux.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Regarding her recurrent positive urine cultures, what is the most appropriate antibiotic strategy?
options: Treat asymptomatic bacteriuria with antibiotics at every positive culture
Treat only symptomatic UTIs and focus on lowering bladder pressure and improving emptying; avoid treating asymptomatic bacteriuria
Start indefinite daily prophylactic antibiotics for all patients with neurogenic bladder
Switch her to a chronic indwelling catheter to reduce infections
stages.explanation: In neurogenic bladder, asymptomatic bacteriuria is common and should not be treated, because treating it drives antimicrobial resistance without benefit; antibiotics are reserved for symptomatic UTIs (fever, increased spasticity, autonomic dysreflexia, suprapubic/flank pain, malaise, new incontinence). The most effective way to reduce recurrent infections is addressing the underlying urodynamic problem—lowering storage pressures and ensuring complete, regular emptying. Routine indefinite prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended for most patients due to resistance. Switching to a chronic indwelling catheter typically increases, not decreases, bacteriuria and complications and is not the answer.

cases.diagnosis: Neurogenic bladder with detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia (spinal cord injury)
pearls: Urodynamic studies characterize neurogenic bladder—capacity, compliance, detrusor pressures, and detrusor-sphincter coordination—and guide management.
High detrusor storage pressures with detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia (and leak point pressure >40 cm H2O) threaten the upper tracts and cause hydronephrosis.

Goal is a low-pressure reservoir emptied regularly: antimuscarinics (or intradetrusor botulinum toxin) plus scheduled clean intermittent catheterization; avoid Crede and chronic indwelling catheters when possible.
Treat only symptomatic UTIs—do not treat asymptomatic bacteriuria; reducing pressures and improving emptying lowers infection recurrence.

cases.title: Below-Knee Limb Loss and a New Leg
cases.chiefComplaint: He returns six weeks after a below-knee surgery wanting to know when he can get a prosthetic leg and start walking again.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man with longstanding type 2 diabetes and peripheral arterial disease underwent a right transtibial amputation six weeks ago for a nonhealing foot ulcer with wet gangrene. He completed the acute hospital stay and a brief inpatient rehab course and now lives at home with his wife. The surgical wound has healed without dehiscence. He reports the residual limb feels swollen and 'lumpy' and he has not yet been fitted for anything. PMH: type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.4%), hypertension, dyslipidemia, 30-pack-year smoking history, quit at surgery. Meds: metformin 1000 mg BID, insulin glargine 24 units nightly, lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: retired warehouse worker, ranch-style home with three entry steps, motivated to walk and drive again. Family history: father had a stroke; mother type 2 diabetes. He performs basic self-care independently using a wheelchair and walker for short transfers.
cases.exam: Alert, well-nourished man in a wheelchair. Right transtibial residual limb: healed incision with no drainage, erythema, or fluctuance; bulbous distal contour with redundant soft tissue; mild pitting edema; non-tender. Residual limb length adequate (~15 cm below knee). Knee flexion contracture absent, full passive knee extension. Sensation intact proximally. Left foot: intact skin, diminished protective sensation to monofilament, palpable but weak dorsalis pedis pulse. Strength 5/5 in both hips and the right knee. No proximal joint contractures. Cardiopulmonary exam unremarkable; no signs of active infection.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His residual limb is healed but edematous and bulbous. What is the most appropriate next step to prepare the limb for prosthetic fitting?
options: Fit a definitive prosthesis immediately since the wound is healed
Begin residual limb shaping with elastic shrinker or figure-of-eight wrapping and edema control
Defer all intervention for three more months to allow spontaneous shrinkage
Order arterial bypass surgery before any prosthetic consideration
stages.explanation: An edematous, bulbous residual limb must be shaped into a tapered, cylindrical form before fitting; a shrinker sock or figure-of-eight elastic wrap controls edema and matures the limb. A definitive (permanent) prosthesis is premature because volume will keep changing for months—an early-fit or preparatory prosthesis is used first. Waiting passively for three months wastes rehab time and risks contracture and deconditioning; active shaping accelerates readiness. Arterial bypass is not indicated—his wound healed and the limb is viable; revascularization is reserved for ischemic limbs at risk, not as a routine prosthetic prerequisite.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP DATA
stages.content:
Residual limb after 6 weeks of shrinker use: tapered cylindrical contour, edema resolved
HbA1c: 8.4%
Ankle-brachial index (contralateral left limb): 0.78
Nutrition: albumin 3.9 g/dL, normal
K-level mobility assessment: community ambulation goals, able to traverse uneven terrain and curbs

stages.label: PROSTHETIC PRESCRIPTION
stages.question: His functional assessment supports community ambulation including variable cadence and uneven terrain. Which prosthetic foot component best matches this Medicare Functional Classification Level?
options: Solid-ankle cushion-heel (SACH) foot for a household-only ambulator
No prosthesis; recommend permanent wheelchair use
Energy-storing/dynamic-response foot for a K3 community ambulator
Microprocessor knee unit
stages.explanation: He meets K3 criteria—ability to ambulate with variable cadence and traverse most environmental barriers, typical of a community ambulator. K3 patients benefit from an energy-storing (dynamic-response) foot that returns energy for efficient, variable-cadence gait. A SACH foot suits K1 household ambulators with fixed cadence and would under-serve him. Recommending a wheelchair ignores his demonstrated potential and motivation. A microprocessor knee is irrelevant for a transtibial amputee—his anatomic knee is intact, so knee componentry does not apply. Component prescription should match documented K-level to optimize function and justify coverage.

stages.label: SKIN AND LIMB CARE
stages.question: Given his diabetes and dysvascular etiology, which counseling point is most important to prevent the most common cause of prosthetic abandonment in this population?
options: Avoid any weight-bearing on the prosthesis for the first year
Daily residual-limb skin inspection and contralateral foot care, with prompt reporting of redness or breakdown
Apply heating pads to the residual limb nightly to improve circulation
Discontinue the shrinker sock permanently once the prosthesis is delivered
stages.explanation: Skin breakdown on the insensate, dysvascular residual limb and loss of the remaining contralateral limb are the dominant threats; daily skin inspection (a mirror helps), proper sock-ply management, and meticulous contralateral foot care prevent ulceration and abandonment. Avoiding weight-bearing for a year defeats rehabilitation and causes deconditioning. Heating pads are dangerous on insensate, ischemic tissue and cause burns. The shrinker is still used when the prosthesis is off (e.g., at night) to control volume—it is not discontinued. Protecting both the residual limb and the sound limb is paramount in dysvascular amputees.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Three months later he reports the socket feels loose, the limb 'pistons' inside it, and the distal end is reddened. What is the most likely explanation and appropriate response?
options: Residual limb volume loss requiring added sock ply or socket adjustment
Deep venous thrombosis requiring immediate anticoagulation
Recurrent gangrene mandating revision to transfemoral amputation
Normal break-in; advise no change and continue use
stages.explanation: A loose socket with pistoning and distal-end redness from 'verrucous'/choke-type irritation reflects residual-limb volume loss as the limb continues to mature—the classic fix is adding prosthetic sock ply or, if persistent, a socket modification or refit. DVT presents with calf swelling, warmth, and pain, not socket looseness, and is not suggested here. Recurrent gangrene would show necrosis, drainage, or systemic signs—absent in this scenario. It is not benign break-in: ignoring pistoning leads to skin breakdown. Volume fluctuation is expected in the first year and is managed by sock-ply titration before considering a new socket.

cases.diagnosis: Transtibial (below-knee) amputation rehabilitation in a dysvascular/diabetic amputee, with residual-limb shaping and K3-level prosthetic prescription
pearls: Residual limb shaping with a shrinker or figure-of-eight elastic wrap precedes any fitting; use a preparatory prosthesis before a definitive one because volume changes for up to a year.
Medicare K-levels drive component selection: K1 SACH foot for household ambulators, K3 energy-storing/dynamic-response foot for variable-cadence community ambulators.
Daily skin inspection of the residual limb and meticulous contralateral foot care are critical—skin breakdown and loss of the sound limb are leading complications in dysvascular amputees.
A loose, pistoning socket with distal redness usually means volume loss—titrate sock ply first before refitting.

cases.title: Pain in a Limb That Isn't There
cases.chiefComplaint: He describes burning, cramping pain in his missing foot four months after a traumatic below-knee amputation.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man underwent left transtibial amputation four months ago after a motorcycle crush injury. He healed well and ambulates with a preparatory prosthesis. He now reports a vivid, distressing sensation that his absent left foot is still present and is 'twisted into a cramp,' with intermittent burning, electric, and squeezing pain rated 6/10 that worsens at night and with stress. He distinguishes this from soreness over the end of his stump when he removes the prosthesis. The phantom pain disrupts sleep and mood. PMH: otherwise healthy. Meds: acetaminophen as needed, with limited relief; previously prescribed oxycodone in hospital, which he disliked. Allergies: none. Social: works as an electrician, eager to return to work, lives with his partner, social alcohol use, no tobacco. Family history: noncontributory. He denies fevers, drainage, or new redness at the residual limb. He has been told 'the pain is in your head' by a prior clinician and is frustrated and anxious about it.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, anxious man. Left transtibial residual limb: well-healed incision, no erythema, drainage, fluctuance, or warmth; no neuroma palpable at the incision; mild nonfocal tenderness only. No Tinel sign over a discrete nodule. Sensation altered distally with reported phantom sensations on light touch. Knee range of motion full, no flexion contracture. Strength 5/5 proximally. Contralateral limb intact with normal pulses and sensation. No signs of infection or vascular compromise. Affect anxious but cooperative; no acute distress.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: He reports pain perceived in the absent foot, distinct from soreness at the end of the stump. Which feature most reliably distinguishes phantom limb pain from residual limb pain?
options: Phantom limb pain is localized to the surgical incision and reproduced by palpation
Phantom limb pain is perceived in the missing body part, whereas residual limb pain is felt in the remaining stump tissue
Residual limb pain never improves with prosthetic adjustment
Phantom limb pain only occurs in dysvascular, not traumatic, amputations
stages.explanation: The defining distinction is location of perception: phantom limb pain is felt in the body part that no longer exists (the missing foot), while residual limb pain is felt in the remaining stump and is often reproducible by palpation or linked to socket fit, neuroma, or bone spurs. Incisional, palpable pain describes residual limb pain, not phantom pain. Residual limb pain frequently does improve with socket/prosthetic adjustment—so that statement is false. Phantom pain occurs after both traumatic and dysvascular amputations and is extremely common regardless of etiology. Correctly separating the two guides therapy.

stages.label: INITIAL MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond reassurance that the pain is real, which non-pharmacologic therapy has the best evidence to try first for phantom limb pain?
options: Strict bed rest and immobilization of the residual limb
Immediate surgical revision of the residual limb
Mirror therapy using the intact limb's reflection
Long-term opioid escalation
stages.explanation: Mirror therapy—using the reflection of the intact limb to create a visual illusion of the missing limb moving—can relieve phantom pain by addressing maladaptive cortical reorganization and is a low-risk first-line nonpharmacologic option. Bed rest and immobilization worsen deconditioning and contractures without helping phantom pain. Surgical revision is reserved for a clear structural cause such as a symptomatic neuroma or bone spur, not undifferentiated phantom pain. Opioid escalation is discouraged—opioids carry dependence risk, modest benefit for neuropathic phantom pain, and he already disliked them. Multimodal, mechanism-based therapy beginning with mirror therapy and desensitization is preferred.

stages.label: RESIDUAL LIMB EVALUATION
stages.content:
Residual limb radiograph: no bone spur or heterotopic ossification
No palpable neuroma; negative Tinel sign over the incision
Residual limb skin intact; socket fit assessed as adequate
Sleep: averaging 4-5 hours, frequent awakenings from pain
PHQ-9: 11 (mild-moderate depressive symptoms), screening negative for suicidality

stages.label: PHARMACOTHERAPY
stages.question: Mirror therapy and desensitization give partial relief but he still has burning, electric pain disrupting sleep. Which medication is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic choice?
options: Scheduled oxycodone around the clock
A gabapentinoid such as gabapentin or pregabalin, titrated upward
Intramuscular ketorolac as needed
Oral prednisone taper
stages.explanation: Phantom limb pain is neuropathic, so a gabapentinoid (gabapentin or pregabalin) titrated to effect is a reasonable first-line agent; it targets neuropathic mechanisms and can also aid sleep. Tricyclics and SNRIs are alternative neuropathic agents. Scheduled opioids are not first-line: limited efficacy for neuropathic phantom pain and high dependence risk, especially given his dislike of them. Ketorolac is an NSAID for nociceptive/inflammatory pain and is ineffective for neuropathic phantom pain, with renal/GI risks. Prednisone has no role—there is no inflammatory or autoimmune process here. Choose a neuropathic-targeted agent and continue multimodal therapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which additional intervention best addresses the broader picture, given his disrupted sleep, low mood, and frustration after being dismissed?
options: Discharge from clinic with no follow-up since labs are normal
Refer for a multidisciplinary approach including psychological support/CBT and continued prosthetic optimization
Recommend immediate transfemoral revision amputation
Prescribe a benzodiazepine for nightly use indefinitely
stages.explanation: Phantom limb pain is best managed multidisciplinary: validating the pain, optimizing the prosthesis and graded desensitization/mirror therapy, addressing mood and sleep with cognitive-behavioral therapy, and using neuropathic agents. His PHQ-9 and dismissive prior care make psychological support especially valuable. Discharging him ignores ongoing suffering and modifiable factors. Higher-level revision amputation does not treat phantom pain and may create a new phantom. Chronic benzodiazepines risk dependence, falls, and worsened mood without treating neuropathic pain. Coordinated, biopsychosocial care produces the best outcomes.
