cases.diagnosis: Phantom limb pain following traumatic transtibial amputation (distinct from residual limb pain)
pearls: Phantom limb pain is perceived in the absent body part; residual limb pain is felt in the remaining stump and is often reproducible by palpation or tied to socket/neuroma/bone spur.
Start with low-risk, mechanism-based therapy: mirror therapy and graded desensitization, plus reassurance that the pain is real.
Treat the neuropathic component with gabapentinoids, TCAs, or SNRIs—avoid opioid escalation, which has poor efficacy and high risk.
A palpable, Tinel-positive nodule suggests a symptomatic neuroma (a residual-limb cause) that may warrant injection or surgical attention.

cases.title: A Boy Who Walks on His Toes with Scissoring Legs
cases.chiefComplaint: His parents report worsening tightness in both legs with toe-walking and frequent tripping.
cases.history: A 9-year-old boy born at 28 weeks' gestation (NICU stay with intraventricular hemorrhage) has a known diagnosis from infancy affecting both legs more than arms. He is a community ambulator with a posterior walker but his parents note increasing leg stiffness, a 'scissoring' gait, and more frequent falls over the past year, plus difficulty with hygiene because his thighs are hard to separate. He has no seizures and attends mainstream school with an aide; cognition is age-appropriate. PMH: spastic diplegia, prematurity, prior bilateral heel-cord tightness. Meds: none currently; previously trialed oral baclofen 5 mg TID, stopped for sedation and drooling. Allergies: none. Social: lives with both parents and a younger sibling; engaged in adaptive sports; uses ankle-foot orthoses. Family history: noncontributory; sibling healthy. Developmental history: sat at 12 months, walked with support at 3 years. Parents are asking whether 'injections' or 'a pump' would help reduce the tightness and improve his walking.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative boy. Lower extremities: increased tone with a catch on rapid passive stretch at both hip adductors, hamstrings, and gastrocsoleus. Hip adductor and ankle plantarflexor tone graded; bilateral hamstring and heel-cord tightness limit terminal knee extension and dorsiflexion. Deep tendon reflexes brisk at knees and ankles with bilateral ankle clonus and upgoing toes. Scissoring posture with crouch and equinus during gait. Upper extremities near-normal tone and function. No focal weakness disproportionate to spasticity. Sensation intact. No spinal deformity at rest; mild hamstring-driven posterior pelvic tilt.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: To quantify his lower-extremity tone before treatment, which bedside scale and finding are most appropriate to document?
options: Glasgow Coma Scale score of the limbs
Modified Ashworth Scale, grading the catch and resistance to passive movement
Mini-Mental State Examination
Berg Balance Scale only, without tone grading
stages.explanation: The Modified Ashworth Scale (MAS) grades resistance to passive movement on a 0–4 scale (with a 1+), capturing the velocity-dependent 'catch' of spasticity—exactly what is needed to document tone and track treatment response. The Glasgow Coma Scale measures consciousness, not limb tone. The Mini-Mental State Examination assesses cognition, irrelevant to spasticity grading. The Berg Balance Scale assesses balance/fall risk and is useful functionally but does not quantify tone. The Tardieu scale (measuring angle of catch at different velocities) is a complementary spasticity-specific tool. MAS is the standard bedside tone measure.

stages.label: EXAM QUANTIFICATION
stages.content:
Modified Ashworth Scale: hip adductors 3, hamstrings 2, gastrocsoleus 3 bilaterally
Gross Motor Function Classification System (GMFCS): level II
Selective motor control: relatively preserved
Hip surveillance radiograph: migration percentage 22% (within monitoring range)
Gait analysis: dynamic equinus and crouch, scissoring from adductor overactivity

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His spasticity is focal-predominant in the adductors and gastrocsoleus, limiting gait and hygiene, with preserved selective control. Which intervention is most appropriate to target these specific muscles?
options: Intrathecal baclofen pump implantation as first-line
Botulinum toxin injection to the overactive adductors and gastrocsoleus, combined with therapy and orthoses
Restart high-dose oral baclofen despite prior sedation
Bilateral femoral osteotomy now
stages.explanation: When spasticity is focal—confined to a few identifiable muscles impairing function or care—botulinum toxin chemodenervation of those target muscles (adductors, gastrocsoleus) combined with stretching, strengthening, serial casting, and orthoses is the appropriate, reversible, focal intervention. An intrathecal baclofen pump is reserved for severe, generalized spasticity, typically in nonambulatory or more involved children, and is excessive for a GMFCS II ambulator with focal findings. Re-escalating oral baclofen is unwise given prior intolerable sedation and drooling. Femoral osteotomy is an orthopedic procedure for established bony deformity/hip subluxation, not first-line for dynamic spasticity with an acceptable migration percentage.

stages.label: PATIENT SELECTION
stages.question: His parents ask when a baclofen pump would be considered instead of injections. Which scenario best fits intrathecal baclofen pump therapy?
options: Isolated tightness of one calf muscle
Severe, generalized spasticity affecting multiple limbs causing pain or impairing care, refractory to oral and focal measures
Mild spasticity well-controlled with stretching alone
Spasticity that has fully resolved with orthoses
stages.explanation: Intrathecal baclofen is best for severe, diffuse spasticity involving multiple limbs—causing pain, hygiene difficulty, or impaired positioning—when oral agents are limited by systemic side effects and focal chemodenervation cannot cover the burden. It delivers drug directly to the spinal cord, reducing systemic sedation. Isolated single-muscle tightness is a focal problem best treated with botulinum toxin, not a pump. Mild spasticity controlled by stretching needs no invasive therapy. Resolved spasticity obviously needs no pump. Pump candidacy hinges on spasticity severity, distribution, functional impact, and failure of less invasive options, often confirmed by a screening intrathecal trial dose.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Alongside chemodenervation, which ongoing measure is most important to prevent long-term orthopedic complications in spastic diplegia?
options: Avoiding all weight-bearing activity
Routine hip surveillance with serial exams and radiographs plus stretching, orthoses, and therapy
Permanent wheelchair use to rest the legs
Discontinuing physical therapy once tone improves
stages.explanation: Children with spastic diplegia are at risk for progressive hip subluxation/dislocation and fixed contractures; structured hip surveillance (periodic exams and radiographs tracking migration percentage) plus ongoing stretching, orthoses (AFOs), and physical therapy preserve range, alignment, and function and catch problems early. Avoiding weight-bearing promotes osteopenia, contractures, and deconditioning. Defaulting to a wheelchair sacrifices ambulation he currently has. Stopping therapy when tone improves loses the strengthening and range gains that maintain function—therapy must continue. Spasticity care is longitudinal and multidisciplinary, combining tone reduction with musculoskeletal preservation.

cases.diagnosis: Spastic diplegic cerebral palsy with lower-extremity spasticity (GMFCS II), managed with focal botulinum toxin
pearls: The Modified Ashworth Scale (0–4, with 1+) grades velocity-dependent resistance to passive stretch and is the standard bedside spasticity measure; the Tardieu scale adds angle-of-catch detail.

Focal spasticity in a few key muscles is treated with botulinum toxin chemodenervation plus therapy, casting, and orthoses; severe generalized spasticity is the niche for an intrathecal baclofen pump.
Oral baclofen is limited by sedation and is often poorly tolerated; intrathecal delivery achieves spinal effect with less systemic side effect.
Lifelong hip surveillance (migration percentage on radiographs) and continued therapy prevent subluxation and contractures in spastic diplegia.

cases.title: A Burning, Swollen Hand After a Wrist Cast Came Off
cases.chiefComplaint: She has severe, disproportionate burning pain and swelling in her hand months after a wrist fracture healed.
cases.history: A 44-year-old woman sustained a left distal radius (Colles) fracture in a fall 4 months ago, treated with closed reduction and casting; the fracture healed radiographically. Since cast removal 10 weeks ago she has developed escalating, constant burning pain in the left hand and forearm that is far out of proportion to the original injury. Light touch from clothing or a breeze is excruciating, and even hanging the arm down is intolerable. She reports the hand looks swollen, sometimes red and warm, other times bluish and cold, with shiny skin and changes in sweating and hair growth. She guards the limb and barely uses it. PMH: migraine, mild anxiety. Meds: ibuprofen with minimal relief, sumatriptan as needed. Allergies: none. Social: graphic designer, right-hand dominant, nonsmoker, occasional wine; the disability is affecting her work and mood. Family history: noncontributory. She denies fever, recent infection, prior hand surgery, or new trauma. No proximal weakness or other joint complaints.
cases.exam: Anxious woman guarding the left hand. Left hand/forearm: diffuse edema, mottled bluish skin that is cooler than the right, shiny appearance with increased sweating, and altered nail/hair growth compared with the contralateral side. Marked allodynia to light touch and hyperalgesia to pinprick over the dorsum and digits. Decreased active range of motion of the wrist and fingers from pain and stiffness, but no fixed deformity. No erythematous streaking, fluctuance, warmth suggestive of cellulitis, or lymphangitis. Distal pulses intact; no focal motor deficit in a single nerve distribution. Sensory changes are non-dermatomal. No proximal joint synovitis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her constant burning pain, allodynia, edema, and vasomotor/sudomotor and trophic changes after a fracture, without a defined nerve lesion, point to which diagnosis and criteria?
options: Complex regional pain syndrome type II (causalgia) from a major nerve injury
Complex regional pain syndrome type I, diagnosed clinically by the Budapest criteria
Cellulitis of the hand requiring intravenous antibiotics
Acute compartment syndrome requiring fasciotomy
stages.explanation: Disproportionate, persistent regional pain with sensory (allodynia/hyperalgesia), vasomotor (color/temperature asymmetry), sudomotor/edema, and motor/trophic changes after a fracture—without an identifiable major nerve injury—defines CRPS type I, diagnosed clinically using the Budapest criteria (symptoms in 3 of 4 categories and signs in 2 of 4 at evaluation, with no better explanation). CRPS type II (causalgia) requires a confirmed nerve lesion, which she lacks. Cellulitis would show spreading erythema, warmth, fever, and leukocytosis, not fluctuating vasomotor changes and allodynia. Compartment syndrome is an acute surgical emergency with tense compartments and pain on passive stretch, not a 4-month evolving picture.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Left hand radiograph: healed distal radius fracture; patchy periarticular osteopenia
CBC: WBC 7.1 ×10³/µL (normal)
ESR: 12 mm/hr; CRP: 0.4 mg/dL (normal)
Three-phase bone scan: increased periarticular uptake in delayed phase
Nerve conduction study: no focal compressive or major nerve lesion identified

stages.label: INITIAL MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her workup supports CRPS type I. What is the cornerstone first-line treatment to restore function and reduce sensitization?
options: Strict immobilization of the limb in a cast
Early, progressive physical/occupational therapy emphasizing desensitization and graded motion, with pain control to enable participation
Immediate spinal cord stimulator implantation
Long-term immobilizing splint and opioid monotherapy
stages.explanation: Functional restoration through early, graded physical and occupational therapy—desensitization, active range of motion, edema control, and progressive loading—is the cornerstone of CRPS care, with analgesia provided mainly to enable therapy participation. Immobilization worsens stiffness, disuse, and central sensitization and is counterproductive. A spinal cord stimulator is an option for refractory CRPS after conservative measures fail, not an initial step. Immobilizing splints plus opioid monotherapy promote disuse and dependence without addressing the disorder; opioids have limited efficacy in CRPS. The guiding principle is 'move it to improve it' within a multimodal, multidisciplinary program.

stages.label: PHARMACOTHERAPY
stages.question: To control her neuropathic pain and support participation in therapy, which adjunctive medication strategy is most appropriate?
options: Scheduled high-dose opioids as the primary analgesic
Neuropathic agents (gabapentinoid or TCA/SNRI); consider a short bisphosphonate course; bone scan/osteopenia supports trial
Systemic broad-spectrum antibiotics
No medications; therapy alone is always sufficient
stages.explanation: Neuropathic pain agents—gabapentinoids, tricyclics, or SNRIs—are appropriate adjuncts to facilitate therapy participation; bisphosphonates have evidence in CRPS, especially with bone scan uptake and periarticular osteopenia, and can be considered. Short courses of corticosteroids early may also help inflammatory features. High-dose opioids are not primary therapy—poor efficacy and dependence risk in CRPS. Antibiotics are irrelevant: there is no infection (normal WBC, ESR, CRP). Saying therapy alone always suffices is too rigid; many patients need pharmacologic and multidisciplinary support, including psychological therapy, to engage in functional restoration.

stages.label: REFRACTORY CASE
stages.question: Despite 3 months of therapy and neuropathic medications she has persistent severe pain limiting all hand use. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Amputate the affected hand to eliminate the pain
Refer for an interventional/multidisciplinary escalation such as sympathetic nerve block, and consider spinal cord stimulation if refractory
Discontinue all therapy and recommend complete rest
Repeat the same regimen indefinitely without change
stages.explanation: For CRPS refractory to first-line functional and pharmacologic management, escalation to interventional options is appropriate—a sympathetic (stellate ganglion) block can reduce pain enough to advance therapy, and spinal cord stimulation is supported for refractory CRPS. These are pursued within continued multidisciplinary care. Amputation is not a treatment for CRPS pain and risks recurrence and phantom pain in the residual limb. Complete rest worsens disuse and sensitization. Repeating an ineffective regimen unchanged abandons the patient. Stepwise escalation that keeps the limb moving while adding targeted interventions offers the best chance of functional recovery.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which statement about CRPS type I outcomes is most accurate to counsel this patient?
options: CRPS always progresses to permanent disability regardless of treatment
Early recognition and prompt functional rehabilitation improve the likelihood of meaningful recovery
CRPS is purely psychiatric and resolves with antidepressants alone
Surgery on the original fracture site cures CRPS
stages.explanation: Outcomes are better with early diagnosis and prompt initiation of functional rehabilitation; many patients improve substantially when treated early in a multidisciplinary program, so timely recognition matters. CRPS does not invariably end in permanent disability—delays worsen prognosis, but early, active treatment helps. It is not purely psychiatric; while psychological support is important, CRPS involves peripheral and central neurologic and inflammatory mechanisms and is not cured by antidepressants alone. Operating on the healed fracture site does not cure CRPS and may aggravate it. Educating the patient that early, active engagement improves prognosis encourages adherence.

cases.diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I of the left hand following a distal radius fracture (Budapest criteria)
pearls: CRPS type I is a clinical diagnosis by the Budapest criteria: disproportionate pain plus sensory, vasomotor, sudomotor/edema, and motor/trophic features without a defined nerve lesion (type II/causalgia has a confirmed nerve injury).
Functional restoration—early graded therapy, desensitization, and edema control—is the cornerstone; immobilization worsens the syndrome.
Adjuncts include neuropathic agents (gabapentinoids, TCAs/SNRIs), short steroid courses for inflammatory features, and bisphosphonates (supported when bone scan/osteopenia present).
Refractory cases escalate to sympathetic blocks and spinal cord stimulation; early recognition and active rehab improve prognosis.

cases.title: Aching All Over with Exhaustion and Poor Sleep
cases.chiefComplaint: She reports widespread body pain, fatigue, and unrefreshing sleep for over a year.
cases.history: A 39-year-old woman reports more than 12 months of diffuse pain affecting her neck, shoulders, back, hips, and limbs bilaterally, described as a constant deep ache with stiffness. She wakes unrefreshed despite adequate time in bed, has 'brain fog' with poor concentration and memory, and is persistently fatigued. Symptoms wax and wane but never fully remit and worsen with stress and poor sleep. She has irritable-bowel-type symptoms and frequent tension headaches. Multiple prior labs and imaging were unremarkable. PMH: depression (on treatment), migraine, irritable bowel syndrome. Meds: sertraline 50 mg daily, ibuprofen and acetaminophen as needed with little benefit; she was briefly given tramadol elsewhere. Allergies: none. Social: middle-school teacher, married with two children, sedentary lately due to pain, nonsmoker, rare alcohol. Family history: mother with 'chronic pain' and depression. Review of systems negative for true joint swelling, rash, dry eyes/mouth, fevers, weight loss, or focal weakness.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, mildly anxious woman. Diffuse soft-tissue tenderness to palpation across the neck, trapezii, lateral elbows, buttocks, greater trochanters, and medial knees, symmetric and widespread. No joint swelling, effusion, warmth, or synovitis; full joint range of motion. Strength 5/5 throughout with normal reflexes and sensation; no focal neurologic deficit. No rash, no sclerodactyly, no malar rash, no lymphadenopathy, no thyromegaly. Gait normal. No muscle atrophy. Mood mildly depressed; affect appropriate. The remainder of the cardiopulmonary and abdominal exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her chronic widespread pain with fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, and cognitive symptoms, with a normal exam, best fits which diagnosis and diagnostic approach?
options: Rheumatoid arthritis, confirmed by tender joints
Fibromyalgia, diagnosed clinically using the widespread pain index and symptom severity scale
Polymyositis, confirmed by proximal weakness
Hypothyroid myopathy, diagnosed by tender points alone
stages.explanation: Chronic widespread pain with fatigue, nonrestorative sleep, and cognitive ('fog') symptoms, plus a normal inflammatory exam, fits fibromyalgia. Modern criteria are clinical, using the Widespread Pain Index (number of painful regions) and Symptom Severity Scale (fatigue, sleep, cognition, somatic symptoms) over ≥3 months without another explanation—no tender-point count is required. Rheumatoid arthritis causes synovitis with swollen, warm joints and serologic/inflammatory markers—absent here. Polymyositis causes objective proximal weakness and elevated CK, not diffuse tenderness with full strength. Hypothyroid myopathy must be excluded with TSH but is not diagnosed by tender points; the picture and normal strength point to fibromyalgia after reasonable exclusion of mimics.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
CBC: normal
ESR: 8 mm/hr; CRP: 0.3 mg/dL (normal)
TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (normal)
CK: 90 U/L (normal)
ANA and rheumatoid factor: negative
25-OH vitamin D: 28 ng/mL

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: Her inflammatory and autoimmune workup is normal. How should these results guide the diagnosis and counseling?
options: Normal labs exclude any real condition; reassure and discharge with no plan
Normal inflammatory/autoimmune labs support fibromyalgia as a clinical diagnosis after excluding mimics
Order an MRI of every joint to find hidden inflammation
Start high-dose corticosteroids empirically
stages.explanation: Normal ESR/CRP, TSH, CK, ANA, and RF are expected in fibromyalgia and help exclude inflammatory, thyroid, myopathic, and autoimmune mimics, supporting a positive clinical diagnosis—not a diagnosis of exclusion alone but consistent with central sensitization. Telling her nothing is wrong is invalidating and false; fibromyalgia is a real central-pain disorder. Imaging every joint is low-yield with a normal exam and risks incidental findings and cost. Corticosteroids have no role in fibromyalgia and cause harm without benefit. The labs reassure and reinforce the diagnosis, allowing a confident, empathetic explanation and a management plan.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which intervention is the most evidence-based foundation of fibromyalgia management?
options: Long-term opioids for pain control
Patient education plus a graded aerobic exercise program and sleep optimization
Strict bed rest until the pain resolves
Chronic systemic corticosteroids
stages.explanation: Nonpharmacologic therapy is foundational: patient education, graded aerobic exercise, sleep optimization, and cognitive-behavioral therapy have the strongest evidence and improve pain, function, and fatigue. Opioids are not recommended—poor long-term efficacy, risk of dependence and opioid-induced hyperalgesia—and tramadol/strong opioids should be avoided. Bed rest worsens deconditioning, pain, and fatigue; activity, started low and increased gradually, is therapeutic. Chronic corticosteroids are inappropriate (no inflammation) and harmful. Building the plan around graded exercise and education sets realistic expectations and addresses central sensitization, with medications as adjuncts.

stages.label: PHARMACOTHERAPY
stages.question: She needs adjunctive medication for pain, fatigue, and mood. Which agent is most appropriate, and which should be avoided?
options: Start chronic oxycodone; avoid exercise
Start an SNRI such as duloxetine (also covering her depressed mood); avoid escalating to opioids
Start chronic NSAIDs as monotherapy and avoid all antidepressants
Start a benzodiazepine daily and avoid duloxetine
stages.explanation: An SNRI such as duloxetine is FDA-approved for fibromyalgia, addresses both pain and depressed mood, and is preferred over opioids; pregabalin/gabapentin and milnacipran are other approved/effective options, and a low-dose TCA can aid sleep and pain. Opioids should be avoided for the reasons noted, and exercise should be encouraged, not stopped. NSAID monotherapy is largely ineffective for fibromyalgia's central pain and avoiding antidepressants forgoes the best-evidence pharmacotherapy. Daily benzodiazepines risk dependence, falls, and worsened cognition/depression without treating central pain. Duloxetine fits her dual needs while steering away from opioids.

cases.diagnosis: Fibromyalgia (chronic central sensitization syndrome) diagnosed by widespread pain index and symptom severity
pearls: Fibromyalgia is a clinical diagnosis using the Widespread Pain Index and Symptom Severity Scale over ≥3 months; tender-point counts are no longer required.
Normal ESR/CRP, TSH, CK, ANA, and RF are expected and help exclude inflammatory, thyroid, and autoimmune mimics—supporting, not refuting, the diagnosis.
Graded aerobic exercise, education, sleep optimization, and CBT are the evidence-based foundation; opioids and corticosteroids are not recommended.
Preferred medications include SNRIs (duloxetine, milnacipran) and gabapentinoids (pregabalin); a low-dose TCA can target sleep—choose agents that also address comorbid mood.

cases.title: Shoulder and Hip Stiffness That Lifts with a Pill
cases.chiefComplaint: She reports several weeks of severe stiffness and aching in both shoulders and hips, worst in the morning.
cases.history: A 72-year-old woman describes 6 weeks of bilateral aching and profound stiffness in her shoulders, neck, and hip girdles. Morning stiffness lasts more than an hour, and she struggles to rise from a chair, comb her hair, or roll over in bed. The onset was relatively abrupt. She has malaise, low-grade fatigue, and a few pounds of weight loss but no true muscle weakness when she pushes through the stiffness. She denies headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, or visual changes. PMH: hypertension, osteoarthritis of the knees. Meds: amlodipine 5 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social: retired librarian, widow, lives alone, independent until this illness, nonsmoker. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: no rash, no Raynaud, no dysphagia, no proximal weakness once warmed up, no recent infection. She is worried because over-the-counter analgesics have not helped and the stiffness is disabling.
cases.exam: Well-appearing older woman who moves stiffly and rises slowly from the chair. Painful, restricted active range of motion at both shoulders and hips due to stiffness/pain, but muscle strength is preserved (5/5) when tested gently—no true proximal weakness. No synovitis of the small joints, no swelling or warmth. No scalp tenderness, no temporal artery tenderness or nodularity, temporal pulses normal. Visual acuity and fundoscopic exam normal. No rash, no muscle atrophy. Neck range of motion limited by stiffness. Reflexes and sensation normal. No lymphadenopathy or thyromegaly.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An older woman with abrupt bilateral shoulder and hip-girdle pain and stiffness, prolonged morning stiffness, and preserved strength most likely has which condition?
options: Polymyositis
Polymyalgia rheumatica
Bilateral adhesive capsulitis
Fibromyalgia
stages.explanation: Age over 50 with new bilateral shoulder and pelvic-girdle pain and stiffness, morning stiffness lasting over 45–60 minutes, constitutional symptoms, and preserved strength (pain limits movement, not weakness) is the classic picture of polymyalgia rheumatica, typically with elevated inflammatory markers. Polymyositis causes objective proximal weakness with elevated CK, not just stiffness with intact strength. Bilateral adhesive capsulitis would not explain hip-girdle involvement, systemic symptoms, and elevated inflammatory markers. Fibromyalgia presents with chronic widespread pain, normal inflammatory markers, and prominent fatigue/sleep issues, usually in younger patients, without the abrupt girdle-limited pattern and high ESR seen here.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
ESR: 78 mm/hr (markedly elevated)
CRP: 6.4 mg/dL (elevated)
CBC: mild normocytic anemia, Hgb 11.4 g/dL
CK: 95 U/L (normal)
TSH: 2.4 mIU/L (normal); rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP: negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her ESR and CRP are markedly elevated with normal CK. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: High-dose corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone 40–60 mg) as for giant cell arteritis
Low-to-moderate dose oral corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone 15 mg daily) with planned taper
Methotrexate monotherapy as first-line
NSAIDs alone indefinitely
stages.explanation: Polymyalgia rheumatica responds dramatically to low-to-moderate dose corticosteroids—typically prednisone around 12.5–25 mg daily (often ~15 mg)—with a gradual taper guided by symptoms and inflammatory markers; a rapid, near-complete response supports the diagnosis. High-dose steroids (40–60 mg) are reserved for giant cell arteritis to protect vision, not uncomplicated PMR. Methotrexate is a steroid-sparing adjunct for relapsing or steroid-dependent disease, not routine first-line monotherapy. NSAIDs alone are inadequate for PMR's inflammatory burden and risk GI/renal harm in an older patient. Starting moderate-dose prednisone with a structured taper is standard.

stages.label: COMPLICATION SURVEILLANCE
stages.question: Which associated condition must you specifically warn her about and screen for at every visit because it can cause irreversible blindness?
options: Rheumatoid arthritis
Giant cell (temporal) arteritis
Systemic lupus erythematosus
Osteoarthritis flare
stages.explanation: PMR is closely linked to giant cell (temporal) arteritis; up to a notable fraction of PMR patients develop GCA, which can cause sudden, permanent vision loss. Counsel her to report immediately any new headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, or visual symptoms, and screen at each visit—suspected GCA warrants urgent high-dose steroids and temporal artery biopsy/imaging. Rheumatoid arthritis is in the differential for joint disease but does not threaten vision this way. Lupus is not the characteristic association with PMR and her serologies/picture do not suggest it. An osteoarthritis flare is not a vision-threatening systemic vasculitis. Vigilance for GCA is the key safety issue.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Given prolonged corticosteroid therapy in a 72-year-old woman, which preventive measure is most important to initiate?
options: Routine prophylactic antibiotics
Bone protection (calcium, vitamin D, and assessment for bisphosphonate) plus monitoring glucose and blood pressure
Permanent activity restriction and bed rest
Immediate taper off steroids within one week regardless of symptoms
stages.explanation: Long-term glucocorticoids in an older woman risk osteoporosis, hyperglycemia, hypertension, and infection; bone protection with calcium, vitamin D, and consideration of a bisphosphonate (glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis prophylaxis) plus monitoring of glucose and blood pressure is essential, alongside gentle exercise. Routine prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated. Bed rest worsens deconditioning, bone loss, and function—graded activity is encouraged. Tapering off within a week typically causes relapse; PMR usually needs months of slow taper guided by symptoms and inflammatory markers. Anticipating and preventing steroid complications protects this patient through her treatment course.

cases.diagnosis: Polymyalgia rheumatica (with vigilance for associated giant cell arteritis)
pearls: PMR: age >50, bilateral shoulder/hip-girdle pain with prolonged morning stiffness, preserved strength, and elevated ESR/CRP with normal CK.
It responds rapidly to low-to-moderate dose prednisone (~12.5–25 mg/day) with a slow taper; a dramatic response supports the diagnosis.
Always screen for giant cell arteritis (headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, visual changes)—GCA needs urgent high-dose steroids to prevent blindness.
Anticipate steroid complications: start bone protection (calcium, vitamin D, consider bisphosphonate) and monitor glucose and blood pressure.

cases.title: Regaining Strength After Paralysis That Climbed Up the Body
cases.chiefComplaint: He transfers to rehab profoundly weak and fatigued after an ICU stay for rapidly ascending paralysis.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man presents to inpatient rehab after a 3-week acute hospitalization. He developed ascending symmetric weakness and numbness beginning in the feet over several days about 2 weeks after a gastrointestinal illness, progressing to inability to walk and then respiratory failure requiring intubation and ICU care. He received IVIG. He has been extubated for one week, is now off the ventilator, and is medically stable but severely deconditioned and easily fatigued. He can move his arms against gravity but legs are weak; he cannot stand unassisted. PMH: hypertension, prediabetes. Meds: enoxaparin prophylaxis, gabapentin 300 mg TID for neuropathic pain, lisinopril, acetaminophen. Allergies: none. Social: accountant, married, two-story home, motivated to return to work and self-care. Family history: noncontributory. He reports tingling dysesthesias in hands and feet and worries about pushing too hard versus not hard enough in therapy. No dysphagia now; swallow cleared. No bowel/bladder retention currently.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative, fatigued man. Cranial nerves intact; mild residual bilateral facial weakness resolving; no current bulbar deficit, swallow safe. Motor: upper extremities 4-/5 proximally and distally; lower extremities 3/5 hip flexion, 2/5 ankle dorsiflexion, symmetric. Deep tendon reflexes absent throughout (areflexia). Sensation: distal-predominant decreased light touch and proprioception with dysesthesias. No spasticity, tone normal-to-low. Unable to stand without maximal assist; sits with support. No pressure injuries. Autonomic check: orthostatic symptoms with upright positioning; no current arrhythmia. Respiratory effort adequate at rest.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Symmetric ascending weakness with areflexia following a GI illness, progressing to respiratory failure, is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
Guillain-Barré syndrome (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy)
Myasthenia gravis
Acute spinal cord compression
stages.explanation: An ascending, symmetric flaccid weakness with areflexia developing days to weeks after a gastrointestinal or respiratory infection, sometimes progressing to respiratory failure, is classic Guillain-Barré syndrome; CSF shows albuminocytologic dissociation and treatment is IVIG or plasmapheresis. ALS is a progressive degenerative disease with mixed upper and lower motor neuron signs (including hyperreflexia and spasticity), not a monophasic ascending areflexic illness post-infection. Myasthenia gravis causes fatigable weakness with ptosis/diplopia and normal reflexes, not areflexia with sensory loss. Acute cord compression typically gives a sensory level, bowel/bladder dysfunction, and often hyperreflexia below the lesion, not symmetric ascending areflexic weakness.

stages.label: PRIOR WORKUP
stages.content:
CSF (acute admission): protein 110 mg/dL, WBC 3 /µL (albuminocytologic dissociation)
Nerve conduction studies: demyelinating polyneuropathy with conduction slowing/block
Vital capacity (current): improving, no longer requiring ventilatory support
Plateau of weakness reached; now in early recovery phase
Functional Independence Measure: low, maximal assist for mobility and transfers

stages.label: REHAB PRINCIPLE
stages.question: Designing his therapy program in the recovery phase of GBS, which principle is most important to avoid harming his recovering nerves and muscles?
options: Maximal high-intensity resistance training to rebuild strength as fast as possible
Submaximal, graded exercise that avoids overwork weakness and excessive fatigue, with adequate rest
Complete bed rest until strength fully returns
Eccentric maximal loading of the weakest muscles daily
stages.explanation: In GBS recovery, reinnervating and partially denervated muscles are vulnerable to overwork (overuse) weakness; therapy should be submaximal and graded, balancing activity with rest, monitoring for excessive fatigue or post-exertional weakness, and progressing as tolerated. High-intensity maximal resistance or daily eccentric maximal loading can paradoxically worsen weakness and damage recovering units. Complete bed rest causes deconditioning, contractures, pressure injuries, and DVT and is also harmful. The art of GBS rehab is pacing—enough activity to rebuild endurance and function without overworking fragile motor units.

stages.label: COMPLICATION PREVENTION
stages.question: He is immobile, has dysesthesias and orthostasis, and recently had respiratory failure. Which set of preventive measures is most appropriate?
options: Stop DVT prophylaxis since he is now stable
DVT prophylaxis, pressure-injury prevention/skin care, orthostatic precautions, neuropathic pain control, and pulmonary hygiene
Withhold neuropathic pain medication to avoid sedation
Immediate independent ambulation drills without assistance
stages.explanation: An immobile GBS patient needs multifaceted prevention: continued DVT prophylaxis (high thromboembolism risk while non-ambulatory), pressure-injury prevention with repositioning and skin checks, orthostatic precautions and gradual upright tolerance for dysautonomia, neuropathic pain control (gabapentinoids), and pulmonary hygiene given recent respiratory failure. Stopping prophylaxis prematurely invites clots while he remains immobile. Withholding pain control worsens function and participation; dysesthetic pain is common and treatable. Pushing unassisted ambulation in a man at 2–3/5 lower-limb strength risks falls and injury. Comprehensive preventive care underpins safe rehabilitation.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: His family asks about recovery. Which counseling statement is most accurate for GBS?
options: GBS is a progressive degenerative disease with no recovery expected
Most patients improve substantially over months, though recovery can be prolonged and some have residual deficits/fatigue
Full recovery always occurs within two weeks
Strength returns fastest with the most aggressive maximal exercise
stages.explanation: GBS is typically monophasic; most patients improve substantially over weeks to months, though recovery can be prolonged and a subset retain residual weakness, sensory symptoms, or fatigue—so realistic, hopeful counseling emphasizes gradual gains. It is not a relentlessly degenerative disease like ALS; meaningful recovery is the norm. Recovery rarely completes in two weeks—the timeline is months, sometimes longer. Aggressive maximal exercise does not speed recovery and risks overwork weakness; paced, graded rehabilitation optimizes outcomes. Setting expectations of slow but substantial improvement supports adherence and morale.

cases.diagnosis: Guillain-Barré syndrome (post-infectious AIDP) in the rehabilitation/recovery phase
pearls: GBS: ascending symmetric flaccid weakness with areflexia after an antecedent infection; CSF shows albuminocytologic dissociation; treat with IVIG or plasmapheresis (not steroids).
In rehab, use submaximal, graded exercise with rest to avoid overwork (overuse) weakness in reinnervating muscles—pacing is key.
Prevent complications of immobility: DVT prophylaxis, pressure-injury prevention, orthostatic/dysautonomia precautions, neuropathic pain control, and pulmonary hygiene.
Prognosis is generally favorable—substantial recovery over months—though some patients have residual deficits and fatigue.

cases.title: Worsening Hand Weakness with Twitching and Slurred Speech
cases.chiefComplaint: He reports progressive hand and arm weakness with muscle twitching and increasing trouble with speech and swallowing.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man has 10 months of painless, progressive weakness that began in the right hand (trouble with buttons and keys), spread to the left hand and forearms, and now involves the legs with foot drop and frequent tripping. He notes muscle twitching (fasciculations), cramps, and muscle wasting, plus new slurred speech and occasional choking on thin liquids. He has lost weight. There is no sensory loss, no pain, and no bowel or bladder dysfunction. PMH: hyperlipidemia. Meds: atorvastatin 20 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: retired machinist, married, lives in a two-story home, former smoker. Family history: no known neuromuscular disease. He has been told this is a serious neurologic condition and is at the multidisciplinary clinic for ongoing care. He reports breathlessness lying flat and morning headaches, and his wife notes he sleeps poorly and is fatigued during the day. He remains cognitively sharp but emotionally labile at times.
cases.exam: Alert man with dysarthric speech and a brisk gag and jaw jerk. Motor: asymmetric weakness with visible atrophy of intrinsic hand muscles and forearms, foot drop bilaterally; widespread fasciculations in arms, legs, and tongue. Tone increased in the legs with spasticity. Reflexes paradoxically brisk in wasted, weak limbs; positive Babinski bilaterally; brisk jaw jerk. Tongue shows atrophy and fasciculations. Sensation intact throughout. No ptosis, no fatigable ophthalmoplegia. Coordination limited by weakness. No sensory level. Cognition grossly intact; affect with pseudobulbar features (easy laughing/crying).

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Progressive painless weakness with both atrophy/fasciculations and hyperreflexia/spasticity/Babinski, plus bulbar signs and intact sensation, indicates which diagnosis?
options: Cervical myelopathy alone
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (combined upper and lower motor neuron disease)
Myasthenia gravis
Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy
stages.explanation: ALS is defined by combined upper motor neuron signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski, brisk jaw jerk) and lower motor neuron signs (atrophy, fasciculations, weakness) across multiple regions, with bulbar involvement and preserved sensation—exactly this picture. Cervical myelopathy can mimic UMN/LMN at one level but would not explain tongue fasciculations, bulbar dysarthria, and diffuse multi-region spread with normal imaging of a single level. Myasthenia gravis causes fatigable weakness with ocular/bulbar features and normal reflexes, no fasciculations or UMN signs. CIDP is a demyelinating polyneuropathy with sensory involvement and areflexia, not mixed UMN/LMN degeneration.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC DATA
stages.content:
EMG: active and chronic denervation with fasciculations in multiple regions (cervical, thoracic, lumbosacral, bulbar)
Nerve conduction: normal sensory responses; motor amplitudes reduced
MRI brain/cervical spine: no compressive lesion explaining findings
Forced vital capacity (FVC): 58% predicted, declining
Sniff nasal inspiratory pressure: reduced; nocturnal hypoventilation suspected

stages.label: RESPIRATORY MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has orthopnea, morning headaches, FVC 58% predicted, and signs of nocturnal hypoventilation. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure and recheck breathing in one year
Initiate non-invasive ventilation (NIV/BiPAP) for respiratory support and monitor respiratory function regularly
Proceed directly to tracheostomy and invasive ventilation now
Start high-flow oxygen alone as the sole intervention
stages.explanation: Symptoms of nocturnal hypoventilation (orthopnea, morning headaches, daytime fatigue) with declining FVC and reduced sniff pressure indicate respiratory muscle weakness; non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP) is the appropriate intervention—it improves survival and quality of life and is typically started around these thresholds (e.g., FVC <50%, symptomatic hypoventilation, or abnormal sniff/nocturnal oximetry). Waiting a year ignores progressive, life-threatening decline and the need for serial monitoring. Tracheostomy/invasive ventilation is a later, goals-of-care–dependent decision, not the immediate next step. Oxygen alone can worsen hypercapnia in neuromuscular respiratory failure and does not address hypoventilation—ventilatory support (NIV) is needed.

stages.label: MULTIDISCIPLINARY CARE
stages.question: Given his dysarthria, dysphagia with choking, weight loss, and progressive limb weakness, which combination best reflects appropriate multidisciplinary management?
options: Aggressive resistance exercise to reverse weakness and no swallowing evaluation
Speech therapy/AAC for communication, swallow evaluation with nutritional support (consider PEG), assistive technology and adaptive equipment, and disease-modifying therapy (riluzole)
Opioids and sedatives only, with no other interventions
Immediate nursing-home placement with no therapy services
stages.explanation: ALS care is multidisciplinary and supportive: speech-language therapy and augmentative/alternative communication (AAC) for dysarthria, swallow evaluation with nutritional optimization and timely gastrostomy (PEG) consideration before respiratory decline, assistive technology/adaptive equipment and mobility aids for function, and disease-modifying therapy such as riluzole (and edaravone where appropriate) plus symptom management. Aggressive resistance exercise cannot reverse motor neuron loss and may cause overwork weakness; skipping swallow assessment risks aspiration and malnutrition. Opioids/sedatives have a role in end-of-life symptom control but are not the whole plan. Reflexively placing him in a nursing home without services abandons quality-of-life–enhancing interventions.

stages.label: GOALS OF CARE
stages.question: Which proactive step is most important in this incurable, progressive disease to honor his autonomy?
options: Avoid discussing prognosis to maintain hope
Early advance care planning and goals-of-care discussions, including ventilation and feeding preferences, with palliative care involvement
Defer all decisions until a respiratory crisis occurs
Mandate tracheostomy regardless of his wishes
stages.explanation: Because ALS is progressive and incurable, early advance care planning—clarifying his wishes about NIV, tracheostomy, gastrostomy, and end-of-life care—while he can communicate clearly, with palliative care integrated alongside disease-directed therapy, best honors autonomy and reduces crisis-driven decisions. Avoiding prognosis discussions deprives him of informed choice. Deferring until a respiratory crisis forces rushed decisions under duress, often misaligned with his values. Mandating any intervention regardless of wishes violates autonomy. Timely, compassionate goals-of-care conversations are a cornerstone of high-quality ALS care.

cases.diagnosis: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (combined upper and lower motor neuron degeneration)
pearls: ALS combines UMN signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski, brisk jaw jerk) and LMN signs (atrophy, fasciculations) across regions with preserved sensation; EMG shows widespread denervation.
Monitor respiration serially (FVC, sniff nasal pressure); start non-invasive ventilation for symptomatic hypoventilation/declining FVC—NIV improves survival and quality of life; avoid oxygen alone.
Manage multidisciplinary: SLP/AAC, swallow evaluation with timely PEG, assistive technology, riluzole (±edaravone), and symptom control; avoid overwork weakness from aggressive resistance training.
Begin advance care planning early—ventilation/feeding preferences and palliative care—while communication is intact.

cases.title: Heat-Triggered Leg Weakness and Crushing Fatigue
cases.chiefComplaint: She reports stiff, weak legs that worsen with heat and exercise, plus overwhelming daily fatigue.
cases.history: A 35-year-old woman with a known relapsing-remitting demyelinating CNS disorder (diagnosed 4 years ago) reports gradually worsening leg stiffness with spasms, a spastic 'catching' gait, and fatigue that dominates her day. She notes that hot showers, exercise, or a warm day transiently worsen her vision and leg weakness, which recover when she cools down. She has urinary urgency. Her last clinical relapse was 14 months ago; she is neurologically stable on disease-modifying therapy. PMH: relapsing-remitting MS, depression. Meds: a disease-modifying agent (injectable), vitamin D, sertraline 50 mg daily, occasional baclofen 10 mg at night. Allergies: none. Social: occupational therapist (now part-time due to fatigue), married, lives in a two-story home, nonsmoker. Family history: aunt with MS. Review of systems: no current acute focal deficit suggesting new relapse, no fever, no dysuria/infection. She wants strategies to walk better, manage spasticity, and combat fatigue without sedation that worsens her function.
cases.exam: Alert, pleasant woman. Cranial nerves: subtle bilateral internuclear ophthalmoplegia-type slowing, otherwise intact; vision baseline at rest. Motor: lower-extremity spasticity with increased tone, hyperreflexia at knees/ankles, bilateral ankle clonus, and extensor plantar responses; strength 4/5 in hip flexors and dorsiflexors. Spastic, slightly scissoring gait with reduced foot clearance; uses no device but reports stumbling. Upper extremities near normal. Sensation: mildly decreased vibration in the feet. Coordination intact in arms. No current acute relapse signs. Fatigue evident with sustained exertion during exam.

stages.label: SYMPTOM RECOGNITION
stages.question: Her transient worsening of vision and leg weakness with heat or exercise, reversing on cooling, is best explained by which phenomenon?
options: A new MS relapse requiring high-dose steroids
Uhthoff phenomenon—temporary worsening of symptoms with increased body temperature
Urinary tract infection causing pseudo-relapse
A stroke
stages.explanation: Uhthoff phenomenon is the transient, reversible worsening of MS symptoms when core temperature rises (hot showers, exercise, fever, hot weather); demyelinated axons conduct less reliably when warm, and symptoms recover with cooling. It is not a true relapse, so it does not by itself warrant steroids—management is temperature control and cooling strategies. A true relapse causes new or worsening deficits lasting >24 hours not tied to heat. A UTI can cause a pseudo-relapse via fever/infection, but the hallmark here is heat-triggered, rapidly reversible symptoms without infection signs. A stroke produces sudden, persistent focal deficits, not heat-dependent fluctuation.

stages.label: FATIGUE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her dominant complaint is disabling fatigue. Which is the most appropriate first-line rehabilitation approach?
options: Strict rest and avoidance of all activity
Energy conservation/activity pacing, cooling strategies, exercise as tolerated, and addressing sleep/mood
Daily high-dose stimulants as first-line for everyone
Bed rest plus a sedating muscle relaxant during the day
stages.explanation: MS-related fatigue is best managed first with rehabilitation strategies: energy conservation and activity pacing (the 4 P's—prioritize, plan, pace, position), cooling strategies to counter heat sensitivity, regular exercise as tolerated (which reduces fatigue and improves function), and treating contributors like poor sleep, depression, and medication side effects. Strict rest worsens deconditioning and fatigue. Stimulant medications (e.g., for refractory fatigue) are adjuncts after nonpharmacologic measures and treatment of contributors, not a blanket first-line for everyone. Daytime sedating muscle relaxants would worsen fatigue. A structured, conservative, multimodal plan addresses fatigue most effectively.

stages.label: FUNCTIONAL ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Modified Ashworth Scale: lower-extremity tone 2-3 (hip adductors, gastrocsoleus)
Timed 25-foot walk: prolonged, with reduced foot clearance
Modified Fatigue Impact Scale: high (physical and cognitive fatigue)
Bladder: post-void residual mildly elevated; urinalysis negative for infection
MRI: stable demyelinating lesions, no new enhancing lesion

stages.label: SPASTICITY MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her lower-limb spasticity impairs gait. Which management approach is most appropriate?
options: High-dose daytime baclofen sufficient to abolish all tone, regardless of weakness or sedation
Stretching and physical therapy plus titrated antispasticity medication (e.g., baclofen or tizanidine), with focal botulinum toxin for problematic muscles
No treatment, since spasticity is never functionally significant
Immediate intrathecal baclofen pump for this ambulatory patient
stages.explanation: MS spasticity is managed with a stepwise, multimodal plan: regular stretching and physical therapy, plus oral antispasticity agents (baclofen, tizanidine) titrated to balance tone reduction against weakness and sedation, with focal botulinum toxin for specific overactive muscles limiting gait. Abolishing all tone with high-dose daytime baclofen can unmask weakness and cause sedation, worsening function—some tone may aid standing. Ignoring spasticity is wrong when it impairs gait and causes spasms. An intrathecal baclofen pump is reserved for severe, refractory spasticity not controlled by oral/focal measures, not first-line for this ambulatory patient. Treat to function, not to zero tone.

stages.label: GAIT AND SAFETY
stages.question: She stumbles from poor foot clearance and reports near-falls. Which intervention most directly improves her gait safety?
options: Advise her to walk faster to compensate
Ankle-foot orthosis (or functional electrical stimulation) for foot drop, gait training, and fall-prevention strategies
Restrict her to a wheelchair permanently
Increase sedating medications to relax the legs
stages.explanation: Reduced foot clearance/foot drop is directly addressed with an ankle-foot orthosis or functional electrical stimulation to dorsiflex the foot, combined with gait training, balance work, an appropriate assistive device if needed, and home fall-prevention strategies. Telling her to walk faster increases fall risk. Permanent wheelchair use needlessly sacrifices her ambulation and worsens deconditioning. Increasing sedating medications heightens fall risk and fatigue. Targeted orthotic/FES support plus therapy and environmental modification maximizes safe mobility and independence while respecting her energy limitations.

cases.diagnosis: Multiple sclerosis with lower-limb spasticity, fatigue, and heat sensitivity (Uhthoff phenomenon)
pearls: Uhthoff phenomenon is reversible, heat-triggered symptom worsening—not a true relapse; manage with cooling, not steroids.
MS fatigue is treated first with energy conservation/pacing (the 4 P's), cooling, exercise as tolerated, and addressing sleep/mood; stimulants are adjuncts.

Treat spasticity to function (stretching/PT, titrated baclofen or tizanidine, focal botulinum toxin)—avoid abolishing all tone, which can unmask weakness; reserve intrathecal baclofen for refractory cases.
For foot drop, use an AFO or functional electrical stimulation with gait training and fall-prevention strategies.

cases.title: Frozen Feet at the Doorway and Repeated Falls
cases.chiefComplaint: He reports his feet 'freeze' when starting to walk or turning, leading to several falls.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man with a 6-year history of a progressive movement disorder reports that his feet feel 'glued to the floor' when he initiates walking, turns, or approaches doorways, after which he takes short shuffling steps and sometimes falls forward. He has had three falls in two months, one with a wrist contusion. He also notes a resting tremor in the right hand, slowness, stiffness, smaller handwriting, and a soft voice. Symptoms are worse when his medication wears off. PMH: the known movement disorder, hypertension, mild orthostatic symptoms. Meds: carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 three times daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: retired teacher, married, lives in a home with throw rugs and steps, nonsmoker. Family history: noncontributory. He has reduced his activity for fear of falling and is becoming deconditioned. No dementia; mild cognitive slowing only. He wants to reduce falls and improve walking and is asking about specialized exercise programs and 'tricks' to get unstuck.

vitals.BP: 138/82 mmHg (drops to 120/76 standing with mild dizziness)

cases.exam: Alert man with hypomimia and soft, hypophonic speech. Resting 'pill-rolling' tremor of the right hand, improving with action. Cogwheel rigidity at the wrists, bradykinesia on finger taps (decrementing amplitude). Gait: reduced arm swing, stooped posture, short shuffling steps, start hesitation and freezing on turns and at the doorway, with festination; positive pull test (retropulsion). No cerebellar signs, no nystagmus, no early severe autonomic failure beyond mild orthostasis, normal eye movements (no vertical gaze palsy). Strength and sensation intact. Reflexes normal. Cognition grossly intact.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, and asymmetric onset responsive to levodopa, with freezing of gait, are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Essential tremor
Idiopathic Parkinson disease
Normal-pressure hydrocephalus
Progressive supranuclear palsy
stages.explanation: The cardinal features—asymmetric resting (pill-rolling) tremor, bradykinesia with decrementing amplitude, cogwheel rigidity, postural instability, and a levodopa response with motor fluctuations—define idiopathic Parkinson disease; freezing of gait is a common, fall-prone feature. Essential tremor is an action/postural tremor without bradykinesia, rigidity, or levodopa response. Normal-pressure hydrocephalus causes a magnetic gait with urinary incontinence and cognitive decline but lacks resting tremor and asymmetric parkinsonism with this levodopa pattern. Progressive supranuclear palsy features early falls, axial rigidity, and vertical supranuclear gaze palsy with poor levodopa response—his eye movements are normal and he responds to levodopa, favoring Parkinson disease.

stages.label: REHAB INTERVENTION
stages.question: He asks about a specialized exercise program shown to help Parkinson motor function. Which is the most appropriate referral?
options: LSVT BIG (high-amplitude movement training) physical/occupational therapy
Generic gentle stretching only, with no amplitude focus
Bed rest to conserve energy
Aquatic therapy contraindicated in Parkinson disease
stages.explanation: LSVT BIG is an evidence-based, amplitude-focused therapy that trains large-amplitude movements to counter the hypokinesia/bradykinesia of Parkinson disease, improving gait, balance, and functional mobility (its companion LSVT LOUD targets hypophonia). Generic gentle stretching alone lacks the intensive amplitude-driven retraining that benefits parkinsonian movement. Bed rest worsens deconditioning, freezing, and fall risk—activity and structured exercise are therapeutic. Aquatic therapy is not contraindicated and can help balance for some patients; the statement is false. Referring him to LSVT BIG directly targets his bradykinetic, small-amplitude gait and is the best-supported choice.

stages.label: FUNCTIONAL ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Timed Up and Go: prolonged, with freezing on turns
Pull test: positive (retropulsion, postural instability)
Freezing of Gait Questionnaire: high score, triggered by turns and doorways
Falls: 3 in 2 months, one with injury
Home hazards: throw rugs, poor lighting, cluttered pathways

stages.label: FREEZING STRATEGY
stages.question: To help him 'unstick' during freezing episodes, which strategy is most effective?
options: Tell him to try harder and push through with willpower
External cueing—visual cues (lines on the floor) and auditory/rhythmic cues (a metronome or counting) to initiate and pace steps
Increase walking speed immediately when frozen
Avoid all turns and doorways permanently
stages.explanation: Freezing of gait responds to external cueing strategies that bypass dysfunctional internal movement generation: visual cues such as stepping over a line or laser pointer on the floor, and auditory/rhythmic cues like a metronome, counting, or marching to a beat, help initiate and pace stepping. 'Trying harder' is ineffective and can increase anxiety and freezing. Suddenly increasing speed when frozen promotes festination and falls. Avoiding all turns and doorways is impractical and isolating; instead, teach cueing and weight-shift techniques to navigate them. Cueing plus gait training and medication optimization reduces freezing and falls.

stages.label: FALL PREVENTION
stages.question: Given recurrent falls and mild orthostatic hypotension, which combination best reduces his fall risk?
options: Add a sedative-hypnotic nightly and remove no home hazards
Home safety modifications (remove throw rugs, improve lighting), gait/balance training and assistive device, orthostatic precautions, and optimizing levodopa timing for 'off' periods
Restrict him to bed to prevent falls
Stop all Parkinson medications to reduce dizziness
stages.explanation: Comprehensive fall prevention combines home safety modifications (removing throw rugs, improving lighting, clearing clutter), gait and balance training with an appropriate assistive device, orthostatic precautions (slow position changes, hydration, review of antihypertensives) for his mild orthostasis, and optimizing levodopa timing/dosing to minimize 'off'-period freezing. Adding a sedative-hypnotic increases falls and confusion. Bed rest causes deconditioning and paradoxically raises fall risk on transfers. Stopping Parkinson medication worsens bradykinesia, rigidity, and freezing, increasing falls. A multifactorial strategy targeting both the disease and the environment most effectively reduces his falls.

cases.diagnosis: Idiopathic Parkinson disease with freezing of gait and recurrent falls
pearls: Parkinson disease: asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia (decrementing amplitude), rigidity, postural instability, and a clear levodopa response; freezing of gait drives falls.
LSVT BIG (amplitude-based PT/OT) improves parkinsonian movement and gait; LSVT LOUD targets hypophonia—activity is therapeutic, not bed rest.
External cueing—visual floor lines and auditory/rhythmic cues (metronome, counting)—helps overcome freezing; 'trying harder' and speeding up worsen it.
Fall prevention is multifactorial: home hazard removal, gait/balance training and devices, orthostatic precautions, and optimizing levodopa timing for 'off' periods.

cases.title: New Leg Weakness in a Childhood Survivor
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive weakness and fatigue in her right leg over the past two years.
cases.history: A 66-year-old retired teacher presents with insidious new weakness in her right leg and generalized fatigue developing over two years. She contracted paralytic poliomyelitis at age 5, with right leg involvement requiring leg braces in childhood; she recovered substantial strength by adolescence and walked independently for decades, even hiking regularly into her 50s. Over the last two years she notices her right knee 'giving way,' new aching in previously stable muscles, and exhaustion after activities she once tolerated. She also reports new cold intolerance in the affected limb and difficulty climbing stairs. She denies bowel or bladder changes, sensory loss, or neck pain. PMH: hypertension on lisinopril 20 mg daily, osteoarthritis. No diabetes. Medications also include acetaminophen as needed. Allergies: none. Social: lives with her husband, former smoker (quit 20 years ago), no alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. She is frustrated because she has been pushing herself harder at the gym to 'fight' the weakness, which seems to worsen her symptoms.
cases.exam: Alert, well-appearing. Right lower extremity shows atrophy of the quadriceps and calf with a shortened limb and pes cavus. Manual muscle testing: right knee extension 4-/5, right ankle dorsiflexion 4/5, hip flexion 4/5; left leg 5/5. Diffuse fasciculations noted in the right thigh. Deep tendon reflexes diminished at the right knee and ankle, normal on the left. Sensation intact to light touch, pinprick, and vibration throughout. No spasticity, no Hoffmann or Babinski sign. Gait: antalgic with right knee hyperextension and a Trendelenburg pattern. No tenderness over the lumbar spine.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation most strongly suggests post-polio syndrome rather than a new neurologic disease?
options: Symmetric distal sensory loss in a stocking distribution
New weakness and fatigue decades after stable recovery from poliomyelitis, in previously affected muscle groups
Upper motor neuron signs with hyperreflexia and Babinski
Acute onset of weakness over 48 hours with areflexia
stages.explanation: Post-polio syndrome (PPS) is defined by new, gradually progressive muscle weakness and fatigue appearing decades after a stable period following acute paralytic polio, typically in previously affected or seemingly spared motor units. This patient's two-year decline after decades of stability fits perfectly. Symmetric stocking sensory loss (wrong) points to a length-dependent polyneuropathy, not PPS, which spares sensation. Upper motor neuron signs with Babinski (wrong) indicate a central/myelopathic process, not the lower motor neuron picture of PPS. Acute weakness with areflexia over 48 hours (wrong) suggests Guillain-Barre syndrome, which is acute, not the slowly progressive course here. PPS is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion built on this characteristic history.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which study is most useful to support the diagnosis and exclude alternative causes of her new weakness?
options: Lumbar spine MRI as the single confirmatory test
Muscle biopsy to confirm post-polio syndrome
Electromyography showing chronic neurogenic changes with new active denervation, plus labs to exclude mimics
Repetitive nerve stimulation looking for a decremental response
stages.explanation: PPS is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion; EMG is the key supportive study, showing chronic neurogenic changes (large, long-duration motor units from prior reinnervation) often with new active denervation reflecting ongoing motor unit degeneration. Labs (TSH, CK, glucose, B12) help exclude mimics. Lumbar MRI alone (wrong) may show incidental degenerative change and cannot confirm PPS, though it helps if radiculopathy is suspected. Muscle biopsy (wrong) is nonspecific and not required. Repetitive nerve stimulation (wrong) screens for neuromuscular junction disorders like myasthenia, not PPS. EMG distinguishes PPS from ALS, radiculopathy, and compressive neuropathies.

stages.label: STUDIES
stages.content:
EMG/NCS: Chronic neurogenic changes in right L2-S1 myotomes with large-amplitude, long-duration, polyphasic motor units; reduced recruitment
Scattered fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves in right quadriceps and gastrocnemius (active denervation)
Left lower extremity: mild chronic neurogenic changes, no active denervation
Sensory nerve conduction studies: normal
No decremental response on repetitive stimulation
TSH 2.1 mIU/L | CK 142 U/L | B12 480 pg/mL | HbA1c 5.4%
Lumbar MRI: mild multilevel spondylosis, no significant stenosis or nerve root compression

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She insists that intensive resistance training will rebuild her strength. What is the most appropriate exercise counseling?
options: Recommend high-intensity, high-repetition resistance training to maximal fatigue
Advise complete rest and avoidance of all physical activity
Prescribe non-fatiguing, paced exercise with adequate rest, energy conservation, and activity pacing
Refer for immediate surgical tendon transfer to restore strength
stages.explanation: In PPS, surviving motor units are already overworked; overuse and exercising to exhaustion accelerate motor unit degeneration and worsen weakness. The cornerstone is non-fatiguing, submaximal exercise with frequent rest, energy conservation, and activity pacing to avoid overuse injury. High-intensity training to maximal fatigue (wrong) is exactly what harms these patients and likely explains her decline. Complete rest (wrong) causes disuse atrophy and deconditioning, also detrimental. Surgical tendon transfer (wrong) is not a primary treatment and ignores the underlying motor neuron pathology. Pacing, weight management, and pain control form the conservative backbone of PPS care.

stages.label: REHABILITATION
stages.question: Regarding her bracing, she stopped using a brace decades ago. What is the best orthotic recommendation now?
options: Continue without any orthosis since she managed without one for years
Reassess and prescribe an appropriate orthosis (e.g., updated KAFO or AFO) to stabilize the knee/ankle and reduce energy expenditure
Recommend a rigid full-leg cast for immobilization
Prescribe a wheelchair as the first-line mobility solution
stages.explanation: Bracing should be reassessed in PPS as new weakness emerges. A lightweight, modern KAFO or AFO can stabilize her hyperextending knee and weak ankle, prevent falls, reduce energy cost of walking, and protect overworked muscles, all aligned with energy conservation. Older heavy steel braces are often replaced with lighter carbon-fiber designs that patients tolerate better. Continuing without an orthosis (wrong) ignores her knee instability and fall risk. A rigid full-leg cast (wrong) immobilizes unnecessarily and causes disuse atrophy. A wheelchair as first-line (wrong) is premature; mobility aids and bracing should preserve ambulation before resorting to a wheelchair, which is reserved for advanced disability.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: She develops new daytime sleepiness, morning headaches, and orthopnea. What complication of post-polio syndrome should be evaluated?
options: Diabetic ketoacidosis
Sleep-disordered breathing and respiratory muscle weakness causing nocturnal hypoventilation
Acute coronary syndrome
Normal-pressure hydrocephalus
stages.explanation: Patients with prior bulbar or respiratory polio involvement are at risk for late respiratory muscle weakness and sleep-disordered breathing. Morning headaches, daytime hypersomnolence, and orthopnea are classic for nocturnal hypoventilation; evaluation includes pulmonary function testing, overnight oximetry/polysomnography, and consideration of noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation. DKA (wrong) is unlikely given her normal HbA1c and absence of diabetes. Acute coronary syndrome (wrong) would present with chest pain or dyspnea on exertion, not this constellation. NPH (wrong) causes the triad of gait apraxia, dementia, and incontinence, not orthopnea and morning headaches. Recognizing respiratory involvement in PPS is high-yield and potentially life-saving.

cases.diagnosis: Post-polio syndrome
pearls: Post-polio syndrome is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion: new progressive weakness/fatigue decades after stable recovery from paralytic polio, with sensation spared.
EMG shows chronic neurogenic motor units (prior reinnervation) often with new active denervation; use labs and imaging to exclude mimics like ALS and radiculopathy.
Management centers on non-fatiguing paced exercise, energy conservation, weight control, and reassessing orthotics; avoid exercising to exhaustion which accelerates motor unit loss.
Screen for late respiratory muscle weakness and sleep-disordered breathing, especially with prior bulbar/respiratory involvement.

cases.title: Drooping Eyelids That Worsen by Evening
cases.chiefComplaint: Intermittent double vision and droopy eyelids that worsen as the day goes on.
cases.history: A 29-year-old graphic designer reports three months of fluctuating double vision and bilateral eyelid drooping. Symptoms are minimal on waking but worsen progressively through the day and after prolonged screen work, improving with rest. Over the past month she also notices fatigue with chewing toward the end of meals and her voice becoming nasal during long conversations. She denies limb weakness at rest but feels her arms tire when blow-drying her hair. No pain, numbness, headache, or bowel/bladder symptoms. No recent infection. PMH: hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Medications: levothyroxine; no recent antibiotics. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, occasional wine, works long hours at a computer. Family history: mother has autoimmune thyroid disease; aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. Review of systems notable for the fatigable pattern of all complaints. She is anxious that her symptoms fluctuate so much that physicians have questioned whether they are real.
cases.exam: Alert, comfortable. Bilateral ptosis worse on the right, increasing after 60 seconds of sustained upgaze. Extraocular movements show fatigable diplopia on lateral gaze; pupils equal and reactive. Cogan lid twitch present. Facial weakness with reduced cheek puff; nasal voice after counting aloud. Neck flexion 4/5. Proximal limb strength initially 5/5 but declines to 4/5 after repetitive shoulder abduction. Deep tendon reflexes normal and symmetric. Sensation intact throughout. No fasciculations, no muscle atrophy, no rash. Bedside ice-pack test over the right lid improves ptosis. Respiratory effort comfortable, single-breath count 35.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The fatigable, fluctuating ptosis and diplopia that worsen with use and improve with rest localize the lesion to which structure?
options: Anterior horn cell (motor neuron)
Neuromuscular junction (postsynaptic)
Dorsal root ganglion (sensory neuron)
Corticospinal tract
stages.explanation: Fatigable weakness that worsens with sustained activity and recovers with rest, with normal sensation and reflexes, is the hallmark of a neuromuscular junction disorder, and the postsynaptic acetylcholine receptor is the target in myasthenia gravis. Ocular and bulbar predominance with the Cogan lid twitch and ice-pack response further support the NMJ. Anterior horn cell disease (wrong) causes fixed weakness with atrophy and fasciculations, not fatigability or ptosis. Dorsal root ganglion involvement (wrong) produces sensory loss, absent here. Corticospinal tract lesions (wrong) cause upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, spasticity, Babinski), which are absent. The pattern is classic for postsynaptic NMJ failure.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of tests is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Acetylcholine receptor antibodies and repetitive nerve stimulation (with single-fiber EMG if needed)
Muscle biopsy and serum aldolase
MRI brain with contrast and lumbar puncture
Nerve conduction studies showing conduction block across the elbow
stages.explanation: Acetylcholine receptor (AChR) antibodies are highly specific for myasthenia gravis and positive in most generalized cases; repetitive nerve stimulation showing a decremental response (and single-fiber EMG with increased jitter if RNS is negative) confirms NMJ dysfunction. Muscle biopsy and aldolase (wrong) evaluate myopathy, not NMJ disease. Brain MRI and LP (wrong) target central or inflammatory CNS processes, not the fatigable peripheral pattern here. Conduction block across the elbow (wrong) describes a focal compressive neuropathy like cubital tunnel, unrelated to this generalized fatigable picture. If AChR antibodies are negative, test for MuSK antibodies. A chest CT to evaluate for thymoma is also indicated once MG is confirmed.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS
stages.content:
Acetylcholine receptor (AChR) binding antibodies: 6.8 nmol/L (elevated; normal <0.5)
MuSK antibodies: negative
Repetitive nerve stimulation (3 Hz, right facial and spinal accessory): 18% decrement in CMAP amplitude (abnormal; >10%)
Single-fiber EMG (orbicularis oculi): increased jitter with blocking
TSH 2.4 mIU/L | normal CK
Chest CT with contrast: 3.5 cm anterior mediastinal mass suggestive of thymoma
Bedside spirometry: FVC within normal limits, NIF adequate

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond starting symptomatic therapy, what additional management is indicated given the imaging finding?
options: Observation of the mediastinal mass with repeat CT in one year
Surgical thymectomy for the thymoma
Radiation alone without surgery as first-line for the mass
Immediate plasma exchange and ICU admission
stages.explanation: An anterior mediastinal mass consistent with thymoma is an indication for thymectomy in myasthenia gravis; resection removes the neoplasm and may improve myasthenic control. Pyridostigmine provides symptomatic relief and immunotherapy (steroids, steroid-sparing agents) addresses the autoimmune process, but the thymoma itself mandates surgery. Observation with repeat imaging (wrong) is inappropriate for a resectable thymoma. Radiation alone (wrong) is not first-line for an operable thymoma; surgery is preferred. Immediate plasma exchange and ICU admission (wrong) are reserved for myasthenic crisis with respiratory compromise, which she does not have (normal FVC/NIF). Thymectomy is also considered in many generalized AChR-positive patients even without thymoma.

stages.label: TREATMENT PITFALL
stages.question: Which medication should be used cautiously or avoided because it can precipitate a myasthenic exacerbation?
options: Acetaminophen
Levothyroxine
Intravenous magnesium and fluoroquinolone or aminoglycoside antibiotics
Vitamin D supplementation
stages.explanation: Magnesium impairs presynaptic acetylcholine release and can dramatically worsen neuromuscular transmission; fluoroquinolones and aminoglycosides also impair NMJ function and can trigger exacerbation or crisis. These should be avoided or used with great caution in myasthenia. Acetaminophen (wrong) is safe. Levothyroxine (wrong) treats her hypothyroidism and is not a recognized myasthenia trigger; uncontrolled thyroid disease can affect symptoms, so it should be optimized. Vitamin D (wrong) is benign. Other agents to use cautiously include beta-blockers, certain anesthetics, and macrolides. Knowing the list of MG-aggravating drugs prevents iatrogenic crises and is high-yield for boards.

stages.label: REHABILITATION
stages.question: What activity and lifestyle counseling best fits her fatigable disease?
options: Encourage maximal-effort endurance training until exhaustion to build stamina
Recommend energy conservation, activity pacing, scheduled rest breaks, and avoiding heat and overexertion
Advise strict bed rest and avoidance of all activity indefinitely
Tell her symptoms are psychogenic and require no accommodation
stages.explanation: Because strength declines with sustained activity, patients with myasthenia benefit from energy conservation, activity pacing, scheduled rest breaks, and avoiding triggers like heat, stress, infection, and overexertion that worsen fatigability. Planning demanding tasks for the morning when strength is best is helpful. Maximal endurance training to exhaustion (wrong) provokes the very fatigue that defines the disease. Strict indefinite bed rest (wrong) causes deconditioning; gentle, paced activity is preferable. Dismissing symptoms as psychogenic (wrong) is wrong and harmful, especially given her objective antibody, RNS, and imaging findings. Pacing plus appropriate immunotherapy optimizes function and quality of life.

cases.diagnosis: Myasthenia gravis
pearls: Fatigable, fluctuating ptosis/diplopia and bulbar weakness that worsen with use and improve with rest, with normal sensation and reflexes, localize to the postsynaptic neuromuscular junction.
Confirm with AChR antibodies and repetitive nerve stimulation (decrement >10%); single-fiber EMG (increased jitter) is most sensitive; always image the chest for thymoma.
Thymectomy is indicated for thymoma; pyridostigmine plus immunotherapy controls symptoms; watch for and treat myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure).
Avoid NMJ-impairing drugs: magnesium, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, and certain others; counsel energy conservation and trigger avoidance.

cases.title: Tripping Over His Own Feet for Years
