cases.chiefComplaint: Frequent tripping and ankle sprains with longstanding foot deformities.
cases.history: A 23-year-old graduate student reports years of frequent tripping, recurrent ankle sprains, and difficulty running. He has always been 'clumsy' and was the slowest runner in gym class. Over the past few years he notices his feet slapping the ground when walking and trouble feeling the pedals while driving. He has high-arched feet and curled toes that he assumed were inherited 'family feet.' No back pain, bowel/bladder changes, or symptoms suggesting a stroke. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, social alcohol, no recreational drugs, no exposure to toxins. Family history is striking: his father wears leg braces and has 'weak ankles,' and a paternal aunt has similar high arches and walking problems; the pattern spans generations. Review of systems notable for hand clumsiness with buttons and zippers developing more recently. He is worried about whether he can pass this condition to future children.
cases.exam: Slender build. Bilateral pes cavus with hammertoes and an 'inverted champagne bottle' appearance of the lower legs from distal calf atrophy. Distal weakness: ankle dorsiflexion and eversion 3/5, plantarflexion 4/5, intrinsic hand muscles 4/5; proximal strength normal. Bilateral foot drop with a steppage gait. Deep tendon reflexes absent at the ankles and reduced at the knees; upper extremity reflexes diminished. Distal sensory loss to vibration and proprioception in a length-dependent pattern; pinprick mildly reduced in the feet. Palpable enlarged peroneal nerves. No spasticity, no Babinski. Romberg positive. No scoliosis tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of distal symmetric weakness, pes cavus, hammertoes, areflexia, length-dependent sensory loss, and a multigenerational family history most suggests what category of disorder?
options: An acquired demyelinating polyneuropathy (e.g., CIDP)
A hereditary (inherited) sensorimotor polyneuropathy
A lumbosacral polyradiculopathy from spinal stenosis
An upper motor neuron disorder of the spinal cord
stages.explanation: Slowly progressive, distal, symmetric sensorimotor deficits with skeletal foot deformities (pes cavus, hammertoes), distal atrophy, areflexia, and a clear multigenerational inheritance pattern point to a hereditary sensorimotor polyneuropathy (Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease). The foot deformities reflect chronic in utero/childhood muscle imbalance, a hallmark of inherited neuropathy. CIDP (wrong) is acquired, progresses over weeks-months, and lacks lifelong foot deformities and family history. Lumbosacral polyradiculopathy from stenosis (wrong) is uncommon at 23, causes neurogenic claudication and back/leg pain, and would not produce symmetric childhood-onset deformities. An upper motor neuron disorder (wrong) causes hyperreflexia and spasticity, opposite to this areflexic, atrophic picture.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Nerve conduction studies are obtained. Which finding would best support the most common (demyelinating) form of this hereditary neuropathy?
options: Normal conduction velocities with reduced sensory amplitudes only
Uniformly and markedly slowed conduction velocities in a symmetric pattern
Conduction block localized across the fibular head
Decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation
stages.explanation: The most common form, CMT1 (demyelinating), shows uniformly and markedly slowed nerve conduction velocities (often <38 m/s in the arms) symmetrically, reflecting diffuse hereditary demyelination, frequently with palpable hypertrophic nerves as seen here. Normal velocities with isolated sensory amplitude reduction (wrong) suggests an axonal sensory neuropathy or ganglionopathy, not the classic demyelinating CMT1. Focal conduction block across the fibular head (wrong) describes acquired peroneal compression, not a diffuse hereditary process. A decremental response on repetitive stimulation (wrong) indicates a neuromuscular junction disorder, not a polyneuropathy. Genetic testing (e.g., PMP22 duplication for CMT1A) confirms the diagnosis.

stages.label: STUDIES
stages.content:
NCS: Symmetric, uniform marked slowing of motor conduction velocities (median motor 24 m/s; peroneal 22 m/s)
Reduced CMmP and SNAP amplitudes distally; no focal conduction block
EMG: chronic distal denervation/reinnervation in intrinsic foot and hand muscles
Genetic testing: PMP22 gene duplication on chromosome 17 (consistent with CMT1A)
B12 540 pg/mL | HbA1c 5.2% | TSH 1.8 mIU/L | SPEP normal
Foot radiographs: pes cavus with hammertoe deformities, no fracture

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For his bilateral foot drop and steppage gait, what is the most appropriate orthotic prescription?
options: Bilateral knee-ankle-foot orthoses (KAFOs)
Bilateral ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) to control foot drop and improve gait stability
A wheelchair for community mobility
Rigid postoperative casts to both ankles
stages.explanation: Ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) are the mainstay for foot drop in CMT: they hold the ankle in a neutral position, prevent the toes from catching during swing, improve clearance and stability, and reduce falls and ankle sprains. KAFOs (wrong) are excessive because his knee extensors and proximal strength are normal; AFOs address the distal deficit. A wheelchair (wrong) is unnecessary in an ambulatory young man and would promote deconditioning. Rigid casts (wrong) immobilize unnecessarily and cause disuse atrophy. AFOs combined with stretching and appropriate footwear optimize his mobility while preserving function.

stages.label: REHABILITATION
stages.question: What is a key rehabilitation goal to prevent worsening of his foot deformities and maintain function?
options: Routine high-impact running to strengthen the feet
Stretching and contracture prevention (heel cord/plantar fascia), low-impact exercise, and physical/occupational therapy
Prolonged immobilization to rest the deformed joints
Avoiding all exercise to prevent nerve damage
stages.explanation: Stretching of the heel cords and plantar fascia, contracture prevention, low-impact strengthening and aerobic exercise (swimming, cycling), and PT/OT to maintain range of motion and address hand dexterity are central to CMT rehabilitation. These slow the progression of contractures and preserve mobility and independence. Routine high-impact running (wrong) stresses unstable ankles and risks injury without benefit. Prolonged immobilization (wrong) accelerates contractures and deconditioning. Avoiding all exercise (wrong) is a myth; appropriate exercise does not damage nerves and helps maintain strength and cardiovascular health. Orthotic management, fall prevention, and foot care round out the plan.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: He asks about passing the condition to his children. What is the most accurate counseling for the most common form (CMT1A)?
options: It is not heritable and poses no risk to offspring
It follows autosomal dominant inheritance, conferring roughly a 50% chance of transmission; offer genetic counseling
It is X-linked, so only his daughters can be affected
It is autosomal recessive, so children are affected only if the partner is a carrier
stages.explanation: CMT1A (PMP22 duplication) is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, giving each child approximately a 50% chance of inheriting the duplication; formal genetic counseling is appropriate, especially given his multigenerational family history. Saying it is non-heritable (wrong) contradicts the dominant family pattern and the confirmed genetic mutation. X-linked inheritance (wrong) describes CMTX (GJB1/connexin-32), where transmission patterns differ and affected fathers cannot pass it to sons. Autosomal recessive forms (CMT4) exist but are rarer and not consistent with his dominant pedigree. Accurate inheritance counseling supports informed reproductive decisions.

cases.diagnosis: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease
pearls: Charcot-Marie-Tooth presents with childhood-onset distal symmetric sensorimotor neuropathy, pes cavus, hammertoes, distal atrophy ('inverted champagne bottle legs'), areflexia, and steppage gait.
CMT1 (most common) is demyelinating with uniformly slowed conduction velocities; CMT1A from PMP22 duplication is autosomal dominant (~50% transmission risk).
AFOs are the orthotic mainstay for foot drop; emphasize stretching, contracture prevention, low-impact exercise, and foot care.
Offer genetic counseling and clarify the inheritance pattern (AD for CMT1A, X-linked for CMTX).

cases.title: Burning Feet and a Recent Fall
cases.chiefComplaint: Numbness, tingling, and burning in both feet for the past year.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man with long-standing type 2 diabetes reports a year of gradually worsening numbness, tingling, and burning in both feet, now creeping up to his mid-shins. He has recently begun to feel unsteady on his feet and had a fall last month when he could not feel a curb. The burning is worse at night and disrupts his sleep. He cannot feel pebbles in his shoes and once found a blister he never felt forming. PMH: type 2 diabetes for 14 years (last HbA1c 9.2%), hypertension, hyperlipidemia. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, glipizide, lisinopril, atorvastatin. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker, drinks two beers nightly, sedentary. Family history: father had diabetes and an amputation. Review of systems: denies back pain, leg pain with walking that resolves with rest, weakness of the arms, or bowel/bladder dysfunction. He admits he rarely checks his feet and wears tight shoes.
cases.exam: Overweight. Feet show dry skin, a healed callus, and reduced hair; no active ulcer today but a small interdigital fissure. Symmetric distal sensory loss to vibration, pinprick, light touch, and 10-g monofilament in a stocking distribution to the mid-shins; hands beginning to show fingertip sensory loss (glove). Ankle reflexes absent, knee reflexes reduced. Distal weakness minimal (ankle dorsiflexion 5-/5). Proprioception reduced at the toes; Romberg positive. Pedal pulses palpable but diminished. No focal deficit, no spinal tenderness. Gait cautious and slightly wide-based.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His symmetric, distal, length-dependent 'stocking-glove' sensory loss with absent ankle reflexes most likely reflects what process?
options: A mononeuropathy such as carpal tunnel syndrome
A length-dependent distal symmetric sensory polyneuropathy
A lumbosacral radiculopathy from a herniated disc
An upper motor neuron lesion in the spinal cord
stages.explanation: Symmetric sensory loss that starts in the toes and ascends, reaching the hands once the legs are involved to mid-shin ('stocking-glove'), with absent ankle reflexes, is the classic picture of a length-dependent distal symmetric polyneuropathy, most commonly from diabetes. The longest axons are affected first, explaining the distal-to-proximal gradient. Carpal tunnel (wrong) is a focal median mononeuropathy affecting specific digits, not a symmetric stocking-glove pattern. A herniated-disc radiculopathy (wrong) causes dermatomal pain/weakness in one limb, not symmetric distal sensory loss. An upper motor neuron lesion (wrong) produces hyperreflexia and spasticity, opposite to the areflexia here. His poor glycemic control strongly supports diabetic polyneuropathy.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
HbA1c 9.2% | Fasting glucose 188 mg/dL
B12 410 pg/mL | TSH 2.0 mIU/L
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL | eGFR 78 mL/min
SPEP: no monoclonal protein
Urine microalbumin: elevated
10-g monofilament: insensate at multiple plantar sites bilaterally

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which intervention is most important to slow progression of his neuropathy over the long term?
options: Improving glycemic control toward individualized targets
Starting high-dose vitamin B12 despite a normal level
Prescribing bed rest to protect the feet
Beginning broad-spectrum antibiotics prophylactically
stages.explanation: Optimizing glycemic control is the single most important measure to slow progression of diabetic peripheral polyneuropathy; tight control reduces neuropathy progression, particularly in type 2 diabetes when combined with cardiovascular risk-factor management. His HbA1c of 9.2% is well above target and should be improved. High-dose B12 (wrong) helps only if deficient; his level is normal, so it will not help. Bed rest (wrong) promotes deconditioning and does nothing for the underlying metabolic cause. Prophylactic antibiotics (wrong) are not indicated absent infection and risk resistance and side effects. Glycemic and risk-factor optimization plus foot protection is the foundation of care.

stages.label: TREATMENT
stages.question: For his painful, sleep-disrupting burning dysesthesias, which first-line agent for neuropathic pain is most appropriate?
options: Oral oxycodone scheduled around the clock
Duloxetine, pregabalin, gabapentin, or a TCA such as amitriptyline
Topical antibiotic ointment to the feet
Acetaminophen alone at maximum dose
stages.explanation: First-line pharmacotherapy for painful diabetic neuropathy includes duloxetine (SNRI), pregabalin or gabapentin (gabapentinoids), and tricyclics like amitriptyline; these target neuropathic pain mechanisms and are guideline-recommended. Around-the-clock opioids such as oxycodone (wrong) are not first-line due to limited long-term efficacy, tolerance, and addiction risk. Topical antibiotic ointment (wrong) treats infection, not neuropathic pain. Acetaminophen alone (wrong) is generally ineffective for neuropathic pain. Choice among first-line agents is guided by comorbidities (e.g., duloxetine if depression, caution with TCAs in cardiac or elderly patients). Reassess efficacy and titrate to effect.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Given his insensate feet and recent fall, which counseling is most critical to prevent serious complications?
options: Soak feet in hot water nightly to improve circulation
Daily foot inspection, proper footwear, podiatric care, and home fall-prevention measures
Walk barefoot at home to toughen the soles
Trim calluses aggressively with a razor at home
stages.explanation: Because he cannot feel injuries, daily foot inspection (using a mirror if needed), well-fitting protective footwear, regular podiatric care, and fall-prevention measures (removing rugs, good lighting, grab bars, assistive devices, vision check) are critical to prevent ulcers, infections, amputations, and fall-related injuries. Hot-water soaks (wrong) risk unnoticed burns in insensate feet. Walking barefoot (wrong) invites unfelt trauma and foreign-body injury. Aggressive home callus removal with a razor (wrong) risks wounds and infection and should be done by a professional. Patient education on foot care and fall prevention is high-yield and directly reduces morbidity in diabetic neuropathy.

cases.diagnosis: Diabetic peripheral polyneuropathy
pearls: Diabetic polyneuropathy is a length-dependent distal symmetric sensorimotor neuropathy: stocking-glove sensory loss, absent ankle reflexes, worse with poor glycemic control.
Glycemic control plus cardiovascular risk-factor management is the most important step to slow progression; check B12, TSH, and SPEP to exclude other causes.
First-line neuropathic pain agents: duloxetine, pregabalin, gabapentin, or TCAs; avoid making opioids first-line.
Insensate feet demand daily inspection, protective footwear, podiatry, and fall-prevention counseling to avoid ulcers, amputation, and falls.

cases.title: Numb Pinky and a Weak Grip
cases.chiefComplaint: Numbness in the ring and little fingers with a weakening hand grip.
cases.history: A 46-year-old draftsman is referred for electrodiagnostic evaluation of several months of numbness and tingling in his right ring and little fingers, along with a sense that his grip and pinch are weakening. Symptoms are worse when he leans on his elbows at his drafting table or talks on the phone with the elbow flexed for long periods; he often wakes with the hand 'asleep.' He has begun dropping objects and notices his hand looks 'bonier.' No neck pain, no symptoms in the thumb or index finger, no leg complaints. PMH: hypertension. Medications: amlodipine. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, rare alcohol, plays recreational tennis; habitually rests elbows on hard surfaces. Family history: noncontributory. Occupational history notable for prolonged elbow flexion and pressure on the medial elbow. Review of systems otherwise negative; no diabetes, no thyroid disease, no prior elbow fracture, though he recalls a remote elbow injury in his teens.
cases.exam: Right hand shows mild interosseous and hypothenar atrophy with early clawing of the ring and little fingers. Weakness of finger abduction/adduction (interossei), little-finger abduction, and thumb adduction. Positive Froment sign (thumb IP flexion when pinching paper). Sensory loss over the little finger and the ulnar half of the ring finger, including the dorsal ulnar hand. Tinel sign positive over the cubital tunnel; elbow flexion test reproduces symptoms. Sensation over the thenar eminence and index finger is normal. No weakness of the abductor pollicis brevis. Reflexes normal and symmetric; no neck tenderness; Spurling negative.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: The sensory loss including the DORSAL ulnar hand, positive Froment sign, and symptom reproduction with elbow flexion best localize the lesion to where?
options: The ulnar nerve at Guyon's canal (wrist)
The ulnar nerve at the elbow (cubital tunnel)
The C8-T1 nerve roots (lower trunk/radiculopathy)
The median nerve at the carpal tunnel
stages.explanation: Involvement of the dorsal ulnar cutaneous territory (dorsomedial hand) localizes the lesion proximal to the wrist, because the dorsal ulnar cutaneous branch arises in the forearm and is spared in wrist (Guyon's canal) lesions. Combined with symptom provocation by elbow flexion and a positive Tinel at the cubital tunnel, this points to ulnar neuropathy at the elbow. A Guyon's canal lesion (wrong) spares the dorsal ulnar hand and dorsal branch. C8-T1 radiculopathy (wrong) typically involves neck pain, a positive Spurling, and muscles beyond the ulnar distribution (e.g., thenar/median C8-T1 muscles). Carpal tunnel (wrong) affects the median nerve (thumb, index, middle), not the little finger. The dorsal ulnar sensory finding is the key discriminator.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which electrodiagnostic finding would most specifically confirm and localize ulnar neuropathy at the elbow?
options: Prolonged median distal motor latency at the wrist
Focal slowing or conduction block of the ulnar motor response across the elbow (below- vs above-elbow segment)
Diffuse symmetric slowing of all motor conduction velocities
A decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation
stages.explanation: Focal slowing of ulnar motor conduction velocity across the elbow segment, or conduction block (a drop in CMAP amplitude between below-elbow and above-elbow stimulation), specifically localizes the lesion to the elbow. This 'across-elbow' comparison is the cornerstone electrodiagnostic test. Prolonged median distal motor latency (wrong) indicates carpal tunnel syndrome, a different nerve. Diffuse symmetric slowing (wrong) suggests a generalized polyneuropathy, not a focal entrapment. A decremental response on repetitive stimulation (wrong) indicates a neuromuscular junction disorder. Needle EMG showing denervation in ulnar-innervated muscles (first dorsal interosseous, ADM) but sparing of C8-T1 non-ulnar muscles further supports localization to the ulnar nerve at the elbow.

stages.label: ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC RESULTS
stages.content:
Ulnar motor (recording ADM): below-elbow CV 52 m/s; across-elbow CV 33 m/s (focal slowing)
CMAP amplitude drop of 28% from below-elbow to above-elbow stimulation (conduction block)
Ulnar sensory (recording 5th digit): reduced SNAP amplitude
Dorsal ulnar cutaneous sensory response: reduced (lesion above wrist)
Median motor and sensory studies: normal (no carpal tunnel)
Needle EMG: denervation in first dorsal interosseous and ADM; flexor carpi ulnaris mildly involved; C8-T1 non-ulnar muscles (e.g., APB, FPL) normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this mild-to-moderate cubital tunnel syndrome without severe axonal loss, what is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Immediate ulnar nerve transposition surgery
Conservative measures: activity modification, elbow padding, nighttime extension splinting, and avoiding prolonged elbow flexion/leaning
Wrist splinting in neutral for carpal tunnel
Long-term oral corticosteroids
stages.explanation: Mild-to-moderate cubital tunnel syndrome is treated conservatively first: activity modification, elbow padding, avoiding prolonged elbow flexion and leaning on the elbow, and nighttime splinting in relative elbow extension to reduce nerve tension and compression. Many patients improve without surgery. Immediate surgical transposition (wrong) is reserved for severe, progressive, or refractory cases with significant weakness/atrophy, not first-line for mild-moderate disease. Wrist splinting for carpal tunnel (wrong) targets the wrong nerve and joint. Long-term oral corticosteroids (wrong) have no established role in focal entrapment neuropathy and carry systemic risks. Surgical decompression/transposition is considered if conservative care fails or atrophy progresses.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding would most strongly favor proceeding to surgical decompression rather than continued conservative care?
options: Intermittent tingling only at night with no weakness
Progressive hypothenar/interosseous atrophy and persistent weakness despite conservative measures
A single positive Tinel sign with normal strength
Symptoms present for less than two weeks
stages.explanation: Progressive muscle atrophy and persistent or worsening weakness despite adequate conservative treatment indicate ongoing axonal loss and are strong indications for surgical decompression or transposition to prevent irreversible deficit. Purely intermittent nocturnal tingling without weakness (wrong) often responds to conservative care and does not mandate surgery. An isolated positive Tinel with normal strength (wrong) is a supportive sign but not an indication for surgery by itself. Symptoms of less than two weeks (wrong) warrant a conservative trial first, as many resolve. The presence of motor weakness, atrophy, and significant axonal loss on EMG drives the decision toward surgery.

cases.diagnosis: Ulnar neuropathy at the elbow (cubital tunnel syndrome)
pearls: Ulnar neuropathy at the elbow involves the dorsal ulnar cutaneous territory (dorsomedial hand), which is SPARED in wrist/Guyon's canal lesions, the key localizing clue.
Froment sign (thumb IP flexion via FPL to compensate for weak adductor pollicis) and clawing of the 4th/5th digits indicate ulnar motor weakness.
Electrodiagnosis confirms localization via focal slowing or conduction block across the elbow segment, with sparing of C8-T1 non-ulnar muscles.
Treat mild-moderate cases conservatively (elbow padding, avoid flexion/leaning, night extension splinting); reserve surgery for progressive weakness/atrophy or failed conservative care.

cases.title: A Foot That Won't Lift After Losing Weight
cases.chiefComplaint: Right foot drop and tripping that began a few weeks ago.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man is referred for electrodiagnostic testing after three weeks of right foot drop. He cannot lift the front of his foot and trips frequently, catching his toes on stairs. He recalls no acute injury but has lost 40 pounds over four months through dieting and now sits cross-legged for long periods and habitually crosses his legs. He also took a long road trip with prolonged sitting. He notices numbness over the top of the foot and the outer shin. No back pain, no pain radiating from the spine, no bowel/bladder symptoms. PMH: type 2 diabetes (well controlled), hyperlipidemia. Medications: metformin, atorvastatin. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, recently very sedentary during weight loss. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: denies hip girdle weakness, no thigh symptoms, no saddle anesthesia. He is anxious it could be a 'pinched nerve in the back' like his brother had.
cases.exam: Right foot drop with a steppage gait. Weakness of ankle dorsiflexion (2/5), great toe extension (2/5), and foot eversion (3/5). Ankle inversion (tibialis posterior, L5 via tibial nerve) and plantarflexion are normal (5/5). Hip abduction and knee flexion normal. Sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and the lateral lower leg; the sole and medial foot are spared. Tinel sign positive at the fibular head. Deep tendon reflexes symmetric and preserved, including the ankle jerk. No back tenderness; straight-leg raise negative. No saddle anesthesia.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Foot drop with weak dorsiflexion and eversion but PRESERVED inversion (tibialis posterior) and a normal ankle jerk best localizes to which lesion?
options: L5 radiculopathy
Common peroneal (fibular) neuropathy at the fibular head
Sciatic neuropathy
Tibial neuropathy at the tarsal tunnel
stages.explanation: The peroneal (fibular) nerve supplies dorsiflexion (deep peroneal) and eversion (superficial peroneal). Sparing of ankle inversion is the critical clue: tibialis posterior is L5 but travels in the tibial nerve, so if inversion is normal while dorsiflexion/eversion are weak, the lesion is in the peroneal nerve, not the L5 root. A positive Tinel at the fibular head and his risk factors (weight loss, leg crossing) support peroneal neuropathy there. L5 radiculopathy (wrong) would also weaken inversion (tibialis posterior) and often hip abduction, and may cause back/radicular pain. Sciatic neuropathy (wrong) affects both peroneal and tibial divisions, so plantarflexion and inversion would be impaired. Tarsal tunnel (wrong) affects the tibial nerve (sole), not dorsiflexion.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which electrodiagnostic finding would best confirm a focal peroneal lesion at the fibular head and distinguish it from L5 radiculopathy?
options: Conduction block or focal slowing of the peroneal motor response across the fibular head, with normal tibialis posterior on EMG
Denervation in the tibialis posterior and gluteus medius
Prolonged H-reflex (S1) latency
Diffuse slowing of all nerves consistent with polyneuropathy
stages.explanation: Demonstrating conduction block or focal slowing of the peroneal motor response across the fibular head (comparing below- and above-fibular-head stimulation) localizes the lesion there, and a normal needle exam of the tibialis posterior (L5, tibial nerve) and other L5 muscles outside the peroneal distribution argues against L5 radiculopathy. Denervation in tibialis posterior and gluteus medius (wrong) would indicate an L5 root or more proximal lesion, not a focal peroneal one. A prolonged H-reflex (wrong) reflects S1 pathology, irrelevant here. Diffuse slowing (wrong) indicates polyneuropathy, not focal entrapment. The short head of the biceps femoris and proximal peroneal-supplied muscles can help further localize above versus at the fibular head.

stages.label: ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC RESULTS
stages.content:
Peroneal motor (recording EDB and tibialis anterior): conduction block with 45% CMAP amplitude drop and focal slowing across the fibular head
Superficial peroneal sensory response: reduced amplitude
Sural and tibial motor responses: normal
Needle EMG: denervation in tibialis anterior and peroneus longus
Tibialis posterior (L5, tibial), gluteus medius, and short head of biceps femoris: NORMAL
Lumbar paraspinals: normal
HbA1c 6.4%

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a demyelinating conduction block from external compression without severe axonal loss, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Urgent lumbar laminectomy
Avoid leg crossing/pressure at the fibular head, use a padded knee position, prescribe an AFO for foot drop, and PT; expect recovery
Immediate surgical exploration of the peroneal nerve
Lifelong wheelchair use
stages.explanation: A compressive peroneal neuropathy with conduction block (demyelination) and preserved axons has a good prognosis. Management is conservative: eliminate the source of compression (stop leg crossing, avoid pressure at the fibular head, pad the area, reposition during prolonged sitting), prescribe an AFO to control the foot drop and prevent tripping, and provide physical therapy; recovery over weeks to months is expected. Urgent laminectomy (wrong) treats spinal stenosis/radiculopathy, not a peripheral compression. Immediate surgical exploration (wrong) is reserved for traumatic transection, mass lesions, or failure to recover with severe axonal loss. Lifelong wheelchair use (wrong) is unwarranted given the favorable prognosis with bracing and removing compression.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which factor most likely predisposed this patient to peroneal nerve injury at the fibular head?
options: His statin therapy
Recent significant weight loss reducing protective fat padding, plus habitual leg crossing and prolonged sitting
His well-controlled diabetes alone
Excess upper body muscle mass
stages.explanation: Rapid or significant weight loss reduces the subcutaneous fat that cushions the superficial peroneal nerve at the fibular head ('slimmer's palsy'), and habitual leg crossing plus prolonged sitting add direct external compression, the combination that injured this nerve. Statins (wrong) can cause myopathy but not focal compressive peroneal palsy. Well-controlled diabetes alone (wrong) increases susceptibility to compression but is not the primary precipitant here given the strong mechanical history. Excess upper body muscle mass (wrong) is unrelated. Counseling to avoid leg crossing and pressure at the fibular head helps prevent recurrence.

cases.diagnosis: Peroneal (fibular) neuropathy at the fibular head
pearls: Foot drop with weak dorsiflexion/eversion but PRESERVED inversion (tibialis posterior) and a normal ankle jerk points to peroneal neuropathy at the fibular head, not L5 radiculopathy.
Confirm with conduction block/focal slowing across the fibular head and a normal needle exam of L5 muscles outside the peroneal distribution (tibialis posterior, gluteus medius).
Weight loss ('slimmer's palsy'), leg crossing, and prolonged compression are classic precipitants; demyelinating conduction block has a good prognosis.
Treat conservatively: remove compression, AFO for foot drop, physical therapy; reserve surgery for trauma, mass, or severe non-recovering axonal loss.

cases.title: Wrist Drop After Sleeping It Off
cases.chiefComplaint: He woke unable to lift his right wrist and fingers.
cases.history: A 37-year-old man presents to the emergency department after waking with a 'limp' right hand. He had been drinking heavily at a party and fell asleep on a couch with his right arm draped over the hard armrest for several hours. On waking he could not lift his wrist or fingers and noticed numbness on the back of his hand. He denies neck pain, trauma to the arm beyond the prolonged pressure, chest pain, or weakness elsewhere. No prior similar episodes. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: episodic heavy alcohol use, smokes occasionally, no IV drug use. Family history: noncontributory. He is alarmed and worried he had a stroke. Review of systems otherwise negative: no facial droop, no speech difficulty, no leg weakness, no headache. He recalls the arm being 'dead asleep' when he awoke, with gradual return of some sensation but persistent inability to extend the wrist.
cases.exam: Awake, oriented, no facial asymmetry or dysarthria. Right wrist drop: cannot extend the wrist, fingers (at the MCP joints), or thumb. Wrist and finger flexion intact; grip appears weak only because the wrist cannot be stabilized in extension. Triceps strength 5/5 and triceps reflex preserved. Brachioradialis weak. Sensory loss over the dorsal radial hand and the dorsal first web space (snuffbox). Sensation over the palm and the volar fingertips is normal. No weakness of shoulder abduction or elbow flexion. Biceps reflex normal. No neck tenderness, Spurling negative. Cranial nerves intact; no leg findings.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Wrist and finger drop with PRESERVED triceps strength and reflex, weak brachioradialis, and dorsal radial-hand sensory loss localizes the radial nerve lesion to where?
options: The posterior cord/axilla (proximal to the triceps branches)
The spiral groove of the humerus (distal to the triceps branches)
The posterior interosseous nerve (PIN) in the forearm
The C7 nerve root
stages.explanation: The triceps branches leave the radial nerve before it enters the spiral groove, so a spiral-groove lesion spares the triceps (normal strength and reflex) while affecting wrist/finger extensors, brachioradialis, and the dorsal radial sensory branch, exactly this 'Saturday night palsy' pattern from prolonged humeral compression. A posterior cord/axillary lesion (wrong) would also weaken the triceps. The PIN (wrong) is purely motor and arises after the brachioradialis and superficial radial sensory branch, so PIN lesions spare sensation and spare the brachioradialis and radial wrist extensors (causing finger drop with radial wrist deviation), not this sensory loss with brachioradialis weakness. C7 radiculopathy (wrong) weakens the triceps and causes neck/radicular symptoms.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes a spiral-groove radial neuropathy from a posterior interosseous nerve (PIN) syndrome?
options: Presence of dorsal hand sensory loss and brachioradialis weakness (seen in spiral groove, not PIN)
Weakness of wrist flexion
Loss of the triceps reflex
Numbness of the palm and volar fingers
stages.explanation: The PIN is a purely motor branch and arises distal to the takeoff of the superficial radial sensory branch and the brachioradialis; therefore PIN lesions spare sensation and spare the brachioradialis, producing finger drop with radial deviation on wrist extension but NO sensory loss. Dorsal hand sensory loss plus brachioradialis weakness indicates a more proximal lesion (spiral groove). Wrist flexion (wrong) is median/ulnar mediated and unaffected in radial lesions. Loss of the triceps reflex (wrong) would indicate a lesion proximal to the spiral groove (posterior cord/axilla) or C7, not the discriminator between spiral groove and PIN. Palmar/volar numbness (wrong) is median territory, irrelevant to the radial nerve.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Point-of-care glucose 96 mg/dL
Non-contrast head CT: no acute intracranial abnormality
Upper extremity exam reconfirmed: triceps 5/5, wrist/finger extensors 0-1/5, brachioradialis weak
Humerus radiograph: no fracture
NIHSS: 0 (no central deficit)
Nerve conduction studies (outpatient, 1 week later): focal conduction block of the radial motor response across the spiral groove; reduced superficial radial sensory amplitude

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a compressive radial neuropathy at the spiral groove with conduction block and no fracture, what is the best management?
options: Emergent surgical decompression of the radial nerve
A cock-up wrist splint (wrist extension orthosis), occupational therapy, and observation, with expected recovery over weeks to months
Thrombolysis for presumed stroke
Immediate tendon transfer surgery
stages.explanation: A compressive ('Saturday night') radial palsy with conduction block reflects focal demyelination from external pressure and typically recovers over weeks to a few months. Management is supportive: a cock-up wrist (extension) splint to maintain function and prevent contracture, occupational/physical therapy for range of motion, and observation while the nerve remyelinates. Emergent surgical decompression (wrong) is not indicated for a simple compressive neuropathy without fracture or mass. Thrombolysis (wrong) is for acute ischemic stroke; his deficit is peripheral with a normal NIHSS and CT. Tendon transfer (wrong) is considered only for permanent deficits after failed recovery with significant axonal loss, not initially. Counsel on avoiding recurrent compression and alcohol-related risk.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate disposition and counseling from the emergency department?
options: Admit to the stroke unit for tPA candidacy
Discharge with a wrist splint, outpatient neurology/EMG follow-up, reassurance about favorable prognosis, and alcohol-use counseling
Schedule urgent MRI brain with contrast
Refer for immediate hand surgery the same day
stages.explanation: This is a peripheral compressive radial neuropathy, not a stroke, so the appropriate disposition is discharge with a cock-up wrist splint, outpatient electrodiagnostic and neurology follow-up to confirm localization and prognosis, reassurance that compressive palsies usually recover, and counseling on alcohol use and avoiding prolonged limb compression. Admission for tPA (wrong) is for acute ischemic stroke; his exam is peripheral with NIHSS 0 and normal CT. Urgent contrast brain MRI (wrong) is unnecessary given the clear peripheral localization. Immediate same-day hand surgery (wrong) is not warranted for a conservatively managed compressive neuropathy. Setting expectations and arranging EMG follow-up are key.

cases.diagnosis: Radial neuropathy at the spiral groove (Saturday night palsy)
pearls: Spiral-groove radial palsy spares the triceps (branches leave before the groove) and causes wrist/finger drop, brachioradialis weakness, and dorsal radial-hand sensory loss.
PIN syndrome is purely motor: finger drop with radial wrist deviation, NO sensory loss and brachioradialis spared, distinguishing it from a spiral-groove lesion.
Compressive ('Saturday night') palsy with conduction block usually recovers over weeks to months; treat with a cock-up wrist splint, OT/PT, and observation.
Distinguish from stroke (normal NIHSS, peripheral pattern) and C7 radiculopathy (triceps weakness, neck/radicular symptoms).

cases.title: Severe Shoulder Pain Then a Weak Arm
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe shoulder pain followed days later by arm weakness.
cases.history: A 41-year-old man presents with shoulder and upper arm weakness that followed an episode of excruciating right shoulder pain. About three weeks ago he developed abrupt, severe, deep aching pain around the right shoulder and scapula that kept him awake at night and was unresponsive to NSAIDs; it lasted about ten days. As the pain subsided, he noticed marked weakness lifting his arm and a winging of the shoulder blade. He recalls a viral upper respiratory illness and a flu vaccination about two weeks before symptom onset. No neck pain radiating down the arm, no precipitating trauma, no bowel/bladder issues. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, social alcohol, works in sales, right-hand dominant. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: denies fever now, no rash, no significant sensory complaints aside from a small numb patch over the lateral shoulder. He is frustrated that the severe pain has been replaced by surprising weakness.
cases.exam: Right shoulder shows atrophy of the supraspinatus/infraspinatus and medial scapular winging with arm elevation (serratus anterior weakness). Weakness of shoulder abduction (deltoid 3/5), external rotation (4-/5), and elbow flexion (4/5) in a patchy, non-dermatomal/non-single-nerve distribution. Reflexes: biceps reduced on the right. Mild sensory loss over the lateral deltoid (axillary territory). Neck range of motion full and painless; Spurling negative. No Horner syndrome. Distal hand strength and grip normal. No long-tract signs. Contralateral arm normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Abrupt severe shoulder pain followed by patchy multifocal weakness/atrophy (deltoid, spinati, serratus anterior) crossing single nerve and root territories, after a recent viral illness/vaccination, most suggests what?
options: Cervical radiculopathy from a herniated disc
Parsonage-Turner syndrome (neuralgic amyotrophy / idiopathic brachial plexopathy)
Rotator cuff tear
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
stages.explanation: Neuralgic amyotrophy (Parsonage-Turner syndrome) classically presents with sudden, severe shoulder/arm pain lasting days to weeks, followed as the pain resolves by patchy, multifocal weakness and atrophy that does not fit a single nerve or root, often after an antecedent viral illness, vaccination, surgery, or stress. Involvement of the long thoracic nerve (serratus anterior, scapular winging), suprascapular, and axillary nerves is typical. Cervical radiculopathy (wrong) follows a single dermatome/myotome with neck pain and a positive Spurling. A rotator cuff tear (wrong) causes mechanical shoulder weakness/pain but not multifocal denervation or scapular winging from serratus involvement. ALS (wrong) is painless, progressive, with upper and lower motor neuron signs, not an acute painful monophasic plexopathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test best supports the diagnosis and excludes a structural cervical cause?
options: EMG/NCS showing patchy denervation in multiple nerves/trunks of the brachial plexus, with cervical MRI to exclude root compression
Shoulder ultrasound alone
Repetitive nerve stimulation
Electrocardiogram and troponin
stages.explanation: EMG/NCS is the key study, demonstrating patchy denervation across multiple nerves and trunks of the brachial plexus (e.g., long thoracic, suprascapular, axillary, anterior interosseous), a pattern that does not fit a single root or nerve, supporting neuralgic amyotrophy; cervical MRI excludes a compressive radiculopathy and MRI of the plexus/muscles may show denervation edema. Shoulder ultrasound alone (wrong) evaluates the rotator cuff, not the neurologic process. Repetitive nerve stimulation (wrong) tests the neuromuscular junction (e.g., myasthenia), not a plexopathy. ECG and troponin (wrong) evaluate cardiac ischemia, irrelevant here. The combination of EMG and MRI confirms the diagnosis and rules out mimics.

stages.label: STUDIES
stages.content:
EMG/NCS: active denervation (fibrillations, positive sharp waves) and reduced recruitment in deltoid, supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and serratus anterior
Pattern crosses multiple peripheral nerves/upper trunk; not confined to a single root or nerve
Sensory studies: mildly reduced lateral antebrachial cutaneous response
Cervical MRI: mild spondylosis, no significant nerve root compression or cord lesion
ESR/CRP mildly elevated; CK normal
Chest imaging: normal (no apical mass), no Horner findings

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management during the acute and subacute phases?
options: Immediate brachial plexus surgical exploration
Pain control, a short course of oral corticosteroids if early, and early physical/occupational therapy focused on range of motion and scapular stabilization
Long-term opioid therapy as the mainstay
Complete immobilization of the arm in a sling for three months
stages.explanation: Treatment of Parsonage-Turner syndrome is largely supportive: aggressive pain control during the painful phase, a short course of oral corticosteroids if started early (which may shorten pain duration and possibly improve recovery), and early physical/occupational therapy emphasizing range of motion, preventing contractures/adhesive capsulitis, and scapular stabilization. Most patients recover over months to a couple of years. Immediate surgical exploration (wrong) is not indicated for idiopathic neuralgic amyotrophy. Long-term opioids (wrong) are not the mainstay; neuropathic agents and short-term analgesia are preferred. Prolonged complete immobilization (wrong) causes stiffness and adhesive capsulitis; early gentle mobilization is essential. Reassurance about generally favorable prognosis is important.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most accurate statement about the natural history of this condition?
options: It is rapidly fatal without treatment
It is typically monophasic with gradual recovery over months to 1-2 years, though some patients have residual weakness and recurrences can occur
It always resolves completely within one week
It inevitably progresses to quadriplegia
stages.explanation: Neuralgic amyotrophy is usually a monophasic illness with gradual recovery over many months up to one to two years; many patients regain substantial function, though some are left with residual weakness or scapular winging, and recurrences or contralateral episodes can occur (notably in hereditary forms). It is not fatal (wrong) and does not cause quadriplegia (wrong); it is a peripheral plexopathy, not a central or motor neuron disease. Complete resolution within one week (wrong) is unrealistic; the painful phase alone often lasts days to weeks and motor recovery takes much longer. Setting realistic expectations supports adherence to rehabilitation.

cases.diagnosis: Parsonage-Turner syndrome (neuralgic amyotrophy)
pearls: Parsonage-Turner syndrome: abrupt severe shoulder/arm pain for days to weeks, then patchy multifocal weakness/atrophy crossing nerve and root territories, often after viral illness, vaccination, or surgery.
Watch for long thoracic nerve involvement (serratus anterior, scapular winging) plus suprascapular/axillary nerve patterns.
EMG shows patchy plexus denervation; cervical MRI excludes compressive radiculopathy; management is supportive with pain control, early steroids if caught early, and PT/OT.
Course is usually monophasic with recovery over months to 1-2 years; residual deficits and recurrences can occur.

cases.title: Hand Numbness and a Heavy Arm with Overhead Work
cases.chiefComplaint: Aching arm and numbness in the inner forearm and last two fingers, worse with overhead activity.
cases.history: A 32-year-old massage therapist reports months of aching in the right arm with numbness and tingling along the inner forearm and into the ring and little fingers. Symptoms worsen with overhead activity and carrying bags, and she notes her grip tiring and early thenar/hypothenar muscle wasting. She denies neck trauma; pain is not clearly positional with neck movement. No symptoms in the thumb-index region predominate. She has noticed coolness and occasional color change in the hand but no swelling. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: oral contraceptive. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, frequent overhead and repetitive arm work, long hours with arms elevated. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: denies chest pain, no leg symptoms, no bowel/bladder dysfunction. On further history, she had imaging showing a 'small extra rib' in the neck years ago. She is concerned about progressive hand weakness affecting her career.
cases.exam: Subtle wasting of the thenar (abductor pollicis brevis) and hypothenar/intrinsic hand muscles on the right (Gilliatt-Sumner hand pattern, thenar predominant). Weakness of thumb abduction and finger intrinsics. Sensory loss along the medial forearm (medial antebrachial cutaneous) and the ulnar two digits. Reflexes normal. Radial pulse intact at rest; supraclavicular fullness with tenderness over a palpable bony prominence. Provocative testing: elevated arm stress test (Roos) reproduces arm heaviness, paresthesias, and fatigue within 60 seconds. Adson test variably positive. Neck range of motion full; Spurling negative. No long-tract signs.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Lower trunk (C8-T1) symptoms with medial forearm and ulnar-digit numbness, thenar-predominant intrinsic hand wasting, a cervical rib, and symptom reproduction on arm elevation best suggest what diagnosis?
options: Carpal tunnel syndrome
Neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome (true, lower-trunk type)
Ulnar neuropathy at the elbow
C8 radiculopathy from disc herniation
stages.explanation: True neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome compresses the lower trunk of the brachial plexus (C8-T1), often over a cervical rib or fibrous band, producing medial forearm and ulnar-hand sensory loss plus intrinsic hand wasting with a characteristic thenar-predominant (median-innervated APB) atrophy (the Gilliatt-Sumner hand). Symptom reproduction with arm elevation (Roos/EAST) supports it. Carpal tunnel (wrong) involves the median nerve at the wrist (thumb-middle finger), not the medial forearm. Ulnar neuropathy at the elbow (wrong) spares the medial antebrachial cutaneous nerve and the thenar APB. C8 radiculopathy (wrong) usually has neck/radicular pain, positive Spurling, and follows a single root; the cervical rib and positional, lower-trunk pattern with thenar wasting here point to TOS.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which study combination best supports true neurogenic TOS and excludes mimics?
options: EMG/NCS showing a lower-trunk/medial-cord pattern (low median motor and ulnar sensory amplitudes, reduced MABC) plus cervical spine and chest imaging for a cervical rib/band
Echocardiogram and stress test
Carpal tunnel ultrasound only
Repetitive nerve stimulation
stages.explanation: True neurogenic TOS produces a classic electrodiagnostic signature reflecting lower-trunk/medial-cord involvement: reduced median motor (CMAP) amplitude (APB is C8-T1 median), reduced ulnar sensory amplitude, and reduced medial antebrachial cutaneous (MABC) sensory response, while median sensory studies are relatively preserved. Imaging (cervical spine and chest radiographs, CT/MRI) identifies a cervical rib or fibrous band. Echocardiogram/stress test (wrong) evaluate cardiac disease. Carpal tunnel ultrasound only (wrong) addresses the median nerve at the wrist, a different lesion. Repetitive nerve stimulation (wrong) tests the neuromuscular junction. The combination of a lower-trunk EMG pattern and a structural cervical rib strongly supports true neurogenic TOS.

stages.label: STUDIES
stages.content:
NCS: low median motor (APB) CMAP amplitude; reduced ulnar sensory amplitude; reduced medial antebrachial cutaneous (MABC) sensory amplitude
Median sensory amplitude relatively preserved
Needle EMG: chronic denervation/reinnervation in APB and first dorsal interosseous (C8-T1)
Chest/cervical radiograph: right cervical rib articulating toward the first rib
MRI: fibrous band from the cervical rib indenting the lower trunk of the brachial plexus
Arterial duplex: no significant arterial compromise at rest

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For symptomatic neurogenic TOS without acute vascular compromise or severe progressive deficit, what is the appropriate first-line management?
options: Immediate first-rib/cervical-rib resection for all patients
A trial of conservative rehabilitation: posture correction, scapular and neck stabilization exercises, activity/ergonomic modification, and avoiding aggravating overhead positions
Long-term anticoagulation
Stellate ganglion block as definitive treatment
stages.explanation: Most patients with neurogenic TOS are managed first with a structured conservative rehabilitation program: postural correction, strengthening of scapular stabilizers and deep neck flexors, stretching of tight pectoral/scalene muscles, ergonomic and activity modification, and avoiding provocative overhead positions and heavy carrying. Surgery (cervical/first-rib resection) is reserved for failure of conservative care or progressive/severe neurologic deficit, not as the initial step for everyone (wrong). Long-term anticoagulation (wrong) addresses venous (Paget-Schroetter) thrombosis, a vascular form, not the neurogenic type without thrombosis. A stellate ganglion block (wrong) is not a definitive treatment for the mechanical compression of neurogenic TOS. Conservative rehab first, then surgery for refractory or progressive cases.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which scenario would most appropriately prompt referral for surgical decompression?
options: Mild intermittent tingling that resolves with posture changes
Progressive intrinsic hand muscle atrophy and weakness, or failure of an adequate conservative trial, with a structural cervical rib/band
A single positive Adson test with normal strength
Symptoms present for under two weeks
stages.explanation: Surgical decompression (resection of the cervical rib/fibrous band, often with first-rib resection and scalenectomy) is appropriate when there is progressive neurologic loss, fixed intrinsic hand atrophy and weakness, or failure of an adequate conservative rehabilitation trial, especially with a clearly identified structural lesion compressing the lower trunk, as in this patient if she worsens. Mild intermittent tingling relieved by posture (wrong) should continue conservative care. An isolated positive Adson test with normal strength (wrong) is nonspecific and not an indication for surgery. Very recent symptoms (wrong) warrant conservative management first. Documented progressive motor deficit or refractory disease drives the surgical decision.

cases.diagnosis: Neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome
pearls: True neurogenic TOS compresses the lower trunk (C8-T1), often over a cervical rib/fibrous band: medial forearm and ulnar-hand numbness with thenar-predominant intrinsic wasting (Gilliatt-Sumner hand).
EMG signature: low median motor (APB) amplitude, reduced ulnar sensory and medial antebrachial cutaneous responses, with relatively preserved median sensory; image for a cervical rib.
Provocative tests (Roos/EAST, Adson) support the diagnosis but are nonspecific; correlate with EMG and imaging.
Treat conservatively first (posture, scapular/neck stabilization, ergonomics); reserve cervical/first-rib resection for progressive deficit or failed conservative care.

cases.title: Leg Pain When Walking, Better Leaning on a Cart
cases.chiefComplaint: Aching, heaviness, and tingling in both legs when walking that eases when he sits or bends forward.
cases.history: A 71-year-old man reports a year of bilateral leg aching, heaviness, and tingling that come on after walking a block or two and force him to stop. The symptoms ease when he sits or leans forward, and he can walk much farther while pushing a shopping cart. Walking downhill is easier than uphill. He has chronic low back pain and stiffness. He denies rest pain, night pain in the calves, or symptoms purely related to standing still. No bowel/bladder dysfunction or saddle numbness. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, osteoarthritis. Medications: amlodipine, atorvastatin, acetaminophen. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker, no alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. Vascular review: pedal pulses normal, no claudication relieved simply by standing still. Review of systems otherwise negative. He is limited in walking his dog and grocery shopping, which prompted the visit.
cases.exam: Reduced lumbar extension that reproduces his leg symptoms; flexion relieves them. Strength largely 5/5 with mild bilateral L5 (EHL) weakness 4+/5. Sensation mildly reduced in the feet bilaterally. Deep tendon reflexes symmetric, ankle jerks slightly diminished. Straight-leg raise negative. Pedal pulses 2+ and symmetric; feet warm with normal capillary refill and no trophic changes. Gait stooped-forward posture; able to walk farther when flexed. No saddle anesthesia, normal rectal tone, no clonus or Babinski.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Bilateral leg symptoms brought on by walking and lumbar extension but RELIEVED by sitting or forward flexion (and by leaning on a cart) most suggest what?
options: Peripheral arterial disease (vascular claudication)
Neurogenic claudication from lumbar spinal stenosis
Hip osteoarthritis
Acute lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy
stages.explanation: Neurogenic claudication from lumbar spinal stenosis classically produces bilateral leg pain/heaviness/paresthesias provoked by walking and lumbar extension and relieved by flexion (sitting, leaning forward, pushing a shopping cart, walking uphill or downhill differences), because flexion enlarges the spinal canal. His normal pulses argue against vascular claudication (wrong), where pain is relieved simply by stopping (standing still) regardless of posture and is associated with diminished pulses and trophic changes. Hip osteoarthritis (wrong) causes groin pain with hip motion, not posture-dependent bilateral leg paresthesias. An acute disc herniation with radiculopathy (wrong) typically causes a positive straight-leg raise and single-dermatome pain, not posture-dependent neurogenic claudication; his SLR is negative.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes his NEUROGENIC claudication from vascular claudication?
options: Relief requires forward flexion/sitting rather than merely stopping; pulses are normal and walking uphill (flexed) may be easier
Pain is relieved by standing still regardless of posture
Symptoms correlate with diminished pedal pulses and foot pallor
Calf pain is reliably reproduced by a fixed walking distance and relieved within 2 minutes of standing
stages.explanation: Neurogenic claudication is posture-dependent: relief requires lumbar flexion (sitting, leaning on a cart, bending forward), pulses are normal, and patients often tolerate uphill walking or cycling (both flexed) better, whereas extension (downhill standing erect, prolonged standing) worsens symptoms. Vascular claudication (the other options) is relieved by stopping/standing still regardless of posture, correlates with diminished pulses, pallor, and trophic skin changes, and is reproduced at a fairly fixed claudication distance with quick relief on rest. His normal pulses, warm feet, and posture-dependent pattern fit neurogenic, not vascular, claudication. The 'shopping-cart sign' (walking farther while flexed over a cart) is a classic discriminator.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Ankle-brachial index: 1.05 bilaterally (normal; argues against PAD)
Lumbar MRI: multilevel degenerative changes with moderate-to-severe central canal stenosis maximal at L4-L5
Ligamentum flavum hypertrophy and facet arthropathy contributing to canal narrowing
Bilateral lateral recess narrowing at L4-L5
No acute disc extrusion, no cord signal change (conus ends at L1), no fracture

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has no red-flag deficits. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate decompressive laminectomy
Conservative care: physical therapy (flexion-based exercises, core strengthening), activity modification, NSAIDs/analgesia, with consideration of an epidural steroid injection for symptom relief
Urgent vascular bypass surgery
Strict bed rest for six weeks
stages.explanation: Without red-flag findings (no cauda equina signs, no progressive motor deficit), lumbar spinal stenosis is managed conservatively first: physical therapy emphasizing flexion-based exercises and core strengthening, activity modification, analgesics/NSAIDs, and consideration of an epidural steroid injection for temporary symptom relief and to aid participation in therapy. Immediate laminectomy (wrong) is reserved for refractory symptoms, progressive neurologic deficit, or cauda equina syndrome. Vascular bypass (wrong) treats arterial disease, which his normal ABI excludes. Strict prolonged bed rest (wrong) worsens deconditioning and is not recommended. Surgical decompression becomes appropriate if conservative measures fail or neurologic deficits progress.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which development would most strongly indicate the need for urgent surgical evaluation?
options: Continued mild leg aching with walking that improves with sitting
New urinary retention/incontinence, saddle anesthesia, or rapidly progressive bilateral leg weakness
An occasional need to rest after walking several blocks
Stable symptoms responsive to an epidural steroid injection
stages.explanation: New bowel or bladder dysfunction (urinary retention/incontinence), saddle anesthesia, and rapidly progressive bilateral leg weakness are red flags for cauda equina syndrome or significant progressive compression, mandating urgent imaging and surgical evaluation/decompression. Continued mild posture-relieved aching (wrong) reflects ongoing stenosis amenable to continued conservative care. Occasional rest after several blocks (wrong) indicates good functional tolerance, not an emergency. Stable symptoms responding to an epidural steroid injection (wrong) suggest effective conservative management. Recognizing cauda equina red flags is high-yield and time-critical to prevent permanent deficits.

cases.diagnosis: Lumbar spinal stenosis
pearls: Neurogenic claudication: bilateral leg pain/heaviness/paresthesias provoked by walking and lumbar extension, relieved by flexion (sitting, leaning on a shopping cart, walking uphill).
Distinguish from vascular claudication: neurogenic relief is posture-dependent with normal pulses; vascular relief comes from simply stopping, with diminished pulses and a normal/low ABI absent.

MRI shows central canal/lateral recess stenosis (ligamentum flavum hypertrophy, facet arthropathy); manage conservatively first with flexion-based PT, analgesia, and consider epidural steroid injection.
Urgent surgery is indicated for cauda equina red flags: new bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, or rapidly progressive weakness.

cases.title: Dropping Buttons and a Wobbly Walk
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive hand clumsiness and unsteady walking over the past 8 months.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man presents with 8 months of worsening hand clumsiness. He drops coffee mugs, struggles to button shirts, and his handwriting has deteriorated. Over the same period his wife notes he walks like he is 'on a boat,' grabbing furniture at home. He denies frank weakness but reports diffuse arm and leg stiffness and intermittent electric sensations down his spine when he flexes his neck. There is no bowel or bladder dysfunction, no recent trauma, and no neck or radicular arm pain to speak of. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.1%). Meds: lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily. Allergies: none. Social: retired electrician, 30 pack-year smoking history, quit 5 years ago; one beer most evenings. Family history: father had 'arthritis of the neck.' He has fallen twice in the last month without injury. He has no headaches, vision changes, or dysarthria.
cases.exam: Alert, normal cranial nerves and speech. Motor: subtle intrinsic hand atrophy bilaterally with reduced grip; he cannot perform rapid finger taps (clumsy). Strength is grossly 5-/5 in arms and legs. Tone is increased in the legs. Reflexes are diffusely brisk (3+) at biceps, triceps, patellae, and ankles; sustained ankle clonus on the right and bilateral upgoing toes. Hoffman sign positive bilaterally. Sensory exam shows reduced vibration and proprioception at the toes; pinprick intact. Romberg positive. Gait is wide-based and spastic. No lumbar spine tenderness; straight-leg raise negative bilaterally.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of examination findings most strongly localizes this patient's process to the cervical spinal cord?
options: Distal sensory loss and absent ankle reflexes in a stocking distribution
Hand atrophy with diffuse hyperreflexia, Hoffman sign, clonus, and upgoing toes
Resting tremor, bradykinesia, and cogwheel rigidity
Proximal weakness with preserved reflexes and elevated creatine kinase
stages.explanation: The picture of lower motor neuron signs in the hands (intrinsic atrophy, clumsiness) combined with upper motor neuron signs below the lesion (diffuse hyperreflexia, Hoffman sign, clonus, Babinski) localizes to a cervical cord compressive myelopathy. Distal sensory loss with absent reflexes describes a length-dependent peripheral neuropathy, not a myelopathy. Resting tremor, bradykinesia, and cogwheel rigidity describe parkinsonism, a basal-ganglia disorder without long-tract signs. Proximal weakness with elevated CK suggests a myopathy, which spares reflexes and produces no UMN signs or sensory changes. The mixed UMN/LMN pattern at the cervical level, plus Lhermitte phenomenon, is the hallmark of cord compression here.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the single most appropriate next diagnostic test to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Plain cervical radiographs with flexion-extension views
Electromyography and nerve conduction studies of all four limbs
MRI of the cervical spine without contrast
Non-contrast CT of the cervical spine
stages.explanation: MRI of the cervical spine is the gold standard: it directly visualizes the cord, degree of canal stenosis, disc-osteophyte complexes, and intramedullary T2 signal change indicating myelomalacia. Plain films show alignment and bony spurs but cannot image the cord or soft tissue and miss the actual compression. EMG/NCS helps exclude peripheral neuropathy or ALS but does not image the cord and is supportive, not confirmatory. Non-contrast CT shows bone well and is useful for surgical planning but is inferior to MRI for cord and ligamentous detail. Given progressive myelopathic signs, MRI is the definitive study to confirm cervical cord compression and guide surgical decision-making.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI cervical spine without contrast:
Multilevel spondylosis with disc-osteophyte complexes C4-C5, C5-C6, C6-C7
Severe central canal stenosis at C5-C6 with cord flattening
Intramedullary T2 hyperintensity at C5-C6 consistent with myelomalacia
Anteroposterior canal diameter 6 mm at C5-C6 (normal >12 mm)
No fracture, no enhancing mass, no syrinx
Modified Japanese Orthopaedic Association (mJOA) score: 12 of 18

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given moderate myelopathy with cord signal change and progressive deficits, what is the most appropriate management recommendation?
options: Continue observation with repeat MRI in 12 months
Refer for surgical decompression (e.g., anterior cervical discectomy and fusion or laminoplasty)
Prescribe a soft cervical collar and a 6-week course of oral prednisone
Begin a high-velocity cervical manipulation program with chiropractic care
stages.explanation: For moderate-to-severe cervical spondylotic myelopathy (mJOA <15) with progressive deficits and cord signal change, surgical decompression is recommended to halt progression and offer the best chance of functional stabilization or improvement. Observation is reasonable only for mild, non-progressive disease; this patient is progressing with falls. A soft collar and steroids do not relieve mechanical cord compression and steroids have no established role in chronic compressive myelopathy. Cervical manipulation is contraindicated in significant canal stenosis and risks acute neurological deterioration or cord injury. The combination of progressive signs, myelomalacia, and severe stenosis crosses the surgical referral threshold.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which statement best reflects the natural history you should convey when counseling this patient about prognosis without surgery?
options: Untreated cervical spondylotic myelopathy reliably resolves with physical therapy alone
Cervical myelopathy with cord signal change tends to follow a stepwise or progressive decline, and earlier decompression generally yields better neurological recovery
Surgery should be deferred until he develops complete quadriplegia to justify the operative risk
Symptom severity has no relationship to the timing of surgical intervention
stages.explanation: The natural history of symptomatic CSM, especially with myelomalacia, is typically stepwise or steady decline; spontaneous resolution is rare. Earlier surgical decompression, before severe or long-standing deficits develop, is associated with better functional outcomes, so timely referral matters. That option is false: PT may help adjacent symptoms but cannot reverse mechanical compression. Another option is dangerous and wrong: waiting for quadriplegia abandons the window for meaningful recovery and risks irreversible cord injury from a fall. A third option is incorrect because greater duration and severity of deficits predict poorer recovery, making symptom severity and progression central to timing. Counseling should emphasize that intervening before fixed deficits accrue optimizes outcome.

cases.diagnosis: Cervical spondylotic myelopathy (C5-C6 with myelomalacia)
pearls: Mixed signs—LMN findings in the hands plus UMN findings (hyperreflexia, Hoffman, clonus, Babinski) below the lesion—localize to a cervical compressive myelopathy.
MRI of the cervical spine is the confirmatory test; intramedullary T2 signal (myelomalacia) signals worse baseline cord injury.
Moderate-to-severe or progressive CSM (mJOA <15) with cord signal change warrants surgical decompression; observation is reserved for mild, stable disease.
Cervical manipulation is contraindicated in significant canal stenosis—it risks acute cord injury.

cases.title: Numb Saddle and a Bladder That Won't Empty
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe low back pain with new numbness 'where I sit' and inability to urinate for the past day.
cases.history: A 48-year-old warehouse worker presents to the ED with 4 days of severe low back pain after lifting a heavy pallet, which acutely worsened yesterday. Since this morning he has been unable to urinate despite a strong urge, and he noticed numbness around his scrotum, perineum, and inner thighs while wiping. He also reports new weakness lifting both feet and a sense that his legs feel 'far away.' He had a small amount of stool leakage overnight that he could not feel coming. There is no fever, no IV drug use, and no prior cancer. PMH: chronic intermittent low back pain, obesity (BMI 33). Meds: ibuprofen as needed; no anticoagulants. Allergies: none. Social: smokes half a pack daily; occasional alcohol; works in shipping with heavy lifting. Family history: noncontributory. He has taken extra ibuprofen with minimal relief. He denies recent infection, weight loss, or night sweats. The pain radiates down the backs of both legs.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable, lying still. Lumbar paraspinal tenderness without midline step-off or warmth. Motor: ankle dorsiflexion and great-toe extension 3/5 bilaterally; plantarflexion 4/5; hip flexion 4+/5. Reflexes: patellar 1+, ankle reflexes absent bilaterally. Sensory: decreased pinprick over the perineum, scrotum, and posterior thighs (saddle distribution). Anal sphincter tone is reduced and the patient cannot voluntarily squeeze; bulbocavernosus reflex absent. Straight-leg raise positive bilaterally at 30 degrees. Bladder is palpably distended and dull to percussion. Distal pulses intact; no skin changes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which bedside maneuver provides the most useful immediate objective evidence to support the suspected diagnosis and quantify urinary retention?
options: Plain abdominal radiograph to assess stool burden
Post-void residual measurement by bladder scan or catheterization
Urinalysis and urine culture
Digital examination of the prostate for size and tenderness
stages.explanation: A post-void residual (PVR) by bladder scan or in-and-out catheterization objectively documents urinary retention, a key feature of cauda equina syndrome; a PVR >200 mL strongly supports the diagnosis and relieves the distended bladder. An abdominal film for stool does not address the neurologic emergency. Urinalysis/culture screens for UTI but will not explain saddle anesthesia or sphincter weakness and should not delay imaging. Prostate exam is reasonable to assess sphincter tone and rule out prostatic obstruction but does not quantify retention; in this man the perineal sensory loss and areflexia point to a neurologic cause. PVR is the fast, high-yield bedside step while arranging definitive imaging.

stages.label: BEDSIDE & LABS
stages.content:
Post-void residual by catheterization: 750 mL
Urinalysis: no nitrites, no leukocyte esterase, few epithelial cells
WBC 8.4 ×10³/µL
CRP 6 mg/L
ESR 14 mm/hr
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Plain lumbar radiograph: degenerative changes, no fracture or destructive lesion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate and urgent next diagnostic step?
options: Emergent MRI of the lumbosacral spine
Outpatient MRI within 2 weeks once an authorization is obtained
Lumbar puncture to evaluate for inflammatory cells
CT myelogram after a trial of bed rest and analgesics
stages.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome is a surgical emergency, and emergent MRI of the lumbosacral spine is the test of choice to identify the compressive lesion (commonly a large central disc herniation) and localize the level for decompression. Delaying for an outpatient MRI is unacceptable and risks permanent bladder, bowel, and sexual dysfunction. Lumbar puncture is not indicated and could be harmful below a compressive mass. CT myelogram is a reasonable alternative only when MRI is contraindicated, and a trial of bed rest before imaging wastes the narrow therapeutic window. The mantra is clear: do not delay MRI when CES is suspected.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI lumbosacral spine without contrast:
Large central disc extrusion at L4-L5 filling the canal
Severe compression of the cauda equina nerve roots with effacement of CSF
No epidural abscess, no enhancing tumor, no hematoma
Conus medullaris terminates normally at L1; no cord signal abnormality

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive management, and within what timeframe should it ideally occur?
options: Urgent surgical decompression, ideally within 24-48 hours of symptom onset
High-dose IV methylprednisolone for 48 hours, then reassess
Inpatient physical therapy and scheduled opioids with elective surgery in 1 month
CT-guided epidural steroid injection at L4-L5
stages.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome requires urgent surgical decompression; earlier surgery (commonly targeted within 24-48 hours of onset, and as soon as feasible) gives the best chance of recovering bladder, bowel, and sexual function and preventing permanent deficits. High-dose steroids have no proven benefit in compressive CES and would delay definitive care. PT with elective surgery in a month abandons the patient to likely permanent dysfunction. An epidural steroid injection does not relieve mechanical compression and is contraindicated as primary treatment of CES. The established principle is rapid decompression once the compressive lesion is confirmed.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: After decompression, the patient still cannot sense bladder filling and has persistent saddle numbness. What is the most appropriate rehabilitation-oriented next step?
options: Leave an indwelling Foley catheter indefinitely and discharge
Initiate clean intermittent catheterization with a timed voiding/bladder program and bowel regimen, plus pelvic-floor rehabilitation
Restrict all fluids to under 500 mL/day to reduce urine output
Repeat MRI weekly until sensation returns before any rehab
stages.explanation: Persistent neurogenic bladder and bowel after CES require a structured rehabilitation program: clean intermittent catheterization protects the upper tracts and avoids chronic indwelling-catheter complications, while a timed voiding schedule, scheduled bowel regimen, and pelvic-floor rehabilitation maximize function and continence. An indefinite Foley raises infection, stone, and bladder-injury risk and is not first-line for a recovering neurogenic bladder. Severe fluid restriction risks dehydration, concentrated urine, infection, and stones without improving outcomes. Repeating MRI weekly before rehab is unnecessary and delays functional recovery; imaging is driven by clinical change, not a fixed schedule. CIC with a comprehensive bladder/bowel program is the rehabilitative standard.

cases.diagnosis: Cauda equina syndrome from a large central L4-L5 disc herniation
pearls: Saddle anesthesia, urinary retention with high post-void residual, reduced anal tone, and bilateral leg signs define cauda equina syndrome.
A post-void residual is a fast, high-yield bedside test; urinary retention with overflow incontinence is the most consistent CES finding.
CES is a surgical emergency—obtain emergent MRI and pursue urgent decompression (ideally within 24-48 hours). Do not delay imaging for outpatient scheduling or steroids.
Residual neurogenic bladder/bowel is managed with clean intermittent catheterization plus timed voiding and bowel programs, not an indefinite Foley.

cases.title: The Gymnast Whose Back Hurts When She Arches
cases.chiefComplaint: Low back pain for 3 months that is worst when arching backward during gymnastics.
cases.history: A 16-year-old competitive gymnast presents with 3 months of activity-related low back pain centered at the beltline, worse with back-walkovers, bridges, and dismounts that require extension. The pain eases with rest and is minimal in the morning. There is no leg pain, numbness, weakness, or bowel/bladder change. She denies night pain, fevers, or weight loss. Training has increased to 20 hours per week ahead of a regional meet. PMH: unremarkable; menarche at age 13, regular menses. Meds: occasional ibuprofen. Allergies: none. Social: lives with parents, honor-roll student, no tobacco/alcohol. Family history: mother with 'low back arthritis' in her 50s. She has tried rest for a few days at a time with temporary relief but the pain returns with extension-loading. She has had no acute injury or fall and reports good appetite and energy. She is anxious about missing the upcoming competition.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, comfortable at rest. Normal gait and posture. Focal tenderness over the lower lumbar paraspinals without midline step-off. Pain is reproduced with lumbar extension and worsened with single-leg standing lumbar extension (stork test) on the right. Forward flexion is pain-free with normal range. Neurologic exam is normal: 5/5 strength in both legs, normal sensation, symmetric 2+ patellar and Achilles reflexes, downgoing toes. Straight-leg raise negative bilaterally. Hamstring tightness noted. No spinal deformity, scoliosis, or skin lesions.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the history and exam, what is the most likely cause of this adolescent athlete's back pain?
options: Lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy
Pars interarticularis stress injury (spondylolysis)
Ankylosing spondylitis
Vertebral osteomyelitis
stages.explanation: Extension-related, activity-provoked low back pain in a young athlete who performs repetitive hyperextension (gymnast), with a positive single-leg hyperextension (stork) test and a normal neuro exam, is classic for a pars interarticularis stress injury (spondylolysis), which can progress to isthmic spondylolisthesis. Disc herniation typically causes radicular leg symptoms and a positive straight-leg raise, absent here. Ankylosing spondylitis causes inflammatory pain that is worst in the morning and improves with activity—the opposite pattern, and it is uncommon at this age in a girl. Vertebral osteomyelitis would usually present with night pain, fever, or constitutional symptoms, none of which are present.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
