stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study to evaluate for a suspected pars defect?
options: AP and lateral lumbar radiographs
Whole-body bone scan
Standing flexion-extension radiographs only
Lumbar discography
stages.explanation: AP and lateral lumbar radiographs are the appropriate first imaging step; the lateral view assesses for a pars defect and any vertebral slippage (spondylolisthesis) and lets you grade the slip if present. Dedicated oblique views are no longer routinely required, and if plain films are nondiagnostic but suspicion remains, MRI (preferred in adolescents to limit radiation) or CT can follow. A whole-body bone scan over-irradiates and is not the first step. Flexion-extension films alone assess instability but are an adjunct, not the initial screen. Discography evaluates discogenic pain and has no role in suspected spondylolysis. Start with plain radiographs and escalate to advanced imaging as needed.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Lateral lumbar radiograph:
Defect through the pars interarticularis at L5
Grade I anterolisthesis of L5 on S1 (approximately 20% slip)
Disc heights preserved; no fracture elsewhere
Follow-up MRI: bilateral L5 pars stress reaction with marrow edema; no nerve-root compression, no disc herniation

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this symptomatic Grade I isthmic spondylolisthesis with an active pars stress reaction, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate posterolateral spinal fusion
Activity modification with relative rest from extension-loading, followed by core and hamstring rehabilitation, with consideration of bracing
Continue full gymnastics training and reassess in 6 months
Long-term bed rest and opioids until the defect fully ossifies
stages.explanation: Symptomatic spondylolysis/low-grade isthmic spondylolisthesis is managed conservatively first: relative rest from pain-provoking hyperextension activities, a graded core-stabilization and hamstring-flexibility program, and consideration of a brace (e.g., anti-lordotic) for persistent pain, with return to sport once pain-free and rehabilitated. Surgery/fusion is reserved for refractory pain, high-grade or progressive slip, or neurologic deficit—not first-line low-grade disease. Continuing full training perpetuates the stress injury and risks slip progression. Prolonged bed rest with opioids causes deconditioning and dependence and is not indicated; activity modification, not immobilization, is the goal. Conservative, rehab-focused care resolves most cases.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate counseling regarding return to gymnastics and follow-up?
options: She may return to full competition immediately with an over-the-counter brace
She should permanently retire from all athletics
Gradual, criteria-based return once she is pain-free with extension and has completed rehab, with monitoring of the slip on follow-up imaging
Return is determined solely by a fixed 2-week calendar regardless of symptoms
stages.explanation: Return to sport should be gradual and criteria-based: she resumes progressive activity once she is pain-free (including with extension loading) and has restored core strength and flexibility, with periodic clinical follow-up and imaging to ensure the low-grade slip is not progressing. Immediate full competition with a brace ignores the need for symptom resolution and rehabilitation and risks reinjury. Permanent retirement is unnecessary for low-grade disease that typically does well with conservative care. A fixed 2-week calendar ignores individual healing and symptom status; recovery time varies. Criteria-based progression plus surveillance for slip progression is the appropriate, balanced plan.

cases.diagnosis: Isthmic spondylolisthesis (L5 pars defect with Grade I slip)
pearls: Extension-related back pain in an adolescent who repetitively hyperextends (gymnast, diver, lineman), with a positive stork test and normal neuro exam, suggests spondylolysis/isthmic spondylolisthesis.
Start with lateral lumbar radiographs to detect the pars defect and grade any slip; MRI is preferred over CT in adolescents to limit radiation while assessing a stress reaction.

Low-grade symptomatic disease is treated conservatively: relative rest from extension-loading, core/hamstring rehab, and bracing as needed—fusion is reserved for refractory, high-grade, progressive, or neurologically symptomatic cases.
Return to sport is criteria-based (pain-free, rehabbed) with imaging surveillance for slip progression—not dictated by the calendar alone.

cases.title: Stiff Back That Loosens Up as the Day Goes On
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of low back pain and morning stiffness that improves with movement.
cases.history: A 27-year-old man reports more than 6 months of insidious low back and buttock pain. The pain and stiffness are worst in the early morning and after sitting, lasting over an hour, and they improve markedly with exercise and a hot shower. He sometimes wakes in the second half of the night with back pain. He denies preceding trauma, leg radiation, weakness, or bowel/bladder changes. He had one episode of a painful red eye last year that resolved with drops. PMH: otherwise healthy. Meds: ibuprofen 400 mg as needed, with partial relief. Allergies: none. Social: works at a desk, plays recreational soccer, never smoked, drinks socially. Family history: a maternal uncle with 'fused spine' and an aunt with psoriasis. Review of systems notable for occasional heel pain at the Achilles insertion and mild fatigue; no diarrhea, no urethral discharge, no skin rash currently. He is frustrated that NSAIDs only partly help and worries about long-term stiffness.
cases.exam: Fit young man, no acute distress. Reduced lumbar range of motion with a modified Schober test of 3 cm (reduced; normal >5 cm increase). Tenderness over both sacroiliac joints; FABER test reproduces buttock pain bilaterally. Mild tenderness at the right Achilles insertion (enthesitis). Chest expansion is mildly reduced at 3 cm. Neurologic exam is normal: full strength, intact sensation, symmetric reflexes, negative straight-leg raise. No peripheral joint swelling, no psoriatic plaques, no dactylitis. Eyes currently white and quiet.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which features of this patient's history most strongly suggest inflammatory rather than mechanical back pain?
options: Pain worse with activity and relieved by rest, with no morning stiffness
Insidious onset before age 40, morning stiffness over an hour, improvement with exercise, and night pain
Acute onset after heavy lifting with radiation down the leg
Pain localized to a single point that resolves completely within 48 hours
stages.explanation: Inflammatory back pain is defined by insidious onset before age 40, duration over 3 months, prolonged morning stiffness (>30-60 minutes), improvement with exercise but not rest, and night pain (often second half of the night). This patient fits all of these, plus a history suggesting acute anterior uveitis and enthesitis. That option describes mechanical pain (worse with activity, better with rest, minimal stiffness). Another option describes acute discogenic/radicular pain. A third option describes a transient, self-limited mechanical strain. Recognizing the inflammatory pattern—and associated extra-articular features—is the key to suspecting axial spondyloarthritis here.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial set of investigations to evaluate suspected axial spondyloarthritis?
options: Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies only
Pelvic (sacroiliac) imaging plus inflammatory markers and HLA-B27 testing
Lumbar discography and EMG
Serum uric acid and joint aspiration for crystals
stages.explanation: Evaluation of suspected axial spondyloarthritis centers on imaging of the sacroiliac joints (plain pelvic radiographs first; MRI of the SI joints if films are normal but suspicion is high, to detect early sacroiliitis), plus inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) and HLA-B27, which supports the diagnosis in the right clinical context. RF and anti-CCP target rheumatoid arthritis and are typically negative here. Discography/EMG evaluate discogenic pain and radiculopathy, not spondyloarthritis. Uric acid and crystal analysis evaluate gout, which does not fit this axial, inflammatory presentation. SI joint imaging with markers and HLA-B27 is the high-yield workup.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR 32 mm/hr (elevated)
CRP 18 mg/L (elevated)
HLA-B27: positive
Rheumatoid factor: negative
Anti-CCP: negative
AP pelvis radiograph: bilateral grade 2 sacroiliitis with erosions and sclerosis
MRI SI joints: bone marrow edema in both sacroiliac joints

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for this patient with active axial spondyloarthritis?
options: Systemic glucocorticoids tapered over 6 weeks
Scheduled NSAIDs combined with a structured physical therapy program emphasizing posture, spinal mobility, and exercise
Strict bed rest until inflammatory markers normalize
Immediate initiation of a TNF inhibitor before any other therapy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of axial spondyloarthritis combines regularly dosed NSAIDs with a structured exercise and physical therapy program focused on posture, spinal mobility, stretching, and aerobic conditioning—the cornerstone for preserving function and reducing stiffness. Systemic glucocorticoids have limited efficacy in axial disease and are not first-line. Bed rest worsens stiffness and deconditioning and is exactly the wrong advice, since movement improves symptoms. TNF inhibitors are effective and indicated when patients have persistently high disease activity despite adequate NSAID trials, but they follow, rather than precede, NSAIDs plus exercise. The posture/mobility program is central to long-term outcomes.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Six months later he reports persistently high disease activity and night pain despite adherence to two different NSAIDs at full dose and his exercise program. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Add chronic daily oral opioids for pain control
Escalate to a biologic DMARD such as a TNF inhibitor (or IL-17 inhibitor)
Start methotrexate monotherapy for axial disease
Discontinue exercise because it is no longer helping
stages.explanation: When axial spondyloarthritis remains highly active despite adequate trials of at least two NSAIDs plus exercise, guidelines recommend escalating to a biologic—typically a TNF inhibitor or an IL-17 inhibitor—which effectively reduces axial inflammation and symptoms. Chronic opioids do not modify disease, carry dependence risk, and are not recommended for inflammatory axial pain. Methotrexate lacks efficacy for purely axial spondyloarthritis (it is used for peripheral arthritis), so it is not the answer here. Discontinuing exercise is counterproductive; the exercise program should continue alongside biologic therapy. Biologic escalation after failed NSAIDs is the guideline-concordant next step.

cases.diagnosis: Ankylosing spondylitis (radiographic axial spondyloarthritis), HLA-B27 positive
pearls: Inflammatory back pain: insidious onset before 40, morning stiffness >30-60 min, improvement with exercise (not rest), and night pain—opposite of mechanical pain.
Look for associated features: acute anterior uveitis, enthesitis, dactylitis, and a family history of spondyloarthritis or psoriasis.
Workup centers on SI joint imaging (plain films, then MRI if needed) plus ESR/CRP and HLA-B27; RF and anti-CCP are typically negative.
First-line therapy is scheduled NSAIDs plus a posture/mobility exercise program; escalate to a TNF or IL-17 inhibitor if disease stays active after two adequate NSAID trials.

cases.title: Sudden Mid-Back Pain After Bending to Garden
cases.chiefComplaint: Sharp mid-back pain that began suddenly after light gardening 5 days ago.
cases.history: A 77-year-old woman presents with 5 days of sharp, localized mid-back pain that began after she bent to pull weeds. The pain is worse with standing, walking, coughing, and rolling in bed, and better lying still. She denies a fall or direct trauma. There is no leg weakness, numbness, saddle anesthesia, or bowel/bladder dysfunction, and no fever or weight loss. She has lost about 4 cm of height over recent years. PMH: postmenopausal since age 50, prior wrist (Colles) fracture at 70, hypothyroidism, GERD. Meds: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, omeprazole 20 mg daily, calcium 600 mg with vitamin D daily; takes no bisphosphonate. Allergies: sulfa (rash). Social: lives alone, former smoker (quit at 60), minimal alcohol, sedentary. Family history: mother had a 'dowager's hump' and a hip fracture. She has been taking acetaminophen with limited relief and is afraid to move much. She has had no prior spine imaging and no glucocorticoid use.
cases.exam: Thin, mildly kyphotic woman in pain with movement. Focal tenderness to percussion over the mid-thoracic spinous processes (around T8). No paraspinal mass, warmth, or erythema. Neurologic exam intact: 5/5 strength in both legs, normal sensation including the perineum, symmetric 2+ reflexes, downgoing toes, negative straight-leg raise, normal anal tone. Gait is cautious and antalgic but stable. No abdominal tenderness or pulsatile mass; distal pulses normal. No costovertebral angle tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the presentation, what is the most appropriate initial imaging study?
options: MRI of the thoracic spine with contrast
Plain thoracic spine radiographs (AP and lateral)
Whole-body PET-CT
Bone scan of the entire skeleton
stages.explanation: Plain AP and lateral thoracic radiographs are the appropriate first study to evaluate suspected osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture: they show vertebral height loss and wedging at low cost and radiation. MRI with contrast is reserved for red flags (neurologic deficit, suspected malignancy or infection) or to distinguish acute from chronic fractures and assess marrow edema when management hinges on it. Whole-body PET-CT is for malignancy staging, not an uncomplicated fragility fracture. A bone scan can date a fracture but is not the initial screen. With a classic mechanical fragility presentation and no neurologic red flags, start with plain films.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Thoracic spine radiographs (AP and lateral):
Anterior wedge compression fracture of T8 with approximately 30% height loss
Diffuse osteopenia
No posterior cortex retropulsion, no canal compromise
No lytic or blastic lesions; pedicles intact
Mild multilevel degenerative changes

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this acute, neurologically intact osteoporotic compression fracture, what is the most appropriate initial management plan?
options: Strict bed rest for 6 weeks with high-dose opioids
Multimodal analgesia, early mobilization with activity as tolerated, and consideration of a thoracolumbar orthosis (brace) for pain
Urgent vertebroplasty on the day of diagnosis for all patients
Spinal fusion of T7-T9
stages.explanation: Most acute osteoporotic compression fractures without neurologic compromise are managed conservatively: multimodal pain control (acetaminophen, short-course NSAIDs if tolerated, judicious analgesics, and calcitonin in selected cases), early mobilization to avoid deconditioning, and a thoracolumbar orthosis for comfort and posture in some patients. Strict prolonged bed rest with high-dose opioids accelerates bone loss, muscle wasting, DVT, and delirium and should be avoided. Vertebroplasty/kyphoplasty is considered for refractory pain after a conservative trial, not reflexively for everyone on day one. Fusion is overtreatment for a stable fragility fracture without instability or deficit. Pain control plus early movement and selective bracing is the standard.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Recognizing this as a fragility fracture, what is the most appropriate bone-health workup and treatment step?
options: No further workup is needed because she already takes calcium
Obtain a DXA scan and basic secondary-workup labs (calcium, 25-OH vitamin D, renal/liver function, TSH), then start antiresorptive therapy such as a bisphosphonate
Start a TNF inhibitor for bone protection
Recommend daily megadose vitamin A supplementation
stages.explanation: A fragility fracture (low-energy vertebral fracture) is itself diagnostic of osteoporosis and mandates a bone-health evaluation: DXA for baseline bone density and labs to screen for secondary causes (serum calcium, 25-OH vitamin D, renal and hepatic function, TSH, and others as indicated), followed by pharmacologic therapy—commonly a bisphosphonate—to reduce future fracture risk. Doing nothing because she takes calcium misses a high-risk patient who needs antiresorptive therapy. A TNF inhibitor treats inflammatory disease, not osteoporosis. Megadose vitamin A is harmful and can worsen bone loss. Workup plus antiresorptive treatment is essential after a fragility fracture.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which additional intervention best addresses her ongoing fracture and fall risk?
options: Advise her to avoid all physical activity permanently
Arrange physical therapy for posture, back-extensor strengthening, balance/fall-prevention training, plus a home safety and fall-risk assessment
Prescribe long-term benzodiazepines for muscle relaxation
Recommend a wheelchair for all mobility going forward
stages.explanation: Comprehensive rehabilitation reduces future fractures: physical therapy emphasizing posture, back-extensor strengthening, and balance/gait training, combined with a home-safety and fall-risk assessment (lighting, rugs, footwear, assistive devices, medication review), targets the modifiable drivers of recurrent fragility fractures. Avoiding all activity accelerates bone loss and deconditioning—movement is protective. Long-term benzodiazepines increase fall and fracture risk and cause sedation in older adults, so they are contraindicated. A wheelchair for all mobility promotes immobility and worsens bone and muscle health. Active rehabilitation and fall prevention complete the management plan.

cases.diagnosis: Acute osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture (T8)
pearls: Sudden focal back pain after minor exertion in an older adult with risk factors and height loss suggests an osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture; start with plain radiographs if neurologically intact.
Manage uncomplicated fractures conservatively: multimodal analgesia, early mobilization, and selective bracing—avoid prolonged bed rest and high-dose opioids.
A fragility fracture is diagnostic of osteoporosis—obtain DXA and secondary-cause labs and start antiresorptive therapy (e.g., a bisphosphonate).
Add posture and back-extensor strengthening, balance/fall-prevention training, and a home-safety assessment to reduce recurrent fractures.

cases.title: Groin Pain That Stops Him Putting On Socks
cases.chiefComplaint: Right groin pain and stiffness that makes it hard to put on his shoes and socks.
cases.history: A 65-year-old man reports 2 years of gradually worsening right groin pain that is worst with weight-bearing and at the end of the day, with stiffness after sitting that loosens within minutes of walking. The pain radiates to the anterior thigh and occasionally the knee. He has trouble putting on socks and shoes and getting out of a low car. There is no back pain, leg numbness, weakness, or bowel/bladder change, and no night pain at rest until recently. PMH: obesity (BMI 31), hypertension, prior right hip labral injury playing rugby in his 20s. Meds: amlodipine 5 mg daily; acetaminophen and intermittent ibuprofen with partial relief. Allergies: none. Social: retired contractor, former smoker, social drinker, walks his dog but distance is declining. Family history: mother had a hip replacement. He is increasingly limping and worried about needing surgery. He has had no fevers, recent infection, or steroid use.
cases.exam: Antalgic gait favoring the right leg with a positive Trendelenburg sign on the right. Right hip range of motion reduced and painful, especially internal rotation and abduction; the log-roll test reproduces groin pain. FABER reproduces anterior groin pain. No tenderness over the greater trochanter or lumbar spine. Neurovascular exam intact: 5/5 strength, normal sensation, symmetric reflexes, negative straight-leg raise, palpable distal pulses. No effusion, warmth, or erythema; leg lengths equal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination finding most specifically points to intra-articular hip pathology as the source of his pain?
options: Tenderness directly over the greater trochanter
Groin pain reproduced by loss of internal rotation on log-roll and FABER testing
Positive straight-leg raise at 30 degrees
Point tenderness over the lumbar paraspinal muscles
stages.explanation: Groin pain reproduced by hip internal rotation (log-roll) and FABER, with painful, restricted internal rotation, localizes the problem to inside the hip joint—the classic pattern of hip osteoarthritis. Greater trochanteric tenderness indicates greater trochanteric pain syndrome/bursitis, a periarticular cause with lateral hip pain. A positive straight-leg raise suggests lumbar radiculopathy, not hip joint disease. Lumbar paraspinal tenderness points to a spinal source. Because hip and spine pathology often coexist in older adults, finding pain with internal rotation and a normal neuro exam helps confirm the hip as the pain generator and directs imaging appropriately.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study?
options: MRI of the hip with contrast arthrogram
Weight-bearing AP pelvis and lateral hip radiographs
Lumbar spine MRI
Nuclear bone scan
stages.explanation: Weight-bearing plain radiographs (AP pelvis and a lateral view) are the first-line study for suspected hip osteoarthritis, demonstrating joint-space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis, and cysts, and they correlate with disease severity. MR arthrography is reserved for suspected labral tears or early disease when films are normal, not as the initial study in clear OA. Lumbar spine MRI is appropriate only if the history/exam suggest a spinal source, which is not the case here. A bone scan is nonspecific and not the initial test. Standing radiographs are quick, inexpensive, and diagnostic for typical hip OA.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Weight-bearing AP pelvis and lateral right hip radiographs:
Superior joint-space narrowing of the right hip
Subchondral sclerosis and marginal osteophytes
Small subchondral cysts in the femoral head
No fracture, no lytic lesion, no avascular necrosis
Left hip and sacroiliac joints unremarkable

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management before considering surgery?
options: Immediate total hip arthroplasty for all symptomatic patients
A structured program of exercise/physical therapy, weight loss, activity modification, and analgesia (acetaminophen and/or NSAIDs)
Long-term daily opioids
Strict immobilization of the hip in a brace
stages.explanation: First-line management of hip osteoarthritis is conservative and multimodal: land- or water-based exercise and physical therapy to strengthen hip and core muscles, weight loss to reduce joint load, activity modification, gait aids when needed, and analgesia with acetaminophen and/or NSAIDs (topical or oral). Immediate arthroplasty skips effective nonoperative care and is reserved for those who fail conservative measures. Long-term opioids are not recommended for chronic OA given limited benefit and significant harms. Immobilization in a brace promotes stiffness and muscle atrophy and is not used for hip OA. Exercise-based prehabilitation also improves outcomes if surgery is later needed.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After a year of conservative care he now has constant pain, night pain, and inability to walk more than a block, with severe joint-space loss. What best determines the timing of total hip arthroplasty?
options: His chronological age alone, deferring surgery until he is over 80
Persistent pain and functional limitation refractory to optimized nonoperative management, weighed against his health and goals
The radiographic grade in isolation, regardless of symptoms
Proceeding only after he becomes completely nonambulatory
stages.explanation: Arthroplasty timing is driven primarily by symptoms and function: persistent pain (including night/rest pain) and functional limitation that no longer respond to optimized conservative care, with end-stage radiographic disease, justify surgery, individualized to the patient's overall health, activity goals, and shared decision-making. Age alone should not bar surgery; healthy older patients do well, and arbitrary deferral prolongs disability. Radiographic grade in isolation is not the trigger—imaging and symptoms can be discordant. Waiting until he is nonambulatory leads to deconditioning and worse postoperative recovery. The right time is when refractory symptoms and function, not the calendar, demand it.

cases.diagnosis: Hip osteoarthritis (right)
pearls: Hip OA causes groin pain with early loss of internal rotation; pain on log-roll/FABER and a positive Trendelenburg point to intra-articular disease.
Weight-bearing AP pelvis radiographs are first-line and show joint-space narrowing, osteophytes, sclerosis, and cysts.
First-line treatment is exercise/physical therapy, weight loss, activity modification, and acetaminophen/NSAIDs—avoid chronic opioids.
Total hip arthroplasty timing is driven by refractory pain and functional limitation and patient goals, not by age or radiographic grade alone.

cases.title: Sore, Stiff Hands That Won't Open the Jar
cases.chiefComplaint: Painful, stiff hands with early finger drifting that make daily tasks difficult.
cases.history: A 54-year-old woman with seropositive rheumatoid arthritis diagnosed 3 years ago presents for help with hand function. She has symmetric pain and swelling of the MCP and PIP joints, more than an hour of morning stiffness, and difficulty opening jars, turning keys, and buttoning clothes. She has noticed her fingers starting to drift toward the little-finger side and her grip weakening. There is no acute red, hot, single joint to suggest infection, and no fever. Disease activity has improved on her DMARD but hand mechanics remain a problem. PMH: RA, hypothyroidism. Meds: methotrexate 20 mg weekly with folic acid, hydroxychloroquine 200 mg twice daily, levothyroxine; occasional NSAIDs. Allergies: penicillin (hives). Social: works as a bookkeeper (lots of typing), nonsmoker, lives with spouse. Family history: mother with RA. She is frustrated that pain and deformity limit her work and self-care and asks what she can do day to day, beyond medications, to protect her joints.
cases.exam: Symmetric soft-tissue swelling and tenderness of the second and third MCPs and several PIPs with mild early ulnar deviation at the MCPs; no fixed contractures yet. Reduced grip strength bilaterally. Wrists mildly swollen with reduced extension. No single hot, erythematous joint; no draining sinus. DIP joints relatively spared. Neurovascular exam intact: normal sensation including median nerve distribution, negative Tinel at the wrists, intact capillary refill. No rheumatoid nodules on the elbows today.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Beyond optimizing her DMARD therapy, which rehabilitation-focused intervention best addresses her difficulty with daily tasks and emerging deformity?
options: Complete rest of the hands with avoidance of all activity
Referral to occupational therapy for joint-protection education, adaptive equipment, and splinting
Immediate surgical MCP arthroplasty for all affected joints
High-impact resistance training to 'work through' the pain
stages.explanation: Occupational therapy is central to functional management of RA hands: it teaches joint-protection principles, provides adaptive equipment, and supplies splints to support alignment and reduce pain, directly targeting her difficulty with jars, keys, and buttons and her early ulnar drift. Complete rest/inactivity leads to stiffness, weakness, and contracture; the goal is protected activity, not immobility. Immediate MCP arthroplasty for all joints is premature—surgery is considered for refractory pain or advanced, fixed deformity, not early changes. High-impact 'work through the pain' training can aggravate inflamed joints and accelerate damage. OT-led joint protection, equipment, and splinting is the appropriate next step.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which set of joint-protection principles should the occupational therapist emphasize?
options: Use a tight pinch grip for prolonged periods and carry loads on individual fingers
Use larger/stronger joints and both hands for tasks, avoid sustained tight grips, distribute loads, and use built-up handles and labor-saving tools
Maintain static positions for long periods to 'rest' the joints
Apply maximal force quickly to finish tasks faster
stages.explanation: Core joint-protection principles aim to reduce deforming forces: use the largest/strongest available joints (e.g., palms and forearms rather than fingertips), use two hands, avoid sustained tight grips and ulnar-deviating forces, distribute load over multiple joints, pace activities with rest breaks, and use adaptive tools such as built-up grips, jar openers, and key turners. A prolonged tight pinch and loading single fingers increases stress on small joints and promotes ulnar drift. Holding static positions for long periods causes stiffness and is not 'rest' in the protective sense. Applying maximal rapid force increases joint stress and risks injury. Energy conservation and load distribution preserve function.

stages.label: INTERVENTION
stages.question: Which splinting approach is most appropriate for her early MCP ulnar drift and weak grip during daily activities?
options: Rigid casting of both wrists and fingers in full extension continuously
Functional/working splints (e.g., resting hand splints for night and ulnar-deviation or wrist supports for tasks) to support alignment and reduce pain
No splinting because splints have no role in rheumatoid arthritis
A splint that locks all finger joints permanently in flexion
stages.explanation: Appropriate splinting in RA is task- and time-specific: resting hand splints (often worn at night) reduce pain and support joint position, while functional working splints—such as ulnar-deviation supports and wrist splints—improve alignment, stability, and grip during activities, helping counter early ulnar drift. Continuous rigid casting in full extension immobilizes joints, causing stiffness and atrophy, and is not used. Saying splints have no role is incorrect—splinting is a recognized adjunct for symptom relief and support. Locking joints permanently in flexion would induce fixed contractures, the opposite of the goal. Selective resting and functional splints are the right tools.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which additional measure best supports long-term hand function and her work as a bookkeeper?
options: Advise her to stop using her hands for typing entirely and go on permanent disability
Provide a gentle ROM and hand-strengthening program plus ergonomic workstation modifications, and coordinate ongoing rheumatology disease-activity control
Recommend daily prolonged heat with no exercise
Switch to twice-daily opioids for pain to keep working
stages.explanation: Long-term hand function is best preserved by combining a gentle, regular range-of-motion and strengthening program (to maintain mobility and grip without flaring joints), ergonomic modifications at her workstation (supportive keyboard/mouse, breaks, adaptive tools), and continued tight control of disease activity with rheumatology, since controlling inflammation prevents further deformity. Stopping all hand use and going on disability is excessive and worsens deconditioning when work is feasible with accommodations. Prolonged heat without exercise gives only transient relief and neglects mobility. Twice-daily opioids are not appropriate for chronic inflammatory joint pain and carry dependence risk. Integrated rehab, ergonomics, and disease control sustain function.

cases.diagnosis: Rheumatoid arthritis with early hand deformity (functional management)
pearls: Functional management of RA hands centers on occupational therapy: joint protection, adaptive equipment, and splinting—alongside medical disease control.
Joint-protection principles: use larger/stronger joints, use two hands, avoid sustained tight grips and ulnar-deviating forces, distribute loads, pace activities, and use built-up tools.
Splinting is selective and functional—resting hand splints at night and working/ulnar-deviation splints for tasks—not continuous rigid immobilization.
Combine gentle ROM/strengthening and ergonomics with ongoing rheumatology control; surgery is reserved for refractory pain or advanced fixed deformity.

cases.title: A New Knee That Won't Bend Far Enough
cases.chiefComplaint: Difficulty bending the operated knee during rehabilitation after knee replacement.
cases.history: A 69-year-old woman is seen for rehabilitation after a right total knee arthroplasty performed for end-stage osteoarthritis. She is now several days postoperative, working on range of motion, strengthening, and gait with an assistive device. Pain is controlled with a multimodal regimen, and she is motivated but anxious about 'doing it right.' There is no calf pain, no chest pain or dyspnea, and no wound drainage or fever. PMH: osteoarthritis, hypertension, type 2 diabetes (well controlled), obesity (BMI 32). Meds: lisinopril, metformin, acetaminophen scheduled, short-course oxycodone for breakthrough pain, and prescribed VTE prophylaxis. Allergies: none. Social: lives with her husband in a two-story home with a stair rail, retired teacher, nonsmoker. Family history: noncontributory. She asks what range of motion she should be reaching and how to know if she is falling behind. The surgical wound is clean and dry with staples intact.
cases.exam: Right knee with a clean, dry incision; mild expected swelling and warmth, no spreading erythema or purulent drainage. Active range of motion approximately 5 to 75 degrees with discomfort at end range; able to perform a straight-leg raise with minimal extensor lag. Quadriceps strength 4/5. Distal pulses intact, sensation normal, no calf tenderness, Homans sign negative. Ambulates with a walker, partial improvement in gait. Contralateral knee unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which early postoperative rehabilitation goals are most appropriate after total knee arthroplasty?
options: Strict bed rest with the knee immobilized until staples are removed
Early mobilization with progressive range of motion, quadriceps activation/strengthening, edema control, and gait training
Immediate return to running and jumping within the first week
Avoidance of all knee flexion to protect the prosthesis
stages.explanation: Modern TKA rehabilitation emphasizes early mobilization: progressive range-of-motion work (both flexion and extension), quadriceps activation and strengthening (straight-leg raises, quad sets), edema management, and gait training with an assistive device—all begun promptly to prevent stiffness and deconditioning. Strict bed rest with immobilization is exactly what causes arthrofibrosis and weakness. Returning to running and jumping in the first week is unsafe and far too aggressive for healing tissues. Avoiding all flexion guarantees a stiff knee and poor function; regaining flexion is a primary goal. Early, graded motion and strengthening optimize outcomes.

stages.label: MILESTONES
stages.question: Approximately what knee flexion range of motion is a commonly cited functional target to achieve in the early weeks after TKA (needed for daily activities like sitting and stair use)?
options: About 45 degrees of flexion
At least roughly 90 degrees early, progressing toward about 110-120 degrees of flexion
Full 160 degrees of flexion by postoperative day 3
Flexion is irrelevant; only full extension matters
stages.explanation: A practical early milestone is achieving at least about 90 degrees of flexion in the first weeks—enough for sitting and basic stair negotiation—while progressing toward roughly 110-120 degrees for fuller function; achieving and maintaining full extension is equally important. About 45 degrees is inadequate for daily tasks and signals lagging progress. A full 160 degrees by day 3 is unrealistic and not expected even long term in most replaced knees. Saying flexion is irrelevant is wrong—both adequate flexion and full extension are required for normal gait, sitting, and stairs. Tracking these targets helps identify patients who are falling behind.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.content:
Reassessment at postoperative week 6:
Active range of motion only 5 to 70 degrees despite adherence to therapy
Persistent stiffness with a firm end-feel to flexion
No erythema, no warmth beyond expected, no effusion suggesting infection
WBC 7.8 ×10³/µL, CRP trending down
Radiographs: components well-positioned, no loosening or hardware problem

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: At 6 weeks she remains stuck at roughly 70 degrees of flexion with a firm block despite diligent therapy and no signs of infection. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue the same home exercises unchanged and reassess in 6 months
Refer to the surgeon for evaluation for manipulation under anesthesia (MUA) for arthrofibrosis/stiffness
Begin long-term opioids and stop physical therapy
Immediately revise the prosthesis
stages.explanation: Persistent flexion limitation (e.g., not reaching about 90 degrees) at around 6 weeks despite adequate therapy, with a firm mechanical end-feel and no infection or component malposition, suggests arthrofibrosis and warrants surgeon referral for manipulation under anesthesia—most effective when performed within roughly the first 6-12 weeks before scar matures. Continuing unchanged for 6 months risks permanent stiffness by missing the MUA window. Long-term opioids and stopping therapy worsen function and address neither the scar nor motion. Immediate revision is far more invasive and not indicated when components are well-positioned; MUA is the appropriate, less invasive step first. Recognizing the window for MUA is key.

cases.diagnosis: Total knee arthroplasty rehabilitation with postoperative stiffness (arthrofibrosis) requiring consideration of manipulation under anesthesia
pearls: Early TKA rehab emphasizes prompt mobilization: progressive ROM (flexion and full extension), quad activation/strengthening, edema control, and gait training.
Aim for at least ~90 degrees of flexion early, progressing toward ~110-120 degrees, while restoring full extension for normal gait and stairs.
Persistent stiffness (failing to reach ~90 degrees) at ~6 weeks despite good therapy, with a firm end-feel and no infection, suggests arthrofibrosis.
Refer for manipulation under anesthesia within roughly the first 6-12 weeks before scar matures—don't wait months.

cases.title: New Hip, Big Rules About How to Move
cases.chiefComplaint: Learning how to move safely after hip replacement to avoid dislocating the new joint.
cases.history: A 72-year-old man is admitted to the inpatient rehabilitation unit after a right total hip arthroplasty via a posterior approach for advanced osteoarthritis. He is progressing with mobility but needs to learn precautions and safe transfers. He denies hip 'giving way,' new severe pain, or a sensation of the joint slipping. There is no calf pain, chest pain, dyspnea, fever, or wound drainage. PMH: hip OA, hypertension, BPH, mild cognitive complaints but oriented. Meds: amlodipine, tamsulosin, acetaminophen scheduled, short-course opioid for breakthrough pain, and VTE prophylaxis as prescribed. Allergies: none. Social: lives alone in a single-story home, retired accountant, uses no alcohol, former smoker. Family history: noncontributory. He is eager to go home and asks which movements he must avoid and how to manage stairs, chairs, and his bed safely. The incision is clean and dry, and leg lengths appear equal.
cases.exam: Posterolateral hip incision clean and dry without erythema or drainage. Right hip with expected mild soft-tissue swelling; able to perform assisted straight-leg raise. Strength 4/5 about the hip; sensation intact; distal pulses normal; no calf tenderness. Legs of equal length, no rotational deformity. Ambulates with a walker and standby assistance; transfers improving. Neurologic exam grossly intact; no foot drop. Contralateral lower extremity unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After a posterior-approach total hip arthroplasty, which set of hip precautions should be taught to reduce dislocation risk?
options: Avoid hip flexion beyond 90 degrees, avoid adduction past midline, and avoid internal rotation of the operated hip
Avoid all hip extension and avoid any external rotation
Keep the hip in maximal flexion and crossed-leg positions at all times
There are no movement precautions after hip replacement
stages.explanation: The posterior surgical approach disrupts the posterior capsule and external rotators, so dislocation risk is highest with the combined position of hip flexion past 90 degrees, adduction across midline, and internal rotation; precautions therefore prohibit exactly those movements. That option (avoiding extension and external rotation) describes precautions relevant to an anterior approach, not posterior, and would mis-instruct this patient. Keeping the hip maximally flexed and crossing the legs reproduces the highest-risk dislocation positions and is dangerous. Saying there are no precautions is incorrect and exposes the patient to early dislocation. Matching precautions to the surgical approach is essential.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which practical strategies and equipment best help him honor posterior hip precautions during early mobility and self-care?
options: A low soft sofa, sitting on the floor, and bending fully at the waist to dress
An elevated toilet seat, a firm chair with armrests, an abduction pillow in bed, and long-handled reachers/sock aids with a hip-kit
Crossing the legs to put on shoes and twisting the trunk over the operated hip
Deep squatting to pick items off the floor
stages.explanation: Adaptive equipment lets him perform daily tasks without violating precautions: an elevated toilet seat and a firm high chair with armrests keep hip flexion under 90 degrees, an abduction pillow prevents adduction past midline in bed, and long-handled reachers, sock aids, and a long shoehorn (a 'hip kit') allow dressing and retrieving objects without bending past 90 degrees. A low soft sofa, floor-sitting, and bending fully at the waist force excessive flexion and risk dislocation. Crossing the legs and twisting over the hip combine adduction and rotation—high-risk positions. Deep squatting exceeds safe flexion. Proper equipment makes precaution-adherent function possible.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During a transfer he suddenly develops severe groin/buttock pain, the leg appears shortened and internally rotated, and he cannot bear weight. What is the most likely complication and the appropriate immediate action?
options: Likely simple muscle strain; continue therapy and reassess tomorrow
Likely prosthetic hip dislocation; stop mobilization, keep him still, and obtain urgent radiographs for reduction
Likely deep vein thrombosis; start anticoagulation and ambulate immediately
Likely wound infection; begin antibiotics and continue transfers
stages.explanation: A sudden severe pain with a shortened, internally rotated, and adducted leg and inability to bear weight after a posterior THA is the classic picture of a posterior prosthetic dislocation; the correct immediate action is to stop mobilizing, keep the patient still and the limb supported, and obtain urgent radiographs to confirm and arrange prompt closed reduction. A muscle strain would not deform the limb or cause sudden inability to weight-bear and delaying care risks neurovascular injury. DVT causes calf swelling/pain, not limb-length and rotational deformity, and ambulating a dislocated hip is harmful. Wound infection presents subacutely with erythema/drainage, not acute deformity. Recognizing dislocation enables timely reduction.

cases.diagnosis: Total hip arthroplasty (posterior approach) rehabilitation with dislocation prevention
pearls: Posterior-approach THA precautions: avoid hip flexion >90 degrees, adduction past midline, and internal rotation—the combined position that dislocates.
Match precautions to the surgical approach; anterior-approach patients instead avoid extension and external rotation.
Adaptive equipment (elevated toilet seat, firm armchair, abduction pillow, reacher/sock aid hip-kit) enables safe self-care within precautions.
A shortened, internally rotated, adducted, non-weight-bearing leg with sudden pain signals posterior dislocation—immobilize and get urgent radiographs for reduction.

cases.title: Cleared by the Calendar, but Is the Knee Ready?
cases.chiefComplaint: Wants to return to competitive soccer after knee ligament reconstruction.
cases.history: A 21-year-old collegiate soccer player presents about 7 months after right ACL reconstruction with a hamstring autograft following a noncontact pivoting injury. She has completed structured rehabilitation, has no pain or swelling, and feels 'ready,' but her physical therapist flagged residual quadriceps weakness on the operated side. She denies giving-way, locking, or instability with daily activities. There is no new injury. PMH: prior ACL tear (current), otherwise healthy; no prior surgeries. Meds: none regularly; occasional ibuprofen. Allergies: none. Social: full-time student-athlete, nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, highly motivated to return for the upcoming season. Family history: noncontributory. She has been told by teammates that 'six months means you're cleared,' and she wants to know whether time alone is enough or whether she must meet specific tests before returning to cutting and pivoting sports. She has been doing jogging and light agility drills.
cases.exam: Well-healed arthroscopic portals. No effusion. Full, symmetric knee extension and flexion. Ligament exam stable: negative Lachman and anterior drawer, negative pivot-shift, stable to varus/valgus. Notable quadriceps atrophy of the operated thigh with measurable girth difference and weaker single-leg performance on that side. Single-leg hop testing shows reduced limb symmetry. Patellofemoral exam nontender. Neurovascular exam intact; gait normal without antalgia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate basis for clearing this athlete to return to cutting and pivoting sports?
options: A fixed timeline of 6 months postoperatively, regardless of strength or function
Meeting criteria-based milestones, including strength symmetry, hop-test symmetry, movement quality, and psychological readiness
Her subjective confidence that she 'feels ready' alone
Resolution of the surgical scar appearance
stages.explanation: Return-to-sport after ACL reconstruction should be criteria-based, not time-based: athletes should meet objective milestones—quadriceps and hamstring strength symmetry (commonly limb symmetry index ≥90%), symmetric hop-test battery, good movement quality on jump-landing assessment, and psychological readiness—before returning to cutting/pivoting. A fixed 6-month timeline alone ignores that many athletes have persistent deficits at that point, raising reinjury risk. Subjective confidence alone overestimates readiness and does not capture strength asymmetry. Scar appearance has no bearing on functional readiness. Criteria-based clearance reduces graft reinjury and contralateral ACL injury risk compared with time-based clearance.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Which objective measure is most important to quantify before considering return to sport in this athlete?
options: Resting heart rate compared with preinjury values
Quadriceps strength limb symmetry index (operated vs. uninjured leg), typically targeting ≥90%
Body mass index
Ankle dorsiflexion range of motion only
stages.explanation: Quadriceps strength symmetry is a cornerstone metric: a limb symmetry index (operated divided by uninjured leg) of at least about 90% is a widely used threshold, because persistent quadriceps deficits are linked to poorer function and higher reinjury risk; it is typically assessed alongside hamstring strength and hop symmetry. Resting heart rate reflects general fitness, not knee-specific readiness. BMI does not gauge limb-specific strength or function. Ankle dorsiflexion alone is a minor contributor and does not address the quadriceps deficit that is this patient's key limitation. Quantifying quad strength symmetry directly targets her identified weakness.

stages.label: FUNCTIONAL TESTING
stages.content:
Isokinetic quadriceps strength: limb symmetry index 78% (operated vs. uninjured)
Single-leg hop for distance: limb symmetry index 82%
Triple hop and crossover hop: limb symmetry index 80-83%
Jump-landing (drop vertical jump) assessment: dynamic knee valgus noted on the operated side
Return-to-sport psychological readiness (ACL-RSI) score: lower than the typical pass threshold

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given quadriceps and hop symmetry in the high 70s-low 80s percent with dynamic valgus on landing, what is the most appropriate recommendation?
options: Clear her for full competition now because she is past 6 months
Continue targeted rehabilitation (quadriceps strengthening, neuromuscular/landing retraining) and defer return until criteria are met, then progress through a graded return-to-sport program
Stop all rehabilitation and rest completely until the next season
Proceed directly to revision surgery for the strength deficit
stages.explanation: Because she has not met strength/hop symmetry thresholds (~90%), shows dynamic knee valgus, and has low psychological readiness, she should not yet return; the right plan is continued targeted rehabilitation—progressive quadriceps strengthening and neuromuscular/landing (valgus-control) retraining—then a graded return-to-sport progression once criteria are satisfied. Clearing her now at 6+ months is the time-based fallacy that increases reinjury risk. Complete rest would worsen her deficits and deconditioning—she needs active, focused training, not inactivity. Revision surgery is unwarranted: her graft is stable on exam, and the issue is a rehabilitation/strength deficit, not graft failure. Criteria-based progression protects the reconstructed and contralateral knees.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What additional counseling reduces her risk of a second ACL injury after she eventually returns?
options: Reassure her that once she returns, reinjury risk is the same as someone who never tore an ACL

Emphasize ongoing neuromuscular/injury-prevention training, completion of a graded return progression, and that meeting symmetry and movement-quality criteria lowers (but does not eliminate) reinjury risk
Advise that strength training is unnecessary once she is cleared
Tell her psychological readiness has no effect on outcomes
stages.explanation: After ACL reconstruction, athletes remain at elevated risk for graft and contralateral ACL injury, so counseling should stress continued neuromuscular and injury-prevention training, completing a graded return progression, and that achieving strength/hop symmetry and good landing mechanics reduces—though does not eliminate—reinjury risk. Claiming risk equals an uninjured person is false and falsely reassuring. Saying strength training is unnecessary once cleared invites recurrent weakness and reinjury; maintenance training is essential. Dismissing psychological readiness ignores evidence that fear/low readiness affects return success and reinjury. Honest, prevention-focused counseling supports safe, durable return to sport.

cases.diagnosis: ACL reconstruction rehabilitation with incomplete criteria-based readiness for return to sport
pearls: Return to sport after ACL reconstruction is criteria-based, not time-based: strength symmetry, hop symmetry, movement quality, and psychological readiness.
Quadriceps limb symmetry index ≥90% is a common threshold; persistent quad deficits predict worse function and higher reinjury risk.
Dynamic knee valgus on landing flags the need for neuromuscular/landing retraining before return.
Even after meeting criteria, reinjury risk (graft and contralateral) stays elevated—ongoing prevention training and a graded progression are essential.

cases.title: Shoulder Stiffness Six Weeks After Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: He returns for a postoperative follow-up and asks when he can start lifting weights with his repaired shoulder.
cases.history: A 58-year-old right-hand-dominant man presents to the outpatient clinic 6 weeks after arthroscopic repair of a large full-thickness supraspinatus tear of the right shoulder. The tear followed a fall onto an outstretched hand 4 months ago, and he failed a trial of conservative therapy before surgery. He has been wearing his abduction sling and reports the wound has healed well. He is eager to return to recreational tennis and weightlifting and has been trying to lift his arm overhead and do light dumbbell curls on his own. He denies fever, redness, or drainage. Pain is now 2/10, much improved from the 7/10 preoperatively. PMH: hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, and acetaminophen 1 g as needed. He stopped oxycodone 3 weeks ago. Allergies: none. Social: insurance broker, drinks 2-3 beers weekly, never smoked. Family history: father with osteoarthritis. He lives with his wife, who helps with dressing.
cases.exam: Well-healed arthroscopic portal incisions without erythema, warmth, or drainage. Mild diffuse atrophy of the deltoid. Passive range of motion: forward flexion to 120°, external rotation to 30°, comfortable. Active forward flexion limited to 60° with guarding and apprehension. No drop-arm sign elicited (not tested against resistance per protocol). Neurovascular exam intact distally with normal radial pulse, capillary refill, and sensation. No cervical spine tenderness; Spurling test negative. Contralateral shoulder exam normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: At 6 weeks after a large rotator cuff repair, the patient has been performing self-directed active overhead lifting. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Counsel him to stop active elevation and continue protected passive range of motion only
Encourage him to continue active strengthening to prevent stiffness
Order an MRI to assess the integrity of the repair
Refer him for immediate revision surgery
Initiate a corticosteroid injection into the subacromial space
stages.explanation: Standard rehabilitation after rotator cuff repair protects the healing tendon-to-bone interface, which is biomechanically weakest in the early weeks. The first phase (roughly 0-6 weeks) permits passive and pendulum motion only; active and resisted elevation is deferred (typically until 6-12 weeks depending on tear size) to avoid retear. Counseling him to stop active elevation and continue passive ROM is correct. Continuing active strengthening risks disrupting the repair. MRI is premature without signs of failure and is hard to interpret postoperatively. Revision surgery is unwarranted with a healing repair and no documented failure. A subacromial steroid injection impairs tendon healing and is contraindicated this early.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Why is early active elevation specifically discouraged in the first phase after rotator cuff repair?
options: Active elevation increases the risk of adhesive capsulitis
Passive motion causes more pain than active motion
Active contraction of the repaired muscle generates tension that can disrupt the tendon-bone healing site
Active elevation accelerates glenohumeral osteoarthritis
It increases the risk of axillary nerve injury
stages.explanation: The repaired tendon must heal to a bone trough, and this enthesis is mechanically vulnerable early. Active or resisted elevation requires the repaired rotator cuff to contract, placing direct tensile load across the repair and risking gap formation or retear. Passive motion moves the joint without loading the repair, preserving the construct while preventing stiffness. Adhesive capsulitis is actually a risk of immobilization, not of early active motion. Passive motion is generally less painful than active. Active elevation does not meaningfully accelerate osteoarthritis in this timeframe. Axillary nerve injury is a surgical risk, not a rehabilitation-phase concern.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which rehabilitation activity is appropriate to prescribe at this 6-week visit?
options: Resisted internal and external rotation with elastic bands
Overhead military press with light dumbbells
Plyometric throwing program
Continued passive and active-assisted range of motion with scapular stabilization
Full return to recreational tennis
stages.explanation: At 6 weeks, progression typically advances to active-assisted range of motion and gentle active-assisted work, often adding scapular stabilization and posture work, while still protecting the repair from resisted loading. Continued passive and active-assisted ROM with scapular stabilization is appropriate. Resisted band rotation loads the cuff and is usually deferred to roughly 8-12 weeks. Overhead pressing, a plyometric throwing program, and full return to tennis all impose high tensile and dynamic loads far beyond what a 6-week repair can tolerate and risk catastrophic retear.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP DATA
stages.content:
Operative note (reviewed): Large full-thickness supraspinatus tear, ~3 cm retraction
Repair: double-row suture anchor technique, good tissue quality
Surgeon protocol: sling x 6 weeks; passive ROM weeks 0-6; AAROM/active weeks 6-12; resisted strengthening after week 12
Current passive forward flexion: 120°
No signs of infection; CRP not indicated

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: The patient is frustrated and asks why he cannot just push through and strengthen now. What is the best counseling point and timeline?
options: Strengthening can begin now because the tear was small
Resisted strengthening typically starts around 12 weeks once tendon healing is more secure, and pushing earlier risks retear
He should avoid all shoulder movement until 6 months to be safe
He can return to tennis now if he uses a brace
Strengthening timeline does not affect retear risk
stages.explanation: Per his surgeon's protocol and general principles for a large double-row repair, resisted strengthening begins around 12 weeks, with return to sport typically 4-6 months postoperatively. Premature loading is the leading modifiable cause of retear, especially in large tears. Telling him to strengthen now is wrong and contradicts the protocol; the tear was large, not small. Complete immobilization for 6 months causes stiffness and adhesive capsulitis. Returning to tennis now even with a brace is dangerous given the dynamic overhead loads. The timeline absolutely affects retear risk; honoring it is protective.

cases.diagnosis: Rotator cuff full-thickness tear, status post arthroscopic repair (early rehabilitation phase)
pearls: Early rehab after cuff repair protects the tendon-bone interface: passive/pendulum motion first, active and resisted motion deferred to protect the construct.
The repair site is weakest in the first weeks; active or resisted elevation loads the cuff and is the leading modifiable cause of retear.
Larger tears and poorer tissue quality warrant more conservative progression; resisted strengthening typically begins around 12 weeks.
Prolonged total immobilization risks adhesive capsulitis, so protected passive ROM is maintained throughout the early phase.

cases.title: Sudden Calf Pop While Playing Basketball
cases.chiefComplaint: He felt a sudden pop and sharp pain in the back of his ankle while pushing off during a recreational basketball game.
cases.history: A 43-year-old man presents to the emergency department 2 hours after a sudden injury during a pickup basketball game. As he pushed off to sprint, he felt a sharp blow to the back of his right ankle, as if someone had kicked or hit him, accompanied by an audible pop. He fell and could not continue playing. He now has difficulty pushing off and walking, especially climbing stairs, but can bear some weight with a flat-footed gait. He denies numbness, paresthesias, or prior ankle problems. He recalls being prescribed a fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin) for a urinary infection about 3 weeks ago. PMH: well-controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social: accountant, exercises sporadically on weekends, does not smoke, drinks socially. Family history: noncontributory. He has no history of Achilles tendinopathy and takes no corticosteroids.
cases.exam: Swelling and ecchymosis over the posterior distal right calf. A palpable gap is appreciated approximately 4 cm proximal to the calcaneal insertion. Tenderness over the area. Active plantarflexion is weak but present (recruiting accessory flexors). Squeezing the calf with the patient prone fails to produce plantarflexion of the foot. Single-leg heel raise cannot be performed on the affected side. Distal pulses intact, sensation normal, ankle not warm or erythematous. No bony tenderness over the malleoli or midfoot.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Squeezing the calf fails to plantarflex the foot. What does this finding indicate?
options: A positive Homans sign suggesting deep vein thrombosis
An intact Achilles tendon, ruling out rupture
A positive Simmonds test for posterior tibial tendon dysfunction
A positive Thompson test indicating Achilles tendon rupture
A negative test that excludes significant injury
stages.explanation: The Thompson (Simmonds-Thompson) test is performed with the patient prone; squeezing the calf normally plantarflexes the foot via an intact gastrocnemius-soleus-Achilles complex. Absent plantarflexion (a positive test) signals a complete Achilles tendon rupture, consistent with the audible pop, palpable gap, and inability to heel-raise. Homans sign relates to DVT, not tendon integrity, and would not explain the gap. The test does NOT indicate an intact tendon. It is not a test for posterior tibial tendon dysfunction. It is a positive, not negative, finding and clearly indicates significant injury.

stages.label: RISK FACTOR
stages.question: Which element of his history is the most relevant predisposing factor for this injury?
options: His penicillin allergy
Recent fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin) use
Metformin therapy
His occupation as an accountant
A single episode of social alcohol use
stages.explanation: Fluoroquinolones (e.g., ciprofloxacin) carry a well-described boxed-warning association with tendinopathy and tendon rupture, especially the Achilles, with risk persisting for weeks after exposure. Recent ciprofloxacin use is the key predisposing factor here, layered on the typical mechanism of forced push-off in middle age. A penicillin allergy is irrelevant to tendon integrity. Metformin and sedentary occupation are not established rupture risk factors. Occasional social alcohol is not a meaningful contributor. Other risks include corticosteroids and pre-existing tendinopathy, neither present here.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Ankle radiographs (AP, lateral, mortise): No fracture; soft-tissue swelling posteriorly
Kager fat pad appears distorted on lateral view
Bedside ultrasound: Discontinuity of the Achilles tendon ~4 cm above the calcaneal insertion with a hypoechoic gap
Dynamic exam: tendon ends separate with dorsiflexion, confirming full-thickness rupture

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms a full-thickness Achilles rupture. Regarding definitive management, which statement best reflects current evidence?
options: Functional rehabilitation with early controlled motion gives outcomes comparable to surgery for many patients, with surgery reducing rerupture risk somewhat but adding wound and nerve complications
Surgical repair is mandatory because nonoperative treatment cannot heal the tendon
The foot should be casted in maximal dorsiflexion to approximate the tendon ends
Immediate weight-bearing in a neutral shoe without immobilization is the standard of care
Long-term anticoagulation alone will allow the tendon to heal
stages.explanation: Modern functional (accelerated) nonoperative protocols using early protected motion and progressive weight-bearing in equinus, then gradual neutralization, yield functional outcomes comparable to surgery for many patients; surgery modestly lowers rerupture rate but adds risks of wound infection and sural nerve injury. Shared decision-making is appropriate. Nonoperative care can heal the tendon (A is false). Casting must place the foot in plantarflexion/equinus, not dorsiflexion, to approximate ends. Immediate unprotected weight-bearing without immobilization risks rerupture. Anticoagulation does not heal tendons; VTE prophylaxis may be considered separately.

stages.label: REHABILITATION
stages.question: He chooses functional bracing. What is the correct initial immobilization position and rehabilitation principle?
options: Neutral ankle position with immediate aggressive stretching into dorsiflexion
Maximal dorsiflexion to lengthen the healing tendon
Plantarflexion (equinus) in a boot with heel wedges, then gradual reduction of the wedge toward neutral with progressive weight-bearing
Strict non-weight-bearing in a cast for 12 weeks with no motion
Free ankle motion immediately with no boot
stages.explanation: Functional rehabilitation begins with the ankle in plantarflexion (equinus) using a boot with heel wedges to approximate the tendon ends and reduce tension on the repair/healing tissue. Wedges are progressively removed over weeks to bring the ankle toward neutral as controlled motion and weight-bearing advance. Neutral with aggressive dorsiflexion stretching and maximal dorsiflexion overstretch and risk rerupture or tendon lengthening. Twelve weeks of strict immobilization without motion is outdated and causes stiffness and atrophy. Free immediate motion with no boot provides no protection and risks rerupture.

cases.diagnosis: Acute complete rupture of the right Achilles tendon
pearls: A positive Thompson test (no plantarflexion on calf squeeze) plus a palpable gap and inability to single-leg heel-raise strongly indicates complete Achilles rupture.
Fluoroquinolones carry a boxed warning for tendon rupture; risk persists for weeks after exposure, especially with concurrent corticosteroids.
Functional (accelerated) nonoperative rehab yields outcomes comparable to surgery for many patients; surgery modestly lowers rerupture but adds wound and sural nerve risk.
Immobilize initially in plantarflexion/equinus with heel wedges, then progressively reduce toward neutral with controlled motion and weight-bearing.

cases.title: Aching Inner Knee in a Weekend Runner
cases.chiefComplaint: He reports several weeks of medial right knee pain that worsens with twisting and squatting.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man presents to sports medicine clinic with 6 weeks of insidious medial right knee pain. He cannot recall a specific injury but notes the pain began after he increased his recreational running mileage and started doing deep squats at the gym. The pain is worse with twisting, pivoting, and getting up from a low chair, and he occasionally feels a mild catching sensation but denies true locking or giving way. There has been no significant swelling. He has no night pain, fever, or systemic symptoms. PMH: mild obesity (BMI 31) and gastroesophageal reflux. Medications: omeprazole 20 mg daily, occasional ibuprofen 400 mg for the knee. Allergies: none. Social: works in IT, runs 3 times weekly, drinks socially, non-smoker. Family history: mother with knee osteoarthritis. He is otherwise healthy and walks without an assistive device. He is concerned he may need surgery and wants to keep exercising.
cases.exam: Antalgic gait is absent; he ambulates normally. No effusion on ballottement; small or no joint effusion. Tenderness localized to the medial joint line. McMurray test reproduces pain with a palpable click on internal/external rotation with the knee flexed. Negative Lachman and anterior drawer; negative posterior drawer. Valgus and varus stress stable. Full passive extension; flexion mildly limited by discomfort at end range. Patellar grind mildly uncomfortable. Distal neurovascular exam intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Medial joint-line tenderness with a positive McMurray test in this patient most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Anterior cruciate ligament tear
Medial collateral ligament sprain
Medial meniscal tear
Patellofemoral pain syndrome
Pes anserine bursitis
stages.explanation: Medial joint-line tenderness combined with a positive McMurray test (pain and palpable click on rotation of the flexed knee) is the classic presentation of a medial meniscal tear, here a degenerative/overuse pattern given the insidious onset and no acute trauma. An ACL tear typically follows a pivoting injury with effusion and a positive Lachman, which is negative here. MCL sprain produces pain with valgus stress, which is stable. Patellofemoral pain is anterior and worsens with stairs, not joint-line. Pes anserine bursitis causes tenderness inferomedial to the joint line, not at the line itself, and lacks the McMurray click.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the easy, non-traumatic, non-locking presentation, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Weight-bearing radiographs of the knee and a clinical diagnosis with conservative management
Immediate MRI of the knee
Diagnostic arthroscopy
CT scan of the knee
Bone scan to rule out stress fracture
stages.explanation: For a suspected degenerative meniscal tear without mechanical locking, weight-bearing radiographs are appropriate first-line to assess for concurrent osteoarthritis and joint-space narrowing, and the diagnosis is largely clinical, allowing a trial of conservative management. Immediate MRI rarely changes initial management and is not first-line for a degenerative tear without red flags. Diagnostic arthroscopy is not indicated before a conservative trial and may not improve outcomes in degenerative tears. CT poorly evaluates soft tissue. A bone scan is unnecessary without features of stress fracture such as focal bony tenderness.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Weight-bearing knee radiographs (AP, lateral, sunrise): Mild medial compartment joint-space narrowing
Small medial tibial osteophyte
No fracture, no loose body, no large effusion
Alignment within normal limits
Findings consistent with early degenerative change

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Radiographs show early degenerative change without locking. What is the best initial management?
options: Refer for arthroscopic partial meniscectomy
Structured physical therapy focusing on quadriceps and hip strengthening, activity modification, weight management, and NSAIDs as needed
Recommend strict bed rest and knee immobilization
Long-term opioid therapy for pain control
Intra-articular antibiotic injection
stages.explanation: For degenerative meniscal tears, especially with early osteoarthritis, guideline-concordant first-line care is conservative: physical therapy emphasizing quadriceps and hip strengthening, activity modification, weight loss, and short-course NSAIDs. Multiple trials show arthroscopic partial meniscectomy offers no durable benefit over PT for degenerative tears without locking, so referral now is premature. Strict bed rest and immobilization cause deconditioning and stiffness. Long-term opioids are inappropriate for a benign musculoskeletal condition. Intra-articular antibiotics are nonsensical without infection. Surgery is reserved for true mechanical locking or failure of a structured conservative trial.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He completes 3 months of physical therapy with substantial improvement but asks when surgery would ever be warranted. What is the best answer?
options: Surgery is warranted only if he ever wants to run competitively
Surgery should be scheduled now as a preventive measure
Surgery is never an option for any meniscal tear
Surgery should be considered mainly for true mechanical symptoms such as persistent locking or a displaced bucket-handle tear, or failure of adequate conservative care
He should have an MRI every 6 months indefinitely
stages.explanation: For degenerative tears, surgery is reserved for true mechanical symptoms, particularly persistent locking or a displaced/bucket-handle fragment, or when an adequate course of conservative care fails to control symptoms. His good response to PT argues against operating. Surgery is not dictated by a desire to run recreationally. Scheduling preventive surgery contradicts the evidence that meniscectomy does not prevent osteoarthritis and may accelerate it. Surgery is sometimes appropriate, so 'never' is wrong. Routine surveillance MRI every 6 months is unnecessary and not evidence-based.

cases.diagnosis: Degenerative medial meniscal tear with early medial-compartment osteoarthritis
pearls: Medial joint-line tenderness plus a positive McMurray test is the classic clinical signature of a medial meniscal tear.
Degenerative meniscal tears are managed conservatively first; arthroscopic partial meniscectomy shows no durable benefit over PT absent mechanical locking.
Weight-bearing radiographs help detect coexisting osteoarthritis; MRI rarely changes initial management in degenerative tears.
Reserve surgery for true mechanical symptoms (persistent locking, displaced bucket-handle tear) or failure of structured conservative care.

cases.title: Buttock and Low-Back Pain Since Delivery
cases.chiefComplaint: She reports persistent right buttock and low-back pain that began during her recent pregnancy and worsened after delivery.
cases.history: A 33-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with 4 months of right-sided low-back and buttock pain that started in the third trimester and persisted after a vaginal delivery 10 weeks ago. The pain is localized below the belt line over the posterior pelvis, sometimes radiating to the groin and posterior thigh but not past the knee. It worsens with transitional movements: rolling in bed, standing from sitting, climbing stairs, and single-leg loading such as dressing. She denies bowel or bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, leg weakness, or numbness. She is breastfeeding. PMH: uncomplicated pregnancy, mild iron-deficiency anemia. Medications: prenatal vitamin, ferrous sulfate, acetaminophen as needed. Allergies: none. Social: stay-at-home mother of two, no tobacco, rare alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. She has no fever, weight loss, or history of inflammatory back disease. Sleep is disrupted by pain and infant care.
cases.exam: Point tenderness over the right sacral sulcus near the posterior superior iliac spine. Pain is reproduced by FABER (Patrick), thigh thrust, sacral compression, and distraction; Gaenslen test positive on the right. Straight-leg raise negative bilaterally. Strength 5/5 in all lower-extremity myotomes; sensation intact; reflexes 2+ and symmetric. No saddle anesthesia. Lumbar range of motion mildly limited by pain; no midline spinous tenderness. Gait mildly antalgic. Hip internal rotation full and painless.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Multiple provocation maneuvers reproduce her posterior pelvic pain while neurologic exam and SLR are normal. Which cluster of positive tests best supports the likely diagnosis?
options: Positive Spurling and Hoffman signs
Positive Lachman and McMurray tests
Positive Trendelenburg and Ober tests
Three or more positive SI provocation tests (e.g., thigh thrust, distraction, compression, FABER, Gaenslen)
Positive Tinel and Phalen signs
stages.explanation: Sacroiliac joint dysfunction is supported when three or more validated provocation tests (thigh thrust, distraction, compression, FABER/Patrick, Gaenslen) are positive, as in this postpartum patient with peripartum ligamentous laxity and asymmetric pelvic loading. Spurling and Hoffman assess cervical radiculopathy/myelopathy, not the pelvis. Lachman and McMurray are knee tests. Trendelenburg and Ober evaluate hip abductor strength and IT band tightness, not the SI joint. Tinel and Phalen test for carpal tunnel syndrome. A normal neurologic exam and negative SLR also argue against lumbar radiculopathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most helps distinguish her SI joint dysfunction from lumbar radiculopathy?
options: Pain centered over the posterior pelvis below L5 that does not radiate past the knee, with a normal neurologic exam and negative SLR
Pain that radiates below the knee with a positive straight-leg raise
Diminished ankle reflex and dermatomal numbness
Pain relieved by lumbar flexion and worsened by extension
Bilateral lower-extremity weakness
stages.explanation: SI joint pain localizes over the posterior pelvis below the L5 level, may refer to the groin or posterior thigh but characteristically not below the knee, and is accompanied by a normal neurologic exam and negative SLR. Radiation below the knee with a positive SLR, diminished reflexes and dermatomal numbness, and lower-extremity weakness all point to nerve-root involvement (radiculopathy), which she lacks. A flexion-relief/extension-worsening pattern is more typical of facet or stenosis-related pain and is nonspecific. Her clean neurologic exam is the key discriminator.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management for this postpartum patient?
options: Immediate sacroiliac joint fusion
Lumbar MRI and urgent neurosurgical referral
Physical therapy targeting lumbopelvic stabilization and a pelvic support belt, with activity modification and acetaminophen/NSAIDs as tolerated
Long-term opioid therapy
Strict bed rest for 6 weeks
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of SI joint dysfunction, particularly postpartum, is conservative: physical therapy focused on lumbopelvic/core stabilization and load transfer, a pelvic support belt, activity modification, and analgesics such as acetaminophen or NSAIDs (with attention to breastfeeding compatibility). SI fusion is a last resort after failed comprehensive conservative and injection therapy. Lumbar MRI with urgent neurosurgery is unwarranted without neurologic red flags. Long-term opioids are inappropriate. Strict bed rest promotes deconditioning and worsens outcomes; early guided activity is preferred.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
