stages.content:
After 6 weeks of physical therapy and a pelvic belt: pain reduced from 7/10 to 5/10 but persistent
Continues to limit transfers and sleep
Neurologic exam remains normal
Inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) normal; no features of axial spondyloarthritis
Patient asks about an injection to both confirm the source and relieve pain

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She has had a partial response to conservative care. What is the role of an image-guided sacroiliac joint injection here?
options: It is purely cosmetic and has no diagnostic value
A fluoroscopy- or ultrasound-guided intra-articular injection of local anesthetic with corticosteroid can be both diagnostic (relief confirms the SI joint as the pain source) and therapeutic
It permanently cures SI joint dysfunction in all patients
It should be avoided because it is contraindicated postpartum
It replaces the need for any physical therapy
stages.explanation: An image-guided intra-articular SI joint injection serves a dual role: significant pain relief after local anesthetic helps confirm the SI joint as the pain generator (diagnostic), while the corticosteroid provides therapeutic anti-inflammatory benefit, often facilitating continued rehabilitation. It is not cosmetic and does not permanently cure all patients; effects can be temporary. It is not contraindicated postpartum, though breastfeeding-compatible agents and dosing should be considered. It complements rather than replaces physical therapy, which remains the foundation of treatment.

cases.diagnosis: Postpartum sacroiliac joint dysfunction
pearls: Diagnose SI joint dysfunction clinically when three or more provocation tests (thigh thrust, distraction, compression, FABER, Gaenslen) are positive.
SI joint pain sits over the posterior pelvis below L5 and typically does not radiate below the knee; a normal neuro exam and negative SLR distinguish it from radiculopathy.
Peripartum ligamentous laxity and asymmetric pelvic loading predispose to postpartum SI pain; first-line care is lumbopelvic stabilization PT plus a pelvic belt.
An image-guided SI joint injection is both diagnostic (anesthetic relief confirms the source) and therapeutic (corticosteroid) when conservative care is incomplete.

cases.title: Deep Buttock Pain Shooting Down the Leg
cases.chiefComplaint: She describes months of deep right buttock pain that radiates down the back of her thigh, worse with prolonged sitting.
cases.history: A 38-year-old woman presents with 5 months of deep right buttock pain radiating down the posterior thigh, occasionally to the calf. The pain is worst with prolonged sitting (she drives for rideshare), climbing stairs, and getting up from a chair, and improves with standing and walking. She points to a spot deep in the buttock as the epicenter. She denies back pain at the midline, numbness in a clear dermatome, bowel or bladder changes, or weakness. A prior lumbar MRI obtained by her primary physician showed only minimal disc bulging without nerve-root compression. She had two epidural steroid injections without benefit. PMH: depression, obesity (BMI 33). Medications: sertraline 100 mg daily, occasional naproxen. Allergies: sulfa (hives). Social: rideshare driver (long sitting), non-smoker, rare alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. She recently increased gym time with squats and lunges. She is frustrated after months without a clear diagnosis.
cases.exam: Tenderness on deep palpation over the right piriformis/sciatic notch, reproducing buttock pain. Pain and weakness reproduced by the FAIR maneuver (flexion, adduction, internal rotation) and by resisted external rotation/abduction (Pace and Freiberg signs positive). No midline lumbar tenderness. Straight-leg raise negative; Lasègue negative. Strength 5/5 throughout; deep tendon reflexes 2+ symmetric; sensation intact in all dermatomes. No saddle anesthesia. Hip joint internal rotation full and painless, with no groin pain on FABER. Gait normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her sciatica-like symptoms occur despite a lumbar MRI without nerve-root compression. Which exam finding most specifically supports the suspected diagnosis?
options: Reproduction of buttock and leg pain with the FAIR maneuver and tenderness over the sciatic notch
Positive straight-leg raise at 30 degrees
Diminished Achilles reflex
Positive Spurling test
Groin pain with FABER and limited hip internal rotation
stages.explanation: Piriformis syndrome is suggested by deep buttock tenderness over the sciatic notch and reproduction of symptoms with maneuvers that stretch or contract the piriformis against the sciatic nerve, especially the FAIR test (flexion, adduction, internal rotation), plus Pace and Freiberg signs. A positive SLR at 30 degrees and a diminished Achilles reflex suggest lumbar radiculopathy, which her normal neuro exam and prior MRI argue against. Spurling is a cervical test. Groin pain with FABER and limited internal rotation point to intra-articular hip pathology, not the piriformis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Why does a normal lumbar MRI and negative straight-leg raise help shift the diagnosis toward piriformis syndrome rather than discogenic radiculopathy?
options: Piriformis syndrome always produces a positive straight-leg raise
MRI is unable to detect disc herniation
A negative straight-leg raise confirms there is no sciatic nerve involvement at all
Sciatic nerve irritation from the piriformis muscle can mimic radicular pain without disc-related nerve-root compression, so a normal MRI and negative tension signs point away from a discogenic cause
Radiculopathy never causes buttock pain
stages.explanation: In piriformis syndrome the sciatic nerve is irritated extraspinally as it passes near or through the piriformis, producing sciatica-type pain without disc-related root compression. Thus a lumbar MRI lacking nerve-root compression and negative dural-tension signs (SLR/Lasègue) make discogenic radiculopathy less likely and support an extraspinal source. Piriformis syndrome does not always give a positive SLR. MRI does detect herniation. A negative SLR does not prove the sciatic nerve is uninvolved; the nerve is irritated distally, not at the root. Radiculopathy can cause buttock pain, so that absolute is false.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC DATA
stages.content:
Lumbar MRI (reviewed): minimal L4-L5 disc bulge, no nerve-root compression, no stenosis, no annular fissure
Hip radiographs: no osteoarthritis or femoroacetabular impingement
Electrodiagnostics: no lumbosacral radiculopathy; FAIR-position H-reflex prolongation noted on the right
ESR/CRP: normal
Prior two lumbar epidural steroid injections: no relief

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Electrodiagnostics show no radiculopathy and prior epidurals failed. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment now?
options: Lumbar microdiscectomy
Physical therapy emphasizing piriformis and hip-external-rotator stretching, hip strengthening, posture and sitting modification, with NSAIDs
Long-term opioid therapy
Immediate surgical release of the piriformis muscle
Repeat lumbar epidural steroid injection
stages.explanation: First-line management of piriformis syndrome is conservative: a physical therapy program centered on piriformis and hip external-rotator stretching, hip and core strengthening, ergonomic/sitting modification (especially relevant for a rideshare driver), and NSAIDs. Microdiscectomy is illogical when MRI and EMG exclude radiculopathy. Long-term opioids are inappropriate for a benign musculoskeletal pain syndrome. Surgical piriformis release is a last resort reserved for refractory cases after exhausting conservative and injection options. Repeating a lumbar epidural targets the spine, which has already failed twice and is not the pain source.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After several weeks of dedicated physical therapy she remains symptomatic. What is the most appropriate next intervention?
options: Schedule open piriformis tenotomy without further workup
Image-guided (ultrasound or fluoroscopic) injection of local anesthetic and corticosteroid into the piriformis muscle, sometimes with botulinum toxin in refractory cases
Start gabapentin and discharge from clinic
Begin chronic opioid therapy
Order a whole-body bone scan
stages.explanation: When supervised physical therapy is insufficient, the next step is an image-guided injection into the piriformis muscle with local anesthetic and corticosteroid; this is both diagnostic (relief supports the diagnosis) and therapeutic, and botulinum toxin injection is an option in refractory cases to reduce muscle spasm. Open tenotomy is reserved for failure of all conservative and injection measures. Gabapentin with discharge abandons targeted treatment. A whole-body bone scan is not indicated without systemic red flags. Chronic opioids are inappropriate for this condition.

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: She asks what ergonomic change would most directly reduce her recurrent piriformis irritation given her job. What is the best advice?
options: Sit for longer continuous stretches to build tolerance
Avoid all stretching of the hip
Take regular standing/walking breaks, avoid prolonged sitting on a wallet or hard edge, and use a cushion to offload the buttock
Wear a lumbar corset at all times
Switch to driving exclusively at night
stages.explanation: Prolonged sitting compresses the piriformis and sciatic nerve, so the most direct ergonomic intervention for a rideshare driver is to take regular standing/walking breaks, avoid sitting on a wallet or hard edge, and use a cushion to offload the buttock, complementing her stretching program. Sitting longer to 'build tolerance' aggravates the problem. Avoiding all hip stretching abandons a key therapeutic component. A lumbar corset worn constantly targets the spine, not the piriformis, and promotes deconditioning. Driving only at night does not change the underlying sitting load and is irrelevant to the mechanism.

cases.diagnosis: Piriformis syndrome (extraspinal sciatic nerve entrapment)
pearls: Piriformis syndrome causes sciatica-type pain from extraspinal sciatic irritation; suspect it when MRI lacks nerve-root compression and tension signs are negative.
The FAIR maneuver plus Pace and Freiberg signs and deep sciatic-notch tenderness support the diagnosis; FAIR-position H-reflex prolongation can corroborate it.
Prolonged sitting and hip external-rotator overload (e.g., driving, squats) are common contributors; ergonomic and sitting modifications matter.
Treat conservatively first (stretching, strengthening, NSAIDs); escalate to image-guided piriformis injection (anesthetic/steroid, then botulinum toxin) before considering surgical release.

cases.title: Neck and Shoulder Knots With Headaches
cases.chiefComplaint: She reports months of aching tightness across the top of both shoulders with tension headaches.
cases.history: A 45-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with 4 months of aching, tight pain across the upper trapezius and base of the neck, worse by the end of her workday. She works long hours at a computer with her monitor set low and frequently cradles her phone between ear and shoulder. The pain sometimes refers upward to the temple as a tension-type headache and to the medial scapular border. She notices tender 'knots' she can feel with her fingers. Pressing on them reproduces the pain and the headache. She denies arm numbness, weakness, fevers, weight loss, or night pain that wakes her. PMH: anxiety, gastroesophageal reflux. Medications: escitalopram 10 mg daily, omeprazole, occasional ibuprofen. Allergies: none. Social: marketing manager, two children, sedentary, non-smoker, social alcohol. Family history: mother with fibromyalgia. Stress at work has been high. Sleep is fair. No recent trauma.
cases.exam: Palpable taut bands in the bilateral upper trapezius with discrete hyperirritable nodules. Firm palpation of a nodule on the right reproduces her pain and refers to the ipsilateral temple; a local twitch response is elicited. Cervical range of motion mildly limited by discomfort; no midline cervical spine tenderness. Spurling test negative bilaterally. Upper-extremity strength 5/5, sensation intact, reflexes 2+ symmetric. No lymphadenopathy or thyromegaly. Shoulder range of motion full. Posture shows forward head and rounded shoulders.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Palpation of a taut-band nodule reproduces her pain, refers it to the temple, and produces a local twitch response. What is this hallmark finding called?
options: A tender point of fibromyalgia
A Heberden node
A myofascial trigger point
A rheumatoid nodule
A lipoma
stages.explanation: A myofascial trigger point is a hyperirritable nodule within a palpable taut band of skeletal muscle that reproduces the patient's characteristic referred pain and may elicit a local twitch response on snapping palpation. This matches her trapezius findings. Fibromyalgia tender points are diffuse, do not produce referred pain or a twitch response, and accompany widespread pain and other systemic symptoms. Heberden nodes are bony osteoarthritic enlargements at the distal interphalangeal joints. Rheumatoid nodules are subcutaneous and associated with seropositive RA. A lipoma is a soft, mobile fatty mass that is non-tender and does not refer pain.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes her condition from fibromyalgia?
options: Localized taut bands and trigger points producing reproducible referred pain, rather than diffuse, widespread tenderness with systemic symptoms
Presence of widespread pain in all four body quadrants
Chronic fatigue and cognitive 'fog'
Symmetric small-joint swelling
Elevated inflammatory markers
stages.explanation: Myofascial pain syndrome is regional: localized taut bands and trigger points produce reproducible referred pain in specific patterns. Fibromyalgia, by contrast, is a centralized pain disorder with widespread pain in multiple quadrants, fatigue, sleep disturbance, and cognitive symptoms, without focal taut bands/twitch responses. Widespread four-quadrant pain and fatigue/cognitive fog are features of fibromyalgia, not myofascial pain syndrome. Symmetric small-joint swelling suggests inflammatory arthritis. Elevated inflammatory markers are not characteristic of either condition and would prompt evaluation for an inflammatory or systemic process.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for her myofascial pain?
options: Long-term opioid therapy
A multimodal program: ergonomic/posture correction, stretching and a physical therapy exercise program, and trigger-point therapy such as dry needling or manual release
Systemic corticosteroids
Immediate cervical spine surgery
Strict cervical collar immobilization
stages.explanation: First-line treatment is multimodal and conservative: correcting ergonomics and posture (her low monitor and phone-cradling are key drivers), a stretching and strengthening physical therapy program, and trigger-point therapy such as dry needling, trigger-point injection, or manual/myofascial release, often with heat and analgesics. Long-term opioids are inappropriate for a benign musculoskeletal condition. Systemic corticosteroids have no role. Cervical surgery is unwarranted with a normal neurologic exam and negative Spurling. A cervical collar promotes deconditioning and worsens myofascial pain rather than helping.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.content:
Workstation assessment: monitor raised to eye level, headset provided to stop phone-cradling
Physical therapy started: upper trapezius and levator scapulae stretching, scapular retraction strengthening
Dry needling to right upper trapezius trigger point: local twitch response obtained, immediate reduction in referred temple pain
Pain diary: headache frequency decreased from 5 to 2 days per week over 4 weeks

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: To prevent recurrence after her trigger points improve, which intervention is most important long term?
options: Continuing dry needling weekly indefinitely regardless of symptoms
Permanent use of muscle relaxants
Avoiding all physical activity to rest the muscles
Sustained ergonomic and postural correction plus a home stretching/strengthening routine and stress management
Routine cervical MRI every year
stages.explanation: Long-term control depends on eliminating perpetuating factors: sustained ergonomic and postural correction (eye-level monitor, headset), a consistent home stretching and strengthening routine, and stress management, since her work stress and forward-head posture drive recurrent trigger points. Indefinite weekly dry needling is unnecessary once symptoms resolve and treats the symptom, not the cause. Permanent muscle relaxants carry side effects and do not address mechanics. Avoiding all activity causes deconditioning and worsens myofascial pain. Annual cervical MRI is not indicated without neurologic red flags and does not prevent recurrence.

cases.diagnosis: Myofascial pain syndrome of the upper trapezius
pearls: A myofascial trigger point is a hyperirritable nodule in a taut band that reproduces referred pain and can elicit a local twitch response.
Myofascial pain is regional with reproducible referral patterns; fibromyalgia is widespread, centralized pain with systemic symptoms and no taut bands.
Treat with multimodal conservative care: ergonomics/posture, stretching and strengthening PT, and trigger-point therapy (dry needling, injection, or manual release).
Address perpetuating factors (poor workstation setup, phone-cradling, stress) to prevent recurrence; avoid opioids and prolonged collar immobilization.

cases.title: Years on Painkillers and Still Hurting
cases.chiefComplaint: He transfers care for chronic low-back pain managed on escalating opioids and feels they no longer help.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man establishes care at the pain clinic for 9 years of chronic mechanical low-back pain after a work injury. He is maintained on oxycodone extended-release 40 mg twice daily plus oxycodone 10 mg up to four times daily for breakthrough (total roughly 140-160 morphine milligram equivalents per day). He says the medication 'barely takes the edge off,' he has needed gradual dose increases, and he feels his pain is more diffuse than before. He is also on diazepam 5 mg at night for anxiety and sleep. He reports low mood and daytime sedation. He denies illicit drug use; he occasionally has 2-3 drinks on weekends. PMH: obesity (BMI 34), obstructive sleep apnea (poorly adherent to CPAP), depression, lumbar degenerative disc disease. Medications: as above, plus sertraline 50 mg. Allergies: none. Social: former warehouse worker, on disability, smokes half a pack daily. Family history: father with alcohol use disorder. PDMP shows all prescriptions from one prescriber; urine drug screens previously consistent.
cases.exam: Overweight man, mildly sedated but arousable and oriented. Diffuse tenderness across the lumbar paraspinals without focal midline tenderness. Range of motion globally limited by pain and guarding. Straight-leg raise negative bilaterally. Strength 5/5 throughout; sensation intact; reflexes 2+ and symmetric; no saddle anesthesia. Generalized tenderness to light palpation in several regions. Gait cautious but independent. No signs of acute intoxication or withdrawal at this visit. Mood appears low with constricted affect.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: He reports needing higher doses for diminishing relief and more diffuse pain. Which phenomenon should be specifically considered alongside tolerance?
options: Malingering
Placebo effect
Acute compartment syndrome
Conversion disorder
Opioid-induced hyperalgesia, in which long-term opioids paradoxically increase pain sensitivity and produce more diffuse pain
stages.explanation: Opioid-induced hyperalgesia is a state in which chronic opioid exposure paradoxically heightens pain sensitivity, often producing diffuse, poorly localized pain that worsens despite dose escalation. It should be considered alongside tolerance when escalating doses yield diminishing benefit and spreading pain, as here. Malingering implies feigning for secondary gain, unsupported by his consistent PDMP and urine screens. Placebo effect does not explain worsening pain on higher doses. Acute compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency with a tense, painful limb, not chronic diffuse back pain. Conversion disorder involves neurologic deficits inconsistent with anatomy, not the picture here.

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: Which combination in his regimen most increases his risk of fatal respiratory depression and warrants urgent attention?
options: Oxycodone plus sertraline
Concurrent high-dose opioids and a benzodiazepine (diazepam), compounded by untreated obstructive sleep apnea
Sertraline plus nicotine
Diazepam plus omeprazole
Oxycodone plus occasional acetaminophen
stages.explanation: The dangerous combination is concurrent opioids and a benzodiazepine (diazepam): both are CNS and respiratory depressants and together carry a boxed warning for profound sedation, respiratory depression, and death. His high MME, poorly treated obstructive sleep apnea, and CPAP nonadherence further amplify nighttime respiratory risk. Oxycodone plus sertraline raises modest serotonergic considerations but is not the leading lethal risk. Sertraline plus nicotine and diazepam plus omeprazole are not principally respiratory-depressant pairs. Oxycodone plus occasional acetaminophen is comparatively low risk. He should be counseled and offered naloxone.

stages.label: DATA REVIEW
stages.content:
Calculated dose: ~140-160 morphine milligram equivalents (MME) per day
PDMP: single prescriber, single pharmacy, no early refills
Urine drug screen: positive for oxycodone and benzodiazepine (both prescribed); no illicit substances
Home sleep study (prior): moderate OSA, AHI 22; CPAP adherence <2 hours/night
PHQ-9: 16 (moderately severe depression)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given high MME, benzodiazepine co-use, OSA, and depression, what is the most appropriate management approach?
options: Abruptly stop all opioids today to eliminate risk
Continue the current regimen unchanged since urine screens are consistent

Initiate a slow, individualized opioid taper with shared decision-making, address the benzodiazepine, optimize OSA and depression, and build a multimodal pain plan (PT, non-opioid analgesics, behavioral therapy)
Increase the opioid dose to overcome tolerance
Switch entirely to as-needed high-dose short-acting opioids
stages.explanation: Guidelines favor a gradual, individualized opioid taper using shared decision-making, while concurrently addressing the co-prescribed benzodiazepine, optimizing OSA treatment (CPAP), treating depression, and transitioning to a multimodal plan (physical therapy, non-opioid analgesics such as duloxetine/NSAIDs, and behavioral/cognitive therapy). Abrupt cessation risks withdrawal, destabilization, and harm and is discouraged outside emergencies. Continuing unchanged ignores escalating risk and probable hyperalgesia. Increasing the dose worsens risk and may aggravate hyperalgesia. Switching to high-dose short-acting opioids increases misuse and overdose risk. Offer naloxone throughout.

stages.label: SAFETY
stages.question: During the taper, what additional safety intervention is most important to provide?
options: Prescribe naloxone and educate the patient and family on overdose recognition and rescue
Recommend he stop attending physical therapy to rest
Add a second benzodiazepine for anxiety during the taper
Encourage alcohol to help with sleep
Discharge him from the clinic for noncompliance
stages.explanation: Prescribing naloxone and training the patient and household contacts to recognize and reverse overdose is a key harm-reduction step, especially with high MME, benzodiazepine co-use, and OSA. Stopping physical therapy removes a core non-opioid treatment. Adding a second benzodiazepine compounds respiratory-depression risk and is contraindicated. Encouraging alcohol adds another CNS depressant and worsens both OSA and overdose risk. Discharging him for noncompliance is not justified; his behavior has been consistent, and abandonment can precipitate harm or transition to illicit use. Naloxone access protects him during the vulnerable taper period.

stages.label: MULTIMODAL TRANSITION
stages.question: As the opioid dose is reduced, which non-opioid pharmacologic agent is most appropriate to help manage his chronic low-back pain and comorbid depression simultaneously?
options: Add a long-acting opioid at a higher dose
Begin chronic systemic corticosteroids
Add carisoprodol (a sedating muscle relaxant) at bedtime
Start duloxetine, an SNRI with evidence for chronic musculoskeletal pain that may also help his depression
Prescribe a second opioid for breakthrough pain
stages.explanation: Duloxetine, a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor, has evidence for chronic musculoskeletal/low-back pain and concurrently treats depression, making it an efficient multimodal choice as opioids are tapered (his sertraline could be transitioned with appropriate cross-taper and monitoring). Adding or escalating opioids (A, E) opposes the goal of the taper and increases risk. Chronic systemic corticosteroids have no role in mechanical low-back pain and cause serious harms. Carisoprodol is sedating, has abuse potential, and dangerously compounds CNS/respiratory depression alongside his opioid and benzodiazepine. Non-opioid, dual-benefit agents like duloxetine fit a multimodal strategy best.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic non-cancer pain on long-term opioids, with tolerance/opioid-induced hyperalgesia and high-risk benzodiazepine co-use
pearls: Suspect opioid-induced hyperalgesia when escalating doses yield diffuse, worsening pain rather than relief; escalation can worsen it.
Concurrent opioids and benzodiazepines carry a boxed warning for fatal respiratory depression, magnified by untreated OSA and CPAP nonadherence.
Taper opioids slowly and individually with shared decision-making; never abruptly discontinue outside emergencies, and avoid patient abandonment.
Build a multimodal plan (PT, non-opioid analgesics, behavioral therapy), treat comorbid OSA and depression, and prescribe naloxone with overdose education.

cases.title: Coughing With Meals After a Stroke
cases.chiefComplaint: She coughs and her voice sounds wet whenever she tries to eat or drink during rehabilitation.
cases.history: A 74-year-old woman is on the inpatient rehabilitation unit 8 days after a left middle cerebral artery ischemic stroke with residual right hemiparesis and mild expressive language difficulty. Nursing reports she coughs during meals, her voice sounds 'wet' after sips of water, and she takes a long time to swallow. She had a one-degree fever yesterday. She denies pain. Before the stroke she was independent and active. PMH: atrial fibrillation, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, prior tobacco use. Medications: apixaban, metoprolol, lisinopril, metformin, atorvastatin. Allergies: none. Social: widowed, lives with her daughter, former smoker (quit 10 years ago). Family history: noncontributory. She is motivated to recover and eager to eat normally. Her admission nursing swallow screen was deferred because she was drowsy on arrival. Family has been bringing her favorite thin soups.
cases.exam: Alert, follows commands with mild expressive aphasia. Right facial droop with mild dysarthria; tongue deviates slightly. Right upper- and lower-extremity weakness (4-/5). Wet, gurgly vocal quality after a sip of water with a delayed cough. Palatal elevation slightly reduced on the right. Gag reflex present but diminished. Lungs with scattered right basal crackles. No accessory muscle use at rest. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam is otherwise intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She coughs during meals with a wet voice and a low-grade fever. What is the most appropriate immediate step?
options: Make her NPO (nothing by mouth) and obtain a formal swallow evaluation by speech-language pathology
Allow her to continue a regular diet since she is alert
Insert a percutaneous gastrostomy tube today
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics and continue oral feeding
Thicken only liquids and otherwise proceed with a regular diet without evaluation
stages.explanation: Signs of coughing with meals, a wet voice, and low-grade fever after stroke raise concern for dysphagia with aspiration. The correct immediate step is to keep her NPO and obtain a formal swallow evaluation by speech-language pathology before further oral intake. Continuing a regular diet or thickening liquids without evaluation risks ongoing aspiration. Placing a gastrostomy tube today is premature before assessing swallow function and prognosis. Starting antibiotics while continuing oral feeding does not address the ongoing aspiration risk; the priority is to stop unsafe oral intake and evaluate the swallow.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test best characterizes the mechanism and timing of her aspiration and directly guides diet texture recommendations?
options: Chest CT angiography
Esophageal manometry
Videofluoroscopic modified barium swallow study (MBSS)
Upper endoscopy (EGD)
Barium enema
stages.explanation: A videofluoroscopic modified barium swallow study (MBSS) visualizes the oropharyngeal swallow in real time across consistencies, identifies penetration/aspiration and its timing (before, during, or after the swallow), and directly informs safe diet texture and compensatory strategies. (Flexible endoscopic evaluation of swallowing is an acceptable alternative.) Chest CT angiography evaluates pulmonary vasculature, not swallowing. Esophageal manometry measures esophageal motility, not the oropharyngeal phase. Upper endoscopy inspects mucosa but does not assess swallow dynamics. A barium enema images the colon and is irrelevant. The MBSS is the standard to tailor texture-modified diets.

stages.label: SWALLOW STUDY
stages.content:
Modified barium swallow study (videofluoroscopy):
Thin liquids: penetration to the level of the vocal folds with silent aspiration before the swallow trigger
Nectar-thick liquids: no aspiration; reduced penetration
Pureed solids: safe swallow, no residue of concern
Regular solids: pharyngeal residue requiring multiple swallows
Chin-tuck maneuver: improved airway protection with thickened liquids

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Based on the swallow study, what is the most appropriate diet and feeding plan?
options: Resume thin liquids and regular solids with no modifications
Make her permanently NPO and place a gastrostomy tube now
Allow thin liquids only if she drinks quickly
Recommend a pureed-solid diet with nectar-thickened liquids, use the chin-tuck maneuver, and continue swallowing therapy with SLP
Provide regular solids but thicken liquids to honey consistency only at night
stages.explanation: The study shows safe swallowing with pureed solids and nectar-thick liquids and improved airway protection with a chin-tuck, while thin liquids cause silent aspiration. The plan is a pureed-solid diet with nectar-thickened liquids plus the chin-tuck maneuver and ongoing swallowing rehabilitation with speech-language pathology. Resuming thin liquids and regular solids or thin liquids if drunk quickly reintroduces aspiration. Permanent NPO with a gastrostomy tube now is unnecessary since she has safe consistencies. Regular solids caused pharyngeal residue, and nighttime-only thickening ignores the daytime aspiration risk.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two days later she spikes a fever to 38.6°C with worsening right basal crackles and increased oxygen need. What is the most likely complication?
options: Pulmonary embolism
Aspiration pneumonia
Congestive heart failure exacerbation
Pneumothorax
Recurrent stroke
stages.explanation: Given documented silent aspiration of thin liquids, prior favorite thin soups from family, and right basal crackles with fever and rising oxygen requirement, aspiration pneumonia is the most likely complication, classically in the dependent (right lower) lobe. Pulmonary embolism typically causes acute dyspnea and pleuritic pain without focal crackles and lobar fever pattern. Heart failure exacerbation usually produces bilateral findings and signs of volume overload. Pneumothorax causes sudden unilateral absent breath sounds and hyperresonance. Recurrent stroke would present with new neurologic deficits, not isolated pulmonary findings. Evaluate with chest imaging and treat accordingly.

cases.diagnosis: Post-stroke oropharyngeal dysphagia with aspiration
pearls: Coughing with meals, a wet/gurgly voice, and delayed swallow after stroke signal dysphagia; make the patient NPO and get a formal swallow evaluation.
Silent aspiration (no protective cough) is common after stroke and is detected by instrumental testing, not bedside screening alone.
The modified barium swallow study characterizes aspiration timing across consistencies and directly guides texture-modified diets and compensatory maneuvers like the chin-tuck.
Aspiration pneumonia classically involves dependent lung segments (right lower lobe) and is a key preventable complication of post-stroke dysphagia.

cases.title: Knows the Word but Cannot Say It
cases.chiefComplaint: Since his stroke he struggles to get words out, speaking in short, effortful phrases.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man is admitted to the inpatient rehabilitation unit 6 days after a left middle cerebral artery ischemic stroke. His family reports he understands what is said to him and nods or shakes his head appropriately, but he speaks in short, halting, effortful phrases and often cannot find words. He becomes visibly frustrated when he cannot express himself and is aware of his errors. He has right-sided weakness, greater in the arm than the leg. He denies headache or visual changes. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, prior transient ischemic attack. Medications: aspirin, atorvastatin, amlodipine, metformin. Allergies: none. Social: retired teacher, married, two adult children, former smoker. Family history: father with stroke. He was right-handed and previously a fluent, articulate speaker. His wife is anxious and unsure how to communicate with him, sometimes finishing his sentences or speaking for him. He is highly motivated to recover his speech.
cases.exam: Alert and attentive. Speech is non-fluent, halting, and effortful with reduced phrase length and agrammatism ('telegraphic' output); naming is impaired. Auditory comprehension is largely preserved for conversational and multi-step commands. Repetition is impaired. He recognizes and is frustrated by his own errors. Right central facial weakness and right hemiparesis (arm 3/5, leg 4/5). Right-sided hyperreflexia with an upgoing plantar response. No left-sided neglect; visual fields full to confrontation. Reading comprehension relatively preserved; writing is effortful.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His speech is non-fluent and effortful with preserved comprehension, impaired repetition, and awareness of errors. Which aphasia type is this?
options: Wernicke (receptive) aphasia
Global aphasia
Conduction aphasia
Broca (expressive, non-fluent) aphasia
Transcortical sensory aphasia
stages.explanation: Broca aphasia is non-fluent and effortful with agrammatic/telegraphic output, impaired naming and repetition, but relatively preserved comprehension, and patients are typically aware and frustrated by their deficits, exactly as described. Wernicke aphasia is fluent with poor comprehension and lack of awareness ('word salad'). Global aphasia impairs both fluency and comprehension severely. Conduction aphasia is fluent with preserved comprehension but a disproportionate repetition deficit, not the effortful non-fluent output here. Transcortical sensory aphasia features poor comprehension with intact repetition, the opposite of this pattern. The lesion localizes to the left posterior inferior frontal region.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: The frequently associated right arm-predominant weakness in Broca aphasia reflects involvement of which adjacent region?
options: Left posterior inferior frontal lobe and adjacent precentral (motor) cortex/corona radiata
Right occipital lobe
Cerebellar vermis
Right temporal lobe
Brainstem pons in isolation
stages.explanation: Broca area lies in the left posterior inferior frontal lobe; nearby involvement of the precentral motor cortex and corona radiata explains the commonly associated contralateral (right) weakness that is often arm- and face-predominant, matching his exam. The right occipital lobe governs left visual field, not language or right-arm strength. The cerebellar vermis affects truncal coordination. The right temporal lobe is not the dominant language area in a right-hander and would not cause this picture. An isolated pontine lesion would not produce a cortical non-fluent aphasia with agrammatism.

stages.label: SLP ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Speech-language pathology evaluation:
Fluency: non-fluent, ~3-4 words per phrase, effortful initiation
Comprehension: 90% on single and two-step commands
Naming (confrontation): impaired, frequent phonemic paraphasias
Repetition: impaired for phrases
Functional goal set: improve functional communication, train compensatory strategies, educate family
Cognition: attention and memory grossly intact

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate rehabilitation approach for his aphasia?
options: Reassure the family that no therapy is needed because he will recover spontaneously
Intensive, individualized speech-language therapy targeting expressive output and functional communication, including compensatory and augmentative strategies
Restrict all attempts at speech to avoid frustration
Begin antipsychotic medication for agitation
Focus exclusively on improving his auditory comprehension
stages.explanation: The cornerstone is intensive, individualized speech-language therapy focused on expressive output and functional communication, incorporating compensatory strategies and augmentative/alternative communication aids as needed. Although some spontaneous recovery occurs, structured therapy improves outcomes, so withholding it is wrong. Restricting speech attempts worsens isolation and frustration and forfeits practice. Antipsychotics are inappropriate; his frustration stems from communication loss, not psychosis. Focusing only on comprehension ignores his primary deficit, which is expressive; comprehension is already relatively preserved.

stages.label: FAMILY EDUCATION
stages.question: His wife tends to finish his sentences and speak for him. What is the best communication coaching for the family?
options: Encourage her to keep finishing his sentences to save time
Tell the family to communicate only in writing
Advise giving him extra time to respond, using simple yes/no questions and supportive cues, and not speaking for him so he can practice communicating
Recommend they avoid talking to him until therapy is complete
Suggest speaking loudly and slowly as if to someone hard of hearing
stages.explanation: Effective family training teaches partners to allow extra response time, use yes/no questions and gestural or written cues, reduce distractions, and resist speaking for him, so he gets practice and retains autonomy and dignity. Finishing his sentences deprives him of practice and fosters dependence. Restricting communication to writing is impractical and not aligned with his preserved comprehension. Avoiding talking to him increases isolation and depression. Speaking loudly and slowly as to a hearing-impaired person misframes the problem; aphasia is a language deficit, not a hearing deficit, and can feel demeaning.

cases.diagnosis: Broca (non-fluent, expressive) aphasia after left MCA stroke
pearls: Broca aphasia: non-fluent, effortful, agrammatic speech with impaired naming/repetition but preserved comprehension and intact awareness/frustration.
Broca area sits in the left posterior inferior frontal lobe; adjacent motor cortex involvement explains the common right arm/face-predominant weakness.
Intensive individualized speech-language therapy plus compensatory/augmentative strategies improves functional communication beyond spontaneous recovery alone.
Coach families to allow time, use yes/no questions and cues, and avoid finishing sentences, preserving the patient's practice and dignity.

cases.title: Bumping Into Things on One Side
cases.chiefComplaint: Staff note she ignores the left side of her tray, bumps her left side into doorframes, and seems unaware anything is wrong.
cases.history: A 68-year-old woman is on the inpatient rehabilitation unit 7 days after a large right middle cerebral artery ischemic stroke. Therapists report she eats only food on the right half of her plate, fails to dress her left arm, collides her left side with doorframes and her wheelchair, and does not turn toward people approaching from the left. When asked about her left arm weakness, she minimizes it and insists nothing is wrong. She has had two near-falls reaching for objects on her left. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, atrial fibrillation (on anticoagulation), prior smoking. Medications: apixaban, metoprolol, atorvastatin, amlodipine. Allergies: none. Social: retired bookkeeper, lives with her husband in a two-story home, independent before the stroke. Family history: noncontributory. Her husband is overwhelmed and worried about safety at home. She is pleasant and cooperative but appears unconcerned about her deficits, which complicates therapy participation.
cases.exam: Alert and pleasant. Left central facial droop and left hemiparesis (arm 2/5, leg 3/5) with left-sided sensory inattention. On line bisection she marks well to the right of midline; on a clock drawing she crowds all numbers on the right side; she omits left-sided items when reading a sentence. Visual fields are difficult to assess but she does not attend to stimuli on the left even when prompted (extinction to double simultaneous stimulation). She denies any weakness of her left arm despite obvious paresis. No aphasia. Gaze tends to deviate rightward.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: She ignores the left side of space, omits left-sided items on line bisection and clock drawing, and shows extinction. What is the diagnosis?
options: Left homonymous hemianopia alone
Left-sided apraxia
Anton syndrome
Gerstmann syndrome
Left hemispatial (unilateral) neglect
stages.explanation: Left hemispatial (unilateral) neglect is failure to attend to or respond to stimuli on the side contralateral to the lesion, classically after a right (non-dominant) parietal/MCA stroke. Rightward line-bisection marks, right-crowded clock drawing, left-sided reading omissions, and extinction to double simultaneous stimulation are hallmark findings. A pure left hemianopia is a visual-field defect; neglect persists even when stimuli fall in seeing fields and includes extinction. Apraxia is a deficit of skilled movement, not spatial attention. Anton syndrome is denial of cortical blindness. Gerstmann syndrome comprises agraphia, acalculia, finger agnosia, and left-right confusion from a dominant parietal lesion.

stages.label: ASSOCIATED FINDING
stages.question: She denies her obvious left arm weakness and seems unconcerned. What is this specific phenomenon called, and why does it matter?
options: Anosognosia, an unawareness of the deficit that impairs safety insight and engagement in therapy
Aphasia, which prevents her from describing symptoms
Confabulation due to amnesia
Malingering for secondary gain
Depression causing psychomotor slowing
stages.explanation: Anosognosia is a lack of awareness of one's own neurologic deficit, common after right hemisphere stroke and frequently accompanying neglect. It matters because patients underestimate their limitations, overreach, attempt unsafe transfers, and engage poorly in rehabilitation, raising fall and injury risk. Aphasia is absent here and would not explain her active denial of weakness. Confabulation from amnesia involves fabricated memories, not denial of a motor deficit. Malingering implies intentional feigning for gain, inconsistent with her genuine unawareness. Depression would not produce specific denial of hemiparesis. Recognizing anosognosia is essential for safety planning.

stages.label: NEUROIMAGING & ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
MRI brain: large right MCA territory infarct involving the inferior parietal lobule and superior temporal cortex
Behavioral Inattention Test: severe left neglect
Line bisection: deviation 28 mm to the right of midline
Functional status: requires maximal assistance for ADLs; unsafe for unsupervised mobility
Falls risk: high; two near-falls reaching to the left

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which rehabilitation strategy is most appropriate for her left neglect?
options: Place all her belongings and food on her right side so she does not miss them
Restrict therapy until the neglect resolves on its own
Use cueing to scan toward the left, environmental adaptations and visual scanning training, and consider prism adaptation, while educating staff and family on safety
Patch her right eye permanently
Avoid approaching her from the right at all times
stages.explanation: Evidence-based neglect rehabilitation actively draws attention to the neglected side: visual scanning training, left-sided cueing, environmental adaptations, limb activation of the left side, and prism adaptation, paired with staff and family education for safety and consistent approach from the left to encourage attention. Placing everything on the right accommodates the neglect and reinforces it rather than retraining attention. Withholding therapy wastes the window of neuroplasticity and worsens outcomes. Permanently patching the right eye is not a standard treatment and can impair function. Avoiding the right side entirely does not address the neglected left and is impractical.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Her husband asks about going home to their two-story house. Given neglect plus anosognosia, what is the most important disposition consideration?
options: Discharge home immediately since she feels fine
Address safety: she needs supervision because impaired awareness and neglect create high fall/injury risk; arrange a home safety evaluation, caregiver training, and continued therapy
Recommend she resume driving as soon as she is discharged
Plan for fully independent living without supports
Delay all discharge planning until neglect fully resolves before any home modifications
stages.explanation: Neglect compounded by anosognosia makes her unsafe to be unsupervised: she underestimates deficits and overreaches, creating high fall and injury risk, especially with stairs. Disposition must prioritize safety with supervision, a home safety evaluation and modifications, caregiver training, and ongoing therapy. Discharging immediately because she 'feels fine' ignores her impaired insight. Resuming driving is unsafe with neglect and is typically contraindicated until formally cleared. Planning fully independent living is unrealistic given her assistance needs. Delaying all planning until complete resolution is impractical, since neglect may persist; planning and home modifications should proceed in parallel with rehabilitation.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Her husband asks how her left neglect will affect her overall stroke recovery compared with similar patients without neglect. What is the most accurate counseling?
options: Neglect has no impact on functional recovery
Neglect guarantees she will never improve
Neglect only affects vision and not daily function
Persistent neglect is associated with poorer functional outcomes, longer rehabilitation, and greater fall risk, making active treatment and safety measures important
Neglect typically resolves within 24 hours in all patients
stages.explanation: Persistent hemispatial neglect is a negative prognostic marker: it is linked to poorer functional outcomes, longer rehabilitation stays, increased fall risk, and reduced independence, which is why active neglect-directed therapy and safety planning matter. Saying it has no impact is false and dangerously reassuring. It does not guarantee she will never improve; many patients show partial recovery with treatment. Neglect is an attentional disorder affecting daily function broadly, not just vision. It does not uniformly resolve within 24 hours; it can persist for weeks to months or become chronic, underscoring the need for ongoing rehabilitation.

cases.diagnosis: Left hemispatial neglect with anosognosia after right MCA stroke
pearls: Left hemispatial neglect follows right (non-dominant) parietal/MCA strokes; look for rightward line bisection, right-crowded clock drawing, and extinction.
Neglect is an attentional disorder distinct from hemianopia; extinction to double simultaneous stimulation persists even within intact visual fields.
Anosognosia (unawareness of deficit) commonly accompanies right hemisphere stroke and markedly increases fall/injury risk and limits therapy engagement.

Rehabilitation actively retrains attention to the left (scanning training, cueing, limb activation, prism adaptation); accommodating the neglect reinforces it. Prioritize supervised safety at discharge.

cases.title: A Dull, Aching Shoulder Three Weeks After Stroke
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening right shoulder pain and a feeling that the arm "hangs heavy" during transfers.
cases.history: A 63-year-old right-handed man is on day 18 of inpatient rehabilitation after a left middle cerebral artery ischemic stroke causing dense right hemiplegia. Over the past several days he reports a deep, aching right shoulder pain rated 6/10, worse when nursing staff pull on the arm during bed mobility and when he sits upright without an armrest. He denies fever, chills, or chest pain. PMH includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and atrial fibrillation. Medications: apixaban 5 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, and acetaminophen 650 mg as needed. No known drug allergies. He is a retired electrician, lives with his wife in a two-story home, and previously walked independently. He drank two beers nightly before admission and quit tobacco 15 years ago. Family history is notable for coronary artery disease. On the affected side he has a flaccid arm (no active movement), with caregivers occasionally lifting him by the axilla during transfers. He is highly motivated but frustrated by the pain limiting therapy participation.
cases.exam: Right upper limb is flaccid with 0/5 strength throughout and absent reflexes; tone is hypotonic. Inspection shows a palpable sulcus between the acromion and humeral head, and a finger-breadth gap is appreciated on the affected side compared with the left. Passive shoulder ROM is painful at end-range external rotation and abduction. There is no warmth, erythema, or focal point tenderness over the joint line, and no calf swelling. Sensation to light touch is diminished on the right. Distal pulses are intact, and the hand shows mild dependent edema. No skin breakdown over bony prominences.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which finding on examination most directly explains this patient's shoulder pain?
options: Restricted external rotation from adhesive capsulitis
Acute septic arthritis of the glenohumeral joint
Inferior glenohumeral subluxation from flaccid rotator cuff and deltoid
Acromioclavicular joint separation from a fall
stages.explanation: The palpable sulcus and finger-breadth gap between the acromion and humeral head in a flaccid limb indicate inferior glenohumeral subluxation: with no rotator cuff or deltoid tone, gravity and traction pull the humeral head out of the glenoid, stretching the capsule and producing pain. Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) can develop later in the flaccid or spastic shoulder but presents with global stiffness and end-range restriction, not a palpable sulcus this early. Septic arthritis would show warmth, erythema, fever, and focal tenderness, which are absent. AC joint separation requires trauma and produces point tenderness over the AC joint, not a humeral-head sulcus.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the primary mechanism by which improper transfer technique is contributing to this patient's problem?
options: Traction on the flaccid limb stretching the joint capsule and causing soft-tissue injury
Repetitive overhead use causing rotator cuff tendinopathy
Direct compression of the suprascapular nerve at the spinoglenoid notch
Crystal deposition within the subacromial bursa
stages.explanation: Pulling on a flaccid arm during transfers applies downward traction across an unsupported glenohumeral joint, stretching the already-lax capsule and surrounding soft tissues and worsening subluxation and pain; this is a leading preventable cause of hemiplegic shoulder pain. Overhead repetitive use is irrelevant in a 0/5 flaccid limb. Suprascapular nerve compression causes posterolateral shoulder pain and infraspinatus/supraspinatus weakness but is not the dominant mechanism here. Crystal deposition (gout/pseudogout) produces an acutely inflamed, warm joint, which is absent. Education for all caregivers and consistent supported handling of the limb are central preventive measures, and any reintroduction of movement should be gentle and within a pain-free range to avoid recurrent capsular injury.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Shoulder radiograph (AP): inferior displacement of humeral head relative to glenoid; no fracture or dislocation
WBC 7.1 ×10³/µL
ESR 14 mm/hr
CRP 0.4 mg/dL
Prothrombin time within therapeutic range on apixaban
Glucose 142 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which intervention is most appropriate to address the subluxation and prevent further injury while the limb remains flaccid?
options: Aggressive passive overhead stretching several times daily
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection
Early manipulation under anesthesia
Proper limb positioning, supportive devices (sling/lap tray/arm trough), and caregiver transfer education
stages.explanation: While flaccid, the cornerstone is mechanical support and protection: positioning the arm in a supported, neutral alignment; using a sling during ambulation/transfers and a lap tray or arm trough when seated to offload the joint; and educating caregivers to never pull on or lift by the affected arm. Gentle (not aggressive) ROM preserves motion. Aggressive overhead stretching can injure the unsupported joint and provoke pain. Intra-articular steroids are not first-line for mechanical subluxation. Manipulation under anesthesia is for refractory adhesive capsulitis, not flaccid subluxation, and would be harmful here. Slings can reduce subluxation and protect the limb during ambulation but should not be worn continuously, since prolonged use promotes adduction/internal-rotation contracture and disuse; functional electrical stimulation of the supraspinatus and posterior deltoid is an evidence-supported adjunct to improve glenohumeral alignment in selected patients.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this patient's shoulder pain is left untreated and he later develops hand swelling, vasomotor changes, and exquisite tenderness, which complication should be suspected?
options: Deep vein thrombosis of the upper extremity
Complex regional pain syndrome (shoulder-hand syndrome)
Cellulitis of the dorsal hand
Carpal tunnel syndrome
stages.explanation: Untreated hemiplegic shoulder pain can evolve into complex regional pain syndrome type I (shoulder-hand syndrome), with hand edema, vasomotor and trophic changes, allodynia, and reduced ROM; early mobilization, edema control, and pain management help prevent it. Upper-extremity DVT causes swelling but not the allodynia and trophic skin changes, and is uncommon in this scenario. Cellulitis presents with warmth, erythema, and fever. Carpal tunnel syndrome causes median-distribution sensory symptoms, not diffuse vasomotor change, and would not explain the proximal-distal pattern. Management of established shoulder-hand syndrome combines pain control, gentle progressive mobilization, edema management, and sometimes a short corticosteroid course; prevention through early supported positioning, avoidance of traction, and active engagement of the limb remains far more effective than treatment once the syndrome is established.

cases.diagnosis: Hemiplegic shoulder pain due to inferior glenohumeral subluxation of the flaccid post-stroke upper limb
pearls: A palpable sulcus between acromion and humeral head in a flaccid limb signals inferior glenohumeral subluxation.
Never pull on or lift a hemiplegic patient by the affected arm; traction stretches the capsule and worsens pain.
Support the flaccid shoulder with positioning, slings during transfers, and lap trays/arm troughs while seated.
Untreated hemiplegic shoulder pain can progress to complex regional pain syndrome (shoulder-hand syndrome).

cases.title: A Clenched Fist and a Bent Elbow Six Months After Stroke
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive tightening of the left arm with the elbow and wrist staying flexed, interfering with dressing and hygiene.
cases.history: A 57-year-old man presents to outpatient PM&R clinic six months after a right hemispheric ischemic stroke. He reports that his left arm has become increasingly tight, with the elbow bent, forearm pronated, wrist and fingers curled into the palm, and the thumb tucked inside the fist. The tightness worsens with effort, anxiety, and walking. He has difficulty cleaning the palm, which has developed a foul odor and macerated skin, and his caregiver struggles to dress him. He denies pain at rest but notes discomfort during stretching. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, prior smoking. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, aspirin 81 mg daily, and baclofen 10 mg three times daily (recently started with only modest benefit and daytime drowsiness). No known allergies. He is a former warehouse supervisor, lives with his wife, and quit smoking after his stroke. He participated in therapy but plateaued. He is motivated to regain hand function and reduce caregiver burden. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: Left upper limb posture: shoulder adducted/internally rotated, elbow flexed, forearm pronated, wrist and fingers flexed, thumb-in-palm. Tone is increased with a velocity-dependent catch on rapid passive stretch (Modified Ashworth 3 at elbow flexors and wrist/finger flexors). Passive elbow extension reaches 30° short of full; passive wrist extension limited but a soft end-feel suggests dynamic rather than fixed contracture. Deep tendon reflexes brisk on the left with sustained ankle clonus and upgoing plantar. Macerated, malodorous skin in the palm. Strength: minimal voluntary finger extension; some gross grasp. Sensation mildly diminished. No fixed bony block.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature best characterizes the increased tone in this patient's affected limb?
options: Lead-pipe rigidity present equally at all velocities
Cogwheel rigidity with a pill-rolling tremor
Velocity-dependent resistance to passive stretch (spasticity)
Gegenhalten (paratonia) that varies with patient cooperation
stages.explanation: The velocity-dependent catch on rapid passive stretch, brisk reflexes, clonus, and upgoing plantar define spasticity from an upper motor neuron lesion. Lead-pipe rigidity is constant across velocities and seen in parkinsonism/neuroleptic effects. Cogwheel rigidity with pill-rolling tremor is parkinsonian. Gegenhalten/paratonia is involuntary resistance proportional to applied force, typical of frontal/diffuse disease, not the velocity-dependent pattern with pyramidal signs seen here. Spasticity reflects loss of descending inhibition after an upper motor neuron lesion, producing hyperexcitable stretch reflexes; it characteristically accompanies the other pyramidal signs seen here (hyperreflexia, clonus, and an extensor plantar response) and worsens with effort, anxiety, noxious stimuli, and rapid movement, all of which fit this patient's presentation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The soft end-feel on passive stretch and partial range suggest the contracture is currently which type, with what implication for treatment?
options: Fixed bony contracture requiring surgical osteotomy
Heterotopic ossification bridging the elbow joint
Pseudo-contracture from volitional guarding only
Predominantly dynamic (spasticity-driven) contracture, potentially responsive to tone reduction and stretch
stages.explanation: A soft, springy end-feel with velocity-dependent resistance indicates a dynamic contracture driven by spastic muscle overactivity rather than a fixed structural change; reducing tone and applying prolonged stretch can improve range. A fixed bony contracture or heterotopic ossification produces a hard end-feel and abrupt block, not seen here. Volitional guarding alone would not produce clonus, brisk reflexes, or a consistent posture. Recognizing it as dynamic supports chemodenervation plus stretching/casting before considering surgery. Distinguishing dynamic from fixed contracture is essential because it dictates the treatment ladder: dynamic spastic contractures respond to chemodenervation, stretching, splinting, and serial casting, whereas a truly fixed contracture with a hard end-feel ultimately requires surgical lengthening or release. Reassessing the end-feel after tone reduction confirms how much range is recoverable conservatively.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For focal management of this flexor-synergy pattern, which set of muscles should be targeted with botulinum toxin injection?
options: Biceps/brachialis/brachioradialis, pronator teres, flexor carpi radialis/ulnaris, and finger flexors
Triceps, extensor carpi radialis, and extensor digitorum
Deltoid, supraspinatus, and infraspinatus only
Interossei and lumbricals exclusively
stages.explanation: Botulinum toxin should target the overactive spastic muscles producing the deformity. The flexor-synergy posture (elbow flexion, forearm pronation, wrist/finger flexion, thumb-in-palm) is driven by the elbow flexors (biceps, brachialis, brachioradialis), pronator teres, wrist flexors (FCR, FCU), and finger flexors (FDS/FDP); adductor pollicis/flexor pollicis for thumb-in-palm. Injecting the extensors (triceps, wrist/finger extensors) would worsen function by weakening the antagonists you want to recruit. Deltoid/cuff injection addresses shoulder posture, not this hand deformity. Intrinsics alone do not produce the dominant flexion synergy. Targeting the correct muscles also limits the total toxin dose and the risk of unwanted spread or systemic effects. Goals should be defined first (hygiene, dressing, pain, or active function), and the injection map tailored to the specific posture; electromyographic or ultrasound guidance improves accuracy in the forearm where many flexors lie close together.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP DATA
stages.content:
Two weeks post-injection: Modified Ashworth at wrist/finger flexors improved from 3 to 1+
Passive wrist extension improved but residual flexion contracture of ~25° persists at the wrist
Palmar skin maceration resolving with improved hygiene access
No new weakness of injected antagonists; no dysphagia or systemic effects

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given residual wrist flexion contracture after tone reduction, which adjunct best lengthens the shortened soft tissue and improves passive range?
options: Immediate surgical tendon lengthening
Serial casting of the wrist with progressive repositioning
Discontinue all therapy and reassess in one year
High-dose oral diazepam as monotherapy
stages.explanation: After botulinum toxin lowers tone, serial casting applies prolonged low-load stretch and is progressively re-molded every few days to incrementally regain range in a dynamic/early contracture; the toxin-induced relaxation makes casting more effective and better tolerated. Surgical tendon lengthening is reserved for fixed contractures refractory to conservative care. Stopping all therapy abandons a recoverable window for range gains. Oral diazepam adds sedation and falls risk without focal benefit and is not a substitute for stretch/casting in residual contracture. Casts are typically changed every few days, each time capturing a little more extension as the soft tissue accommodates, and care is taken to monitor the insensate or hyposensitive limb for skin breakdown. Once range is regained, a maintenance splinting and stretching program preserves the gains and prevents the contracture from recurring.

cases.diagnosis: Post-stroke upper limb spasticity with flexor-synergy dynamic contracture
pearls: Spasticity is velocity-dependent; rigidity is not — this distinction guides the entire workup.
Inject botulinum toxin into the overactive agonists causing the deformity, not the antagonists you want to strengthen.
A soft end-feel implies a dynamic, treatable contracture; a hard block suggests fixed contracture or heterotopic bone.
Pair chemodenervation with serial casting and stretch — toxin opens the window, casting captures the range.

cases.title: A Stiffening Hip Weeks After a Severe Head Injury
cases.chiefComplaint: Nursing reports the patient's right hip is increasingly difficult to move during care, with new firmness and warmth around the joint.
cases.history: A 31-year-old man is on day 40 of inpatient rehabilitation following a severe traumatic brain injury from a motorcycle collision, complicated by a prolonged ICU stay, mechanical ventilation, and agitation now improving. Over the past week, therapists note progressively decreasing right hip range of motion, with the patient grimacing and becoming agitated during hip flexion and abduction. There is new warmth and a firm, deep swelling around the proximal thigh. He has no fever spikes documented. PMH was unremarkable before the injury. Medications: levetiracetam for seizure prophylaxis, enoxaparin 40 mg daily for DVT prophylaxis, famotidine, and scheduled acetaminophen. No known allergies. Socially, he is single, lives alone, and worked in construction; he used alcohol on weekends and denied illicit drugs per family. Family history is noncontributory. He communicates with simple words and follows one-step commands. Functionally he requires maximal assistance for mobility, and the loss of hip ROM is now limiting sitting tolerance and transfer training.
cases.exam: Right hip: markedly reduced passive ROM with a firm end-feel; flexion limited to ~70°, abduction and rotation severely restricted and painful. A deep, firm, warm fullness is palpable anterior and lateral to the joint without overlying erythema or fluctuance. No skin breakdown. Distal pulses intact; no cord or calf tenderness, and calf circumferences are equal. Neurologically he has spasticity in both lower limbs (Ashworth 2) with brisk reflexes. Left hip moves more freely. No joint instability. The firm mass does not transilluminate and feels continuous with deep tissue rather than superficial.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains the firm peri-articular swelling and progressive loss of hip ROM in this post-TBI patient?
options: Septic arthritis of the hip
Deep vein thrombosis of the femoral vein
Greater trochanteric bursitis
Heterotopic ossification
stages.explanation: Progressive loss of ROM with a firm peri-articular mass weeks after severe TBI is classic for heterotopic ossification (HO) — abnormal lamellar bone forming in soft tissues, favoring large proximal joints (hip > knee, elbow, shoulder) after neurologic injury. Septic arthritis causes high fever, effusion, and exquisitely painful micromotion but not a firm bony mass. DVT produces calf/thigh swelling with a soft, tender cord and asymmetric circumference, absent here. Trochanteric bursitis causes lateral point tenderness without progressive global ROM loss or a deep firm mass. Heterotopic ossification typically appears one to several months after neurologic injury and presents insidiously with warmth, swelling, pain on movement, and progressive loss of range, often before mature bone is radiographically visible. Risk factors here include severe TBI, prolonged immobilization, spasticity, and a protracted ICU course, all of which this patient has.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Alkaline phosphatase 412 U/L (elevated)
WBC 8.4 ×10³/µL
ESR 38 mm/hr
CRP 2.1 mg/dL
Calcium 9.4 mg/dL, Phosphate 3.6 mg/dL
Three-phase bone scan: increased uptake in all three phases around the right hip
Hip radiograph: faint immature peri-articular ossification anterior to the joint
Venous duplex of right leg: no DVT

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test is the most sensitive for detecting early heterotopic ossification before it is visible on plain radiographs?
options: Three-phase bone scan
Plain radiograph of the hip
Serum calcium level
Joint aspiration with crystal analysis
stages.explanation: The three-phase bone scan is the most sensitive modality for early HO, turning positive (increased perfusion and blood-pool uptake) weeks before mature bone appears on radiographs. Plain films are insensitive early because immature osteoid is not yet mineralized — they lag the disease. Serum calcium is typically normal in HO and is not diagnostic. Joint aspiration evaluates infection or crystals, not ectopic bone formation, and would be unhelpful and potentially harmful here. Alkaline phosphatase is supportive but nonspecific and can trend with disease activity. Because immature osteoid is not yet mineralized, the bone scan detects increased metabolic activity well before plain films, allowing earlier intervention. Serial alkaline phosphatase can be trended as a marker of disease activity and is useful for timing eventual surgery, but it is nonspecific and may be confounded by concurrent fractures or hepatobiliary disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which pharmacologic agent is most appropriate to limit progression of active, immature heterotopic ossification in this patient?
options: Systemic corticosteroids
Empiric IV vancomycin
A bisphosphonate (e.g., etidronate)
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation
stages.explanation: Bisphosphonates (classically etidronate) inhibit mineralization of the osteoid matrix and are used to slow progression of active, immature HO; NSAIDs (e.g., indomethacin) are also used for prophylaxis/early disease. Corticosteroids are not standard HO therapy and add risks. Antibiotics treat infection, which is excluded by the labs and absence of fluctuance/effusion. Calcium and vitamin D would supply substrate for mineralization and are counterproductive in active HO. Maintaining gentle ROM is essential adjunctively; aggressive forceful manipulation can worsen HO. When bisphosphonate therapy is stopped, rebound mineralization of accumulated osteoid can occur, so treatment is generally continued for a sustained period and tapered carefully. NSAIDs such as indomethacin are commonly used for primary prophylaxis after high-risk surgery or injury, and combining pharmacologic therapy with gentle range-of-motion gives the best functional outcome.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The team asks when surgical resection of the heterotopic bone should be considered if function remains limited. What is the best guidance?
options: Resect immediately to halt progression
Defer resection until the bone matures and disease activity quiets, while maintaining ROM
Surgery is never indicated for heterotopic ossification
Resect as soon as alkaline phosphatase first becomes elevated
stages.explanation: Surgical resection is reserved for mature HO causing functional limitation or nerve/vascular compromise, performed after disease activity has quieted (stabilizing/normalizing alkaline phosphatase, decreasing bone-scan activity, mature radiographic appearance) to reduce recurrence risk; meanwhile, gentle ROM preserves motion. Resecting immature, active bone markedly increases recurrence. Surgery is not categorically contraindicated — it is appropriate for selected mature cases. Operating when alk phos first rises targets the most active phase, the worst possible timing. Operating on immature bone risks profuse bleeding, incomplete resection, and rapid recurrence, so surgeons wait for radiographic maturity and declining metabolic activity. Postoperatively, prophylaxis with NSAIDs and/or low-dose radiation plus continued range-of-motion reduces the chance of recurrence, and realistic functional goals should be set with the patient and rehabilitation team beforehand.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Beyond medication, which rehabilitation measure is most appropriate to maintain joint mobility during active heterotopic ossification?
options: Strict immobilization of the hip in a cast
Forceful manipulation under anesthesia to break up the bone
Gentle, pain-limited range-of-motion exercises
Complete bed rest with no movement of the limb
stages.explanation: Gentle, pain-limited ROM helps preserve available motion and may reduce ankylosis risk without provoking the lesion. Strict immobilization promotes stiffness and contracture and does not stop HO. Forceful manipulation can cause microtrauma, hemorrhage, and acceleration of HO and is contraindicated. Complete bed rest worsens deconditioning, contractures, and other immobility complications. The goal during active disease is preservation of motion through careful, non-traumatic mobilization plus pharmacologic control. Maintaining range also helps preserve the function that surgery would later aim to restore and reduces the risk of permanent ankylosis. Therapy should stay within a pain-tolerated arc, be performed by trained staff, and be coordinated with pharmacologic treatment; combining gentle mobilization with bisphosphonate or NSAID therapy offers the best chance of limiting both progression and stiffness.

cases.diagnosis: Heterotopic ossification of the right hip after traumatic brain injury
pearls: Suspect heterotopic ossification when ROM declines and a firm peri-articular mass appears weeks after TBI or SCI.
Three-phase bone scan is the earliest detector; plain films lag, and alkaline phosphatase is supportive but nonspecific.
Bisphosphonates/NSAIDs limit active immature HO; avoid calcium/vitamin D loading and forceful manipulation.
Delay surgical resection until the bone matures and activity quiets to minimize recurrence.

cases.title: A Non-Healing Wound Over the Tailbone in a Wheelchair User
cases.chiefComplaint: A deep, foul-smelling sore over the lower back that has enlarged despite dressing changes.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man with a T6 complete spinal cord injury from a fall two months ago is admitted to inpatient rehabilitation. Nursing identifies a deep sacral wound that has enlarged over the past two weeks. He cannot feel the area and reports no pain, but caregivers note malodor and drainage. He has been spending long hours sitting in a hospital wheelchair with a worn foam cushion and has limited ability to perform pressure reliefs. PMH: type 2 diabetes, obesity, and depression. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, insulin glargine 20 units nightly, sertraline 50 mg daily, baclofen 10 mg three times daily, and a bowel regimen. No known allergies. He is divorced, lives alone in a single-level apartment, formerly worked as a truck driver, and has limited social support. He smoked one pack daily for 30 years and continues. Nutrition has been poor since injury. Family history is noncontributory. He is learning wheelchair skills and transfers but has not yet mastered independent pressure relief or skin checks.
cases.exam: Over the sacrum there is a 5 × 4 cm wound with full-thickness tissue loss; the base shows exposed firm white tissue consistent with fascia, and a probe contacts a hard structure deep in the wound. Surrounding skin is intact but indurated; there is moderate serous drainage and slough at the margins but no surrounding cellulitic erythema or fluctuance. No undermining tunnels into the pelvis appreciated on gentle probing beyond the wound base. Sensation is absent below the umbilicus; lower-limb tone is increased with brisk reflexes. The current foam cushion is compressed flat. No other pressure injuries on heels, ischia, or trochanters.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on full-thickness loss with exposed fascia and a probe contacting underlying bone-like structure, how should this pressure injury be staged?
options: Stage IV pressure injury
Stage II pressure injury
Stage III pressure injury
Deep tissue pressure injury
stages.explanation: Exposure of (or palpable) fascia, muscle, tendon, or bone defines a Stage IV pressure injury — full-thickness skin and tissue loss with exposed deeper structures and often undermining/tunneling. Stage II is partial-thickness loss with a viable pink dermis (or intact serum-filled blister) and no slough. Stage III is full-thickness loss that may show fat and slough but without exposed fascia, muscle, tendon, or bone. Deep tissue pressure injury is intact or blistered skin with persistent non-blanchable deep red/maroon discoloration, not an open wound with exposed fascia. Accurate staging guides treatment intensity and is documented at each assessment; note that an injury covered by slough or eschar that obscures the wound base is unstageable until the necrotic tissue is removed, and pressure injuries are never reverse-staged as they heal. This wound's MRI changes also raise concern for underlying osteomyelitis given the bone contact.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 11.0 ×10³/µL
ESR 62 mm/hr
CRP 4.8 mg/dL
Albumin 2.6 g/dL
Prealbumin 11 mg/dL
Hemoglobin A1c 8.9%
MRI sacrum: marrow signal changes in the sacrum beneath the ulcer concerning for osteomyelitis

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which intervention is the single most important first step to allow this wound to heal?
options: Begin systemic antibiotics empirically without further evaluation
Apply a wet-to-dry dressing four times daily
Order hyperbaric oxygen therapy
Complete and continuous offloading of pressure from the sacrum
stages.explanation: Pressure injuries will not heal while the causative pressure continues; complete, sustained offloading (frequent repositioning, scheduled pressure reliefs, and avoiding sitting on the wound) is the indispensable first step and the foundation of all wound care. Antibiotics are warranted for documented soft-tissue infection or osteomyelitis but do not heal a wound under ongoing pressure. Wet-to-dry dressings are nonselective and can damage granulating tissue; modern moisture-balanced dressings and selective debridement are preferred. Hyperbaric oxygen is not first-line and does not substitute for offloading. Offloading is achieved through scheduled repositioning (typically every two hours in bed), pressure-redistributing support surfaces, and strict avoidance of sitting directly on the wound until it heals.
