Without addressing the mechanical cause, even meticulous local wound care, antibiotics, and nutrition will fail, which is why pressure relief anchors the entire treatment plan for this patient.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond offloading and local wound care, which factor is most critical to optimize for this patient's wound healing?
options: Strict bed rest with the head of bed elevated above 45 degrees
Nutritional optimization (adequate protein and calories) and glycemic control
Limiting fluids to reduce wound drainage
Daily vigorous wound scrubbing to remove all bacteria
stages.explanation: Healing requires substrate and a favorable metabolic environment: this patient's low albumin/prealbumin and A1c 8.9% indicate malnutrition and poor glycemic control, both of which impair collagen synthesis and immune function. Aggressive nutritional support (adequate protein/calories, often with supplementation) and glucose control are critical. Head-of-bed elevation above 30 degrees increases sacral/coccygeal shear and pressure and is counterproductive. Fluid restriction risks dehydration and impaired perfusion. Vigorous scrubbing damages granulation tissue; gentle cleansing and selective debridement are preferred. Devitalized tissue and slough should be selectively debrided to support granulation, and dressings chosen to maintain a moist wound environment matched to exudate level. If osteomyelitis is confirmed, prolonged culture-directed antibiotics and possible surgical debridement are added, but none of these substitute for the foundational elements of offloading, nutrition, and glycemic control.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: To prevent recurrence once the wound improves, which seating intervention is most appropriate for this T6 complete patient?
options: Continue the existing compressed foam cushion to maintain familiarity
Recommend he remain in bed indefinitely to avoid all sitting
Prescribe a pressure-redistributing wheelchair cushion with seating evaluation and scheduled pressure-relief education
Provide a rigid plywood seat insert for stability
stages.explanation: A formal seating evaluation with a pressure-redistributing cushion (e.g., air, gel, or hybrid) plus a structured pressure-relief schedule (weight shifts/pushups/tilt every 15–30 minutes) and skin-check education targets the root cause — sustained interface pressure over insensate skin. The compressed foam cushion has failed and offers no redistribution. Indefinite bed rest is impractical, harms function/mood, and creates other pressure-injury risks. A rigid plywood insert increases peak pressures and shear and would worsen risk. Patient/caregiver education is essential for durable prevention. Pressure-relief education must be reinforced for both the patient and caregivers, with a realistic schedule the patient can perform given his level of injury and upper-extremity function. Routine skin inspection (including with a mirror or caregiver assistance over insensate areas), moisture management, and continued nutritional support together provide durable protection against recurrence after the wound closes.

cases.diagnosis: Stage IV sacral pressure injury with probable underlying osteomyelitis in a patient with T6 complete spinal cord injury
pearls: Exposed or palpable fascia, muscle, tendon, or bone defines a Stage IV pressure injury.
No pressure injury heals under continued pressure — complete offloading is the non-negotiable first step.
Optimize nutrition (protein/calories, albumin) and glycemic control to enable wound healing.
Prevent recurrence with a pressure-redistributing cushion, scheduled pressure reliefs, and skin-check education.

cases.title: A Swollen, Tender Calf During Stroke Rehabilitation
cases.chiefComplaint: New swelling and aching in the left lower leg noticed during morning therapy.
cases.history: A 66-year-old woman is on day 12 of inpatient rehabilitation after a right MCA ischemic stroke leaving her with left hemiparesis. This morning therapists notice her left calf is swollen and warm, and she reports a dull ache. She has been less mobile than planned because of fatigue and left-sided weakness, spending much of the day in bed or chair. She denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or hemoptysis. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, obesity, and prior left knee osteoarthritis. Medications: aspirin 81 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, amlodipine 5 mg daily; sequential compression devices were ordered but inconsistently applied. No pharmacologic DVT prophylaxis was started after stroke due to early uncertainty about hemorrhagic transformation, now excluded. No known allergies. She is widowed, lives with her daughter, and previously ambulated with a cane. She never smoked and drinks rarely. Family history includes a sister with a pulmonary embolism after surgery. She is anxious to return home and frustrated by slow progress.
cases.exam: Left calf is swollen with a 3.5 cm greater circumference than the right measured 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity; warmth and mild erythema are present, with tenderness along the deep calf. There is pitting edema to mid-shin. Homans sign is present but nonspecific. The left lower limb shows hemiparetic weakness (3/5 proximally, 2/5 distally) with mildly increased tone. Distal pulses are palpable; no skin ulceration. Lungs are clear, heart sounds regular without murmur, and there is no calf cord palpable superficially. No signs of cellulitic abscess or fluctuance. Right calf is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given unilateral calf swelling, warmth, and tenderness in an immobile post-stroke patient, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Empiric antibiotics for cellulitis and observation
CT pulmonary angiography immediately
Elevation and reassessment in one week
Compression venous duplex ultrasonography of the leg
stages.explanation: The pretest probability of deep vein thrombosis is high (immobility, hemiparesis, recent stroke, unilateral swelling with >3 cm asymmetry), so compression venous duplex ultrasonography is the appropriate, first-line, noninvasive confirmatory test. Empiric antibiotics treat the wrong diagnosis and delay anticoagulation; there is no break in skin or spreading cellulitis. CT pulmonary angiography is for suspected PE (dyspnea, hypoxia, pleuritic pain), which she lacks. Watchful waiting risks clot propagation and embolization. A D-dimer would be of limited value given high pretest probability and recent stroke. Clinical prediction rules such as the Wells score stratify pretest probability, but with multiple strong risk factors and classic findings, proceeding directly to compression duplex is most efficient. D-dimer is most useful to rule out DVT when pretest probability is low; here it would likely be elevated nonspecifically by the recent stroke and immobility and would not change management.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS
stages.content:
Compression venous duplex: noncompressible left popliteal and femoral veins with intraluminal thrombus — acute proximal DVT
D-dimer 3.8 µg/mL (elevated)
Platelets 245 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, eGFR >60
Repeat CT head: no hemorrhagic transformation; established right MCA infarct only
SpO2 96% on room air; no PE symptoms

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With confirmed acute proximal DVT and no evidence of hemorrhagic transformation, what is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Therapeutic anticoagulation
Inferior vena cava filter as first-line therapy
Continue mechanical prophylaxis alone
Systemic thrombolysis (tPA)
stages.explanation: Acute proximal DVT warrants therapeutic anticoagulation (e.g., LMWH or a direct oral anticoagulant), now appropriate because hemorrhagic transformation has been excluded on repeat imaging and renal function is adequate. An IVC filter is reserved for patients with a contraindication to anticoagulation or recurrent VTE despite therapy, not as first-line. Mechanical prophylaxis alone is insufficient once a clot is established. Systemic thrombolysis is reserved for limb-threatening (phlegmasia) or massive PE scenarios and carries high bleeding risk, especially after recent stroke. Direct oral anticoagulants or LMWH are generally preferred over warfarin for convenience and predictable effect, with agent choice individualized to renal function, bleeding risk, and the recent stroke. Duration is typically at least three months, and the provoked nature of this DVT (immobility) supports a time-limited course once the provoking factor resolves and mobility improves.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The therapy team asks whether the patient can resume mobilization now that anticoagulation has started. What is the best guidance?
options: Strict bed rest for at least two weeks to prevent embolization
Early mobilization is encouraged once therapeutic anticoagulation is established
No mobilization until repeat ultrasound shows complete clot resolution
Mobilize only after an IVC filter is placed
stages.explanation: Current evidence supports early mobilization (with adequate anticoagulation and as tolerated) rather than prolonged bed rest; ambulation does not increase PE risk in anticoagulated patients with DVT and reduces deconditioning, post-thrombotic complications, and other immobility hazards — important in stroke rehab. Strict bed rest worsens function and adds risk without benefit. Waiting for complete clot resolution would needlessly delay rehabilitation for weeks. An IVC filter is not required to mobilize an anticoagulated patient and is not indicated here. Maintaining mobility also helps prevent the post-thrombotic syndrome and reduces the cascade of immobility complications (further VTE, pressure injury, deconditioning, and pneumonia) that threaten rehab progress. Graduated compression and leg elevation can complement ambulation for symptom relief, and therapy intensity is advanced as tolerated once anticoagulation is confirmed therapeutic.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: What lapse in care most likely contributed to this complication and should be addressed unit-wide?
options: Failure to obtain a baseline echocardiogram
Over-aggressive early mobilization
Excessive use of compression stockings
Delayed/inconsistent venous thromboembolism prophylaxis after stroke
stages.explanation: Immobile stroke patients are at high VTE risk, and timely, consistently applied prophylaxis (pharmacologic once hemorrhage risk allows, plus reliable mechanical devices) is the key preventive measure. Here SCDs were inconsistently applied and pharmacologic prophylaxis was deferred, contributing to the clot. A baseline echocardiogram is unrelated to DVT prevention. Early mobilization helps prevent, not cause, VTE. Compression stockings/devices when properly used reduce risk; the problem was under-use, not over-use. Standardized VTE risk assessment and adherence are appropriate quality targets. A standardized, time-based protocol that prompts reassessment of hemorrhage risk and timely initiation of pharmacologic prophylaxis, paired with reliable application and monitoring of mechanical devices, closes the most common gaps. Staff education and audit of adherence on the rehabilitation unit help ensure that prophylaxis is actually delivered as ordered rather than only prescribed.

cases.diagnosis: Acute proximal deep vein thrombosis of the left lower extremity in a post-stroke rehabilitation patient
pearls: Unilateral calf swelling with >3 cm asymmetry in an immobile patient warrants compression venous duplex first.
Treat confirmed proximal DVT with therapeutic anticoagulation once hemorrhagic transformation is excluded.
Early mobilization is safe and encouraged once anticoagulation is therapeutic — bed rest does not prevent PE.
Consistent VTE prophylaxis (mechanical plus timely pharmacologic) is essential in immobile stroke patients.

cases.title: Lightheadedness Whenever the Bed Is Tilted Upright
cases.chiefComplaint: Dizziness, blurred vision, and near-fainting every time therapy raises him toward upright.
cases.history: A 28-year-old man is on day 21 of inpatient rehabilitation after a diving accident causing a C6 complete (ASIA A) tetraplegia. Therapists report that whenever they attempt tilt-table or transfer to an upright position, he develops lightheadedness, blurred vision, yawning, and near-syncope, forcing them to recline him. Symptoms resolve when supine. He denies chest pain or palpitations. He has not yet tolerated more than 30 degrees of tilt. PMH was unremarkable before injury. Medications: baclofen 10 mg three times daily, enoxaparin 40 mg daily, docusate, senna, and an as-needed bowel regimen. No known allergies. He is a college student, lives in a dormitory, and was very active before injury. He does not smoke and drinks socially. Family history is noncontributory. He is adjusting psychologically and eager to participate but discouraged by repeatedly aborting upright sessions. Fluid intake has been modest, and he often skips compression garments. The episodes are limiting his ability to progress to wheelchair mobility and self-care training.
cases.exam: Supine he is comfortable with stable vitals. On tilting, BP falls from 112/72 to 82/54 mmHg within two minutes with reproduction of lightheadedness and pallor; heart rate rises only modestly. Neurologically he has C6-level tetraplegia: intact biceps and wrist extensors, absent finger flexion and lower-limb movement, sensory level at C6, and absent rectal tone/sensation consistent with complete injury. Lower limbs show early spasticity with brisk reflexes. No focal calf swelling, equal calf circumferences, lungs clear, and heart sounds regular without murmur. Lower extremities are without compression garments at the time of evaluation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely cause of this patient's positional symptoms?
options: Orthostatic hypotension from impaired sympathetic vasoconstriction below the injury
Autonomic dysreflexia
Vasovagal syncope from anxiety
Cardiac arrhythmia
stages.explanation: A reproducible fall in BP (≥20 systolic/≥10 diastolic) on becoming upright, with only a blunted heart-rate response, defines orthostatic hypotension; in high cervical/upper-thoracic SCI it results from loss of supraspinal control of sympathetic outflow, impairing reflex vasoconstriction and venous return below the lesion. Autonomic dysreflexia causes paroxysmal hypertension (not hypotension) from noxious stimuli below the injury. Vasovagal syncope involves bradycardia and a clear trigger and is not reproducibly positional in this way. A primary arrhythmia is unlikely given a regular rhythm and the clear postural BP pattern. Orthostatic hypotension is among the most common autonomic complications early after high spinal cord injury and tends to improve over weeks as the cardiovascular system adapts and conditioning progresses. Recognizing the pattern (positional, reproducible, blunted heart-rate response, resolving when supine) allows targeted management rather than unnecessary cardiac or neurologic testing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which set of nonpharmacologic measures should be implemented first to improve upright tolerance?
options: Loosen all clothing, elevate the head of bed, and restrict fluids
Strict bed rest until symptoms resolve spontaneously
Abdominal binder, lower-extremity compression stockings, adequate hydration/salt, and gradual upright (tilt) training
Rapid full upright positioning to desensitize the patient quickly
stages.explanation: First-line management is nonpharmacologic and mechanical: an abdominal binder and compression stockings reduce venous pooling, adequate fluid and salt intake expands volume, and gradual, progressive tilt-table/upright training reconditions orthostatic tolerance. Loosening clothing and restricting fluids would worsen pooling and hypovolemia, and head-of-bed elevation lowers BP further. Strict bed rest causes deconditioning and worsens orthostatic intolerance over time. Rapid full upright positioning provokes syncope and is unsafe; progression must be graded and monitored. These measures address the underlying problem of venous pooling and reduced effective circulating volume below the lesion. Applying the binder and stockings before coming upright, advancing the angle of tilt slowly with blood-pressure monitoring, and reclining promptly if symptoms appear allow safe, incremental reconditioning while protecting the patient from syncope and falls during transfers and therapy.

stages.label: RESPONSE TO INITIAL MEASURES
stages.content:
With binder, thigh-high compression stockings, increased fluid/salt, and graded tilt: tolerates 45° tilt for 10 minutes
Upright BP now 96/60 mmHg but still symptomatic beyond 60° tilt
Morning supine BP 108/70 mmHg
Electrolytes normal; hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL
ECG: normal sinus rhythm, no conduction abnormality

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Despite optimized nonpharmacologic measures, he remains symptomatic when more upright. Which medication is most appropriate to add?
options: Clonidine
Midodrine
Furosemide
Nitroglycerin
stages.explanation: Midodrine, a peripheral alpha-1 agonist, causes arterial and venous vasoconstriction to raise standing blood pressure and is a first-line pharmacologic agent for orthostatic hypotension when conservative measures are insufficient; fludrocortisone (volume expansion) is an alternative. Clonidine is a central sympatholytic that lowers BP and would worsen symptoms. Furosemide is a diuretic that reduces intravascular volume, exacerbating orthostatic hypotension. Nitroglycerin is a vasodilator and is contraindicated here. Midodrine should be dosed during waking hours and avoided near bedtime to prevent supine hypertension. Fludrocortisone, a mineralocorticoid that promotes sodium and water retention to expand plasma volume, is a reasonable alternative or addition but requires monitoring for hypokalemia, edema, and supine hypertension. Medication is layered on top of, not instead of, the mechanical and volume measures, and the lowest effective dose is used to limit supine hypertension.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is an important precaution when prescribing midodrine in this patient?
options: Administer the last dose at bedtime to ensure overnight coverage
Combine it routinely with a diuretic to balance effects
Avoid dosing within a few hours of lying down to reduce the risk of supine hypertension
It eliminates the need for compression garments
stages.explanation: Midodrine can cause supine hypertension, so the last dose should be timed during waking, upright hours and avoided within several hours of lying down; patients should not take it right before bed. Bedtime dosing is precisely what to avoid. Routinely adding a diuretic would counteract the goal and worsen orthostatic hypotension. Pharmacologic therapy complements rather than replaces mechanical measures; compression garments and abdominal binders remain part of the regimen. Monitoring for supine hypertension and avoiding overcorrection are key safety considerations. Patients on midodrine should have supine and seated blood pressures monitored, sleep with the head of bed elevated if supine hypertension occurs, and be counseled to take doses while upright during the day. Other adverse effects include scalp tingling/piloerection and urinary retention, so the regimen is titrated carefully against both symptom control and these risks.

cases.diagnosis: Orthostatic hypotension secondary to autonomic dysfunction in cervical (C6) spinal cord injury
pearls: High SCI impairs sympathetic vasoconstriction below the lesion, causing postural BP drops with a blunted heart-rate rise.
Start with mechanical/volume measures: abdominal binder, compression stockings, hydration/salt, and graded tilt training.
Midodrine (or fludrocortisone) is added when conservative measures fail; clonidine, diuretics, and vasodilators worsen it.
Avoid midodrine near recumbency to prevent supine hypertension.

cases.title: Unpredictable Accidents and Constipation After a Spinal Injury
cases.chiefComplaint: Alternating constipation and unexpected stool leakage that disrupt his daily routine and work.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man with a T8 complete spinal cord injury from a motor vehicle collision 14 months ago presents to outpatient SCI clinic frustrated by inconsistent bowel control. He has episodes of stool leakage between care sessions and other days of hard, incomplete evacuation requiring prolonged time and manual effort. He has had two episodes of abdominal distension and one emergency visit for impaction. He denies bleeding or weight loss. PMH: SCI with neurogenic bladder managed by intermittent catheterization and spasticity. Medications: baclofen 20 mg three times daily, oxybutynin, docusate, and intermittent senna without a structured schedule. No known allergies. He works from home in software, lives with a partner, and is independent with most self-care. He does not smoke and rarely drinks. Diet is irregular with low fiber and inconsistent fluid intake. Family history is noncontributory. He is highly motivated to establish a predictable, dignified routine so he can return to in-office work and social activities without fear of accidents.
cases.exam: Abdomen is mildly distended, soft, nontender, with palpable stool in the left lower quadrant; bowel sounds present. Neurologic exam shows a complete motor/sensory level at T8 with lower-limb spasticity and brisk reflexes. On rectal exam, anal sphincter tone is increased, the anal wink (anocutaneous) reflex is present, and the bulbocavernosus reflex is intact; voluntary sphincter contraction is absent. There is firm stool in the vault. No external hemorrhoids or fissures, and no perianal skin breakdown. Sensation is absent below the umbilicus. The reflexic findings localize the bowel pattern above the conus.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on increased sphincter tone and intact anocutaneous/bulbocavernosus reflexes, which type of neurogenic bowel does this T8 patient have?
options: Reflexic (upper motor neuron) neurogenic bowel
Areflexic (lower motor neuron) neurogenic bowel
Functional constipation unrelated to the injury
Irritable bowel syndrome
stages.explanation: A lesion above the conus medullaris (T8 here) produces a reflexic, upper motor neuron bowel: the spinal defecation reflex arc is preserved, so sphincter tone is increased and the anocutaneous and bulbocavernosus reflexes are intact, but voluntary control is lost. An areflexic (lower motor neuron) bowel occurs with conus/cauda equina injury and shows flaccid sphincter tone and absent reflexes. The clear neurologic level and reflex findings make functional constipation or IBS unlikely as the primary process; the pattern is injury-determined. Correctly classifying the bowel as reflexic versus areflexic is the key branch point because it determines the entire program design: reflexic bowels are managed by triggering the intact reflex arc, whereas areflexic bowels require gentle manual evacuation and firmer stool. The neurologic level plus the rectal reflex examination together establish the classification reliably.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which bowel program is most appropriate for this patient's reflexic (UMN) neurogenic bowel?
options: Frequent manual disimpaction multiple times daily as the primary method
Maximizing loperamide to keep stool firm and avoid all evacuation
Colostomy as the initial intervention
Scheduled stimulant suppository/digital rectal stimulation to trigger reflex evacuation, with fiber and fluid
stages.explanation: A reflexic bowel can be triggered: a structured program using a stimulant (e.g., bisacodyl/glycerin suppository) plus digital rectal stimulation at a consistent time, ideally after a meal to exploit the gastrocolic reflex, harnesses the intact reflex arc for predictable evacuation; adequate fiber, fluid, and an osmotic/softening agent regulate stool consistency. Relying primarily on manual disimpaction is reactive and traumatic. Maximizing loperamide promotes impaction in a patient already retaining stool. Colostomy is reserved for refractory cases or specific indications, not first-line. Suppositories or mini-enemas chemically stimulate peristalsis, while digital rectal stimulation reflexively relaxes the external sphincter and triggers left-colon propulsion; performing these on a consistent schedule conditions a predictable evacuation. Oral agents are used to keep stool soft and formed, and the regimen is titrated to achieve complete emptying without leakage or impaction between sessions.

stages.label: PROGRAM PARAMETERS
stages.content:
Goal: complete evacuation within 30–60 minutes on a consistent schedule
Timing: 20–30 minutes after a warm meal to use the gastrocolic reflex
Consistency target: soft, formed stool (titrate softener/osmotic agent)
Frequency: at least every 1–2 days to avoid accumulation
Upright/seated position on commode preferred over supine when feasible

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which adjustment will most improve the reliability of his reflex-triggered program?
options: Perform the program at random times based on convenience
Time the program 20–30 minutes after a meal and use a seated/commode position to leverage gravity and the gastrocolic reflex
Eliminate fiber to reduce stool bulk
Restrict fluids to prevent leakage
stages.explanation: Consistency and physiology drive success: scheduling the program shortly after a meal recruits the gastrocolic reflex, and an upright/seated position uses gravity to aid evacuation, improving completeness and predictability. Random timing undermines the conditioned, scheduled reflex that makes the program work. Eliminating fiber and restricting fluids both promote hard stool and impaction, worsening leakage from overflow around retained stool. The aim is regular, complete, scheduled emptying so the rectum does not accumulate stool that leaks between sessions. The overflow incontinence he describes typically results from stool accumulating proximal to a retained, hard fecal mass, so reliable complete emptying on a schedule is the cure. Adequate fiber and fluid keep stool the right consistency, and consistency of timing, position, and technique day to day is what converts an erratic pattern into a dependable, dignified routine.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During digital rectal stimulation, this T8 patient suddenly develops a pounding headache and a sharp rise in blood pressure. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Continue stimulation quickly to finish the program
Lay him flat and elevate the legs
Stop the stimulation, sit him upright, and identify/remove the noxious stimulus while monitoring blood pressure
Administer a fluid bolus and recheck in an hour
stages.explanation: These are signs of autonomic dysreflexia, a potential emergency in injuries at or above T6 (and sometimes T7–T8) triggered by noxious stimuli below the lesion, such as rectal distension/manipulation. Immediate steps: stop the trigger, sit the patient upright (to lower BP via orthostasis), loosen constrictive items, identify and remove the cause (bowel program, full bladder), and monitor BP, using antihypertensives if it remains dangerously high. Continuing stimulation perpetuates the trigger. Lying flat raises BP and worsens the crisis. A fluid bolus does not address the hypertensive emergency or its cause.

cases.diagnosis: Reflexic (upper motor neuron) neurogenic bowel due to T8 complete spinal cord injury
pearls: Lesions above the conus give a reflexic UMN bowel (increased tone, intact reflexes) that can be triggered with suppository plus digital stimulation.
Conus/cauda (LMN) injuries give an areflexic bowel (flaccid sphincter) managed mainly by manual evacuation and bulking.
Time the program after a meal and use a seated position to exploit the gastrocolic reflex and gravity.
Rectal stimulation can provoke autonomic dysreflexia in high lesions — recognize it and stop the trigger, sit upright, and treat.

cases.title: Tight, Scarring Skin and Stiffening Joints After a House Fire
cases.chiefComplaint: Healing burns with skin that is becoming tight and joints that are getting harder to straighten.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man is recovering on the burn unit and transitioning to inpatient rehabilitation three weeks after deep partial- and full-thickness burns to ~25% total body surface area sustained in a house fire, involving the anterior neck, both axillae, the volar surfaces of both elbows and wrists, and the dorsum of both hands. He underwent fluid resuscitation, escharotomy of one forearm, and split-thickness skin grafting to several areas. He now reports tightening skin and stiffening joints, with the neck pulling into flexion and the elbows wanting to bend. He denies fever or graft breakdown today. PMH: hypertension and prior tobacco use. Medications: scheduled and PRN analgesia, gabapentin for neuropathic itch/pain, a proton pump inhibitor, and DVT prophylaxis. No known allergies. He is a married carpenter, lives in a single-level home, and quit smoking during admission. Family history is noncontributory. He is anxious about hand function for his trade and is engaged in therapy. Grafted areas are healing, and the team is emphasizing contracture prevention as he becomes more active.
cases.exam: Healing grafts over neck, axillae, antecubital fossae, volar wrists, and dorsal hands; donor sites granulating. Across the anterior neck and antecubital fossae the skin is tight with early banding that limits extension. Neck extension and shoulder abduction are reduced; elbows rest in slight flexion with tightness on passive extension. Dorsal hand skin is taut, with metacarpophalangeal joints tending toward hyperextension and interphalangeal flexion. No active wound infection, purulence, or graft loss noted today. Distal perfusion and sensation are intact. Edema of the hands is present. The contractures are forming across flexor surfaces and areas crossing joints.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Why are burns over the anterior neck, axillae, and antecubital fossae at especially high risk for contracture?
options: Because these are areas of low skin tension that rarely scar
Because these areas lack any underlying muscle
Because grafts never take in these locations
Because scar tissue contracting across flexor surfaces and joints shortens into the position of comfort (flexion/adduction)
stages.explanation: Maturing burn scar contracts, and when it crosses the flexor aspect of a joint it pulls the joint into its position of comfort — typically flexion and adduction — producing functional contractures; the neck, axilla, and antecubital fossa are classic high-risk sites. These are not low-tension, non-scarring areas; they are mobile flexor creases under repeated motion. The presence or absence of underlying muscle is not the mechanism. Grafts can and do take in these regions; the issue is scar contraction over time, which positioning and splinting counteract. Burn depth matters too: deep partial- and full-thickness wounds and those requiring grafting carry the highest contracture risk, and the longer a wound takes to close, the more myofibroblast-driven contraction occurs. Anticipating contracture at these high-risk flexor sites from the outset, rather than reacting once stiffness develops, is the principle that drives early positioning and splinting.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: To prevent contracture, in which position should each at-risk area be maintained — generally the opposite of the position of comfort?
options: Neck extended/neutral, shoulders abducted ~90°, elbows extended, and hands in the intrinsic-plus (safe) position
Neck flexed, shoulders adducted, elbows flexed, hands in fist
All joints immobilized in whatever position is most comfortable
Only the hands splinted; other areas left free
stages.explanation: Anticontracture positioning opposes the direction of expected scar pull (the position of comfort): keep the neck extended/neutral (often slight extension, no pillow), shoulders abducted around 90 degrees to protect the axilla, elbows extended, and hands in the intrinsic-plus/safe position (MCPs flexed ~70–90°, IPs extended, thumb abducted) to preserve the collateral ligaments and web space. Holding joints in the comfort position (flexion/adduction) accelerates contracture. Immobilizing in comfort is exactly wrong. Hands matter, but the neck, axillae, and elbows must also be positioned, not left free. Splints are typically fabricated to hold these anticontracture positions, often worn at night and during rest while active movement is encouraged during the day. The neck is kept in extension (no pillow), the axilla protected with abduction supports or an airplane splint, and the hand maintained in the safe position to protect the collateral ligaments and first web space.

stages.label: REHAB PLAN COMPONENTS
stages.content:
Custom thermoplastic splints fabricated for neck (soft collar in extension), elbows (extension), and hands (intrinsic-plus)
Daily ranging and stretching of all involved joints by therapy
Axillary positioning with arms abducted using a wedge/airplane support
Scar maturation plan initiated as grafts stabilize
Elevation and edema control for the hands

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: As the grafts mature and hypertrophic scarring develops, which intervention is most appropriate to manage the scar and reduce contracture?
options: Vigorous daily scrubbing of the scar with abrasive sponges
Custom pressure (compression) garments, often combined with silicone, applied to maturing scar
Prolonged sun exposure to soften the scar
Complete immobilization of all joints until scars fully mature
stages.explanation: Custom-fit pressure garments, frequently combined with silicone gel/sheeting, are standard for hypertrophic burn scar: sustained compression helps flatten, soften, and reduce contracture of maturing scar while ranging continues. Abrasive scrubbing damages fragile neoepithelium and grafts. Sun exposure causes hyperpigmentation and harms immature scar, so photoprotection is advised instead. Complete immobilization promotes contracture and stiffness; the regimen pairs compression and scar management with ongoing stretching, splinting, and positioning to preserve range and function. Compression is generally worn most of the day for many months until the scar matures, with garments refitted as edema and body habitus change. Massage, moisturization, and continued range-of-motion complement compression and silicone, and the program is sustained well past wound closure because hypertrophic scar continues to remodel and can still contract during that period.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most important principle to communicate to the patient about preventing burn contractures during rehabilitation?
options: Rest the joints and avoid movement so the skin can heal undisturbed
Once the wounds close, no further therapy is needed
Splints should be worn only if pain is present
Contracture prevention requires sustained, consistent positioning, splinting, and daily ranging — it is an ongoing, long-term effort
stages.explanation: Burn scar continues to mature and contract for many months, so contracture prevention is a sustained, daily commitment combining positioning, splinting (often nighttime and rest periods), active and passive ranging, and scar management with compression/silicone; consistency over time is what preserves function. Resting and avoiding movement promotes exactly the contractures we aim to prevent. Wound closure is not the end of rehabilitation — scar remodeling and contracture risk persist. Splints are used on a schedule to maintain position, not merely in response to pain. Setting this expectation up front improves adherence to a regimen that can feel burdensome over many months. The patient and family should understand the rationale for night splinting, daily ranging, and pressure-garment wear, and follow-up should monitor for progressive tightness so therapy can be intensified or surgical release considered if a contracture becomes fixed despite consistent conservative care.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If the dorsal hand burn is allowed to contract untreated, which characteristic deformity is most likely to develop?
options: A claw hand with MCP hyperextension, IP flexion, and thumb adduction (burn 'intrinsic-minus' deformity)
A swan-neck deformity from volar plate laxity
A boutonnière deformity from central slip rupture
Dupuytren-type palmar fascial cords
stages.explanation: Tight dorsal hand scar pulls the metacarpophalangeal joints into hyperextension while the interphalangeal joints flex and the thumb adducts and the web space contracts — the classic burn claw (intrinsic-minus) deformity that destroys grasp. This is precisely why the hand is splinted in the intrinsic-plus/safe position (MCPs flexed, IPs extended, thumb abducted). Swan-neck and boutonnière deformities arise from specific tendon/volar-plate pathology, not dorsal scar contracture. Dupuytren disease is a palmar fascial proliferation unrelated to burn scar mechanics and would not result from this dorsal injury. Preventing this deformity is exactly why early and consistent hand positioning, splinting in the intrinsic-plus position, edema control, and daily ranging are emphasized from the start. Once a fixed claw deformity develops it severely impairs prehension and often requires surgical scar release, contracture correction, and intensive postoperative therapy, underscoring the value of diligent prevention during the acute and rehabilitation phases.

cases.diagnosis: Burn-related joint contractures (rehabilitation phase) with hypertrophic scarring across flexor surfaces
pearls: Burn scar contracts across flexor surfaces, pulling joints into the position of comfort (flexion/adduction).
Position opposite the deformity: neck extended, shoulders abducted ~90°, elbows extended, hands intrinsic-plus.
Manage hypertrophic scar with pressure garments plus silicone, photoprotection, and continued ranging.
Contracture prevention is a sustained, long-term program — splinting, positioning, and daily stretch must continue after wounds close.

cases.title: Diffuse Weakness and Failure to Come Off the Ventilator
cases.chiefComplaint: Profound generalized weakness and repeated failure to wean from mechanical ventilation.
cases.history: A 62-year-old man has been in the ICU for 16 days with severe community-acquired pneumonia complicated by septic shock and acute respiratory distress syndrome. After initial stabilization, he repeatedly fails spontaneous breathing trials and is noted to be diffusely weak when sedation lifts. PM&R and neurology are consulted for failure to wean with global weakness. He received high-dose corticosteroids during ARDS management, neuromuscular blockade for the first several days, and broad-spectrum antibiotics; he had episodes of hyperglycemia managed with insulin. PMH: type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and obesity. Home medications: metformin, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. No known allergies. He is married, a retired accountant, and was independent and ambulatory at baseline. He does not smoke and rarely drinks. Family history is noncontributory. He is now off neuromuscular blockade and minimally sedated, awake and following commands intermittently, but unable to lift his limbs against gravity or generate adequate respiratory effort to sustain a breathing trial, prompting evaluation of the cause of his weakness.
cases.exam: Awake, follows commands when alert, with preserved facial movement and eye movements (cranial nerves intact). Symmetric, diffuse, flaccid limb weakness, proximal and distal, approximately 2/5 throughout with reduced or absent deep tendon reflexes. Muscle bulk appears reduced. Sensation is grossly difficult to assess but appears relatively preserved to light touch in cooperative moments. There is no ptosis, no fatigable ophthalmoplegia, and no obvious fasciculations. Diaphragmatic/respiratory muscle weakness contributes to weaning failure. No nuchal rigidity. The pattern is symmetric and predominantly motor.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely category of disorder causing this patient's diffuse weakness and weaning failure?
options: An acute brain (upper motor neuron) lesion such as stroke
Guillain-Barré syndrome from a recent viral illness
ICU-acquired neuromuscular disorder (critical illness myopathy and/or polyneuropathy)
Myasthenia gravis
stages.explanation: Symmetric, flaccid, diffuse weakness with reduced reflexes that develops during prolonged critical illness — here with sepsis, ARDS, corticosteroids, and neuromuscular blockade — is the hallmark of ICU-acquired weakness from critical illness myopathy and/or polyneuropathy, classically presenting as failure to wean. An acute UMN/stroke lesion causes focal, often spastic findings with upper motor neuron signs, not symmetric flaccid global weakness. Guillain-Barré is possible but less likely without a preceding trigger and given the clear critical-illness context. Myasthenia causes fatigable, fluctuating weakness with ocular/bulbar involvement and preserved reflexes, absent here. ICU-acquired weakness should be suspected in any patient who is difficult to wean or diffusely weak after a prolonged critical illness, particularly with sepsis, multiorgan failure, hyperglycemia, corticosteroids, or neuromuscular blockade. Distinguishing it from central causes and treatable peripheral mimics (such as Guillain-Barré, prolonged blockade effect, or electrolyte derangements) directs the appropriate workup and avoids missing a reversible diagnosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test best distinguishes critical illness myopathy from critical illness polyneuropathy?
options: MRI of the brain
Electrodiagnostic study (nerve conduction studies and electromyography)
Lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis
Edrophonium (tensilon) test
stages.explanation: Electrodiagnostics are the key discriminator: critical illness polyneuropathy shows a sensorimotor axonal pattern with reduced compound muscle and sensory action potential amplitudes, whereas critical illness myopathy shows low CMAP amplitudes with relatively preserved sensory responses and myopathic motor units, often with abnormal muscle excitability on direct stimulation. MRI brain evaluates central lesions, not this peripheral process. CSF analysis helps with Guillain-Barré (albuminocytologic dissociation) but does not separate myopathy from neuropathy. The edrophonium test assesses myasthenia gravis, a different mechanism. Bedside clinical exam cannot reliably separate the two entities, and they frequently coexist; electrodiagnostics, sometimes supplemented by direct muscle stimulation or muscle biopsy in unclear cases, provide the discrimination. Establishing the diagnosis also helps exclude treatable mimics such as Guillain-Barré or prolonged neuromuscular blockade effect, which carry different management and prognosis.

stages.label: ELECTRODIAGNOSTICS & LABS
stages.content:
Nerve conduction studies: low compound muscle action potential (CMAP) amplitudes; sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs) relatively preserved
EMG: short-duration, low-amplitude motor unit potentials with early recruitment (myopathic)
Creatine kinase: mildly elevated
Sensory responses largely normal
Repetitive nerve stimulation: no decrement
CSF (when obtained): normal protein, no albuminocytologic dissociation

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given preserved sensory responses, low CMAP amplitudes, and myopathic motor units, which diagnosis is most consistent?
options: Critical illness polyneuropathy
Guillain-Barré syndrome
Critical illness myopathy
Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome
stages.explanation: Relatively preserved sensory nerve action potentials with low CMAP amplitudes and short-duration, low-amplitude, early-recruiting (myopathic) motor units indicate critical illness myopathy. Critical illness polyneuropathy, by contrast, reduces both motor and sensory amplitudes (sensorimotor axonal pattern). Guillain-Barré typically shows demyelinating features and CSF albuminocytologic dissociation, absent here. Lambert-Eaton shows an incremental response after exercise/high-rate repetitive stimulation, not seen; repetitive stimulation here showed no decrement, also arguing against myasthenia gravis. The two ICU processes frequently overlap, but the sensory sparing points to myopathy. Bedside clinical exam cannot reliably separate the two entities, and they frequently coexist; electrodiagnostics, sometimes supplemented by direct muscle stimulation or muscle biopsy in unclear cases, provide the discrimination. Establishing the diagnosis also helps exclude treatable mimics such as Guillain-Barré or prolonged neuromuscular blockade effect, which carry different management and prognosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which management approach is most appropriate for critical illness myopathy/polyneuropathy?
options: Supportive care emphasizing minimizing/limiting contributing factors, early mobilization/rehabilitation, glycemic control, and treating sepsis
High-dose IV immunoglobulin and plasma exchange
Long-term high-dose corticosteroids
Pyridostigmine
stages.explanation: There is no specific cure; management is supportive and preventive: treat the underlying sepsis, control glucose, minimize or limit contributors such as prolonged neuromuscular blockade and corticosteroids where feasible, avoid further insults, and prioritize early mobilization and rehabilitation to limit deconditioning and aid recovery. IVIG/plasma exchange treats Guillain-Barré, not ICU-acquired weakness. High-dose corticosteroids can contribute to myopathy and would be counterproductive. Pyridostigmine treats myasthenia gravis, a different disease. Recovery is gradual and rehabilitation-dependent, with weaning progressing as strength returns. Daily interruption of sedation, minimizing benzodiazepines, aggressive treatment of infection, tight-but-safe glucose control, and progressive in-bed and out-of-bed mobilization as hemodynamics allow form the core of an ICU rehabilitation bundle that limits weakness and shortens ventilator and ICU days. Nutrition optimization and early involvement of physical and occupational therapy further support recovery.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most accurate counseling to give the family regarding recovery from critical illness myopathy?
options: Recovery is typically rapid and complete within a few days once sepsis resolves
Weakness from critical illness myopathy is permanent and will not improve
Recovery depends solely on resuming his home statin
Critical illness myopathy generally carries a better recovery prognosis than critical illness polyneuropathy, but improvement is gradual over weeks to months and requires sustained rehabilitation
stages.explanation: Critical illness myopathy generally has a more favorable recovery trajectory than critical illness polyneuropathy, but recovery is gradual — typically weeks to months — and depends on resolution of the underlying illness plus consistent rehabilitation; many patients regain meaningful function while some have residual weakness. Recovery within days is unrealistically optimistic. The weakness is not uniformly permanent; expecting no improvement is inaccurate and counterproductive. Statins can rarely contribute to myopathy, but resuming a home statin is not the determinant of recovery here, and the focus is on treating the illness and rehabilitating.

cases.diagnosis: Critical illness myopathy (with possible overlapping polyneuropathy) causing failure to wean
pearls: Suspect ICU-acquired weakness when a critically ill patient has symmetric flaccid weakness and fails to wean.
Electrodiagnostics separate the two: sensory sparing favors myopathy; reduced sensory and motor amplitudes favor polyneuropathy.
Corticosteroids and prolonged neuromuscular blockade are key modifiable risk factors.
Treatment is supportive — control sepsis and glucose, limit contributors, and emphasize early mobilization and rehab.

cases.title: Too Weak to Sit Up After Weeks on the Ventilator
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe weakness and inability to sit, stand, or perform basic tasks after a long ventilator course.
cases.history: A 71-year-old woman is recovering in the ICU after a prolonged course of mechanical ventilation (18 days) for severe influenza pneumonia complicated by ARDS. She is now extubated and hemodynamically stable but profoundly weak and deconditioned, unable to sit at the edge of the bed without maximal assistance and exhausted by minimal exertion. She had heavy sedation and limited mobility during her ICU stay. She reports feeling 'wiped out' and frightened by how weak she has become. PMH: hypertension, osteoporosis, and mild baseline frailty. Medications: amlodipine, alendronate, vitamin D, and a multivitamin. No known allergies. She is widowed, lives alone in a second-floor apartment with stairs, and was independent in all activities and walked to the market before this illness. She does not smoke and drinks occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. Her daughter lives nearby and is involved. The team consults PM&R to guide recovery, address deconditioning, and plan for safe discharge given her stairs and prior independence.
cases.exam: Awake, alert, and oriented, following commands well with intact cranial nerves. Generalized symmetric weakness, more proximal than distal, roughly 3/5 in the hip and shoulder girdle and 4/5 distally, with globally reduced but present reflexes and diminished muscle bulk. Sensation is intact. She becomes tachycardic and dyspneic with minimal activity and cannot maintain unsupported sitting balance initially. No focal neurologic deficit, no spasticity, and no cranial nerve abnormality. Skin intact. The picture is one of diffuse weakness and poor endurance after prolonged immobility and critical illness, with preserved cognition and sensation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely primary contributor to this patient's profound weakness and poor endurance?
options: A new acute ischemic stroke
ICU-acquired weakness and deconditioning from critical illness and prolonged immobility
Decompensated heart failure as the sole cause
Cervical spinal cord compression
stages.explanation: Symmetric, predominantly proximal weakness with reduced endurance, muscle wasting, and intact cognition/sensation after a long ICU stay with heavy sedation and immobility is the classic picture of ICU-acquired weakness and deconditioning. A new stroke would produce focal, often asymmetric deficits with upper motor neuron signs, not this symmetric pattern. Heart failure can cause fatigue and dyspnea but would not by itself explain the diffuse muscle wasting and proximal weakness. Cervical cord compression causes long-tract signs, sensory levels, and often spasticity, none of which are present here. Deconditioning and ICU-acquired weakness commonly coexist, both driven by inflammation, immobility, and catabolism during critical illness; the result is muscle wasting, reduced strength and endurance, and impaired balance. Confirming preserved cognition, sensation, and the absence of focal or upper motor neuron signs helps exclude stroke, cord compression, and other mimics and supports a rehabilitation-focused plan.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which intervention is most strongly supported to improve functional recovery in this patient?
options: Strict bed rest until strength returns spontaneously
High-dose corticosteroids to rebuild muscle
Early, progressive mobilization and functional reconditioning
Prolonged immobilization with passive range of motion only
stages.explanation: Early, progressive mobilization and functional reconditioning — graded sitting, standing, transfers, and task practice as tolerated — is the evidence-based approach that improves strength, endurance, and functional outcomes and shortens recovery after critical illness. Strict bed rest and prolonged immobilization worsen muscle loss, deconditioning, and complications such as contractures and VTE. Corticosteroids do not rebuild muscle and can cause or worsen myopathy. Passive range of motion alone preserves joint mobility but is insufficient for the active strengthening and conditioning this patient needs to regain independence. Mobilization is advanced in graded steps—edge-of-bed sitting, sit-to-stand, transfers, then ambulation—matched to her hemodynamic and respiratory tolerance, with rest breaks and oxygen titration as needed. This active, progressive approach, combined with nutritional support and management of any residual organ dysfunction, restores strength and endurance far more effectively than passive measures or continued bed rest.

stages.label: FUNCTIONAL ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Sitting balance: requires moderate assistance, fair static balance after several sessions
Stand-pivot transfer: maximal assist of two initially, improving to moderate assist
Grip and proximal strength improving with daily therapy
Endurance: tolerates progressively longer sessions; desaturation resolves with rest and weaning oxygen
No orthostatic intolerance after conditioning; nutrition optimized with dietitian input

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: As she improves, what is the most appropriate disposition planning given her prior independence and second-floor apartment?
options: Discharge directly home alone with no services
Discharge to long-term care permanently
Keep her in the ICU until she walks independently
Transfer to an appropriate intensive rehabilitation setting (e.g., acute inpatient or skilled rehabilitation) to continue functional gains before going home
stages.explanation: A patient with prior independence but current significant functional deficits and a home with stairs benefits from continued rehabilitation in an appropriate setting (acute inpatient rehab if she can tolerate intensive therapy, or skilled nursing/rehabilitation otherwise) to maximize recovery and ensure a safe return home. Discharging home alone with no services is unsafe given her current dependence and stairs. Permanent long-term care is premature given strong recovery potential and prior independence. Remaining in the ICU once medically stable is inappropriate and exposes her to ongoing immobility and ICU complications. The choice between acute inpatient rehabilitation and a skilled nursing facility hinges on whether she can tolerate intensive daily therapy and has the potential to benefit; given her strong premorbid function and improving trajectory, acute rehab is often preferred if she meets the activity threshold. Either way, continued structured therapy bridges the gap between ICU survival and safe independent living at home.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which additional measure best supports her safe eventual return home?
options: Home safety/equipment evaluation, caregiver training, and a graded plan addressing the stairs
Avoid any equipment to encourage unassisted effort
Advise her to move only on the ground floor permanently
Restrict all activity to prevent falls
stages.explanation: Safe community reintegration requires a home safety and equipment assessment (e.g., assistive devices, grab bars, stair strategy or rail), caregiver/family training with her involved daughter, and a graded mobility plan that specifically addresses her stairs and prior activities. Avoiding equipment increases fall risk during recovery. Permanently confining her to the ground floor needlessly limits her independence when stairs can often be managed with training and adaptations. Restricting all activity drives further deconditioning and is counterproductive; progressive, supported activity is the goal. Occupational and physical therapy assess specific tasks (transfers, toileting, stair negotiation) and recommend devices, while the daughter is trained in safe assistance. A graded plan that progresses from supervised practice to independent stair management, combined with home modifications, gives the best chance of restoring her prior independent, community-dwelling lifestyle while minimizing fall and readmission risk.

cases.diagnosis: ICU-acquired weakness and deconditioning after prolonged mechanical ventilation
pearls: Symmetric proximal weakness with wasting and poor endurance after a long ICU stay points to deconditioning/ICU-acquired weakness.
Early, progressive mobilization improves functional outcomes; bed rest and immobilization worsen them.
Match the rehabilitation setting to therapy tolerance — acute inpatient rehab for intensive needs, skilled rehab otherwise.
Plan disposition around the home environment: safety/equipment evaluation, stair strategy, and caregiver training.

cases.title: Tight Cording and a Stiff Shoulder After Breast Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Pain and a tight, rope-like band running down the inner arm with limited shoulder reach since recent breast cancer surgery.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman presents 4 weeks after a left modified radical mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection (12 nodes removed) for stage IIB invasive ductal carcinoma, ER/PR positive, HER2 negative. She reports a painful tight band extending from the axilla down the medial arm to the antecubital fossa and occasionally to the thumb base, worsened when she reaches overhead or straightens the elbow. She has progressive difficulty with hair-combing and reaching a high shelf. There is no diffuse arm swelling, redness, or fever. PMH: hypertension and hypothyroidism. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; she is scheduled to begin anastrozole and adjuvant radiation. Allergies: none. Social: works as a teacher, married, never-smoker, rare alcohol. Family history: mother with breast cancer at 62. She is anxious about whether this represents cancer recurrence and is avoiding using the arm. She has not yet started any formal therapy and has been keeping the arm in a sling for comfort.
cases.exam: Well-healed left chest wall incision without erythema or drainage. With the arm abducted and elbow extended, a visible, palpable taut cord is seen running from the axilla along the medial arm to the antecubital region; it is tender and tents the skin under tension. Active shoulder abduction limited to 110 degrees and flexion to 120 degrees, restricted by the cord and pain rather than capsular block. Passive range is similarly limited at end-range by cording. No pitting edema; arm circumference symmetric to the right. Distal pulses, sensation, and grip strength intact. No supraclavicular or residual axillary lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding most strongly supports the diagnosis of axillary web syndrome (cording) rather than an alternative?
options: Diffuse non-pitting swelling of the entire arm
A visible, palpable taut cord that tents the skin and limits shoulder abduction at end-range
Erythema, warmth, and fever over the chest wall incision
Loss of radial pulse with arm elevation
stages.explanation: Axillary web syndrome is defined by a palpable, often visible taut cord extending from the axilla down the medial arm, which tents the skin under tension and mechanically restricts shoulder abduction/elbow extension — exactly this patient's picture, appearing weeks after axillary surgery. Diffuse non-pitting arm swelling describes lymphedema, a separate complication. Erythema, warmth, and fever suggest cellulitis or wound infection. Loss of radial pulse with elevation suggests thoracic outlet/vascular compression, not cording. The cord represents thrombosed/fibrosed lymphatic or venous channels and is a clinical diagnosis requiring no imaging when classic.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Reassure her the cords resolve spontaneously and continue sling immobilization
Order urgent venous duplex and start therapeutic anticoagulation for DVT
Refer to physical therapy for gentle stretching, manual cord release, and progressive ROM
Obtain MRI of the brachial plexus to exclude tumor infiltration
stages.explanation: Axillary web syndrome responds well to early physical therapy: manual stretching/cord-release techniques, progressive shoulder ROM, and a home stretching program restore motion and reduce pain, typically resolving over weeks to a few months. Continued sling immobilization worsens stiffness and is counterproductive. The cords are not limb-threatening DVT; anticoagulation is not indicated and duplex is not needed for classic superficial cording. MRI of the brachial plexus is unnecessary without neurologic deficit or suspicion of plexopathy/recurrence; her exam is mechanical, not neurologic.

stages.label: THERAPY EVALUATION
stages.content:
PT initial evaluation:
Shoulder abduction (active) 110° -> 130° after manual cord release in session
Shoulder flexion (active) 120°
Palpable cord axilla to antecubital fossa, reproducible with abduction + elbow extension
Arm volume (circumferential) symmetric, no edema
Upper limb neuro exam: strength 5/5, sensation intact
DASH (disability) score: 48/100

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is anxious about starting radiation while her shoulder is stiff. What is the best guidance regarding her shoulder ROM goal before radiation?
options: Defer all radiation until cording fully resolves, regardless of timeline
Restrict shoulder motion to avoid aggravating the surgical site during radiation
Continue ROM rehab to achieve adequate abduction (ideally ~90° or more) so she can tolerate the treatment positioning
Begin high-dose opioids so she can passively tolerate positioning
stages.explanation: Radiation for breast/chest wall requires the patient to abduct and externally rotate the shoulder to clear the field; achieving adequate abduction (commonly ~90° or more) is a functional prerequisite, so continuing ROM rehab is the priority and is fully compatible with proceeding to radiation. Indefinitely deferring radiation harms oncologic outcomes and is not warranted by cording alone. Restricting motion worsens contracture and the very limitation that threatens treatment positioning. High-dose opioids are inappropriate primary therapy for a mechanical, rehab-responsive problem and carry unnecessary risk.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which complication should she be counseled to watch for and report, given her axillary dissection?
options: New persistent arm swelling that does not resolve overnight (possible lymphedema)
Improvement in shoulder range of motion
Spontaneous softening of the cords
Mild fatigue after physical therapy sessions
stages.explanation: After axillary lymph node dissection she is at lifelong risk for lymphedema; new persistent arm swelling (rings/sleeves tight, swelling not resolving overnight, heaviness) warrants prompt evaluation for early complete decongestive therapy, which is most effective when started early. Improving ROM and softening cords are expected favorable signs, not complications. Mild post-session fatigue is normal and self-limited. Early recognition and risk-reduction counseling (skin care, avoiding constriction/injury to the limb, gradual progressive exercise) are central to survivorship care after node dissection.

cases.diagnosis: Axillary web syndrome (lymphatic cording) with post-mastectomy shoulder dysfunction after axillary node dissection
pearls: Axillary web syndrome is a clinical diagnosis: a palpable/visible taut cord from the axilla down the medial arm that tents skin and limits shoulder abduction, appearing within weeks of axillary surgery.
First-line treatment is early physical therapy — manual cord release, stretching, and progressive ROM; most cases resolve over weeks to months. Avoid immobilization.
Adequate shoulder abduction (~90°+) is needed to tolerate breast/chest wall radiation positioning; rehab should continue rather than delay radiation.
Axillary node dissection confers lifelong lymphedema risk — counsel on early reporting of persistent swelling and limb risk-reduction strategies.

cases.title: A Heavy, Swollen Arm a Year After Cancer Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Gradual swelling, heaviness, and tightness of the right arm that no longer goes down overnight.
cases.history: A 56-year-old woman presents with progressive right arm swelling over 6 months. She underwent right breast lumpectomy with axillary lymph node dissection (15 nodes) and adjuvant radiation 14 months ago for invasive ductal carcinoma. The arm feels heavy and tight; rings and watch no longer fit, and the swelling, which once resolved by morning, is now persistent. She denies pain, redness, fever, or chest pain. No recent long travel or immobilization. PMH: type 2 diabetes (A1c 7.1%), obesity (BMI 33). Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, anastrozole 1 mg daily. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social: administrative assistant, lives with husband, no tobacco, social alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. She reports two recent episodes of the arm becoming briefly warm and red that resolved without treatment, and she is worried about both worsening swelling and recurrent infections. She has gained 6 kg since completing treatment and has not engaged in regular exercise.
cases.exam: Right upper limb diffusely enlarged compared with left, most marked in the forearm and dorsum of the hand with loss of normal skin creases. Skin is thickened; a positive Stemmer sign is noted (inability to tent the skin at the base of the second finger). Early pitting that becomes non-pitting and fibrotic proximally. No erythema, warmth, or tenderness today. Circumference: right mid-forearm 31 cm vs left 26 cm; right mid-upper arm 38 cm vs left 33 cm. Distal pulses 2+, sensation intact, full strength. Shoulder ROM functional. No supraclavicular nodes; surgical scar well healed.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which examination finding is most specific for lymphedema?
options: Pitting edema that resolves with elevation
A positive Stemmer sign with skin thickening at the base of the digits
Bilateral symmetric ankle edema
Erythema and warmth tracking up the arm
stages.explanation: A positive Stemmer sign — inability to pinch and tent a skinfold at the base of the second toe/finger due to dermal fibrosis — is highly specific for lymphedema and reflects chronic protein-rich interstitial fluid causing skin thickening. Pitting edema resolving with elevation is nonspecific and typical of early/dependent or venous edema. Bilateral symmetric ankle edema points to systemic causes (cardiac, renal, hepatic), not unilateral post-surgical lymphedema. Erythema and warmth tracking up the arm suggest cellulitis, a complication of lymphedema rather than a diagnostic sign of the lymphedema itself.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the cornerstone first-line treatment for her lymphedema?
options: Long-term diuretic therapy
Surgical lymphovenous bypass as initial therapy
Complete (complex) decongestive therapy
Strict arm immobilization in a sling
stages.explanation: Complete decongestive therapy (CDT) is the gold-standard first-line treatment: an intensive reductive phase (manual lymphatic drainage, multilayer short-stretch compression bandaging, remedial exercise, meticulous skin care) followed by a maintenance phase (compression garments, continued exercise/skin care, self-care). Diuretics are ineffective for lymphedema (the fluid is protein-rich and not responsive) and risk volume depletion. Surgery (lymphovenous anastomosis, vascularized lymph node transfer) is reserved for selected refractory cases after conservative therapy, not first-line. Immobilization promotes stasis and worsens edema; active muscle pumping is beneficial.

stages.label: OBJECTIVE MEASURES
stages.content:
Limb volume (circumferential/perometry): right exceeds left by ~22%
Stage (ISL): Stage II (does not fully reduce with elevation; early fibrosis)
Venous duplex right arm: no DVT
Skin: intact, no open wounds, no current cellulitis
A1c: 7.1%
BMI: 33 kg/m²

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: She returns 2 months later with one day of fever, an expanding warm, red, tender area over the swollen arm. What is the best immediate management?
options: Increase compression bandaging pressure and continue manual lymphatic drainage
Start empiric antibiotics covering streptococci/staphylococci and pause manual drainage until the infection settles
Begin a loop diuretic to reduce the swelling
Schedule outpatient lymphoscintigraphy before any treatment
stages.explanation: Fever with an expanding warm, red, tender area in a lymphedematous limb is cellulitis, a common and recurrent complication. Prompt empiric antibiotics targeting streptococci and staphylococci are indicated, and active manual lymphatic drainage/compression intensification should be paused during acute infection because manipulating infected tissue can worsen spread and pain. Increasing compression on actively infected tissue is inappropriate. Diuretics do not treat infection or protein-rich lymphedema. Lymphoscintigraphy is an elective diagnostic test and must not delay treatment of acute cellulitis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: For long-term maintenance and risk reduction, which recommendation is most appropriate?
options: Avoid all exercise of the affected arm to prevent worsening
Permanent prophylactic antibiotics for all patients with lymphedema
Routine diuretics plus fluid restriction
Daily compression garment use, skin care, weight management, and gradual progressive exercise
stages.explanation: Maintenance lymphedema care centers on consistent compression garment wear, meticulous skin/nail care to prevent cellulitis, weight management (obesity worsens lymphedema), and gradual progressive exercise — supervised resistance and aerobic exercise are safe and beneficial, improving function without worsening swelling. Avoiding all exercise is outdated and harmful; muscle pumping aids lymph flow. Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics are reserved for patients with frequent recurrent cellulitis, not all patients. Diuretics with fluid restriction do not address the protein-rich interstitial fluid and can cause harm.

cases.diagnosis: Secondary upper-extremity lymphedema (ISL Stage II) following axillary node dissection and radiation
pearls: A positive Stemmer sign and skin thickening are specific clinical signs of lymphedema; it is largely a clinical diagnosis.
Complete decongestive therapy (manual lymphatic drainage, compression, exercise, skin care) is first-line; diuretics are ineffective for protein-rich lymphedema.
Cellulitis is a frequent complication — treat promptly with antistreptococcal/antistaphylococcal antibiotics and pause manual drainage during acute infection.
Maintenance hinges on compression garments, skin care, weight management, and gradual progressive exercise; exercise does not worsen lymphedema.

cases.title: A Drooping Head and Stiff Neck Years After Cancer Treatment
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive difficulty holding the head up and a tight, weak neck that developed years after throat cancer treatment.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents with 8 months of progressive neck weakness and a tendency for his chin to drop toward his chest, worse as the day goes on. He must support his forehead with his hand to read or watch television. Six years ago he completed concurrent chemoradiation (high-dose external beam radiation to the neck) plus a neck dissection for stage IVA oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma; he has been in remission with clear surveillance scans. He also notes neck and shoulder tightness, a 'woody' feel to the anterior neck, intermittent jaw stiffness (trismus), and occasional dysphagia to solids. No pain crescendo, night sweats, or weight loss. PMH: hypertension, hypothyroidism (post-radiation). Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. Allergies: none. Social: former 30-pack-year smoker (quit at diagnosis), retired electrician. Family history: noncontributory. He fears recurrence but his oncologist's recent PET/CT was negative. Symptoms are gradually worsening and now limit driving.
cases.exam: Reducible dropped-head posture: chin-on-chest when upright, correctable passively and with manual occiput support. Marked weakness of cervical paraspinal/neck extensors (3/5) with preserved neck flexion. Indurated, fibrotic, 'woody' tissue over the anterior and lateral neck within prior radiation fields; limited cervical extension and rotation. Reduced mouth opening (~30 mm, trismus). Shoulder shrug mildly weak on the dissected side (trapezius). Limb strength, reflexes, and sensation normal; no fasciculations, no bulbar slurring, normal limb tone. No cervical adenopathy or discrete mass; mucosa without visible lesion.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely cause of his progressive dropped-head posture and neck-extensor weakness?
options: Tumor recurrence infiltrating the cervical spine
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
Radiation fibrosis syndrome causing neck-extensor myopathy and soft-tissue fibrosis
Cervical spondylotic myelopathy
stages.explanation: Years after high-dose neck radiation, delayed radiation fibrosis syndrome produces progressive fibrosis of muscles and connective tissue with a neck-extensor myopathy, yielding a reducible dropped-head posture, 'woody' indurated tissue, trismus, and dysphagia — precisely this delayed, painless, progressive picture in a patient with negative surveillance imaging. Tumor recurrence is appropriately considered but his recent PET/CT is clear and there is no mass or pain crescendo. ALS would show fasciculations, mixed UMN/LMN findings, and limb/bulbar involvement, absent here. Cervical spondylotic myelopathy causes long-tract signs (hyperreflexia, gait/hand dysfunction), not this isolated extensor myopathy with field-confined fibrosis.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
MRI cervical spine: diffuse soft-tissue fibrosis in prior radiation field; no epidural mass, no cord compression, no marrow infiltration
PET/CT (recent): no hypermetabolic recurrence
TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (on replacement)
EMG/NCS: myopathic units in cervical paraspinals/extensors; no diffuse denervation, no fasciculations, normal limb study
CK: mildly elevated

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the workup, which feature most reliably distinguishes radiation fibrosis syndrome from a motor neuron disease such as ALS?
options: Presence of dysphagia
Findings confined to the prior radiation field with myopathic EMG and no fasciculations or limb/bulbar spread
Elevated creatine kinase alone
Improvement of weakness with rest
stages.explanation: Radiation fibrosis syndrome characteristically confines its damage to the irradiated territory; here EMG shows myopathic units in the radiated cervical extensors without fasciculations and with a normal limb study, and the deficit does not spread — strongly arguing against ALS, which produces widespread mixed upper- and lower-motor-neuron findings, fasciculations, and progressive limb/bulbar involvement beyond any field. Dysphagia occurs in both and is nonspecific. Mildly elevated CK is nonspecific. Weakness in this myopathy tends to worsen with sustained activity (fatigable dropped head), not reliably improve with rest in a diagnostic way.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate rehabilitation-focused management plan?
options: Restrict neck movement and prescribe prolonged bed rest
Immediate surgical cervical fusion
High-dose corticosteroids indefinitely to reverse the fibrosis
Multimodal program: neck-extensor strengthening, stretching/myofascial release, possible cervical collar for support, and jaw/swallow therapy
stages.explanation: Radiation fibrosis syndrome is managed with an individualized multimodal rehab program: cervical extensor strengthening and postural retraining, stretching and myofascial release for the fibrotic tissue, a supportive cervical collar for functional head positioning when needed, and targeted jaw-opening and swallowing (speech-language pathology) therapy for trismus and dysphagia. Restriction/bed rest accelerates deconditioning and contracture. Surgical fusion does not address a myopathic, soft-tissue fibrotic process and is not indicated without instability or cord compression. Indefinite high-dose steroids do not reverse established fibrosis and carry substantial harm.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On follow-up he reports new tingling and weakness radiating down one arm with hand intrinsic wasting. Which delayed complication of his radiation should be highest on your differential?
options: Radiation-induced brachial plexopathy
Carpal tunnel syndrome
Acute ischemic stroke
De novo rheumatoid arthritis
stages.explanation: Radiation-induced brachial plexopathy is a recognized delayed complication of neck/supraclavicular irradiation, presenting years later with paresthesias, progressive weakness, and intrinsic hand wasting in the irradiated distribution; it must be distinguished from neoplastic plexopathy (which is typically more painful and may show a mass) — imaging and EMG (myokymic discharges favor radiation) help. Carpal tunnel is confined to the median distribution and would not explain a plexus-pattern deficit. Acute stroke produces sudden central signs, not a slowly progressive peripheral plexopathy. Rheumatoid arthritis causes joint synovitis, not a neurologic plexus syndrome.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which long-term principle of care should anchor his survivorship plan?
options: One-time therapy course is sufficient; no further monitoring needed
Ongoing surveillance and maintenance rehab, because radiation fibrosis is progressive and new delayed sequelae can emerge over years
Discontinue thyroid replacement to reduce medication burden
Avoid all neck movement permanently to prevent fibrosis progression
stages.explanation: Radiation fibrosis syndrome is a chronic, often progressive disorder, and new delayed sequelae (plexopathy, cranial neuropathies, dysphagia, hypothyroidism, carotid disease, second malignancy) can appear years later — so survivorship requires ongoing surveillance and maintenance rehabilitation rather than a single therapy course. Stopping thyroid replacement in post-radiation hypothyroidism is dangerous. Permanent neck immobilization causes contracture and deconditioning and does not halt fibrosis. Coordinated multidisciplinary follow-up (PM&R, oncology, SLP, lymphedema/dental, vascular surveillance) optimizes long-term function and catches emerging problems early.

cases.diagnosis: Radiation fibrosis syndrome with neck-extensor myopathy (dropped-head syndrome) after head-and-neck chemoradiation
pearls: Radiation fibrosis syndrome is a delayed, progressive disorder appearing months to years after radiation, with fibrosis confined to the treatment field.
A reducible dropped head from neck-extensor myopathy, 'woody' tissue, trismus, and dysphagia are classic; EMG shows field-restricted myopathy without ALS-type fasciculations or spread.
Always exclude tumor recurrence with imaging, but negative surveillance plus field-confined findings supports radiation fibrosis.
