Management is multimodal rehab (strengthening, stretching/myofascial release, supportive collar, swallow/jaw therapy) with ongoing surveillance for delayed sequelae like brachial plexopathy.

cases.title: An Involuntarily Turning Head That Eases With a Touch
cases.chiefComplaint: The head keeps pulling and twisting to one side on its own, with neck pain.
cases.history: A 47-year-old woman presents with 18 months of involuntary turning of her head to the right, gradually worsening. The pulling is accompanied by neck pain and a sensation of muscle tightness, and she has noticed a slight head tremor. Stress worsens it; sleep relieves it. Remarkably, lightly touching her own chin or cheek temporarily reduces the pulling. There is no limb weakness, numbness, or difficulty walking. She has no exposure to dopamine-blocking medications and no recent neck trauma. PMH: migraine, anxiety. Medications: occasional sumatriptan, sertraline 50 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: graphic designer (long hours at a screen), married, no tobacco, occasional wine. Family history: an aunt with a 'shaky head.' She has tried NSAIDs, a soft collar, and massage with minimal benefit. The abnormal posture is socially embarrassing and is affecting her work and driving. Symptoms are present most of the waking day and are not suppressible for long.
cases.exam: Sustained involuntary rotation of the head to the right (torticollis) with mild laterocollis, intermittent dystonic head tremor that is worse against the direction of pull. Hypertrophy and palpable contraction of the left sternocleidomastoid and right splenius capitis. A geste antagoniste is demonstrable: light self-touch to the chin transiently reduces the deviation. No nuchal rigidity. Cranial nerves intact; no ptosis or facial weakness. Limb tone, strength, reflexes, coordination, and gait normal. No bradykinesia, no rest tremor, no cogwheeling. No myelopathic signs (negative Hoffmann, downgoing toes).

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature is most characteristic of cervical dystonia and helps confirm the diagnosis?
options: Cogwheel rigidity and bradykinesia
Loss of pain and temperature on the contralateral side
A sensory trick (geste antagoniste) that transiently reduces the abnormal posture
Sustained clonus and a positive Babinski sign
stages.explanation: A geste antagoniste — a sensory trick such as lightly touching the chin or cheek that transiently relieves the dystonic posture — is highly characteristic of cervical dystonia and is rarely seen in other movement disorders, strongly supporting the diagnosis along with patterned, directional involuntary head turning and a dystonic head tremor. Cogwheel rigidity and bradykinesia point to parkinsonism. Contralateral pain/temperature loss is a spinal-cord (Brown-Séquard) sign, irrelevant here. Sustained clonus and Babinski indicate upper-motor-neuron/myelopathic pathology, which is absent on her normal limb exam.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Before settling on idiopathic cervical dystonia, which is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start levodopa empirically and observe response
Review the medication and exposure history for dopamine-receptor-blocking agents and consider secondary causes
Order urgent carotid endarterectomy evaluation
Begin lifelong anticoagulation
stages.explanation: Even with a classic presentation, you must screen for secondary/tardive dystonia by reviewing exposure to dopamine-receptor-blocking agents (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine) and other secondary causes (Wilson disease in younger patients, structural lesions, trauma), because management differs. Here she has none, supporting idiopathic (primary) cervical dystonia. Empiric levodopa is the test for dopa-responsive dystonia in select younger/generalized presentations but is not the first step in a classic adult focal cervical dystonia. Carotid surgery and anticoagulation are unrelated to a movement disorder and inappropriate.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
MRI brain and cervical spine: no structural lesion, no basal ganglia abnormality
Medication review: no dopamine-blocking agents, no neuroleptic exposure
Serum ceruloplasmin/copper (given screen): normal
TSH: normal
Neuro exam: isolated focal cervical dystonia; no parkinsonism, no pyramidal signs

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line treatment for idiopathic focal cervical dystonia?
options: Oral baclofen as monotherapy
Deep brain stimulation of the globus pallidus internus
Cervical fusion surgery
EMG-guided botulinum toxin injection into the involved muscles
stages.explanation: Botulinum toxin chemodenervation, ideally guided by EMG (or ultrasound) to target the specific overactive muscles (e.g., contralateral sternocleidomastoid and ipsilateral splenius capitis for rotational torticollis), is the established first-line therapy for cervical dystonia, with repeat injections every ~3 months. Oral agents (baclofen, anticholinergics, benzodiazepines) are adjuncts with limited efficacy and dose-limiting side effects, not first-line monotherapy. Deep brain stimulation is reserved for severe, refractory, or generalized dystonia after botulinum toxin fails. Cervical fusion does not treat a dynamic dystonic disorder and risks harm.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two weeks after her first botulinum toxin injection she reports new difficulty swallowing and mild neck-flexion weakness. What is the most likely explanation and appropriate response?
options: Anaphylaxis requiring epinephrine
Local diffusion of toxin to nearby swallowing muscles; reassure, supportive measures, and reduce dose/adjust technique next cycle
Tumor recurrence requiring biopsy
Permanent toxin-induced paralysis requiring antitoxin
stages.explanation: Transient dysphagia and adjacent muscle weakness are common, dose-related side effects of botulinum toxin in the neck caused by local spread to nearby muscles (e.g., pharyngeal/swallowing muscles); they are self-limited over weeks, managed supportively (diet modification, reassurance) with lower dose or refined injection technique/localization next cycle. This is not anaphylaxis (no airway/circulatory collapse, urticaria). It is not tumor recurrence. Botulinum effects are temporary, not permanent, and routine botulinum antitoxin is not used for these expected focal therapeutic side effects.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which adjunctive measure best complements her botulinum toxin therapy for long-term function?
options: Strict cervical immobilization in a rigid collar at all times
Long-term high-dose opioids
Physical therapy with postural training, stretching, and use of the patient's sensory trick, plus ergonomic adjustments
Avoiding all neck movement and stopping driving permanently
stages.explanation: Physical therapy is a valuable adjunct to botulinum toxin in cervical dystonia: postural retraining, stretching of overactive muscles and strengthening of antagonists, training the patient to use their own sensory trick, and ergonomic workstation adjustments improve function and may extend the benefit between injections. Continuous rigid collar use promotes deconditioning and can paradoxically worsen dystonia. Long-term opioids do not treat dystonia and add risk. Blanket avoidance of neck movement and permanent driving cessation are unnecessary once symptoms are controlled and are not evidence-based.

cases.diagnosis: Idiopathic focal cervical dystonia (spasmodic torticollis)
pearls: A geste antagoniste (sensory trick that transiently relieves the posture) is a hallmark of cervical dystonia and helps distinguish it from other movement disorders.
Screen every dystonia for secondary causes — especially dopamine-receptor-blocking drug exposure (tardive) and, in younger patients, Wilson disease.
EMG- or ultrasound-guided botulinum toxin to the specific overactive muscles is first-line; repeat every ~3 months.
Transient dysphagia/neck weakness after injection reflects local toxin spread — manage supportively and refine dose/technique; DBS is reserved for refractory cases.

cases.title: Weak Hands After a Fall on the Face
cases.chiefComplaint: Burning, weak hands and difficulty using the arms after a forward fall, with relatively preserved leg function.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man is admitted to inpatient rehabilitation after a ground-level forward fall 10 days ago in which he struck his forehead, hyperextending his neck. He developed immediate weakness and burning dysesthesias in both hands, with milder leg weakness. Cervical CT showed no fracture; MRI demonstrated cord signal change at C4-C6 superimposed on pre-existing cervical spondylosis and canal stenosis, without surgical instability. He was managed nonoperatively by spine surgery and is now stable for rehabilitation. PMH: cervical spondylosis, hypertension, BPH. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: retired carpenter, lives in a two-story home with his wife, independent before the fall, no tobacco, occasional beer. Family history: noncontributory. He is frustrated by his inability to button shirts and grip utensils despite being able to move his legs and stand with assistance. He has intermittent urinary urgency and some difficulty initiating voiding.
cases.exam: Disproportionate weakness of the upper limbs (especially hand intrinsics, 3/5) compared with the lower limbs (4/5). Distal worse than proximal in the arms. Hyperreflexia in upper and lower limbs, positive Hoffmann sign bilaterally, equivocal plantar responses. Patchy, burning sensory loss over the hands and forearms (cape-like) with relatively preserved sacral light touch and intact perianal sensation; voluntary anal contraction present. No spinal tenderness or deformity. Bladder scan shows mild postvoid residual. Trunk and lower-limb proprioception largely preserved. No cranial nerve deficits.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which pattern of deficits best identifies central cord syndrome?
options: Legs weaker than arms with a sensory level at the umbilicus
Complete loss of all motor and sensory function below the lesion
Arms (especially hands) weaker than legs after a hyperextension injury, with sacral sparing
Ipsilateral motor loss with contralateral pain/temperature loss
stages.explanation: Central cord syndrome classically follows a cervical hyperextension injury (often in older patients with pre-existing spondylosis/stenosis) and produces motor weakness that is greater in the upper limbs — especially the hands — than the lower limbs, with variable sensory loss and frequent sacral sparing, exactly this man's exam. Legs-worse-than-arms with a thoracic sensory level suggests an anterior or thoracic-level lesion. Complete loss of all function below the lesion is a complete (ASIA A) injury, not the incomplete central cord pattern. Ipsilateral motor with contralateral pain/temperature loss defines Brown-Séquard syndrome, a different hemicord pattern.

stages.label: IMAGING & STUDIES
stages.content:
Cervical CT: no acute fracture; multilevel spondylosis, congenital/acquired canal narrowing
MRI cervical spine: intramedullary T2 signal change C4-C6; central cord edema; no instability, no surgical compressive lesion requiring urgent decompression
ASIA exam: motor incomplete, sacral sensation present, voluntary anal contraction present
Bladder scan: postvoid residual 120 mL

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: When counseling him and his family on prognosis, which statement is most accurate for central cord syndrome?
options: He will almost certainly remain completely paralyzed
Recovery often follows a characteristic order, with legs typically recovering before the hands, and many patients regain ambulation though fine hand function may lag
Full and equal recovery of all functions is guaranteed within one week
Bowel and bladder will recover first, before any limb function
stages.explanation: Central cord syndrome generally has a relatively favorable prognosis among incomplete injuries, and recovery tends to follow a pattern: lower-extremity strength and ambulation often recover earlier and more completely, followed by bladder function, then proximal upper limbs, with distal hand/intrinsic function recovering last and frequently incompletely. Complete permanent paralysis is not the expected course for an incomplete injury with sacral sparing. Guaranteed full recovery within a week is unrealistic; recovery occurs over weeks to months and varies with age and baseline. Bowel/bladder do not reliably recover before all limb function.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His blood pressure is elevated and he has a postvoid residual of 120 mL. What is the most appropriate rehabilitation-phase management priority?
options: High-dose IV methylprednisolone protocol now
Immediate surgical decompression regardless of imaging
Strict bed rest until full strength returns
A comprehensive interdisciplinary rehab program with bladder management (timed voiding/intermittent catheterization as needed), upper-limb therapy, and autonomic dysreflexia precautions
stages.explanation: Inpatient rehabilitation for central cord syndrome is interdisciplinary: occupational/physical therapy for hand and gait recovery, structured bladder management (timed voiding, intermittent catheterization guided by postvoid residuals to prevent retention/UTI), skin and bowel care, and—because the lesion is cervical—monitoring for autonomic dysreflexia. High-dose steroids are not standard for traumatic SCI given uncertain benefit and real harms. Surgery is reserved for instability or a compressive lesion, which imaging excluded here. Strict bed rest causes deconditioning, contractures, and complications; early mobilization and therapy drive recovery.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During therapy he suddenly develops a pounding headache, BP 210/110, flushing above the chest, and sweating; his bladder scan shows a full bladder. What is the immediate first step?
options: Lay him flat and start IV fluids
Sit him upright, loosen constrictive items, and rapidly identify and remove the noxious stimulus by draining the bladder
Administer a beta-blocker and continue therapy
Order an urgent head CT before any intervention
stages.explanation: This is autonomic dysreflexia, a hypertensive emergency in cord lesions at/above T6 triggered by a noxious stimulus below the lesion—most often bladder distension. The immediate steps are to sit the patient upright (orthostatically lowers BP), loosen tight clothing/devices, and urgently find and remove the trigger, beginning with bladder drainage (catheterize/check for kinked catheter); bowel impaction is the next check. If BP remains dangerously high, a rapid-onset, short-acting antihypertensive is added. Lying him flat raises cerebral BP and worsens the emergency. Reaching for a beta-blocker first without addressing the trigger is wrong, and obtaining a head CT before treating the cause dangerously delays care.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: As he prepares for discharge with a two-story home, which is the most appropriate plan?
options: Home evaluation/modifications, adaptive equipment for ADLs, continued outpatient OT/PT, and bladder follow-up
Discharge home with no follow-up or equipment
Permanent placement in a skilled nursing facility regardless of progress
Stop all therapy because recovery has plateaued at 2 weeks
stages.explanation: Safe discharge after central cord syndrome requires a home assessment and modifications (given his two-story home), provision of adaptive equipment for self-care, continued outpatient occupational and physical therapy to maximize hand and gait recovery (which continues for months), and ongoing bladder management/follow-up. Discharging without follow-up or equipment is unsafe. Permanent SNF placement is unwarranted for an incomplete injury with ongoing recovery potential and family support. Stopping therapy at 2 weeks is premature—recovery in central cord syndrome continues well beyond that window and therapy should continue while gains are being made.

cases.diagnosis: Traumatic central cord syndrome (incomplete cervical SCI) from a hyperextension injury
pearls: Central cord syndrome: hyperextension injury (often elderly with spondylosis) → arms weaker than legs, hands worst, with sacral sparing; it is the most common incomplete SCI.
Prognosis is relatively favorable — recovery order is typically legs, then bladder, then proximal arms, with distal hand function last and often incomplete.
Cervical lesions at/above T6 risk autonomic dysreflexia: sit upright, loosen restrictions, and remove the trigger (drain the bladder first).
Steroids are not standard for traumatic SCI; manage with interdisciplinary rehab, bladder protocols, and surgery only for instability/compression.

cases.title: One-Sided Weakness and a Split Pattern of Numbness After a Stabbing
cases.chiefComplaint: Weakness and loss of position sense on one leg with loss of pain sensation on the opposite side after a back stab wound.
cases.history: A 30-year-old man transfers to inpatient rehabilitation 12 days after a penetrating stab wound to the right side of the mid-back during an assault. Acutely he had a right hemothorax (chest tube placed) and developed weakness of the right leg. Spine MRI showed a hemisection injury of the cord at approximately T8-T9 on the right; he was managed nonoperatively after stabilization. He now reports a weak, stiff right leg, loss of awareness of where that leg is in space, and—strikingly—loss of feeling hot/cold and pinprick on the left leg. PMH: none. Medications: enoxaparin prophylaxis, acetaminophen, docusate. Allergies: none. Social: warehouse worker, lives alone in a first-floor apartment, occasional alcohol, no IV drugs. Family history: noncontributory. He is anxious about walking again and confused that his 'numb' side and his 'weak' side are different legs. He has had no fever; the chest tube was removed and the wound is healing. He has intermittent constipation and reports normal bladder sensation.
cases.exam: Right lower limb: weakness (3/5) with increased tone, hyperreflexia, and an upgoing right plantar response below the lesion; loss of vibration and proprioception in the right leg. Left lower limb: strength preserved (5/5) but markedly reduced pinprick and temperature sensation beginning a couple of segments below the lesion level. A narrow band of ipsilateral (right) sensory loss to all modalities at the lesion level. Sacral sensation partially preserved; voluntary anal contraction present; bulbocavernosus reflex intact. Upper limbs normal. Healing posterior right thoracic wound, no drainage or surrounding erythema.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding pattern confirms Brown-Séquard (cord hemisection) syndrome?
options: Bilateral symmetric weakness with a clear sensory level and no crossed findings
Arms weaker than legs with sacral sparing
Ipsilateral motor weakness and loss of proprioception/vibration, with contralateral loss of pain and temperature
Loss of pain/temperature bilaterally with preserved proprioception and weakness
stages.explanation: Brown-Séquard syndrome from a cord hemisection produces ipsilateral weakness (corticospinal tract, which has already decussated in the medulla) and ipsilateral loss of vibration/proprioception (dorsal columns, which ascend uncrossed until the medulla), together with contralateral loss of pain and temperature (spinothalamic tract, which crosses within a segment or two of entry) beginning a few levels below the lesion — exactly this man's crossed pattern. Bilateral symmetric weakness without crossed findings is a transverse/complete pattern. Arms-worse-than-legs with sacral sparing is central cord syndrome. Bilateral pain/temperature loss with preserved proprioception describes anterior cord syndrome.

stages.label: IMAGING & ASIA EXAM
stages.content:
MRI thoracic spine: right hemicord injury at T8-T9; no ongoing compression, no instability
ASIA classification: motor incomplete (ASIA D)
Sacral exam: sensation partially preserved; voluntary anal contraction present; bulbocavernosus reflex present (spinal shock resolved)
DVT screen: none; on prophylaxis
Wound: healing, no infection

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most accurate prognostic counseling for Brown-Séquard syndrome?
options: It carries the worst prognosis of all incomplete cord syndromes
It has among the best prognoses of the incomplete cord syndromes, and most patients regain functional ambulation
Functional recovery is impossible without surgery
Recovery of pain sensation always precedes any motor recovery
stages.explanation: Brown-Séquard syndrome has among the best prognoses of the incomplete spinal cord syndromes: the majority of patients regain functional ambulation and bowel/bladder continence, particularly when, as here, the injury is incomplete with preserved sacral function and the ipsilateral leg retains some strength and proprioception for motor planning. It does not carry the worst prognosis (anterior cord syndrome is generally the least favorable). Recovery is expected with rehabilitation even without surgery when there is no ongoing compression/instability. There is no rule that pain sensation recovers before motor function; motor recovery is often robust.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which rehabilitation safety issue most directly arises from his crossed sensory pattern?
options: He needs no special precautions because one leg has normal strength
He requires permanent bed rest to prevent falls
Vibration loss in the weak leg means he cannot participate in gait training
The leg with intact strength but absent pain/temperature sensation is at risk for unnoticed burns and pressure injuries and needs protective skin/temperature precautions
stages.explanation: The contralateral (left) leg has preserved strength but lost pain and temperature sensation, so it is vulnerable to unrecognized thermal burns and pressure injuries (e.g., hot bath water, heating pads, prolonged pressure). Education on skin checks, temperature testing of water, footwear, and pressure relief is essential. Saying no precautions are needed ignores this real risk. Permanent bed rest is unwarranted and harmful for an ASIA D injury with good ambulation potential. Proprioceptive loss in the right leg complicates but does not preclude gait training—therapists use visual feedback and compensatory strategies, and he should actively participate.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: He develops painful spasticity and clonus in the right leg interfering with sleep and therapy. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Stretching/positioning and an oral antispasticity agent (e.g., baclofen), after checking for noxious triggers
Immediate intrathecal baclofen pump implantation
Complete immobilization of the leg in a cast
Surgical tendon transfer
stages.explanation: Initial management of problematic spasticity is conservative and stepwise: first identify and treat noxious triggers that worsen spasticity (UTI, constipation, pressure injury, DVT), then optimize a stretching/positioning/ROM program, and add an oral antispasticity agent such as baclofen (or tizanidine) titrated to effect. Intrathecal baclofen pumps and focal/surgical interventions are reserved for spasticity refractory to these measures. Casting/immobilization promotes contracture and worsens function. Tendon transfer is a late surgical option for fixed deformity, not initial therapy for dynamic spasticity in early rehabilitation.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He lives alone in a first-floor apartment and is now walking with a cane and supervision. What discharge plan is most appropriate?
options: Discharge home with outpatient PT/OT, a home safety evaluation, skin-protection education, and bowel program follow-up
Indefinite inpatient stay until sensation fully normalizes
Discharge with no services since he can take a few steps
Permanent wheelchair prescription with no further gait training
stages.explanation: Given his favorable trajectory (ambulating with a cane and supervision, ASIA D), the appropriate plan is discharge home with continued outpatient physical and occupational therapy, a home safety/equipment evaluation, explicit skin-protection and temperature-precaution education for his insensate leg, and bowel-program follow-up. Keeping him inpatient until sensation 'fully normalizes' is unrealistic and unnecessary. Discharging with no services abandons ongoing recovery and safety needs. A permanent wheelchair with no further gait training contradicts his ambulatory progress and the good prognosis of Brown-Séquard syndrome.

cases.diagnosis: Brown-Séquard syndrome (spinal cord hemisection) from a penetrating thoracic injury
pearls: Brown-Séquard = cord hemisection: ipsilateral weakness and dorsal-column loss (vibration/proprioception) with contralateral pain/temperature loss a few levels below the lesion.
The crossed pattern reflects tract anatomy: corticospinal and dorsal columns are ipsilateral at cord level; spinothalamic has already crossed.
It has among the best prognoses of incomplete cord syndromes — most patients regain functional ambulation and continence.
Protect the strong-but-insensate limb from burns and pressure injury; manage spasticity conservatively first (treat triggers, stretch, oral agents).

cases.title: Saddle Numbness and Early Bladder Trouble With Mixed Reflexes
cases.chiefComplaint: Back pain with new saddle numbness and difficulty urinating that came on relatively symmetrically.
cases.history: A 44-year-old man is admitted with 5 days of worsening low back pain radiating into both buttocks, new numbness in the saddle region, and difficulty starting and emptying his urine with a sense of incomplete voiding. Symptoms have been relatively symmetric. He notes mild bilateral leg heaviness but is still walking. MRI shows a lesion at the T12-L1 vertebral level compressing the lowest cord segment (conus). PMH: hypertension. Medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: truck driver, married with two children, no tobacco, occasional alcohol. Family history: father with prostate cancer. He has had no fever, IV drug use, or prior cancer diagnosis. He is alarmed by the urinary symptoms and the numbness 'where he sits.' Bowel movements have become less predictable. He denies recent trauma. He has been taking acetaminophen and a short course of an NSAID without relief and was referred in from clinic when the bladder symptoms appeared.
cases.exam: Symmetric saddle (perianal/perineal) sensory loss to pinprick and light touch. Bladder distended; high postvoid residual. Mild, relatively symmetric lower-limb weakness; mixed picture: brisk patellar reflexes but reduced/absent ankle reflexes; equivocal plantar responses. Decreased anal sphincter tone with diminished voluntary contraction; bulbocavernosus reflex preserved. Back exam without midline step-off; mild lumbar tenderness. Vibration/proprioception largely intact in the feet. No upper-limb deficits. Lower-limb pain is less prominent and more symmetric than a classic radicular syndrome.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which features best distinguish conus medullaris syndrome from cauda equina syndrome?
options: Conus medullaris presents with severe asymmetric radicular pain and purely flaccid areflexic legs
Cauda equina spares the bladder entirely
Conus medullaris shows early/prominent symmetric bladder-bowel and saddle dysfunction with mixed UMN/LMN signs, whereas cauda equina is typically more asymmetric, pain-predominant, and purely LMN
Conus medullaris never affects the saddle region
stages.explanation: Conus medullaris syndrome involves the terminal cord (around T12-L1) and characteristically causes early, prominent, relatively symmetric bowel/bladder and saddle dysfunction with a mixed upper- and lower-motor-neuron picture (e.g., brisk knee but lost ankle reflexes, possible up/equivocal plantars) and comparatively less back/leg pain. Cauda equina syndrome (nerve roots below the cord) is typically more asymmetric, pain-predominant (radicular), and purely lower-motor-neuron (flaccid, areflexic). Saying conus is purely flaccid with severe asymmetric pain describes cauda equina. Cauda equina classically does affect the bladder (urinary retention). Conus does affect the saddle region.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given symmetric saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, and a compressive lesion on MRI, what is the most urgent next step?
options: Urgent neurosurgical consultation for decompression and immediate bladder decompression with catheterization
Discharge with oral steroids and outpatient follow-up
Begin physical therapy and reassess in one week
Order an EMG/nerve conduction study before any intervention
stages.explanation: A compressive conus lesion with acute saddle anesthesia and urinary retention is a neurosurgical emergency: urgent surgical decompression offers the best chance of preserving/recovering bowel, bladder, and ambulatory function, and the distended bladder should be immediately decompressed with catheterization to prevent further detrusor injury. Discharge with oral steroids and outpatient follow-up dangerously delays definitive care and risks permanent deficits. Starting PT and reassessing in a week is unacceptable for an acute compressive emergency. EMG/NCS is an elective electrodiagnostic study that must not delay emergent imaging-guided surgical decompression.

stages.label: IMAGING & STUDIES
stages.content:
MRI lumbar/thoracolumbar spine: enhancing lesion at T12-L1 compressing the conus medullaris
Postvoid residual (catheterized): 600 mL
CBC: within normal limits; ESR/CRP mildly elevated
Neuro exam: mixed UMN/LMN signs, symmetric saddle loss, reduced sphincter tone
Neurosurgery consulted: planning urgent decompression and tissue diagnosis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After decompression he has a persistent neurogenic bladder with high residual volumes. What is the most appropriate bladder management on the rehab unit?
options: Leave an indwelling Foley catheter indefinitely as the default
Establish a clean intermittent catheterization program with residual monitoring and urologic follow-up
Restrict fluids severely to avoid the need to void
Rely on Credé/abdominal straining alone without monitoring
stages.explanation: For neurogenic bladder with high postvoid residuals, clean intermittent catheterization is the preferred long-term strategy: it empties the bladder reliably, lowers the rate of UTIs and bladder/upper-tract complications compared with chronic indwelling catheters, and is paired with residual monitoring and urology follow-up (urodynamics as needed). A default indefinite indwelling Foley increases infection, stones, and bladder cancer risk and is reserved for select situations. Severe fluid restriction risks dehydration, stones, and infection and does not address detrusor dysfunction. Credé/straining alone without monitoring can raise upper-tract pressures and is unsafe as a sole method.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He asks whether his bladder and saddle function will return. What is the most accurate counseling?
options: Recovery is guaranteed once the compression is removed
Bladder function never recovers in any spinal injury
He should expect no recovery and proceed directly to permanent urinary diversion
Outcome depends on the severity and duration of compression before decompression; bowel/bladder and saddle deficits may persist, so early surgery matters and ongoing rehab is needed
stages.explanation: Prognosis after conus compression depends heavily on the severity and the duration of compression before decompression — which is precisely why urgent surgery is emphasized. Some patients recover meaningfully, but bowel, bladder, and saddle sensory deficits can persist, so realistic counseling pairs cautious optimism with a structured rehabilitation and bladder-management plan and urologic follow-up. Recovery is not guaranteed simply by removing compression. It is false that bladder function never recovers in spinal injury. Jumping to permanent urinary diversion is premature before observing recovery and trialing conservative neurogenic-bladder management such as intermittent catheterization.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two weeks later he develops fever, cloudy malodorous urine, and rising spasticity. What is the most likely cause and the appropriate response?
options: Urinary tract infection from neurogenic bladder; obtain urinalysis/culture, treat appropriately, and review catheterization technique/frequency
Expected post-surgical change requiring no action
Immediate removal of all rehabilitation activities permanently
Start prophylactic antibiotics for life to prevent any future infection
stages.explanation: Fever with cloudy, malodorous urine and a sudden increase in spasticity (a noxious-stimulus marker) in a patient with neurogenic bladder strongly indicates a urinary tract infection. The appropriate response is to obtain urinalysis and culture, treat symptomatic infection with culture-guided antibiotics, and review the intermittent-catheterization technique and frequency to reduce recurrence. This is not a benign expected post-op change. Permanently stopping rehabilitation is harmful and unwarranted. Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics are not standard for neurogenic bladder and promote resistance; they are considered only in selected recurrent cases under specialist guidance.

cases.diagnosis: Conus medullaris syndrome from a compressive T12-L1 lesion
pearls: Conus medullaris syndrome: lesion at ~T12-L1 with early, symmetric bowel/bladder and saddle dysfunction and MIXED UMN/LMN signs (brisk knees, absent ankles).
Contrast with cauda equina: more asymmetric, pain-predominant, purely LMN (flaccid, areflexic); both can cause urinary retention.
Acute compressive conus with saddle anesthesia and retention is a surgical emergency — decompress urgently and catheterize immediately; outcome depends on time to decompression.
Manage neurogenic bladder with clean intermittent catheterization and residual monitoring; a sudden rise in spasticity plus fever suggests UTI.

cases.title: Headaches and Brain Fog in a Teen Athlete After a Collision
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent headache, dizziness, and trouble concentrating since a head-to-head hit during a football game.
cases.history: A 17-year-old high-school football player presents 3 days after a head-to-head collision. He did not lose consciousness but was briefly dazed and removed from play. Since then he has had a constant pressure headache, dizziness, sensitivity to light and noise, difficulty concentrating in class, and feeling 'slowed down.' He has had trouble falling asleep and is more irritable than usual. There has been no worsening headache, repeated vomiting, weakness, slurred speech, double vision, or seizures. PMH: one prior concussion 2 years ago with full recovery. Medications: none; took ibuprofen with partial relief. Allergies: none. Social: junior in high school, lives with parents, no alcohol, tobacco, or drugs; honor-roll student worried about falling behind. Family history: migraine in mother. His parents ask when he can return to football and to full classwork. He has been resting in a dark room and avoiding all screens and school since the injury, which has not clearly helped and has worsened his mood.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, conversant but appears fatigued and light-averse. Pupils equal and reactive; extraocular movements intact but smooth-pursuit and convergence testing provoke symptoms (vestibulo-ocular symptom provocation). No nystagmus at rest. Cranial nerves otherwise normal. Motor 5/5 throughout, sensation intact, reflexes symmetric, plantar responses downgoing. Gait normal; tandem gait slightly unsteady. Romberg negative. No skull tenderness, no Battle sign, no hemotympanum, no focal neurologic deficit. Neck supple, nontender. Cognitive screen shows mildly reduced concentration and delayed recall.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which finding would mandate emergent neuroimaging rather than clinical concussion management?
options: Photophobia and difficulty concentrating
Progressively worsening headache with repeated vomiting, increasing drowsiness, or focal neurologic deficit
Mild irritability and trouble sleeping
Symptom provocation with convergence testing
stages.explanation: Concussion is a clinical diagnosis and most patients do not need imaging; CT is reserved for red flags suggesting structural injury: progressively worsening or severe headache, repeated vomiting, increasing drowsiness/declining consciousness, focal neurologic deficits, seizures, signs of skull base fracture, or anticoagulation. Photophobia and concentration difficulty are typical concussion symptoms, not imaging triggers. Mild irritability and sleep disturbance are common post-concussive complaints. Symptom provocation with convergence/pursuit reflects a vestibulo-ocular component of concussion that guides targeted rehab, not emergent imaging. Recognizing true deterioration prevents missing an evolving hematoma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His parents have kept him in a dark room avoiding all activity and school for 3 days. What is the best current guidance on early management?
options: After a brief (24-48 hour) period of relative rest, begin gradual return to light cognitive and low-level physical activity below the symptom-exacerbation threshold
Continue strict 'cocoon' rest in a dark room until completely symptom-free
Resume full contact practice immediately since he had no loss of consciousness
Prescribe complete bed rest for two weeks
stages.explanation: Current guidelines have moved away from prolonged strict rest. After a brief initial period of relative rest (about 24-48 hours), patients should gradually resume light cognitive activity and sub-symptom-threshold aerobic activity (e.g., light walking/stationary cycling), advancing as tolerated; early light activity speeds recovery and prolonged 'cocooning' worsens mood and delays recovery. Resuming full contact immediately is dangerous and ignores return-to-play stepwise protocols and second-impact risk. Two weeks of complete bed rest is outdated and harmful. Activity should stay below the level that markedly worsens symptoms, then progress.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: How should his return to school (return-to-learn) be structured?
options: Keep him out of school entirely until all symptoms resolve
Return to full unmodified academic load immediately
A graded, stepwise return to learning with academic accommodations (reduced workload, breaks, extra time), advancing as symptoms allow before full return-to-play
Allow only physical activity but no schoolwork until cleared
stages.explanation: Return-to-learn should be graded and individualized: reintroduce school with temporary accommodations (shortened days, reduced workload, rest breaks, extended time, reduced screen exposure), progressing stepwise as cognitive symptoms tolerate. Return-to-learn generally precedes and is more conservative-paced than return-to-play; a student should be back to full academics before full contact sport. Keeping him out entirely until symptom-free delays recovery and harms academics and mood. Returning to a full unmodified load immediately often exacerbates symptoms. Allowing physical activity while forbidding all schoolwork inverts the appropriate priority of cognitive recovery and learning.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP (2 WEEKS)
stages.content:
Symptoms much improved; headache occasional, concentration near baseline
SCAT/symptom score: markedly reduced from initial visit
Vestibulo-ocular screen: minimal residual provocation
Neuro exam: normal, tandem gait normal
Back to full academics without accommodations; asymptomatic at rest

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He is now symptom-free at rest and back to full academics. What is the correct approach to return-to-play?
options: Clear him for full contact games today since he feels fine
Have him skip the rest of the season regardless of recovery
Allow contact play but only for half the game

Proceed through a stepwise, symptom-limited return-to-sport progression with at least 24 hours per stage, returning to contact only after completing all stages symptom-free and obtaining medical clearance
stages.explanation: Return-to-play follows a stepwise, symptom-limited progression (e.g., light aerobic exercise → sport-specific exercise → non-contact training drills → full-contact practice → game play), spending at least 24 hours at each stage and only advancing if symptom-free; if symptoms recur, the athlete drops back a stage. Final clearance for contact requires completing all stages without symptoms and medical sign-off. Clearing him for full contact the same day he becomes symptom-free skips the protocol and risks recurrent or second-impact injury. Benching him for the season is unnecessary once he recovers. 'Half a game' of contact is not a recognized safe step.

cases.diagnosis: Sport-related concussion (mild traumatic brain injury) with post-concussion symptoms
pearls: Concussion is a clinical diagnosis; image only for red flags (worsening headache, repeated vomiting, declining consciousness, focal deficits, seizures, skull-base signs).
Abandon prolonged strict rest: after ~24-48 hours of relative rest, begin graded sub-symptom-threshold cognitive and physical activity.
Return-to-learn (with academic accommodations) generally precedes return-to-play and is paced by cognitive symptoms.
Return-to-play is a stepwise, symptom-limited progression with ≥24 hours per stage and medical clearance before full contact.

cases.title: Brief Spinning Spells When Rolling Over in Bed
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden, brief episodes of the room spinning triggered by head movements such as rolling over or looking up.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman presents with 2 weeks of recurrent vertigo. Each spell is intense but brief—lasting under a minute—and is reliably triggered by changing head position: rolling over in bed, lying down, getting up, or tilting her head back to reach a shelf. Between episodes she feels well, though slightly unsteady and nauseated. There is no hearing loss, ear fullness, tinnitus, headache, weakness, numbness, slurred speech, double vision, or difficulty walking. She has not had recent upper respiratory illness. PMH: hypertension, osteopenia. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, calcium/vitamin D. Allergies: none. Social: librarian, lives with spouse, no tobacco, rare alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. She is frightened the spinning means a stroke, and the episodes have made her afraid to lie flat or drive. She denies any continuous, persistent vertigo and notes the spells always settle quickly once she stops moving. She has had no falls but feels off-balance after each episode for a short while.
cases.exam: General neuro exam normal: cranial nerves intact, no spontaneous nystagmus on primary gaze, no gaze-evoked nystagmus, normal finger-nose and heel-shin, full strength, symmetric reflexes, normal gait, negative Romberg. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally; tympanic membranes normal. No dysarthria or dysmetria. Head-impulse test normal bilaterally; smooth pursuit and saccades normal. There are no central signs (no skew deviation, no vertical/direction-changing nystagmus). The remainder of the cardiovascular and general exam is unremarkable. She is anxious but in no acute distress between provoked episodes.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which bedside maneuver is the diagnostic test of choice for posterior-canal BPPV?
options: Romberg test
Head-impulse (head-thrust) test alone
Valsalva maneuver
Dix-Hallpike maneuver
stages.explanation: The Dix-Hallpike maneuver is the diagnostic test of choice for posterior-canal BPPV: the patient is moved rapidly from sitting to supine with the head turned 45° and extended, and a positive result reproduces vertigo with a characteristic transient, fatigable, upbeating-torsional nystagmus after a brief latency. The Romberg test assesses proprioceptive/balance integrity, not positional canalithiasis. The head-impulse test evaluates the vestibulo-ocular reflex and helps distinguish peripheral from central acute vestibular syndromes but does not diagnose BPPV. Valsalva is used for other indications (e.g., perilymph fistula screening), not BPPV diagnosis.

stages.label: BEDSIDE MANEUVER
stages.content:
Dix-Hallpike, head turned right: after ~3-5 second latency, intense vertigo with transient upbeating-torsional nystagmus lasting ~20-30 seconds, then resolves (fatigable)
Dix-Hallpike, head turned left: no nystagmus, no vertigo
No vertical or direction-changing nystagmus; no skew
HINTS exam: peripheral pattern (no central signs)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The Dix-Hallpike findings point to right posterior-canal BPPV. Which feature most reassures you the process is peripheral rather than central?
options: Vertigo that is severe
Nystagmus with brief latency, that is transient (fatigable) and upbeating-torsional, without central signs
Presence of any nystagmus at all
Vertigo provoked by position change
stages.explanation: Peripheral (canalithiasis) nystagmus in BPPV is upbeating-torsional, begins after a brief latency, is transient and fatigable, and occurs with an otherwise normal neurologic exam and no central signs (no purely vertical/torsional persistent nystagmus, no direction-changing gaze-evoked nystagmus, no skew deviation, no other deficits). Severity of vertigo alone does not localize the lesion. The mere presence of nystagmus does not distinguish peripheral from central—its characteristics do. Positional provocation occurs in both BPPV and central positional vertigo; it is the latency, fatigability, direction, and absence of central signs that reassure you it is peripheral.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line treatment for confirmed posterior-canal BPPV?
options: Long-term daily meclizine
Urgent MRI brain and vestibular suppressant infusion
Canalith repositioning with the Epley maneuver
Surgical labyrinthectomy
stages.explanation: Canalith repositioning—classically the Epley maneuver—is the first-line, highly effective treatment for posterior-canal BPPV, using a sequence of timed head/body positions to move displaced otoconia out of the affected canal back into the utricle, often resolving symptoms in one or a few sessions. Chronic daily vestibular suppressants like meclizine only mask symptoms, impair central compensation, and are not curative; they are at most short-term adjuncts. Urgent MRI is unnecessary when the exam is classically peripheral with no central signs. Surgical procedures (e.g., canal plugging) are reserved for rare, intractable cases, never first-line.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After a successful Epley maneuver, what is the most appropriate follow-up guidance?
options: Reassure her it can recur, teach home repositioning/balance exercises, and advise return if she develops new neurologic symptoms or persistent/atypical vertigo
Tell her BPPV never recurs and no follow-up is needed
Start lifelong daily benzodiazepines to prevent recurrence
Restrict all head movement permanently
stages.explanation: Appropriate follow-up includes counseling that BPPV is benign but can recur, teaching home repositioning maneuvers and balance/vestibular exercises, and giving clear return precautions—seek care for new neurologic deficits, continuous (non-positional) vertigo, hearing loss, or symptoms that do not fit BPPV, which could indicate a central process. Telling her it never recurs is inaccurate (recurrence is common). Lifelong benzodiazepines are inappropriate, sedating, and hinder central vestibular compensation. Permanently restricting head movement is unnecessary, deconditioning, and counterproductive; resuming normal activity supports recovery.

cases.diagnosis: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (posterior canal, right)
pearls: BPPV: brief (<1 min), position-triggered vertigo with a normal exam between spells and no hearing loss or central signs.
Dix-Hallpike is diagnostic — a positive test shows latent, transient, fatigable upbeating-torsional nystagmus for posterior-canal BPPV.
The Epley canalith-repositioning maneuver is first-line and curative; avoid chronic vestibular suppressants, which impair compensation.
Counsel on recurrence, teach home exercises, and give return precautions for any central features (continuous vertigo, focal deficits, hearing loss).

cases.title: Clumsy, Wide-Based Walking and Shaky Reaching After a Stroke
cases.chiefComplaint: Unsteady, wide-based walking and shaky, inaccurate hand movements since a recent stroke.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man presents 6 weeks after an acute cerebellar stroke for rehabilitation evaluation. He reports a staggering, wide-based gait, a tendency to veer and nearly fall, and shaky, overshooting hand movements that make pouring, writing, and using utensils difficult. He has had no significant weakness or sensory loss, but speech has become 'choppy.' MRI at the time confirmed a right cerebellar hemispheric infarct in the posterior inferior cerebellar artery territory; he was treated medically and the acute period was uncomplicated. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, 25-pack-year smoking history. Medications: aspirin 81 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, lisinopril 20 mg, metformin 1000 mg twice daily. Allergies: none. Social: accountant, lives in a single-story home with his wife, still smoking, social drinker. Family history: father with stroke. He is determined to return to work and driving but has fallen twice at home. He denies vertigo at rest, headache, or new focal weakness. His main functional complaints are imbalance and incoordination rather than paralysis.
cases.exam: Right-sided appendicular ataxia: dysmetria on finger-to-nose with intention tremor and overshoot, dysdiadochokinesia, and heel-to-shin ataxia on the right. Wide-based, unsteady gait; unable to tandem walk; Romberg negative but markedly unsteady with eyes open (truncal ataxia). Scanning, dysarthric speech. Gaze-evoked nystagmus on right lateral gaze; saccadic dysmetria. Strength 5/5, normal tone, reflexes symmetric, sensation (including proprioception) intact, plantars downgoing. No aphasia, no facial droop, no hemiparesis. Coordination deficits are out of proportion to any weakness or sensory loss, localizing to the cerebellum.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which examination finding localizes his deficit to the cerebellum rather than to a sensory (proprioceptive) ataxia?
options: Romberg sign strongly positive (much worse with eyes closed)
Dysmetria, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia, and gait ataxia with intact proprioception and a negative Romberg
Loss of vibration and position sense in the feet
Flaccid weakness with absent reflexes
stages.explanation: Cerebellar ataxia produces dysmetria, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia, scanning dysarthria, gaze-evoked nystagmus, and a wide-based gait, with normal strength and—critically—intact proprioception and a negative/only mildly affected Romberg, because the problem is coordination, not sensory input. A strongly positive Romberg (much worse with eyes closed) indicates a sensory/proprioceptive ataxia, where vision compensates for lost position sense. Loss of vibration/position sense in the feet likewise points to a sensory ataxia, not cerebellar. Flaccid weakness with absent reflexes indicates a lower-motor-neuron process, not cerebellar dysfunction.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of rehabilitation for his cerebellar ataxia?
options: Strict rest and avoidance of challenging balance tasks
High-dose benzodiazepines to suppress the tremor
Wheelchair prescription with no further gait training
Task-specific, progressive balance and coordination training (e.g., gait/balance retraining, repetitive coordination practice, core/trunk stabilization)
stages.explanation: Rehabilitation of cerebellar ataxia centers on intensive, task-specific, progressively challenging balance and coordination training—gait and balance retraining, repetitive coordination practice, trunk/core stabilization, and dynamic postural tasks—which drives motor learning and functional improvement. Strict rest and avoiding challenging tasks prevents the very practice that produces neuroplastic gains. Benzodiazepines do not effectively treat cerebellar intention tremor and cause sedation, falls, and impaired learning. Defaulting to a wheelchair without gait training abandons recovery potential; assistive devices are introduced to enable safe practice and mobility, not to replace rehabilitation.

stages.label: THERAPY EVALUATION
stages.content:
PT/OT evaluation:
Berg Balance Scale: 34/56 (elevated fall risk)
Gait: wide-based, decreased velocity, unable to tandem; veers right
Upper-limb: dysmetria limiting fine motor ADLs (writing, utensil use)
Falls: 2 in past month at home
Cognition/vision: adequate for device training; safety judgment intact

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given his falls and balance score, which assistive device is most appropriate to improve safe mobility in cerebellar ataxia?
options: A weighted/four-wheeled rollator (wheeled walker) to provide a stable, wide base of support
A single-point cane
No device—encourage unaided walking to build strength
Bilateral axillary crutches
stages.explanation: For cerebellar gait ataxia with a wide-based, veering pattern and recurrent falls, a wheeled walker/rollator (often weighted) provides a broad, stable base of support and reduces fall risk while allowing continued gait practice; some patients benefit from added limb weighting to dampen ataxic movements. A single-point cane offers too little stability for significant truncal ataxia. Walking unaided when he is already falling is unsafe. Axillary crutches demand precise coordination and upper-limb control that an ataxic patient lacks, making them unstable and inappropriate. The device should maximize stability while preserving the ability to train.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond therapy, which intervention is most important for his long-term outcome?
options: Continuing to smoke since the stroke already happened
Stopping his statin and antiplatelet to reduce pill burden
Aggressive secondary stroke prevention (smoking cessation, BP, lipid, and glucose control, antiplatelet therapy) alongside ongoing rehabilitation and fall-prevention
Avoiding all physical activity to prevent another stroke
stages.explanation: His long-term outcome depends on aggressive secondary stroke prevention—smoking cessation (he is still smoking), optimized blood pressure, lipid lowering with a statin, glycemic control, and antiplatelet therapy—combined with continued rehabilitation and home fall-prevention, because preventing recurrent stroke protects the gains made in therapy. Continuing to smoke markedly raises recurrence risk. Stopping his statin and antiplatelet removes proven protection and is dangerous. Avoiding all physical activity is counterproductive: regular, appropriately supervised exercise lowers vascular risk and is part of both rehabilitation and prevention, not a threat to be avoided.

cases.diagnosis: Cerebellar ataxia from a right cerebellar (PICA territory) ischemic stroke
pearls: Cerebellar ataxia: dysmetria, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia, scanning dysarthria, and wide-based gait with intact strength/proprioception and a negative Romberg.
A strongly positive Romberg points to sensory (proprioceptive) ataxia instead — vision compensates for lost position sense.
Rehabilitation is task-specific, progressive balance and coordination training; avoid sedatives and unnecessary rest.
Use a stable wheeled walker (rollator), often weighted, for ataxic gait; pair rehab with aggressive secondary stroke prevention and fall-prevention.

cases.title: Teen With a Catheter Routine Wants to Plan for College
cases.chiefComplaint: Established patient with a lifelong lower-limb and bladder condition presenting for routine surveillance and transition planning.
cases.history: A 15-year-old girl with a congenital open neural tube defect closed surgically on day 1 of life and a ventriculoperitoneal shunt placed in infancy presents for a scheduled multidisciplinary visit. She ambulates short household distances with bilateral ankle-foot orthoses and forearm crutches but uses a manual wheelchair for community distances. She performs clean intermittent catheterization every 4 hours and takes oxybutynin 5 mg twice daily for detrusor overactivity; she has a bowel program with polyethylene glycol and timed evacuation. Over the past 3 months she reports new low back pain, worsening difficulty with toe-walking on the right, and two episodes of urinary leakage between catheterizations. She denies fever, dysuria, or shunt-related headaches. PMH: recurrent UTIs, prior bladder augmentation discussion. Allergies: she developed lip swelling and hives after a dental procedure last year. Social: 10th grade, mainstreamed, wants to attend college; sexually inactive. Family history: no other neural tube defects; mother did not take periconceptional folate. She is due for scoliosis and renal surveillance.
cases.exam: Alert, well-appearing adolescent. Head: shunt tract non-tender, no bulging fontanelle (closed). Back: healed lumbosacral surgical scar; mild lumbar scoliosis. Motor: hip flexion and knee extension 5/5; right ankle dorsiflexion 4/5 (was 5/5 last year), plantarflexion 3/5; left ankle 4/5. New mild right hip flexor tightness. Sensation absent below L5 bilaterally; saddle sensation diminished. Reflexes: patellar 2+, ankle absent. Skin intact, no sacral pressure injury. Abdomen soft, palpable suprapubic fullness pre-catheterization. No nuchal rigidity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Before any procedure or examination supply is used in this clinic, which precaution is most important to confirm given her history?
options: Strict latex-free environment and equipment
Use of povidone-iodine rather than chlorhexidine skin prep
Penicillin avoidance and substitution of a macrolide
Pretreatment with diphenhydramine before catheterization
stages.explanation: Children with myelomeningocele have a high rate of latex sensitization from repeated catheterization and surgeries; her history of lip swelling and hives after a dental procedure (latex gloves/dam) signals IgE-mediated latex allergy, so a strict latex-free environment is the priority to prevent anaphylaxis. Iodine vs. chlorhexidine prep is unrelated to her allergy. Penicillin avoidance addresses a different, undocumented allergy. Routine antihistamine premedication does not substitute for avoiding the allergen and is not standard for catheterization. Recognizing and preventing latex exposure is a board-favorite safety point unique to this population.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her new right ankle weakness, worsening back pain, and urinary leakage between catheterizations after years of stability most strongly suggest which complication?
options: Shunt malfunction with hydrocephalus
Symptomatic tethered cord
Simple recurrent urinary tract infection
Progression of neuromuscular scoliosis alone
stages.explanation: New or progressive lower-extremity weakness, back/leg pain, changes in gait, and deterioration in bladder/bowel function in a previously stable child with repaired myelomeningocele are the classic signs of tethered cord syndrome, often unmasked during growth spurts. Shunt malfunction typically causes headache, vomiting, lethargy, or vision changes—absent here with no nuchal rigidity. A simple UTI would not explain new motor decline or back pain and she is afebrile without dysuria. Scoliosis progression alone does not produce focal ankle weakness or new incontinence. Tethered cord is the unifying diagnosis and warrants neurosurgical imaging.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
MRI lumbosacral spine: conus medullaris terminates at L4 with a thickened, fatty filum and dorsal tethering at the repair site; no syrinx
MRI brain: ventricles stable compared with prior, no shunt failure
Urinalysis: clear, no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites, <5 WBC/hpf
Renal/bladder ultrasound: no hydronephrosis; post-void residual elevated
Scoliosis radiograph: Cobb angle 22°
Latex-specific IgE: elevated

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the MRI and clinical picture, what is the most appropriate next step for her neurologic decline?
options: Increase oxybutynin and recheck in 6 months
Neurosurgical referral for surgical cord untethering
Spinal bracing for the 22° curve
Start prophylactic antibiotics for presumed occult UTI
stages.explanation: Symptomatic tethered cord with objective motor decline and bladder changes plus confirmatory MRI is an indication for neurosurgical evaluation and surgical untethering to halt or reverse progressive deficits; delay risks permanent loss. Increasing oxybutynin treats a symptom while ignoring the structural cause and progressive weakness. A 22° curve is below the operative or bracing threshold for neuromuscular scoliosis and does not address the cord. Her urinalysis is negative, so prophylactic antibiotics are inappropriate and contribute to resistance. The decline is structural and time-sensitive, making surgical referral the correct action.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Independent of the tethered cord, which feature of her examination best predicts her community ambulation potential?
options: The Cobb angle of her scoliosis
Her ventriculoperitoneal shunt status
Her neurologic (motor) lesion level
Her post-void residual volume
stages.explanation: Ambulation prognosis in myelomeningocele tracks most closely with the neurologic motor lesion level: sacral-level lesions often achieve community ambulation, mid-lumbar (L4 quadriceps preserved) achieve household or limited community ambulation with bracing, and thoracic/high-lumbar lesions are usually wheelchair users. Her preserved quadriceps with weak ankles fits a mid-lumbar functional level requiring AFOs and crutches. The Cobb angle, shunt status, and post-void residual affect overall health and bladder care but do not define ambulatory capacity. Matching lesion level to expected mobility and bracing needs is a core PM&R concept for these patients.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: As part of transition planning toward adult care and college, which long-term surveillance issue carries the greatest threat to her survival and must be prioritized?
options: Annual scoliosis radiographs
Preservation of renal function through bladder management
Periodic neuropsychological testing
Bone density screening for fracture risk
stages.explanation: Renal failure from a high-pressure neurogenic bladder is the leading cause of premature death in adults with spina bifida, so lifelong urologic surveillance—catheterization adherence, anticholinergics, periodic urodynamics, and upper-tract imaging to protect the kidneys—is the highest-priority transition issue. Scoliosis monitoring matters for function and pulmonary mechanics but is not the chief mortality driver. Neuropsychological testing supports education and independence but is not life-threatening. Bone density and fracture prevention are important for an insensate, non-weight-bearing patient yet rank below renal preservation. Protecting the kidneys is the central message of spina bifida transition care.

cases.diagnosis: Symptomatic tethered cord syndrome in a patient with repaired myelomeningocele (with latex allergy and neurogenic bladder)
pearls: New lower-extremity weakness, back pain, or bladder/bowel decline in a previously stable repaired myelomeningocele patient is tethered cord until proven otherwise.
Latex allergy is common in spina bifida from repeated catheterization and surgery—maintain a strict latex-free environment.
Neurologic motor lesion level is the best predictor of ambulation potential and bracing needs.
Renal failure from neurogenic bladder is the leading cause of death—lifelong upper-tract surveillance and bladder pressure management are paramount.

cases.title: Boy Who Climbs Up His Own Legs to Stand
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report their son is falling frequently, runs slower than peers, and struggles to climb stairs.
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy is referred for progressive difficulty with running, jumping, and climbing stairs over the past 18 months. His parents note frequent falls, a tendency to walk on his toes, and that he pushes on his thighs to rise from the floor. He was a late walker (18 months) and has never run as fast as classmates. He has mild difficulty keeping up academically and receives speech support. No bowel or bladder changes, no sensory complaints, no recent illness. PMH: unremarkable; immunizations current. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Birth history: term, uncomplicated. Social: lives with both parents and a younger sister; attends 2nd grade. Family history: a maternal uncle who used a wheelchair from his teens and died in his early twenties of a heart and breathing problem; the family was told it was a muscle disease. Review of systems notable for calf enlargement that parents attribute to him being muscular and for fatigue with walking long distances.
cases.exam: Alert, interactive boy. Gait: waddling with lumbar lordosis and bilateral toe-walking. Gowers sign present—he uses his hands to walk up his thighs when rising from the floor. Calves are firm and enlarged bilaterally (pseudohypertrophy). Strength: proximal greater than distal weakness, hip extensors and shoulder girdle 3+/5, distal 4+/5. Deep tendon reflexes diminished at knees, ankles preserved. Mild bilateral heel-cord tightness with limited ankle dorsiflexion. Sensation intact throughout. No fasciculations, no tongue involvement. Spine straight. Cardiac and pulmonary exams normal at rest.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the proximal weakness, Gowers sign, calf pseudohypertrophy, and X-linked family history, what is the single most useful initial laboratory test?
options: Serum aldolase
Serum creatine kinase
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate
Thyroid-stimulating hormone
stages.explanation: A markedly elevated serum creatine kinase (often 10–100× normal) is the best initial screen for a dystrophinopathy and is essentially always elevated in Duchenne muscular dystrophy before and at diagnosis. Aldolase can be elevated but is far less sensitive and specific and is not the first-line screen. ESR is a nonspecific inflammatory marker and would not be elevated by a dystrophinopathy. TSH screens for hypothyroid myopathy, which does not cause calf pseudohypertrophy, Gowers sign, or an X-linked lethal pattern. A strikingly high CK in a boy with this presentation rapidly narrows the diagnosis and triggers confirmatory genetic testing.

stages.label: LABS & GENETICS
stages.content:
Creatine kinase 14,800 U/L (normal <200)
AST 210 U/L, ALT 185 U/L (hepatic panel otherwise normal)
TSH 2.1 µIU/mL
DMD gene multiplex testing: deletion of exons 45–50 (out-of-frame)
Echocardiogram: normal LV ejection fraction 62%, no wall-motion abnormality
Pulmonary function: FVC normal for age

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The out-of-frame DMD deletion confirms the diagnosis. Which finding distinguishes Duchenne from the allelic Becker muscular dystrophy?
options: Presence of calf pseudohypertrophy
An out-of-frame (frameshift) mutation abolishing dystrophin
Elevation of serum creatine kinase
X-linked recessive inheritance
stages.explanation: Duchenne results from out-of-frame (frameshift) mutations that abolish functional dystrophin, producing earlier, more severe disease with loss of ambulation typically by the early teens; Becker arises from in-frame mutations yielding reduced or truncated but partially functional dystrophin and therefore a milder, later-onset course (the reading-frame rule). Calf pseudohypertrophy, caused by replacement of muscle with fat and fibrous tissue, occurs in both conditions and does not distinguish them. A markedly elevated CK is seen in both dystrophinopathies, so it cannot separate them either. Both are X-linked recessive and inherited identically. The reading-frame distinction is the molecular basis for the phenotypic difference between Duchenne and Becker and is a high-yield exam point.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Regarding glucocorticoid therapy, which statement best reflects current management of this 7-year-old whose motor function has plateaued and is beginning to decline?
options: Initiate glucocorticoids now to prolong ambulation and delay scoliosis and cardiorespiratory decline
Defer steroids until he loses ambulation to preserve growth
Steroids are contraindicated because of CK elevation
Use steroids only if the echocardiogram shows reduced ejection fraction
stages.explanation: Glucocorticoids (prednisone or deflazacort) are the cornerstone of DMD care and are started when motor skills plateau or begin to decline—typically around ages 4–8—because they prolong independent ambulation, slow progression of scoliosis, and delay cardiac and respiratory decline. Waiting until ambulation is lost forfeits these benefits. Elevated CK is expected and is not a contraindication. Steroids are begun for skeletal-muscle benefit and cardioprotection well before any drop in ejection fraction; they are not withheld pending cardiomyopathy. Early, appropriately timed steroid initiation is the key management decision tested here.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Once he loses independent ambulation, which surveillance program becomes most critical to monitor for during the non-ambulatory phase?
options: Annual hearing testing
Quarterly creatine kinase trending
Scoliosis monitoring with serial spine radiographs and respiratory function
Renal ultrasound surveillance
stages.explanation: Progressive scoliosis develops rapidly after loss of ambulation in DMD and worsens restrictive lung disease, so scoliosis surveillance with serial spine radiographs alongside pulmonary function monitoring is most critical during the non-ambulatory phase; bracing and timely posterior spinal fusion preserve sitting balance and pulmonary mechanics. Hearing is not affected. Trending CK adds nothing once the diagnosis is established and steroids are running. Renal disease is not a feature of DMD. Coordinated spine and respiratory monitoring—plus ongoing cardiac surveillance—drives quality and length of life in the wheelchair-dependent stage.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which organ system, when followed proactively, most affects long-term survival in this disease and warrants scheduled screening even when asymptomatic?
options: Gastrointestinal motility
Hepatic synthetic function
Endocrine pancreatic function
Cardiac function with periodic echocardiography or cardiac MRI
stages.explanation: Dilated cardiomyopathy and respiratory failure are the leading causes of death in DMD; cardiac involvement is often clinically silent until advanced, so scheduled echocardiography or cardiac MRI with early initiation of ACE inhibitors/beta-blockers is essential regardless of symptoms. GI motility can be affected late but is not the survival driver. The transaminase elevation seen here reflects muscle (not liver) origin, and hepatic synthetic function is preserved. Endocrine pancreatic disease is unrelated. Proactive cardiac and respiratory surveillance is what extends survival in modern DMD care.

cases.diagnosis: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (out-of-frame DMD deletion)
pearls: Gowers sign, calf pseudohypertrophy, waddling gait, and markedly elevated CK in a young boy point to a dystrophinopathy—confirm with DMD genetic testing.
The reading-frame rule distinguishes Duchenne (out-of-frame, severe) from Becker (in-frame, milder).
Start glucocorticoids when motor function plateaus or begins to decline to prolong ambulation and delay scoliosis and cardiorespiratory decline.
Cardiomyopathy and respiratory failure cause death—screen the heart and lungs proactively even when asymptomatic.

cases.title: Infant Who Keeps One Arm Limp at His Side
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report their baby does not move his right arm as much as the left.
cases.history: A 4-month-old boy is referred because of persistently reduced movement of the right arm noted since birth. He was born at 40 weeks via vaginal delivery complicated by shoulder dystocia and a birth weight of 4.4 kg to a mother with gestational diabetes. Apgars were 7 and 9. The pediatrician initially advised observation. Now at 4 months, the parents report he reaches for toys and brings the left hand to midline but rarely uses the right arm, which tends to rest at his side. He grasps objects placed in the right hand and moves the wrist and fingers. Feeding and growth are normal; he smiles, coos, and rolls. No respiratory distress, no seizures. PMH: term birth, otherwise healthy. Medications: vitamin D drops. Allergies: none. Social: lives with both parents; first child. Family history: noncontributory. No prior fractures identified on newborn exam, and a clavicle film at birth was reported normal.
cases.exam: Alert, smiling infant. Right upper limb held in adduction and internal rotation at the shoulder, extension at the elbow, and pronation of the forearm—the 'waiter's tip' posture. Spontaneous antigravity movement at the right shoulder and elbow is reduced; active wrist and finger flexion and grasp are intact. Asymmetric Moro with diminished abduction on the right. Right hand grip strong; no Horner syndrome (pupils equal, no ptosis). Biceps reflex absent on the right, present on the left. Sensory testing grossly intact. Clavicles non-tender, no crepitus. Left upper limb and both lower limbs move normally. No respiratory distress.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The 'waiter's tip' posture with preserved hand grip following a difficult delivery localizes the injury to which nerve roots?
options: Lower trunk (C8–T1)
Upper trunk (C5–C6)
Entire brachial plexus (C5–T1)
Posterior cord only
stages.explanation: Erb palsy from a stretch injury to the upper trunk (C5–C6, sometimes C7) produces weak shoulder abductors/external rotators, elbow flexors, and forearm supinators, yielding the classic waiter's-tip posture with preserved hand and wrist function. Lower trunk (C8–T1) injury (Klumpke) causes a clawed hand with intrinsic weakness and often Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis)—the opposite of this preserved-grip picture; the absence of Horner syndrome here is reassuring because T1 involvement carries a worse prognosis. A global C5–T1 (pan-plexus) injury would render the entire limb flaccid, including the hand. Isolated posterior cord injury does not match the segmental motor pattern. The intact grip with proximal weakness and no Horner syndrome pinpoints the upper trunk.

stages.label: EXAM & STUDIES
stages.content:
Chest/clavicle radiograph: no clavicular or humeral fracture; both hemidiaphragms at normal level (no phrenic nerve palsy)
Shoulder ultrasound: glenohumeral joint located, no posterior subluxation
Documented active movement: antigravity shoulder abduction and elbow flexion absent on the right
Hand grip and wrist/finger flexion: intact bilaterally
Horner syndrome: absent
Medical Research Council grading scheduled at monthly follow-up to track biceps recovery

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate mainstay of management for this 4-month-old at the current stage?
options: Immediate surgical exploration and nerve grafting
Immobilize the arm in a sling against the chest
Gentle daily passive range-of-motion and a therapy program to prevent contractures while awaiting recovery
Botulinum toxin injection to the deltoid
stages.explanation: Most upper-trunk neonatal brachial plexus injuries recover spontaneously, so the mainstay is a structured occupational/physical therapy program centered on gentle passive range of motion to prevent shoulder internal-rotation and elbow-flexion contractures while neural recovery is monitored. Early surgical exploration is reserved for those who fail to show meaningful recovery (see next stage), not for routine first-line care. Prolonged immobilization in a sling promotes contractures and is harmful. Botulinum toxin may have a later adjunctive role for co-contraction/contracture but is not the initial mainstay. ROM and developmental therapy protect the joint while spontaneous reinnervation proceeds.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which clinical milestone, if NOT achieved, is the standard trigger to refer for microsurgical (nerve grafting) evaluation?
options: Lack of finger grasp by 2 months
Absence of antigravity elbow flexion (biceps recovery) by around 3–6 months
Failure to reach shoulder abduction past 90° by 12 months
Persistent absence of the Moro reflex at 2 weeks
stages.explanation: Failure to recover antigravity elbow flexion (biceps function) by approximately 3–6 months is the widely used threshold to refer for microsurgical evaluation, because absence of biceps recovery in this window predicts incomplete spontaneous recovery and better outcomes with timely nerve reconstruction. Finger grasp is typically preserved in Erb palsy and is not the trigger. Limited shoulder abduction at 12 months may prompt secondary tendon/orthopedic procedures but is not the primary microsurgical trigger. An absent Moro at 2 weeks is expected early and is not a surgical indicator. Tracking biceps recovery timing guides the referral decision.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Without adequate therapy, which musculoskeletal complication is this infant most at risk of developing over the next year?
options: Shoulder internal-rotation contracture with posterior glenohumeral subluxation
Genu recurvatum
Scoliosis
Hip dysplasia
stages.explanation: In upper-trunk injury the external rotators and abductors are weak while the internal rotators and adductors retain power, and this muscle imbalance commonly produces a shoulder internal-rotation/adduction contracture; if range of motion is not maintained it can progress to posterior glenohumeral subluxation and secondary glenoid dysplasia—hence the emphasis on external-rotation and abduction stretching. Genu recurvatum and hip dysplasia involve the lower limbs, which are entirely uninvolved in an isolated brachial plexus injury. Scoliosis is not a direct consequence of a unilateral plexus lesion. Preventing the shoulder contracture and the resulting bony dysplasia is the central rehabilitation goal that justifies the daily passive range-of-motion program in these infants.

cases.diagnosis: Erb palsy (C5–C6 upper trunk neonatal brachial plexus injury)
pearls: Waiter's-tip posture with preserved hand grip after shoulder dystocia is classic Erb (upper trunk, C5–C6) palsy.
Horner syndrome and a clawed hand suggest lower-trunk/T1 (Klumpke) involvement and a worse prognosis.
Most cases recover spontaneously—mainstay is passive ROM/therapy to prevent contractures.
Failure to recover antigravity elbow flexion (biceps) by about 3–6 months triggers microsurgical referral.

cases.title: Baby Who Always Looks Over One Shoulder
