cases.chiefComplaint: Parents notice their baby's head is always tilted to one side and she prefers to look in one direction.
cases.history: A 3-month-old girl is brought in because her head consistently tilts toward the right and she prefers to turn her face to the left. The tilt was first noticed around 3 weeks of age and has become more apparent. She was born at term via vaginal delivery; labor was prolonged and she was in a frank breech position before an external version. Birth weight 3.6 kg. She feeds well and is gaining weight; she smiles, coos, and tracks faces. Parents note she has more difficulty turning to look toward her right side and seems to favor sleeping with her head turned left. No reported pain with movement, no fevers, no eye-crossing. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: vitamin D drops. Allergies: none. Social: first child; parents are attentive. Family history: noncontributory. Developmental milestones otherwise on track. Parents are worried about a flat spot developing on the back of the head.
cases.exam: Alert, smiling infant. Head tilted toward the right with the chin rotated toward the left. A firm, non-tender, mobile fusiform thickening is palpable within the right sternocleidomastoid muscle. Passive left lateral neck flexion and rightward rotation are limited and meet resistance; the infant tolerates gentle stretch without distress. Mild right-sided occipital flattening (positional plagiocephaly) with slight anterior displacement of the right ear. Red reflex normal bilaterally; extraocular movements full, no nystagmus. Clavicles intact, no crepitus. Hips stable with negative Barlow/Ortolani. Neurologic exam symmetric; tone normal; spontaneous movement of all four limbs.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which statement best explains the direction of the head posture in this infant with a tight right sternocleidomastoid?
options: The head tilts away from and the chin turns toward the tight muscle
The head tilts toward the tight muscle and the chin turns to the opposite side
Both the tilt and chin rotation are toward the tight muscle
Both the tilt and chin rotation are away from the tight muscle
stages.explanation: The sternocleidomastoid flexes the neck to its own side and rotates the face to the opposite side; therefore a tight right SCM causes the head to tilt toward the right (same side) and the chin to rotate toward the left (opposite side)—exactly this infant's posture. Saying the head tilts away from the tight muscle is incorrect. Both tilt and rotation toward the same side, or both away, misstate the muscle's action. Understanding the SCM's biomechanics lets you predict the deformity and design the correct stretching plan.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the classic palpable SCM mass and characteristic posture, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Order cervical spine MRI before any treatment
Begin a conservative program of stretching and repositioning with caregiver education
Refer immediately for surgical SCM release
Obtain ophthalmology referral for the head tilt
stages.explanation: Congenital muscular torticollis is a clinical diagnosis; with the typical SCM fibrosis and posture and a normal neuro exam, first-line management is a conservative physical therapy program of passive SCM stretching, active positioning to the non-preferred side, tummy time, and caregiver education—highly effective when started early. Cervical MRI is reserved for atypical features (e.g., abnormal neuro exam, no SCM mass, or failure to improve). Surgical release is considered only after prolonged failed conservative care, usually after about 12–18 months. Ocular torticollis is a consideration when there is no SCM tightness and the exam suggests strabismus, which is not the case here.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The parents ask about the flat spot on the back of her head. What is the most appropriate guidance regarding the positional plagiocephaly?
options: It requires a cranial molding helmet immediately regardless of severity
It indicates premature suture fusion and needs urgent neurosurgery
It usually improves with repositioning and treatment of the torticollis; monitor and reassess
It is unrelated to the torticollis and can be ignored entirely
stages.explanation: Positional (deformational) plagiocephaly commonly accompanies congenital muscular torticollis because the infant rests on a preferred side; it typically improves with repositioning, tummy time, and correcting the underlying torticollis, so monitoring with reassessment is appropriate first-line care. Helmet therapy is considered only for moderate-to-severe persistent deformity that fails repositioning, not immediately or universally. Craniosynostosis (premature suture fusion) produces a ridged, fixed deformity and is a different diagnosis—this infant's plagiocephaly is positional. It is causally linked to the torticollis, so it should not be ignored. Treating the neck and repositioning usually resolves the head shape.

stages.label: EXAM & IMAGING
stages.content:
Neck ultrasound: focal fusiform thickening of the right sternocleidomastoid with normal echotexture elsewhere; no mass or lymphadenopathy
Neurologic exam: normal tone and symmetric spontaneous movement of all limbs
Red reflex and extraocular movements: normal bilaterally
Hip exam: Barlow and Ortolani maneuvers performed
Hip ultrasound (ordered for breech history): normal acetabular alignment, no dysplasia
Cervical spine: no bony anomaly

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which associated condition should be screened for in any infant diagnosed with congenital muscular torticollis?
options: Developmental dysplasia of the hip
Congenital heart disease
Pyloric stenosis
Sensorineural hearing loss
stages.explanation: Congenital muscular torticollis is associated with other intrauterine 'packaging' or positioning deformities, most notably developmental dysplasia of the hip, so a careful hip exam (Barlow and Ortolani maneuvers) and consideration of hip ultrasound are warranted—particularly in this infant with a documented breech position, which independently raises DDH risk. Metatarsus adductus is another commonly linked positional deformity worth examining for. Congenital heart disease, pyloric stenosis, and sensorineural hearing loss are not part of the recognized association with CMT and would not be screened on the basis of the torticollis. Linking congenital muscular torticollis to developmental dysplasia of the hip is the high-yield co-screening point for these infants and should not be overlooked.

cases.diagnosis: Congenital muscular torticollis (right sternocleidomastoid)
pearls: A tight SCM tilts the head toward the same side and rotates the chin to the opposite side.
CMT is a clinical diagnosis; first-line treatment is early stretching, repositioning, and caregiver education.
Positional plagiocephaly usually resolves with repositioning and torticollis treatment—helmets are reserved for persistent moderate-severe cases.
Screen for developmental dysplasia of the hip, especially with a breech history.

cases.title: Teen Whose Shirt Hem Hangs Crooked
cases.chiefComplaint: A school screening noted uneven shoulders and the parents see a curve in her back.
cases.history: A 13-year-old girl is referred after a school scoliosis screening flagged asymmetry. Her mother noticed one shoulder blade sticks out more and that clothing hangs unevenly. There is no back pain, no leg weakness, numbness, or bowel/bladder changes, and no night pain. She is otherwise healthy and active in volleyball. Menarche occurred 6 months ago; she has had a recent growth spurt and her shoe size keeps changing. PMH: unremarkable, no prior spine problems. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: 8th grade, good student, lives with parents. Family history: a maternal aunt wore a back brace as a teenager. Review of systems negative for café-au-lait spots, foot deformity, or developmental delay. The parents are anxious about whether she will need surgery and whether sports must stop.
cases.exam: Well-appearing adolescent. Standing: right shoulder slightly elevated, right scapular prominence, and asymmetric waist creases. Adams forward-bend test reveals a right thoracic rib hump. Leg lengths equal; pelvis level. Skin: no café-au-lait macules, no midline hair tuft, sacral dimple, or lipoma. Neurologic exam normal—strength 5/5 throughout, sensation intact, deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric including abdominal reflexes, normal gait and tandem gait, no clonus, plantar responses flexor. No focal spinal tenderness. Tanner stage 3.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After a positive Adams forward-bend test, what is the most appropriate next step to characterize the curve?
options: MRI of the entire spine
Standing posteroanterior and lateral full-spine radiographs to measure the Cobb angle
Bone scan
Immediate orthopedic surgical consultation
stages.explanation: A positive forward-bend test (or elevated scoliometer reading) should be followed by standing full-length PA and lateral spine radiographs to measure the Cobb angle, which defines curve magnitude and guides management. MRI is reserved for atypical or 'red-flag' presentations—left thoracic curves, abnormal neuro exam, early-onset or rapidly progressive curves, pain, or neurocutaneous findings—none present here. A bone scan evaluates for tumor/infection, not indicated in a painless, neurologically normal curve. Surgical consultation is premature before the Cobb angle and skeletal maturity are known. Plain radiographs with Cobb measurement are the standard initial imaging.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Standing PA spine radiograph: right thoracic curve, apex T8, Cobb angle 28°
Lateral radiograph: normal sagittal alignment, no spondylolisthesis
Risser sign: grade 1 (early iliac apophysis ossification)
No congenital vertebral anomalies, no widened pedicles
Hand/wrist not obtained; skeletal maturity estimated from Risser plus recent menarche

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What does the combination of a low Risser grade and recent menarche tell you about this patient's risk?
options: She is skeletally mature with negligible progression risk
She still has substantial growth remaining, so the curve has meaningful progression risk
Risser and menarche are unrelated to scoliosis prognosis
The curve will certainly require surgery
stages.explanation: Curve progression risk in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis depends on curve magnitude and remaining skeletal growth. A low Risser grade (1) indicates significant residual growth, and although menarche has occurred, she remains in a period of growth, so a 28° curve carries meaningful progression risk and needs treatment now. A low Risser grade is the opposite of skeletal maturity. Risser sign and growth status are central—not irrelevant—to prognosis. Progression risk does not equal certainty of surgery; bracing is the appropriate next intervention. Matching skeletal maturity to progression risk drives the bracing-versus-observation decision.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a 28° curve with substantial growth remaining (Risser 1), which management is most appropriate?
options: Thoracolumbosacral orthosis (bracing) to prevent progression
Observation with repeat radiographs in 4–6 months only
Posterior spinal fusion
Restriction from all physical activity
stages.explanation: For skeletally immature patients (Risser 0–2) with curves of roughly 25–40°, bracing is indicated to halt progression and reduce the likelihood of reaching the surgical threshold; brace efficacy is dose-dependent on hours of wear. Observation alone is appropriate for smaller curves (under ~20–25°) or skeletally mature patients—her growth potential and curve size warrant active treatment. Surgery (fusion) is generally reserved for curves above ~45–50° or progressive curves despite bracing. Activity restriction is unnecessary and counterproductive; she can continue sports, including volleyball, around brace wear. Bracing is the guideline-based intervention for this curve and maturity.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: At what approximate Cobb angle does surgical correction (e.g., posterior spinal fusion) generally become the recommended treatment in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis?
options: Greater than 10°
Greater than 20°
Greater than 45–50°
Only after the curve exceeds 90°
stages.explanation: Surgical correction is generally recommended for curves greater than about 45–50° in skeletally immature patients or progressive curves beyond ~50° in mature patients, because large curves continue to progress into adulthood and can impair pulmonary function and cause pain/cosmetic deformity. Curves over 10° simply define scoliosis radiographically and need no surgery. Curves over 20° are observed or, with growth remaining, braced—not operated. Waiting until 90° risks severe deformity and cardiopulmonary compromise. Knowing the ~10°/25°/45° thresholds (diagnosis, brace, surgery) is the high-yield framework for AIS decision-making.

cases.diagnosis: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (right thoracic curve, Cobb 28°, Risser 1)
pearls: A positive Adams forward-bend test is followed by standing full-spine radiographs to measure the Cobb angle.
Progression risk depends on curve magnitude and remaining growth (Risser sign, menarcheal status).
Rough thresholds: ~10° defines scoliosis, ~25–40° with growth remaining = brace, >~45–50° = surgery.
Red flags (left thoracic curve, pain, abnormal neuro exam, neurocutaneous signs) warrant MRI to exclude a secondary cause.

cases.title: Young Athlete With a Painful Bump Below the Knee
cases.chiefComplaint: Pain and a tender bump just below the kneecap that worsens with running and jumping.
cases.history: A 12-year-old boy presents with several weeks of pain over the front of both knees, worse on the right, localized to a bony bump just below the kneecap. The pain flares during and after basketball practice—especially with running, jumping, and going up stairs—and eases with rest. He denies any acute injury, locking, giving way, or swelling of the joint itself. No night pain, no fever, no morning stiffness. He recently joined two basketball teams and grew about 7 cm in the past year. PMH: healthy, no prior fractures. Medications: occasional ibuprofen with partial relief. Allergies: none. Social: 7th grade, very active in sports. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems otherwise negative; he is otherwise well, eats and sleeps normally, and has no other joint complaints.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, active boy. Knees: prominent, tender bony enlargement over the right tibial tubercle with mild local swelling; left tubercle mildly tender. Pain reproduced with resisted knee extension and with single-leg squatting. No effusion, no warmth or erythema of the joint, no joint-line tenderness. Full active and passive knee range of motion. Ligamentous exam stable (negative Lachman, anterior/posterior drawer, varus/valgus). Patellar tracking normal, negative apprehension. Tight hamstrings and quadriceps bilaterally. Neurovascular exam intact distally. Hip exam normal with full painless rotation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a growing athlete with activity-related pain and tenderness localized to the tibial tubercle, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Patellar tendon rupture
Osgood-Schlatter disease (traction apophysitis of the tibial tubercle)
Septic arthritis of the knee
Osteosarcoma of the proximal tibia
stages.explanation: Osgood-Schlatter disease is a traction apophysitis at the insertion of the patellar tendon on the tibial tubercle, classic in adolescents during growth spurts who do jumping/running sports; the hallmark is activity-related anterior knee pain and tenderness localized to the tubercle, reproduced by resisted extension. A patellar tendon rupture causes acute inability to extend the knee and a high-riding patella—absent here. Septic arthritis produces fever, effusion, warmth, and pain with any motion, none present. Osteosarcoma causes deep, persistent, often night pain with possible mass or systemic signs; this benign, activity-linked tubercle pain in a growing athlete fits Osgood-Schlatter.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step in this typical presentation?
options: MRI of both knees
Make the diagnosis clinically; imaging is not required in a typical case
Joint aspiration for cell count and culture
Bone biopsy of the tibial tubercle
stages.explanation: Osgood-Schlatter is a clinical diagnosis; in a classic presentation with localized tubercle tenderness, no effusion, normal joint exam, and no red flags, no imaging is required. If obtained, plain radiographs may show fragmentation/irregularity of the tubercle but are used mainly to exclude other pathology when the picture is atypical. MRI is unnecessary for a typical case. Joint aspiration is for suspected septic arthritis or effusion, which is absent. Bone biopsy is reserved for suspected neoplasm—there are no concerning features. Recognizing this as a clinical diagnosis avoids overtesting in a benign, self-limited condition.

stages.label: IMAGING (obtained for reassurance)
stages.content:
Right knee radiograph (lateral): soft-tissue swelling anterior to the tibial tubercle with mild fragmentation/irregularity of the apophysis
No lytic or sclerotic bone lesion, no periosteal reaction, no Codman triangle
No joint effusion, normal joint space
Growth plates open, appropriate for skeletal age
No fracture
Left knee: similar but milder tubercle changes

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of management you should counsel this athlete and his parents on?
options: Strict knee immobilization in a cast for 6 weeks
Activity modification, relative rest, quadriceps/hamstring stretching, ice, and analgesics as needed
Surgical excision of the tibial tubercle now
Complete and permanent cessation of all sports
stages.explanation: Management is conservative and symptom-guided: relative rest and activity modification to tolerable levels, ice after activity, NSAIDs/acetaminophen for pain, and a stretching/strengthening program for the quadriceps and hamstrings; a patellar strap can help. Cast immobilization causes deconditioning and is unnecessary. Surgery is rarely needed and only considered in skeletally mature patients with persistent symptomatic ossicles after conservative care fails—not at presentation. He does not need to quit sports entirely; load is reduced, not eliminated. Reassurance plus a graded activity and flexibility plan addresses the traction overload driving symptoms.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which statement about the natural history of this condition is most accurate to share with the family?
options: It typically progresses to permanent disability and arthritis
Symptoms usually resolve once the tibial growth plate (apophysis) closes, though a painless bony prominence may persist
It requires lifelong NSAID use to prevent recurrence
It commonly converts into a stress fracture of the femur
stages.explanation: Osgood-Schlatter is self-limited: symptoms generally resolve as the tibial tubercle apophysis ossifies and fuses with skeletal maturity, though a residual painless bony prominence at the tubercle may remain into adulthood. It does not progress to arthritis or permanent disability. Lifelong NSAIDs are not needed; analgesics are used only for symptom control during flares. It does not transform into a femoral stress fracture. A clear, reassuring explanation of the benign, self-limited course improves adherence to activity modification and reduces unnecessary anxiety and testing.

cases.diagnosis: Osgood-Schlatter disease (tibial tubercle traction apophysitis)
pearls: Activity-related anterior knee pain localized to the tibial tubercle in a growing athlete is Osgood-Schlatter—a clinical diagnosis.
No imaging is needed in typical cases; radiographs are used only to exclude other pathology when atypical.
Treat conservatively: relative rest, ice, NSAIDs, and quadriceps/hamstring stretching—not casting or surgery.
It is self-limited and resolves with apophyseal closure, though a painless bony bump may persist.

cases.title: Runner With a Sharp Spot of Shin Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressively worsening focal pain along the front of the shin that now hurts even at rest.
cases.history: A 20-year-old collegiate distance runner presents with 4 weeks of progressively worsening right shin pain. It began as a dull ache late in long runs and now produces sharp, pinpoint pain over the mid-tibia that occurs earlier in each run and lingers afterward; over the last few days it aches at rest. She recently increased her weekly mileage by about 40% preparing for a marathon and switched to a harder track surface. She denies acute trauma, ankle swelling, or calf swelling/redness. Menstrual history: menarche at 13, but she has had only 2 periods in the past year. She restricts her diet to 'stay light' and has lost 5 kg over 6 months. PMH: a metatarsal stress fracture 2 years ago. Medications: none; calcium/vitamin D not taken. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, rare alcohol, full-time student-athlete. Family history: maternal osteoporosis.
cases.exam: Lean, well-appearing athlete. Right leg: focal point tenderness over the mid-anterior tibial shaft with localized swelling; pain reproduced by hopping on the right leg and by the single-leg hop test. No tenderness diffusely along the medial border (which would suggest shin splints), no calf tenderness, negative Homans, no ankle effusion. Distal pulses 2+, sensation intact, full ankle and knee range of motion. No erythema or warmth. BMI low. Tuning-fork applied over the focal point increases pain. Remainder of musculoskeletal exam unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A focal, pinpoint area of tibial tenderness with pain on single-leg hop in a runner who rapidly increased mileage most likely represents which diagnosis?
options: Medial tibial stress syndrome (shin splints)
Tibial stress fracture
Deep vein thrombosis
Chronic exertional compartment syndrome
stages.explanation: A discrete, point-tender area of bone pain that worsens with impact, fails to fully resolve with rest, and follows a rapid training-load increase is characteristic of a stress fracture; a positive single-leg hop test supports it. Medial tibial stress syndrome causes diffuse pain along the medial tibial border, not focal point tenderness, and typically eases with warm-up. DVT produces calf swelling/tenderness without an impact-pain pattern and is unlikely in a healthy young runner with a clear overuse history. Chronic exertional compartment syndrome causes cramping/tightness that resolves with rest, not focal bone point tenderness. Focality plus the load history points to stress fracture.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Plain radiographs of the tibia are normal. What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Reassure and return to full running immediately
Obtain MRI of the tibia, the most sensitive test for early stress fracture
Order venous duplex ultrasound
Measure compartment pressures with exertion
stages.explanation: Radiographs are frequently normal in the first 2–3 weeks of a stress fracture, so a negative film does not exclude it; MRI is the most sensitive and specific test, detecting marrow edema and the fracture line early, and helps grade severity. Returning to full running risks progression to a complete fracture. Venous duplex evaluates for DVT, which the focal impact-pain pattern argues against. Exertional compartment pressure testing is for suspected compartment syndrome, not focal bone tenderness. When clinical suspicion for stress fracture is high but X-rays are normal, advanced imaging with MRI is the next step.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI right tibia: periosteal and marrow edema with a discrete cortical stress fracture line, mid-diaphysis anterior cortex
Vitamin D 18 ng/mL (deficient)
Ferritin 14 ng/mL
TSH normal
beta-hCG negative
DXA scan: lumbar spine Z-score -2.1 (low bone mass for age)
Menstrual history corroborates oligomenorrhea

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond load management, her oligomenorrhea, restrictive eating with weight loss, and low bone density point to a syndrome that requires which key intervention?
options: Immediate bisphosphonate therapy as first-line treatment
Start combined oral contraceptives as the primary bone-protective treatment
Address relative energy deficiency—restore energy availability with increased caloric intake and reduced training, plus correct vitamin D, involving a multidisciplinary team
Recommend higher-impact training to strengthen bone
stages.explanation: She meets the female athlete triad/relative energy deficiency in sport (RED-S): low energy availability, menstrual dysfunction, and low bone mineral density. The cornerstone is restoring energy availability—increasing caloric intake to match expenditure and moderating training—usually with a multidisciplinary team (sports medicine, nutrition, sometimes mental health), plus correcting vitamin D deficiency. Bisphosphonates are not first-line in young, potentially fertile women and do not address the root cause. Oral contraceptives may mask the problem and are not proven to restore bone in this setting; nutrition/energy restoration is primary. Higher-impact training worsens injury risk. Energy restoration is the central fix.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding return to running, what is the most appropriate plan for this anterior mid-tibial stress fracture?
options: Continue current training with NSAIDs to mask pain
Strict non-weight-bearing in a long-leg cast for 3 months
Return to full marathon training in 1 week if pain improves
Relative rest with protected weight-bearing, then a gradual, pain-free, graded return-to-run progression once symptoms resolve
stages.explanation: Tibial stress fracture management is activity modification with relative rest and protected weight-bearing as needed for pain, followed by a gradual, symptom-guided graded return-to-run program once she is pain-free, while addressing the underlying energy deficiency and vitamin D. Masking pain with NSAIDs to keep training risks progression to a complete fracture—particularly concerning for the anterior tibial cortex, a recognized high-risk 'dreaded black line' site. Prolonged casting causes deconditioning and is usually unnecessary. Rapid return to high-volume training invites re-injury. A staged, pain-free progression with correction of risk factors is the safe path.

cases.diagnosis: Tibial stress fracture in the setting of the female athlete triad / relative energy deficiency in sport (RED-S)
pearls: Focal point bone tenderness and a positive single-leg hop after a training-load spike suggest a stress fracture; radiographs are often normal early—MRI is most sensitive.
Screen every female athlete with a stress fracture for the triad: low energy availability, menstrual dysfunction, and low bone density.
Restoring energy availability (nutrition, reduced load) and correcting vitamin D is the cornerstone, not bisphosphonates or oral contraceptives.
The anterior tibial cortex is a high-risk stress fracture site at risk of progression—manage cautiously with graded return.

cases.title: Gym-Goer With Dark Urine After a Brutal Workout
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe muscle pain, swelling, and cola-colored urine two days after an extreme workout.
cases.history: A 23-year-old previously healthy man is admitted after developing severe pain, stiffness, and swelling in his thighs and shoulders following his first high-intensity boot-camp class two days ago, where he did hundreds of squats and push-ups. He noticed tea/cola-colored urine yesterday. He felt weak and nauseated but has no chest pain or palpitations. He took no supplements other than a pre-workout containing caffeine, and he had been somewhat dehydrated in a hot gym. He denies recent viral illness, recreational drugs, statins, or known muscle disease. He has had no prior similar episodes. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Family history: no muscle disorders, no anesthesia complications. Social: occasional alcohol, no smoking, gym newcomer. On the ward he is receiving intravenous fluids; he is making good urine output now and the urine is clearing.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable but alert young man. Marked tenderness and firmness of the quadriceps and deltoids bilaterally with painful, limited active range of motion from soreness; strength is limited by pain but grossly preserved. No compartment tense to the point of pulselessness; distal pulses 2+, sensation intact, capillary refill brisk. No rash, no joint effusions, no facial or eyelid heliotrope discoloration. Cardiopulmonary exam normal. Abdomen soft. No flank tenderness. Urine in the bag is amber, lightening with hydration. No edema. Neurologic exam otherwise nonfocal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Cola-colored urine, severe muscle pain after extreme exertion, and a urine dipstick positive for blood but few red cells on microscopy indicate what?
options: Glomerulonephritis with hematuria
Myoglobinuria from rhabdomyolysis
Hemoglobinuria from intravascular hemolysis
Urinary tract infection
stages.explanation: A dipstick positive for blood with few or no red cells on microscopy reflects a heme pigment other than intact red cells—here myoglobin released from injured muscle, i.e., rhabdomyolysis, fitting the exertional history, muscle pain/swelling, and pigmented urine. Glomerulonephritis shows red cells and casts on microscopy and often hypertension/edema. Hemoglobinuria from hemolysis would show signs of anemia, low haptoglobin, and a hemolytic smear without the prominent muscle injury picture. A UTI causes dysuria/pyuria with bacteria, not pigmenturia after exertion. The 'dipstick-positive, RBC-negative' clue is the classic tip-off to myoglobinuria.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Creatine kinase 38,000 U/L
Potassium 5.6 mEq/L
Phosphate 5.4 mg/dL
Calcium 7.8 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL (baseline ~0.9)
Urinalysis: large blood on dipstick, 2 RBC/hpf, pigmented casts
Uric acid elevated
ECG: normal sinus rhythm, no peaked T waves

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important treatment to prevent the major renal complication of his condition?
options: Aggressive intravenous isotonic fluid resuscitation
Early hemodialysis
Oral protein restriction
High-dose loop diuretics as first-line therapy
stages.explanation: Aggressive early intravenous isotonic fluid resuscitation is the cornerstone of rhabdomyolysis management; it restores intravascular volume and promotes urine flow to limit myoglobin-induced acute kidney injury and helps correct electrolyte disturbances. Hemodialysis is reserved for refractory hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, or volume overload with established renal failure, not as the primary preventive measure. Protein restriction has no role in the acute setting. Loop diuretics are not first-line; volume repletion comes first, and diuretics are used selectively only after adequate hydration if needed. The priority is high-volume fluids to protect the kidneys—his improving urine output and lightening urine reflect appropriate therapy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which electrolyte abnormality is the most immediately life-threatening complication to monitor for in rhabdomyolysis?
options: Hypocalcemia causing tetany
Hyperphosphatemia
Hyperkalemia causing cardiac arrhythmia
Hyperuricemia causing gout
stages.explanation: Damaged muscle releases potassium, and combined with renal impairment this can produce hyperkalemia, which is the most immediately life-threatening complication because it can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias—so continuous cardiac monitoring and serial potassium checks are essential (his K is already 5.6). Hypocalcemia occurs as calcium deposits in injured muscle but is usually asymptomatic and not aggressively repleted unless symptomatic. Hyperphosphatemia and hyperuricemia contribute to metabolic derangement and kidney injury but are not the acute lethal threat. Prioritizing potassium and the ECG is the key safety action in rhabdomyolysis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He recovers fully. What is the most appropriate counseling on return to exercise and recurrence risk?
options: He should never exercise again
Gradual, progressive reconditioning with adequate hydration and heat precautions, and evaluation for an underlying metabolic myopathy if rhabdomyolysis recurs with modest exertion
Resume maximal-intensity workouts immediately once CK is falling
Begin daily creatine supplements to prevent recurrence
stages.explanation: After exertional rhabdomyolysis, safe return involves a gradual, progressive reconditioning program once symptoms and CK normalize, with attention to hydration, avoiding extreme heat, and pacing of training volume—most first episodes from unaccustomed extreme exertion do not recur with sensible training. Recurrent episodes, especially with relatively modest exertion or a family history, warrant evaluation for an underlying metabolic myopathy (e.g., a glycogen-storage or fatty-acid oxidation disorder). Never exercising again is unwarranted. Resuming maximal intensity while CK is still falling risks recurrence. Creatine supplements do not prevent recurrence and are not indicated. Sensible graded return plus a low threshold to investigate recurrences is the message.

cases.diagnosis: Exertional rhabdomyolysis with myoglobinuria (recovering)
pearls: Dipstick blood-positive urine with few RBCs on microscopy after intense exertion signals myoglobinuria from rhabdomyolysis.
Aggressive IV isotonic fluids are the cornerstone to prevent pigment-induced acute kidney injury.
Hyperkalemia is the most immediately life-threatening complication—monitor potassium and the ECG.
Most single episodes after extreme unaccustomed exertion don't recur; recurrent or low-threshold episodes warrant a metabolic myopathy workup.

cases.title: Woman Who Can't Lift Her Arms or Rise From a Chair
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive difficulty climbing stairs, rising from chairs, and lifting objects overhead.
cases.history: A 48-year-old woman presents with 3 months of gradually worsening weakness in her shoulders and hips. She struggles to climb stairs, rise from a low chair, lift groceries to a shelf, and wash her hair. She denies pain as the primary problem, though her muscles feel mildly achy. There is no ptosis, double vision, dysphagia for now, or shortness of breath, and no rash. No numbness, tingling, or bowel/bladder changes. Symptoms are symmetric and proximal; she does not fatigue worse at day's end the way one classically describes for a neuromuscular-junction disorder. PMH: hypothyroidism on stable levothyroxine, with a normal recent TSH. Medications: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; no statin. Allergies: none. Social: schoolteacher, nonsmoker. Family history: a sister with rheumatoid arthritis. Review of systems negative for fever, weight loss, Raynaud phenomenon, and skin changes; she has noted some new mild difficulty arising from squatting.
cases.exam: Alert, well-appearing woman. Strength: symmetric proximal weakness—deltoids and hip flexors 4-/5, neck flexors 4/5; distal strength 5/5; grip preserved. She rises from a chair using her arms. No muscle tenderness to speak of, no atrophy or fasciculations. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric; sensation intact throughout; coordination and gait normal aside from proximal weakness. Skin: NO heliotrope rash, NO Gottron papules, no shawl sign, no mechanic's hands. No ptosis; extraocular movements full; no fatigable weakness on sustained upgaze. Lungs clear; no crackles. Cardiac exam normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Symmetric proximal weakness without rash, sensory loss, or fatigability most suggests a myopathy. What is the best initial laboratory test?
options: Acetylcholine receptor antibodies
Serum creatine kinase
Nerve conduction velocities only
Lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: In suspected inflammatory myopathy, serum creatine kinase is the best initial test and is typically elevated, reflecting muscle fiber injury; it helps separate myopathy from neuromuscular-junction and neuropathic causes and tracks disease activity. Acetylcholine receptor antibodies test for myasthenia gravis, which features fatigable weakness and ocular/bulbar involvement—absent here. Nerve conduction velocities alone assess nerves, not the muscle inflammation suspected; needle EMG (not NCV alone) is the relevant electrodiagnostic study. Lumbar puncture evaluates CNS/CSF disease and is not indicated for a proximal myopathy. A high CK directs the workup toward an inflammatory myopathy and triggers EMG, antibodies, and possibly biopsy.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Creatine kinase 4,200 U/L
Aldolase elevated
ALT 70 U/L, AST 95 U/L (muscle source)
TSH 1.8 µIU/mL (normal)
ESR 38 mm/hr
ANA positive 1:160; anti-Jo-1 pending
EMG: short-duration, low-amplitude motor unit potentials with early recruitment, fibrillations and positive sharp waves (irritable myopathy)
Muscle MRI: symmetric proximal muscle edema

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the elevated CK, irritable myopathy on EMG, proximal weakness, and the complete absence of a rash, which diagnosis is most likely?
options: Dermatomyositis
Polymyositis
Myasthenia gravis
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
stages.explanation: Polymyositis presents with symmetric proximal muscle weakness, elevated CK/aldolase, an irritable myopathy on EMG, and no characteristic skin findings. Dermatomyositis shares the muscle picture but is defined by cutaneous signs—heliotrope rash, Gottron papules, shawl sign—which are explicitly absent here. Myasthenia gravis causes fatigable, fluctuating weakness with ocular/bulbar features and a normal CK, not an irritable myopathy. ALS is a motor neuron disease with mixed upper and lower motor neuron signs (atrophy, fasciculations, hyperreflexia) and a neurogenic, not myopathic, EMG. The myopathic EMG plus CK elevation without rash points to polymyositis (biopsy confirms endomysial inflammation).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line pharmacologic treatment for polymyositis?
options: High-dose systemic corticosteroids
Pyridostigmine
Intravenous immunoglobulin as monotherapy first-line
Plasmapheresis
stages.explanation: High-dose corticosteroids are the first-line treatment for polymyositis, with a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant (e.g., methotrexate or azathioprine) typically added early to allow steroid tapering and limit toxicity. Pyridostigmine treats myasthenia gravis by boosting neuromuscular transmission and has no role in an inflammatory myopathy. IVIG is an effective adjunct or used in refractory or dysphagia-threatening disease, but it is not the routine first-line monotherapy. Plasmapheresis is not an established effective therapy for polymyositis. Starting corticosteroids—while screening for malignancy and monitoring CK and strength—is the correct initial step.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: While she is immunosuppressed and weak, which rehabilitation approach is most appropriate?
options: Strict bed rest until strength fully returns
Maximal high-intensity resistance training immediately to rebuild muscle
A supervised, progressive exercise program—gentle range-of-motion and submaximal strengthening that advances as inflammation is controlled
No exercise, because activity always raises CK and worsens myositis
stages.explanation: Contemporary evidence shows that supervised, graded exercise—starting with range-of-motion and submaximal strengthening and progressing as inflammation comes under control—improves function and does not flare disease in inflammatory myopathy; rehabilitation is integral alongside immunosuppression. Strict bed rest causes deconditioning, contractures, and disuse atrophy that compound the myopathic weakness. Immediate maximal high-intensity resistance training during active inflammation is excessive and not appropriate as the starting point. The old belief that any exercise harms myositis is outdated; controlled, progressive activity is beneficial. Pairing immunosuppression with a thoughtfully advanced exercise program optimizes recovery of strength and function.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Beyond muscle weakness, which associated condition should prompt evaluation given her age and diagnosis?
options: Underlying occult malignancy
Multiple sclerosis
Diabetes insipidus
Peripheral arterial disease
stages.explanation: Inflammatory myopathies—most strongly dermatomyositis, but also polymyositis—carry an increased risk of underlying malignancy, so age- and sex-appropriate cancer screening and a targeted evaluation guided by history and symptoms are warranted around the time of diagnosis and during early follow-up. Multiple sclerosis is a demyelinating CNS disease that presents with relapsing focal neurologic deficits and is unrelated to this proximal myopathy. Diabetes insipidus, a posterior pituitary disorder of water handling, has no association with myositis. Peripheral arterial disease is a vascular condition not linked to inflammatory myopathy. Recognizing the paraneoplastic association and completing appropriate malignancy screening is a high-yield, potentially life-saving step in any adult with a newly diagnosed inflammatory myopathy.

cases.diagnosis: Polymyositis (idiopathic inflammatory myopathy)
pearls: Symmetric proximal weakness with elevated CK and an irritable myopathy on EMG, without a rash, suggests polymyositis (rash = dermatomyositis).
Distinguish from myasthenia (fatigable, normal CK) and ALS (neurogenic EMG, mixed UMN/LMN signs).
First-line therapy is corticosteroids with an early steroid-sparing agent; screen for occult malignancy.
Supervised, progressive exercise improves function and does not flare disease—rehab is part of treatment.

cases.title: Schoolboy Who Tires Climbing Stairs but Reads Ahead of His Class
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report increasing weakness, frequent falls, and trouble climbing stairs in an otherwise bright child.
cases.history: A 6-year-old boy is evaluated for difficulty climbing stairs, frequent falls, and trouble running and jumping that have slowly worsened over the past two years. He walked at 15 months and has always been a bit clumsy, but he is academically advanced and socially typical. Parents notice a fine tremor of his hands and that he tires on long walks, requiring carrying. There is no loss of milestones, no regression of speech or cognition, and no sensory complaints, seizures, or bladder/bowel issues. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Birth history: term, normal. Social: kindergarten, mainstreamed, doing well academically. Family history: a sister died in infancy of a 'floppy baby' condition with breathing failure; consanguinity is denied. Review of systems notable for proximal weakness greater than distal and easy fatigue, with preserved intellect and a notably bright, engaged demeanor.
cases.exam: Alert, articulate, cognitively bright boy. Gait: waddling with hyperlordosis; mild Gowers maneuver when rising. Strength: symmetric proximal weakness, hip and shoulder girdle 3+/5, distal strength 4+/5; neck flexors mildly weak. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished to absent throughout (areflexia). A fine postural hand tremor (polyminimyoclonus) is noted. Tongue is examined—occasional fasciculations seen; no facial weakness, no ptosis, full extraocular movements. Sensation intact. No calf pseudohypertrophy. Mild contracture-free joints; spine straight. No respiratory distress; cough strong. Cardiac exam normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Symmetric proximal weakness, areflexia, tongue fasciculations, a fine tremor, and preserved intellect with a family history of a fatal 'floppy baby' point to which category of disease?
options: A dystrophinopathy (e.g., Duchenne)
An anterior horn cell (motor neuron) disorder—spinal muscular atrophy
A demyelinating peripheral neuropathy
A neuromuscular-junction disorder (e.g., myasthenia)
stages.explanation: Areflexia with tongue fasciculations, polyminimyoclonus (fine tremor), symmetric proximal weakness, and preserved cognition reflect degeneration of anterior horn cells—spinal muscular atrophy. Fasciculations and areflexia are signs of lower motor neuron loss, not a myopathy. Duchenne would show calf pseudohypertrophy, markedly elevated CK, and is X-linked (affecting boys, not a sister), without tongue fasciculations. A demyelinating neuropathy causes distal-predominant weakness with sensory involvement, not pure proximal motor loss with fasciculations. Myasthenia produces fatigable, fluctuating weakness with ocular/bulbar features and normal reflexes. The lower-motor-neuron signs with intact intellect localize to the anterior horn cell.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate confirmatory test for suspected spinal muscular atrophy?
options: Muscle biopsy
SMN1 gene deletion/mutation testing
Repetitive nerve stimulation
Serum creatine kinase alone
stages.explanation: Spinal muscular atrophy is caused by biallelic loss of function of the SMN1 gene (typically homozygous deletion), so molecular genetic testing for SMN1 is the confirmatory test of choice and also assesses SMN2 copy number, which modifies severity. Muscle biopsy can show neurogenic atrophy but is invasive and no longer necessary when genetic testing is diagnostic. Repetitive nerve stimulation tests neuromuscular-junction disorders like myasthenia, not anterior horn cell disease. CK may be mildly elevated but is nonspecific and cannot confirm SMA. Genetic confirmation via SMN1 also enables disease-modifying therapy decisions and genetic counseling for the family.

stages.label: GENETICS & STUDIES
stages.content:
SMN1: homozygous deletion of exon 7 — confirmatory
SMN2 copy number: 3 copies
Creatine kinase 280 U/L (mildly elevated)
EMG: neurogenic pattern—large-amplitude, long-duration motor units, fibrillations, reduced recruitment
Pulmonary function: mild restrictive pattern, FVC 85% predicted
Spine radiograph: Cobb angle 18°, monitor

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: This child achieves independent ambulation but tires easily (consistent with type III SMA). What is the primary goal and approach of rehabilitation management?
options: Aggressive eccentric high-load strengthening to reverse weakness
Bed rest to prevent overuse of weak muscles
Maximize and maintain function and independence—appropriate exercise/activity, contracture prevention, energy conservation, and prescription of adaptive equipment and mobility aids as needed
Immediate spinal fusion
stages.explanation: SMA type III is a slowly progressive, milder form with achieved ambulation; rehabilitation focuses on maintaining and maximizing function and independence—submaximal aerobic and functional exercise to limits of tolerance, stretching and contracture prevention, energy conservation, and timely provision of orthoses, mobility aids, and adaptive equipment as needs evolve. Aggressive high-load eccentric strengthening can cause overwork weakness and will not reverse motor neuron loss. Bed rest accelerates deconditioning and contractures. Spinal fusion is reserved for progressive scoliosis crossing operative thresholds, not at an 18° curve. Function maintenance, supportive equipment, and multidisciplinary care—plus disease-modifying SMN-targeted therapy—define management.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which surveillance issue is most important to monitor over time in this ambulatory child with SMA type III?
options: Cognitive decline
Renal function
Premature coronary disease
Progressive scoliosis and respiratory (restrictive lung) function
stages.explanation: In SMA, progressive scoliosis and decline in respiratory (restrictive) lung function are the key long-term surveillance concerns, particularly as weakness advances and especially after loss of ambulation; serial spine radiographs and pulmonary function testing guide bracing/surgery and respiratory support. Cognition is characteristically preserved in SMA, so cognitive decline is not expected. Renal function is not specifically affected by SMA. Premature coronary disease is unrelated. Monitoring the spine and lungs—and supporting the family—best preserves long-term function and quality of life in this disorder.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The parents ask why their bright son has such severe weakness while the sister who died was far more affected. What best explains the variability in SMA severity?
options: The number of SMN2 gene copies, which produces some functional SMN protein and modifies severity
Differences in dystrophin levels
Maternal mitochondrial inheritance
Acquired environmental toxin exposure
stages.explanation: SMA severity is strongly modified by the number of SMN2 gene copies: SMN2 produces a small amount of functional SMN protein, so more SMN2 copies generally yield milder disease (this boy has 3 copies and a milder type III course), explaining variability among affected siblings. Dystrophin is the protein deficient in muscular dystrophies, not SMA. SMA is autosomal recessive (SMN1), not maternally/mitochondrially inherited. It is a genetic, not toxin-acquired, disease. SMN2 copy number as a disease modifier is the high-yield explanation for the phenotypic spectrum and informs prognosis and therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Spinal muscular atrophy type III (SMN1 deletion, 3 SMN2 copies)
pearls: Areflexia, tongue fasciculations, fine tremor (polyminimyoclonus), proximal weakness, and preserved intellect localize to the anterior horn cell—suspect SMA.
Confirm with SMN1 deletion testing; SMN2 copy number modifies severity and prognosis.
Rehab in type III aims to maintain function and independence with appropriate exercise, contracture prevention, and adaptive equipment—avoid overwork weakness.
Monitor scoliosis and restrictive lung function over time; cognition is preserved.
